segments.s651-g681: کنون (now) is an adverb that opens the line setting the temporal frame. ما آمدیم is past tense, 1st-person plural (آمدیم = we came), with the prepositional phrase به نزد شما (به + نزد + شما) meaning 'to/into the presence of you'—نزد here means 'in the presence of.' Word order is typical: temporal adverb + subject + directional prepositional phrase + verb.
segments.s651-g682: This clause lists the things the speakers have set up. سراپرده, گاه, خیمه are three nouns in a simple coordinate series linked by و; زدیم is the past 1st-person plural of زدن used here in the idiomatic sense 'to set up/pitch' (classical Persian often uses زدن with tents). The sequence functions as a compound object of زدیم: 'we put up/struck (pitched) inner tents and (other) encampments.'
segments.s651-g683: در و غار is a coordinated noun phrase ('doors/gates and caves'). جای کمین شماست is a nominal predicate: جای (place) + کمین (ambush) + شماست (شما + ست, the copula) meaning 'are the place of your ambush.' The copula ست attaches after شما to indicate 'is/are yours.'
segments.s651-g684: Another coordinated list: بر و بوم و کوه و زمین forms the subject and is followed by شماست (your + is), so the whole means 'the roofs/abodes and the homeland and mountains and land are yours.' Note the poetic clustering of landscape nouns; بر and بوم together form a set-phrase for territory.
segments.s651-g685: فرستاده آمد بگفت is a compact sequence: فرستاده (the messenger) + آمد (he came) + بگفت (he said). بگفت is an archaic/simple past reporting verb 'said' used in narrative to introduce reported speech. این سخن ('this speech/word') is the direct object that introduces what follows.
segments.s651-g686: که here introduces the content of the report ('that'). سالار ایران is the noun phrase 'the commander/lord of Iran.' The clause ends with the poetic inversion چه افگند بن where افگند (past of افکندن 'to cast/lay') is the verb and بن (foundation/root) is the object; the order is inverted for poetic effect: literally 'what foundation he cast/laid' (= what foundation he established or what ruin he brought).
segments.s651-g687: سپاه الانی شدند انجمن: سپاه (army) is the subject; الانی is an adverbial/temporal modifier 'at once' or 'immediately'; شدند is the past 3rd‑person plural of شدن 'became/assembled'; انجمن is the predicate noun 'assembly/meeting.' Together: 'the army immediately became an assembly' (i.e., gathered into council).
segments.s651-g688: This noun phrase specifies who composed the assembly: بزرگان فرزانه و رای زن — بزرگان فرزانه 'the great/elder wise ones' (bigger/older and wise), and رای زن is a compound meaning 'those who give counsel' (رای = counsel/plan and زن here functions to indicate the agentive notion 'one who gives/strikes out counsel' in older usage). The word order places the epithets after the head noun, a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s651-g329: نبد is an archaic/past negative existential (≈ 'there was not' / 'there was no one'); دادگرتر is the comparative of دادگر ('more just'). The preposition ز here functions as the poetic comparative particle 'than', so the phrase literally reads ‘There was no one more just than Nushin‑ravan.’
segments.s651-g330: که introduces a wish-clause; بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may (it be)'. همیشه is an adverb 'always', روانش is 'his/its soul, spirit' with the possessive suffix -ش, and جوان is a predicate adjective 'young' — together: 'may his spirit always be young.'
segments.s651-g331: نه is a negation ('nor/not'), and the preposition ز plus the enclitic و together form زُو (زو) meaning 'than him' (poetic comparative). پرهنرتر is a comparative adjective 'more learned/cultured/skilled'. به فرزانگی uses به to mark the domain 'in/with respect to' plus فرزانگی 'wisdom' — 'not was there anyone more learned than he in wisdom.'
segments.s651-g332: Repeated به marks spheres or respects ('in/regarding'): به تخت 'in rule/throne', و links items, بداد here is listed as a nominal quality (contextually 'justice' or 'giving of justice'), and به مردانگی 'in manliness/valor' — a standard poetic catalogue of qualities in which no one surpassed him.
segments.s651-g333: ورا is a dative/locative 'to him' (indicating possession or relation). موبدی بود is an existential/past clause 'there was a mobed' (موبدی = Zoroastrian priest; بود = was). بابک بنام is an appositive 'Babak by name / called Babak' (بنام = 'called/named').
segments.s651-g334: A string of epithets in apposition describing Babak: هشیوار 'alert/sane', دانادل 'wise‑hearted' (a compound epithet), and شادکام 'fortunate' — coordinated by و.
segments.s651-g335: بدو is 'to him' (dative). داد here is the past tense of دادن 'gave' (he gave/assigned). دیوان refers to the diwan/administration/royal office; عرض and سپاه are coordinated items in the catalogue (عرض can mean 'presentation' or 'petition' but here functions as part of the list), so the clause lists what was given/entrusted to him (diwan, 'arz/affairs', and the army).
segments.s651-g336: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/ordered'; تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that/so that'), پیش درگاه is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of the court', and شاه is 'the king' — 'and he ordered (them) brought before the king's presence.'
segments.s651-g697: رخ (subject) + نامداران is a genitive phrase 'the faces of the renowned'; ازان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'from that' referring back to the preceding event; تیره گشت is the verbal predicate 'became dark' where گشت is the (archaic/poetic) past/copular verb meaning 'became'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: literal order 'the faces of the renowned from that darkened'.
segments.s651-g698: دل is the (collective) subject 'the heart/hearts'; از here has a causal force 'because of' (it introduces the cause); نام is 'reputation/fame' and نوشین‌روان is an epithet in the genitive modifying نام ('the fame of the sweet‑souled one'); خیره گشت is the predicate where خیره is an adjectival/stative 'astonished/stunned' and گشت again is the past/copular 'became'.
segments.s651-g699: بزرگان is the plural noun 'the great ones' or 'nobles'; آن مرز is a determiner + noun 'that border/region' (آن points to the place just mentioned); و connects to کنداوران 'and the investigators/explorers' (کنداوران is a plural agent noun). The phrase lists two groups as the sentence's subject.
segments.s651-g700: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went/departed'; با is the preposition 'with'; باژ is a noun 'cry/noise' (they left amid cries); و is 'and'; ساو here is presented as a place-name (Saveh) in the manuscript's sense list; گران is an adjective 'heavy/weighty' following ساو and modifying it or forming a poetic compound. The clause is compact and poetic: 'they departed with cries and (to) Saveh, heavy/weighty'.
segments.s651-g701: همه is a distributive/collective 'all' modifying the following list of items; جامه 'garment', برده 'slave' and سیم و زر 'silver and gold' are coordinated nouns (و = 'and'). The list is a catalogue of goods; word order is straightforward noun coordination.
segments.s651-g702: گرانمایه is an adjective/noun meaning 'precious/valuable' modifying اسبان 'horses'; بسیار here functions as 'many' (quantity) modifying اسبان; مر is an enclitic/object pronoun 'to me' (archaic), so the whole phrase says that many valuable horses were (brought) to me. The verb is implied from context (they brought/placed).
segments.s651-g703: از ایشان 'of/from them' sets the domain; هر آنکس که 'every one who/ whoever' introduces a relative clause; پیران is the predicate within that clause 'were elders' and بدند is the archaic past/plural copular/auxiliary 'they were' (used here to state membership: 'who were elders'). Word order is standard for a relative clause introduced by هر آنکس که.
segments.s651-g704: سخن‌گوی 'speaker/eloquent one' and دانش‌پذیران 'the teachable/learners' are predicates that describe those elders; بدند repeats the past/plural copular 'they were'. The coordination 'سخن‌گوی و دانش‌پذیران بدند' states two qualities they possessed: eloquence and receptivity to knowledge.
segments.s651-g1457: چنین بود is a literary inversion where the demonstrative/adverb 'چنین' (thus) precedes the past copula 'بود' (was), giving an emphatic, summative tone: 'thus was...'. آن is a demonstrative determiner 'that/the', coming before the noun شاه (king). خسرونژاد is a post-nominal epithet attached to شاه meaning 'of Khosrow's lineage' (a compound name), so the whole phrase reads 'Thus was that king, scion of Khosrow.'
segments.s651-g1458: بیاراسته and بد are adjectival descriptors (poetic sequence) characterizing the subject introduced earlier (the king or his rule): بیاراسته = 'unadorned / ill-ordered' and بد = 'bad / evil'. جهان را is the definite object ('the world') marked by the object marker را. بداد here names the action of injustice/oppression (the lexeme is nominal in form in the gloss but is used like a past action in classical/epic Persian), so with the preceding subject it means 'he brought/inflicted oppression upon the world.' The actor is supplied by the subject in the previous segment; the clause is past and concise in epic style.
segments.s651-g1257: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the plural noun دروازها ('gates'); جنگ is the direct object/noun 'battle' and برساختند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb ('they raised/made'). The verb is prefixed (بر‑) plus ساختن in past plural, so the clause literally reads 'from the gates battle they-made' = 'they raised battle from the gates.'
segments.s651-g1258: همه ('all/everyone') comes before the coordinated objects تیر و قاروره ('arrows and flasks/vials'); انداختند is the past 3rd-person-plural 'they threw/hurled.' The coordination uses و 'and' in the normal place; overall word order is subject/quantifier → objects → verb.
segments.s651-g1259: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; خورشید تابنده is a noun + participial adjective ('the shining sun') serving as subject, and برگشت is the past verb 'turned/returned.' زرد is a predicate adjective following the verb ('became pale/yellow'). This is a typical Persian intransitive structure: temporal marker → subject → verb → predicate adjective.
segments.s651-g1260: ز is the preposition 'from,' followed by گردنده ('turning/rotating' as an adjectival/participial phrase). یک بهره ('one share/portion') is the phrase that functions as subject of شد ('became'), a past 3rd-person-singular copula; لاژورد is the predicative noun 'lapis/azure.' Word order is prepositional phrase → numeric phrase → copula → predicate.
segments.s651-g1261: ازان باره is a temporal/ablative phrase 'from that time/after that occasion.' دز ('thief') is the (here indefinite/collective) subject and نماند is the past negative sense 'did not remain' (literally 'remained-not'). اندکی ('a little') qualifies the remainder — effectively 'not even a little remained.' The order is temporal phrase → subject → negative verb → quantity adverb.
segments.s651-g1262: همه شارستان ('the whole town') is the subject; با زمی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with the earth' or 'into earth/dust' (poetic locution), شد is the past copula 'became,' and یکی is the predicate 'one.' The clause therefore reads 'the whole town with earth became one' = 'the town turned into one with the earth.'
segments.s651-g1263: خروشی ('a roar/clamor') is the noun subject and برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز introduces the source 'from' and درگاه شاه is the genitive 'the king's court/presence.' The noun+verb + source order yields 'a clamor arose from the king's court.'
segments.s651-g1264: که‌ای here functions as a poetic/vocative particle ('O!') addressing the following phrase; نامداران ایران سپاه is the vocative: 'renowned ones, the army of Iran.' This is an example of an archaic/poetic vocative construction where the particle precedes the title being addressed.
segments.s651-g729: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دریای هندوستان is a genitive construction 'the Sea of India' (دریای + proper name). تا introduces a limit 'as far as' and governs the following میز—here میل is the distance unit 'mil' (not listed in the provided senses), so the phrase means 'from the Indian Sea as far as two mils.'
segments.s651-g730: درم بود is a simple past copular clause 'there were dirhams' (درم = dirhams, بود = was/were). با introduces the accompanying items 'with' and links the following nouns (هدیه, اسب, پیل) as instruments or accompaniment; the final nouns are coordinated by و and listed as things present with the money.
segments.s651-g731: بزرگان همه پیش شاه آمدند is SOV with some flexibility: بزرگان 'the great ones' (subject), همه 'all/everyone' intensifies it, پیش شاه 'in the presence of the king' (prepositional phrase), آمدند is the 3rd person plural past 'they came'.
segments.s651-g732: ز marks source or cause 'from/with' and heads the phrase 'ز دوده دل' (literally 'from soot of heart' — a poetic expression indicating troubled or darkened heart). و links نیک‌خواه 'well-wisher' as an additional description; آمدند repeats the past plural 'they came', coordinating the two ways in which they came.
segments.s651-g733: بپرسید is a past 3rd‑person form 'he asked'. The following کسری is the subject 'Kisra' (postposed for poetic word order). و connects the next verb; بنواختشان is a past form with the enclitic شان 'them' meaning 'he caused (music) to be played for them' or 'he struck/played for them.'
segments.s651-g734: براندازه here is an adverbial phrase 'in proper measure/accordingly'. بر پایگه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the pedestal/platform/dais' (بر + پایگه). ساختشان is past 'he made/set them' with the object pronoun شان attached, so together 'he placed/set them on the dais in due order.'
segments.s651-g735: به دل شاد is an adverbial expression 'with a glad heart' where به functions adverbially ('in a ... way' / 'with'), دل 'heart' is the noun, and شاد 'happy/glad' modifies it. برگشت is the past 'he returned' and ز آن جایگاه 'from that place' gives the origin of his return.
segments.s651-g736: جهانی here denotes 'worldly' or 'a world/realm' and combines with پر از 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of') to describe the place's contents; the coordinated nouns اسب، پیل و سپاه list the things it is full of: horses, elephants and an army (سپاه).
segments.s651-g233: This is a series of locative phrases introduced by the preposition به repeated for emphasis/listing (literary style). به خشکی / به خاک each mark location ('to/on the dry land' / 'to/on the earth'), then بکشتی is a fused expression meaning 'by ship' (ب- prefix + کشتی) and is followed by برآب 'on/over the water'. The sequence lists all places where something can happen.
segments.s651-g234: برخشنده is an adjective 'radiant/shining' modifying روز 'day' (poetic adjective-before-noun order here). و links the clauses. به هنگام is a fixed prepositional phrase 'at the time of' and خواب is the noun 'sleep' (i.e. 'at bedtime' or 'while asleep').
segments.s651-g235: ز introduces a comparison/contrast here ('than' / 'beyond' in poetic usage). بازارگانان‌تر uses the comparative -تر attached to بازارگانان (literally 'more/greater than the merchants' or 'surpassing merchants'—the adjective being implied). و ز خشک continues the same comparative construction with خشک ('the dry' i.e. the austere/ascetic). The comparative quality is implied rather than spelled out.
segments.s651-g236: درم دارد is a straightforward nominal phrase: درم 'dirham(s)' + دارد 'has/possesses' (present). The next در is the preposition 'in' (or 'among/within') introducing the goods 'خوشاب و مشک' — خوشاب 'pleasant drink/sweet wine' and مشک 'musk' — i.e. he possesses wealth both as coin and in luxury goods.
segments.s651-g237: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). تابنده خور is 'the shining sun' (تابنده adjective before خور 'sun'). جز بداد means 'except/other than bad/giving oppression' (جز 'except' + بداد 'oppression'); و به مهر 'and with favor' sets up the contrast that follows.
segments.s651-g238: نتابد is the negative present form of تابیدن 'to shine' (in classical/poetic Persian this present-negative form expresses 'does not/cannot shine'). بریشان is بر + ایشان 'upon them' (preposition + pronoun). ز خم سپهر is a locative/source phrase 'from the curve/bend of the sky' (ز 'from' + خم 'curve/bend' + سپهر 'sky'), describing the expected source of the sun's rays that, here, do not favor them.
segments.s651-g239: برین‌گونه 'in this manner' (adverbial) + رفت is the past tense of رفتن here meaning 'went/vanished/was gone' (poetic sense 'passed away'). از نژاد و گهر is a genitive-like phrase 'of/from the lineage and the jewel' — i.e. 'the line (and its jewel) disappeared.'
segments.s651-g240: This is a simple declarative clause: پسر 'the son' (subject) + تاج یابد همی 'still/always obtains the crown' — یابد is the present subjunctive/indicative of یافتن 'to obtain/attain' used here as 'receives/attains', and همی is an adverb 'still/always/emphatic'. از پدر 'from the father' marks source/possession transfer.
segments.s651-g345: خرامید is a past-tense verb (3rd person) meaning 'strode' used in poetic word order (verb before subject). یک‌یک is an adverbial phrase 'one by one / each' modifying the verb. به درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase (به + noun) indicating destination ('into/to the king's court/presence'), with درگاه 'royal court/presence' and شاه 'king'.
segments.s651-g346: به سر برنهاده is a participial/compound phrase meaning 'having placed on (their) head(s)' — به + سر marks the body‑part/dative and برنهاده (from بر نهادن) is a past participle 'placed/put on'. ز آهن کلاه is a genitive/material phrase 'a helmet of iron' where ز (poetic 'from/of') marks material and کلاه is 'hat/helmet'. The order is compact/poetic: the thing placed (کلاه ز آهن) follows the participial phrase.
segments.s651-g347: زره‌دار is a noun 'armored man/armored warrior'. با introduces an instrumental/associative phrase 'with', so با گرزه means 'with a mace'. گاوسار follows as an appositive (a proper name) identifying that armored man; poetic lines often list a noun plus its name in this way.
segments.s651-g348: کسی is 'someone/one'. کو here functions as an archaic/poetic relative particle 'who' (classical Persian), introducing the clause 'who asks for a dirham'. درم خواهد is the verb phrase: درم 'dirham' (object) and خواهد (3rd person present/future of خواستن) 'wants/asks for'. از شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'from the sovereign/king' marking source. (Note: 'کو' is an older relative particle, not the locative 'where'.)
segments.s651-g349: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came'. به ایوان بابک is a destination phrase: به + ایوان ('iwan/porch') with بابک in postpositive genitive 'Babak's iwan'. سپاه ('army/host') is the subject placed after the verb in poetic inversion (V–S order is common in classical Persian).
segments.s651-g350: هوا شد is an impersonal/indefinite expression 'a mood/atmosphere/stir arose' (هوا here = atmosphere/mood and شد = 'became/occurred'). ز گرد سواران سیاه marks the source: ز (from) + گرد (around/about) + سواران سیاه 'the black horsemen' — literally 'from around the black horsemen'.
segments.s651-g351: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بابک is the subject. سپه را is the definite/direct-object-marked noun 'the army/host' (را marks definiteness/emphasis), and همه modifies سپه meaning 'the whole/entire host'. بنگرید is an older/poetic past form of 'to look/regard' (here 'looked over/inspected'); the verb appears after the conjunction and before the subject–object in typical classical word order.
segments.s651-g352: This is the clause of result/observation: درفش 'the standard/banner' and سر تاج کسری 'the (head/top of the) crown of Khosrow' are the coordinated objects. ندید is past negative 'did not see'. کسری is a genitive modifying تاج (Khosrow's crown). سر here functions as 'top/head' (poetic compound: 'سر تاج' = crown itself or the headpiece of the crown).
segments.s651-g713: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase (به/بر + ایشان) meaning “to/against them” placed before the verb for emphasis. ببخشود is an archaic 3rd-person past form (“he forgave/granted”) and is the main verb; بیدار شاه (lit. “the awake/alert king”) follows as the subject/epithet — poetry frequently inverts the normal subject–verb order.
segments.s651-g714: ببخشید here repeats the verb idea in a slightly different form and means “he forgave.” یک سر is an adverbial compound (یک + سر = یک‌سر) meaning “entirely/wholly.” گذشته گناه is a noun phrase “past sin(s)” — together: “he wholly pardoned the sins of the past.”
segments.s651-g715: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning “he commanded/declared.” تا introduces the subordinate clause with the sense “that/so that.” هرچ = “whatever/anything that,” ویران = “ruined/desolated,” and شدست is an archaic 3rd‑person perfective/resultative (“has become/was become”) closing the subordinate clause.
segments.s651-g716: کنام is a noun meaning “bosom/seat/lair” (here: “the haunt/lair of …”). پلنگان و شیران are coordinated nouns “leopards and lions,” and شدست again is the archaic 3rd‑person resultative “became.” The whole unit says that those ruined places became the lairs of leopards and lions.
segments.s651-g717: یکی here means “a certain” (indefinite), شارستانی is “townsman/citizen,” and برآرند is a 3rd‑person plural verbal form meaning “they brought/produced.” زود is an adverb “quickly/soon,” placed after the verb for brisk, poetic cadence.
segments.s651-g718: بدو is به + او = “to him/unto him” (indirect-object adverb). اندرون جای is a genitive/grouping meaning “an inner place/spot.” کشت here means “cultivation/tilling” rather than killing, and درود is “greeting/blessing” — together the phrase indicates a place assigned for sowing/cultivation and for receiving salutations.
segments.s651-g719: یکی باره‌ای is an adverbial phrase “once/on one occasion” (یکی = one, باره‌ای = instance/occasion). گردش is “a circuit/tour/patrol,” اندر = “in/within,” and بلند = “high/lofty,” describing the nature of that circuit.
segments.s651-g720: بدان here is the compound sense به + آن (“to/at that place” or “there”), used adverbially to point to the location just mentioned. تا introduces purpose “so that/in order that”; ز دشمن = “from the enemy”; نیابد is an archaic negative-subjunctive/optative form of یافتن (“may not find/receive”), and گزند means “harm.” Together: “therein, so that he might not suffer harm from the enemy.”
segments.s651-g1345: بکردیم is a past-tense first-person-plural verb ('we did/made'); تا introduces purpose ('so that' / in order that). هر کسی را uses هر + کسی ('each/every person') with را as the definite/object/affected-marker (marks the person who receives or is arranged for). به کام is a prepositional phrase ('to/for the desire') modifying what follows — literally 'to the wish of'.
segments.s651-g1346: یکی جای is an indefinite nominal phrase ('a certain place' / 'one place'); جای 'place' is the head noun. باشد here is the classical present/subjunctive/optative form of 'be' (used like 'may be' / 'should be' / 'would be'), linking the phrase to the purpose set by تا. سزاوار نام is a postpositive adjective phrase ('worthy/fit of a name') — سزاوار modifies جای and نام ('name' in the sense of reputation).
segments.s651-g1347: ببخشید is a past/perfective verb here meaning 'they gave/bestowed' (classical narrative past). بر introduces the beneficiary/recipient ('upon/to') and distributes over the following list; هر کسی is 'every person' and خواسته is a noun meaning 'the desired / what was wished for' (object meaning 'his wish').
segments.s651-g1348: زمین is the subject 'the earth/land' (here 'land/country'). چون introduces a comparison ('like/as'); بهشتی is an adjective 'heavenly, paradisal'. شد is the past of 'become' and takes آراسته (a past participle/adjectival predicate) to yield 'became adorned' — i.e. 'the land became adorned as if a paradise.'
segments.s651-g1349: ز بس is a poetic compound meaning 'because of so much' or 'from so great an amount' (ز = 'because/from', بس = 'much/enough'). بر governs the following coordinated list, so بر زن و کوی و بازارگاه means 'on/upon women and lanes and marketplaces' (i.e., concerning or directed at those things).
segments.s651-g1350: تو گفتی here functions as 'you said/declared' (classical past 2nd-person), with گفتی introducing the reported content. نماندست is a classical past/perfect negative verbal form meaning 'did not remain / was not left' (the -ست is an older enclitic); بر خاک راه is a prepositional phrase 'on the dust of the road' (بر + خاک + راه), giving the location affected.
segments.s651-g1351: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' (verb before subject in narrative style). یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain' (introducing an unspecified person). پرسخن is an adjective/compound 'much-speaking' / 'talkative' modifying کفشگر 'shoemaker' — together 'a certain talkative shoemaker came.'
segments.s651-g1352: چنین گفت is a formulaic narrative phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت = past 'said'). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; شاه بیدادگر is the noun phrase 'king' qualified by the adjective/noun بیدادگر 'tyrant/cruel one' — so the shoemaker begins addressing 'O tyrant king.'
segments.s651-g1169: غو is a noun 'uproar/clamour' followed by زینهاری 'of warning' (the ezāfe relation is typical but metrically unexpressed in verse), so غو زینهاری = 'the clamour/cry of warning'. و joins the next noun phrase; جوش is a noun 'surge/boiling', while زنان here is the plural participle of زدن ('those striking') and modifies جوش (i.e. 'the surge of those striking').
segments.s651-g1170: برآمد is the simple past 'arose/issued forth'. چو is the comparative/simile 'as/like'. زخم is 'wound/sore', and تبیره‌زنان is an agent compound 'axe‑strikers' (those who strike with axes); together زخم تبیره‌زنان = 'the wound inflicted by the axe‑strikers' and the whole phrase means 'arose like a wound dealt by the axe‑strikers.'
segments.s651-g1171: از ایشان = 'from/among them' (از + third‑person plural pronoun). هر آنکس که is a relative clause meaning 'whichever person who / whoever'. پرمایه is an adjective 'substantial, well‑endowed' and بود is the past copula 'was' completing the clause.
segments.s651-g1172: به گنج و به مردی repeats the preposition به to mark two domains: 'in/with respect to treasure and in manhood'. گنج here means 'treasure/wealth' and مردی is the abstract 'manliness / valor'; گرانپایه is a predicate adjective 'of high rank/high‑standing', and بود is the copula 'was' (repeated to predicate both qualities).
segments.s651-g1173: ببستند is past plural 'they bound/tied' (here transitive, i.e. they fastened something). بر پیل = 'upon the elephant' (بر marking surface/contact). و کردند بار = 'and they made (it) a load/they loaded (it)' with کردند as past plural 'they did/made' and بار 'load/cargo'; word order places the verb before object in the poetic line but the grammatical relations are as described.
segments.s651-g1174: خروش آمد is a nominative phrase 'a roar arose' (خروش 'roar/cry' + آمد 'came/rose up'). و links the following noun phrase; ناله is 'moan/cry' and زینهار here is the substantive 'beware' or 'a warning cry' — together 'and a plaintive cry of
segments.s651-g1175: نبخشود is past negative 'did not forgive/spare' (used here in the sense 'did not spare/relent'). بر کس literally 'upon anyone' = 'to anyone' (i.e. 'no one' was spared). به هنگام رزم = 'at the time of battle' (به هنگام = 'at the time of'). The clause states he showed no mercy during combat.
segments.s651-g1176: This is a negative list introduced by نه 'nor/not', with several nouns governed (or implicitly governed) by the previous verb: بر گنج دینار برگاه بزم = 'for/with respect to treasure, dinar(s), the royal court, (and) feasting.' The preposition بر recurs to mark the items; some repetitions/particles are omitted for metrical economy, a common classical poetic feature.
segments.s651-g801: ز گیلان is a prepositional phrase placed up front ('from Gilan'). هر آنکس که is a relative phrase 'whoever/any person who'; جنگی is a predicate noun/adjective 'warlike, a fighter' that links to the copula بدند. بدند is a 3rd-person plural past copula (poetic/classical usage) meaning 'they were' and here agrees with the generic 'هر آنکس' ('whoever … they were').
segments.s651-g802: This is a coordinated predicate: three adjectives joined by و and governed by the final copula بدند. هشیوار functions adverbially/adjectivally 'alert'; بارای appears as the second coordinated term; سنگی is 'stony/stone-hearted.' The final بدند is the same 3pl past copula carrying the meaning 'they were' for the whole series.
segments.s651-g803: ببستند is a 3rd-person plural past verb here meaning 'they bound/tied.' یک سر specifies manner/limit ('one end' / 'together at one point') and is placed before the object. همه دست خویش is the object phrase 'all (their) hands' with خوی + ش forming the possessive 'their own.' Word order (verb + object elements) reflects a poetic, somewhat inverted syntax.
segments.s651-g804: زنان از پس is a short locative phrase 'the women from behind' where از پس functions together as 'from behind.' و links the following phrase. کودک خرد پیش places noun + adjective + locative adverb: 'the little child(ren) in front' (کودک 'child', خرد 'small', پیش 'in front/before').
segments.s651-g805: خروشان is an adverbial/participial adjective 'tumultuous/roaring' placed before the verb to qualify the action. بر شهریار is a directional phrase 'to/toward the king' with بر used as the preposition. آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past verb 'they came.'
segments.s651-g806: Two adjectival/participial descriptors (دریده‌بر 'torn/ragged' and خاکسار 'contrite/humbly supplicating') are coordinated by و and followed by the same past verb آمدند 'they came,' which applies to both descriptors (a common poetic compression).
segments.s651-g807: شدند is the 3rd-person plural past auxiliary/verb 'they became / they were (set).' اندران is a fused locative 'in/therein' (an archaic or poetic form of اندر + آن). بارگاه انجمن is a noun phrase 'palace assembly' (بارگاه 'royal court/palace', انجمن 'assembly/meeting'), so the whole means 'they were (placed) in that palace-assembly.'
segments.s651-g808: This is a condensed nominal description: همه دستها بسته 'all (their) hands bound' and خسته تن 'and bodies weary' — adjectives/participles (بسته, خسته) describe the state of the nouns (دستها, تن). The copula is omitted because the previous verb covers these predicates; this is typical compressed poetic coordination.
segments.s651-g745: به + NP marks the indirect object/recipient (‘to the Iranians’). گفت is the past tense of گفتن (‘said’) and introduces the reported content that follows; الانان و هند are the coordinated subjects/objects of the reported clause (the Alans and India). Word order is typical Persian: indirect object phrase + reporting verb + reported items.
segments.s651-g746: شد is the past/imperfective meaning ‘became’ or ‘came to be’ and functions here as the copular/event verb for the following state. از بیم is a prepositional phrase ‘out of/from fear (of)’, شمشیر ما is the genitive ‘our sword’, and چون پرند is a simile ‘like a bird’ (چون = like; پرند = bird). The clause therefore gives the result/state caused by fear (‘became like birds’).
segments.s651-g747: بسنده + نباشیم is a negative exhortation: بسنده (sufficient) + نباشیم (lit. ‘may we not be/be not’) expresses ‘let us not be content’. با شهر خویش is a locative/possessive phrase ‘with our own city’ (شهر = city; خویش = own/one's own, here split as خوی + ـش). Word order places the negated predicate before the phrase stating what we should not be content with.
segments.s651-g748: همی is a frequentative/adverbial ‘always/continually’. شیر جوییم is present-subjunctive/indicative ‘we seek/search for a lion(s)’ (جوییم = ‘we seek’). پیچان is an adverb/adjectival participle ‘twisting/stealthy’, modifying the action, and ز میش ‘from/among the ewes’ gives the arena/target of that seeking. The clause expresses continuing action (we will keep seeking lions among the ewes).
segments.s651-g749: بدو is a dative/adverbial ‘to him’ (marks the addressee). گفت is again the past tense ‘said’ (introducing direct speech). گوینده ‘the speaker’ is the subject; کای شهریار is a vocative phrase ‘O king’ (کای as an address 'O', شهریار = king). The clause sets up who spoke and to whom.
segments.s651-g750: به پالیز is a locative phrase ‘in/at the orchard’ (به + place). گل نیست = ‘there is no flower’ (گل = flower; نیست = is not/there is not). بی‌زخم خار is a compound-like phrase meaning ‘without the wound of a thorn’ (بی‌زخم = unwounded/without wound; خار = thorn). The whole is a proverbial statement: no flower in the garden is without a thorn’s wound.
segments.s651-g751: همان مرز ‘that border’ is the topic/subject. تا بود ‘as long as it was/so long as it existed’ uses تا in the temporal/limit sense and بود (past copula) to state existence; با رنج بود ‘it was with hardship’ links the border’s existence to suffering (با + noun = with/through). The sequence says the border persisted only by means of toil.
segments.s651-g752: ز بهر is a purposive/causal phrase ‘for the sake of/for the purpose of’. پراگندن (infinitive/masdar sense here) ‘to scatter/spread’ modifies گنج ‘treasure’, and بود is the copula ‘was’. Together: the treasure existed for the purpose of being scattered (i.e., it was there to be distributed or dispersed).
segments.s651-g617: نکاهد is a classical verbal form meaning 'diminishes/lessens' (here expressed as a general/habitual or narrative present). همی is the old poetic/progressive marker meaning 'continually' or 'indeed' and emphasizes the ongoing action; رنج is the direct object 'suffering' or 'hardship'. کافزایشست is a fused predicate roughly equivalent to 'it is enough' or 'that suffices' (کافَ/کافی + است). The clause therefore states that the suffering is diminishing and that this is sufficient.
segments.s651-g618: This clause begins with the preposition به 'for/to' and the pronoun ما 'us/we', followed by برکنون 'at present' as an adverbial; جای here is a nominal 'place/time/occasion' and بخشایست is a verbal/adjectival predicate built on بخشیدن/بخشش meaning 'pardon/forgiveness' with the copula -است: 'it is (a) pardoning' → 'it is time/to grant pardon'. Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase + temporal adverb + noun + predicate.
segments.s651-g619: نباشد is the negative third‑person form (classical optative/existential sense) meaning 'there is not / may there not be'; به گیتی literally 'in the world' (به + گیتی) is an adverbial phrase; چنین جای شهر literally 'such a place/seat of city' — the adjective چنین modifies جای. The clause asserts that nowhere in the world is there such a city-place.
segments.s651-g620: گر is the conditional 'if'; از داد تو is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/from your justice' (از + داد + تو). ما بیابیم is a first‑person plural verbal form: بیابیم here is subjunctive/optative 'we may find/obtain' (the verb takes the jussive/subjunctive form after a conditional). بهر is an adverb/prepositional noun 'for/for the sake of'. The clause therefore gives the conditional: 'if by your justice we can obtain (one) for ourselves.'
segments.s651-g621: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that same' or 'the very'; آفریدون is the proper name (a legendary king); یزدان‌پرست is a compound adjective 'God‑worshipper' / 'pious'. The three words form a noun phrase ('that very Afrīdūn, the devout').
segments.s651-g622: This clause has an adverbial phrase به بد 'in misfortune/illness/adversity' (به + بد) followed by the directional بر سوی ما 'toward us'; نیازید is the verbal action 'he supplicated/prayed' or 'he stretched out (a) supplicating hand' and دست is the noun 'hand' that completes the idiom دست نیازیدن (to stretch forth the hand in supplication). Word order places the adverbial and directional elements before the verb phrase, a typical classical Persian order.
segments.s651-g623: اگر is the conditional 'if'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; بیند is the third‑person present/subjunctive of دیدن 'he sees' (classical narrative present). به رای بلند is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with a lofty counsel/plan' (به + رای + بلند). The clause sets up the hypothetical condition 'if the king sees (fits) with high counsel...'.
segments.s651-g624: به ما 'for us' (benefactive) begins the result clause. برکند is a verbal form meaning 'uproot/tear up/dislodge' (used figuratively here 'cut off'); راه دشمن is the object 'the enemy's path/way'; ببند is an imperative 'close/shut!' The clause therefore expresses the desired action: 'may he root up (cut off) the enemy's way and shut it.'
segments.s651-g201: نخستین سخن ('first speech/words') is a nominal phrase placed before the clause; چون here functions as a conjunction meaning 'as/when' introducing the temporal clause. گشایش is a verbal noun 'opening/beginning', and کنیم is the 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/hortative 'let us do/let us open' — together 'when we begin/open with the first words'.
segments.s651-g202: جهان‌آفرین را is the definite object (را marks the direct object) 'the Creator'; ستایش کنیم is a present-subjunctive/hortative phrase 'let us praise' (ستایش + کردن), with the verb following the object phrase as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g203: خردمند و بینادل is a coordinated vocative/subject ('the wise and the between-adjective/rare term'), آنرا is the object pronoun 'that/it', and شناس functions as a predicative adjective/verb-like word meaning 'knowing/aware' (command-like or hortative sense: 'let [them] know' / 'be aware').
segments.s651-g204: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'; دارد is the present tense 'has/possesses' (here 'there is/it has'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; دادار کیهان is a genitive phrase 'the Creator of the universe'; سپاس is the noun 'thanks/praise' completing the clause ('that it has praise from the Creator of the world').
segments.s651-g205: بداند is a jussive/imperative form 'let [one] know' or 'one should know'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'; هست او is the copula + pronoun 'he exists/is'; ز ما بی‌نیاز literally 'from us, self-sufficient' = 'independent of us' (ز marking separation/source).
segments.s651-g206: به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase 'near him' (به + نزدیک + pronoun); آشکارست is the fused form of آشکار است 'is manifest/evident'; راز is the noun 'secret/mystery' and functions as the thing that is manifest ('the secret is manifest near him').
segments.s651-g207: کسی را is the indirect object 'to whom' (را marking the person affected); کجا here is rhetorical 'where/to whom' (asking 'to whom/where would he give'); سرفرازی دهد is the verb phrase 'would give honor/glory' — دهد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive meaning 'he gives/would give'.
segments.s651-g208: نخستین ورا combines 'نخستین' ('the first/first place') with ورا (vora) = 'to him' (enclitic form of indirect pronoun), so 'the first to him'; بی‌نیازی دهد follows with دهد 'he gives' and بی‌نیازی 'self-sufficiency/independence' as the thing bestowed.
segments.s651-g57: A relative clause bringing in the addressees: هر آنکس که ('every one who') is followed by the copula هست and a prepositional phrase. اندرین is the literary/dialectal form of این with the prefix ان- meaning 'in this', so اندرین انجمن = 'in this assembly'. Word order is standard classical Persian: quantifier + relative + copula + locative.
segments.s651-g58: شنید here functions as an imperative/hortative addressed to the assembled people ('hear!' / 'listen!'), a concise classical form rather than the modern imperative بشنوید. این modifies the noun phrase that follows; برآورده is a participial/epithetic modifier of آواز (literally 'the voice that has been raised/uttered'), so این برآورده آواز من = 'this, my raised/uttered voice.'
segments.s651-g59: بدانید is the 2nd-person plural imperative 'know' (classical/polite). The conjunction و links the next clause; سرتاسر 'throughout' (an adverbial intensifier) modifies آگاه 'aware'. بید is an archaic form meaning 'be / be awake' (functioning like modern باشید), giving an exhortation 'be wholly aware.'
segments.s651-g60: This is another hortatory clause. همه‌ساله 'every year' sets the time frame; با + بخت means 'with fortune' (i.e. 'accompanied by luck'); همراه here works adverbially/adjectivally 'together/with' and bید repeats the archaic exhortation 'be' (i.e. 'may you be accompanied by fortune each year'). Word order places the time adverbial first, then the accompanying phrase.
segments.s651-g61: The clause begins with the relative/complementizer که 'that/for'. ما is the subject 'we'. تاجداری is an abstract noun 'kingship/sovereignty' and the construction تاجداری به سر برده‌ایم literally means 'we have carried/worn kingship on (our) head(s)' — برده‌ایم is perfect tense ('we have carried/worn'). The preposition به here marks the locus 'on the head' (classical usage: تاج به سر بردن = to wear a crown).
segments.s651-g62: Two coordinated objects of پرورده‌ایم ('we have nurtured'): بداد and خرد رای. پرورده‌ایم is perfect tense (we have fostered/nurtured). خرد رای is a compound noun phrase 'wisdom and counsel' (خرد = wisdom; رای = counsel/decision). بداد here is a lexical/orthographic difficulty in the manuscript tradition (see note): in context it functions as a noun paired with 'خرد رای' to indicate qualities fostered by the rulers; its precise sense is uncertain in the provided sense list, so treated as irregular/uncertain.
segments.s651-g63: ولیکن 'but/yet' introduces a contrast. ز دستور 'from the rule/ordinance' uses the classical genitive/prepositional z to mark source or basis. باید + infinitive (here شنید) expresses necessity: 'one must hear/heed' or 'it is necessary to heed.' شنید is the bare verbal form used after باید rather than a finite present.
segments.s651-g64: A short clause stating consequence: بد و نیک 'bad and good' (i.e. ill and well) is the coordinated subject; بی‌او 'without it/him' refers back to دستور; نیاید پدید is a two-word predicate where نیاید is a negative present/future 'does not come / will not come' and پدید 'appear/come into being' is the verb complement — together: 'does not appear' or 'will not come about.'
segments.s651-g921: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from.' The phrase 'نوشین‌روان شاه فرخ‌نژاد' strings together epithets and the noun: 'نوشین‌روان' (a compound adjective 'sweet‑souled') modifies 'شاه' (king), and 'فرخ‌نژاد' is a post‑nominal epithet meaning 'well‑born' attached to the king. Word order is typical of Persian poetry where several modifiers cluster around the noun.
segments.s651-g922: This continues the name/title: 'جهانگیر' is an epithet 'world‑conquering.' The sequence 'وزنده کن' is actually 'و زنده‌کن' (the conjunction 'و' fused to 'زنده'), where 'زنده‌' (living) plus the formative 'کن' produces the sense 'life‑giving' or 'reviver' (here an epithet). 'کیقباد' is the proper name. The whole unit is a series of epithets applied to Kay Qobad.
segments.s651-g923: 'به نزدیک' functions as a prepositional phrase meaning 'near' or 'in the vicinity of' (literally 'to/at near'). 'قیصر' is 'Caesar' and 'سرافراز' is a post‑nominal adjective 'exalted/lofty' describing him. 'روم' names his realm ('Rome/Byzantium'); in poetic prose the genitive link is often implicit, so 'قیصرِ روم' becomes 'the Caesar of Rome.'
segments.s651-g924: 'نگهبان' is the noun 'guardian/protector.' 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that/the' and introduces the phrase 'آن مرز و آباد بوم' = 'that border and prosperous land' (قابلیتِ شعرانه: 'آباد' modifies 'بوم'). The construction names the territory for which the Caesar is the guardian.
segments.s651-g925: سر نامه is a compound meaning the head of the book or preface ('سر' = the top/heading + 'نامه' = book/treatise). کرد آفرین is a simple past construction: کرد (did/made) + آفرین (acclamation/praise) — 'made praise' or 'set an opening of praise'. از نخست is a temporal modifier placed after the verb phrase, meaning 'from the outset' or 'from the first.'
segments.s651-g926: This is a verb-final clause with the object first: گرانمایگی (dignity/esteem) is what is sought; جز به یزدان is a restricting phrase 'except by/from God' (جز = except, به here = 'by/through'), and نجست is the classical/past third-person form of جستن 'to seek' (a poetic/older past form). The whole reads 'he sought dignity from none except God.'
segments.s651-g927: خداوند is the subject 'the Lord/God.' گردنده is a participial adjective ('rotator' / 'one who turns') modifying or predicatively describing خداوند, and خورشید و ماه are its objects (the things turned): 'the Lord, the one who causes the sun and moon to revolve.' The coordinated خورشید و ماه is linked by و.
segments.s651-g928: کزویست is an archaic fused construction (کز + وی + است) meaning 'which/that is from him' or 'from him it is.' It gives the source for the following nouns, so پیروزی و دستگاه ('victory and dominion/establishment') are presented as coming from him. Word order places the source-predicate before the items sourced, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s651-g49: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. برخیزد is 3rd-person singular (present/subjunctive form) meaning 'he rises/get up' and precedes its subject (شاه) because poetry often places the verb before the noun. از خواب is a standard prepositional phrase 'from sleep' and از نخست is an adverbial phrase 'from the first / from the outset' modifying the following main clause.
segments.s651-g50: ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from' and governs دشمن 'enemy'. بود is the copula in past form 'was' (here functioning as the verbal predicate for the descriptive adjectives). ایمن و تندرست are predicative adjectives joined by و: 'safe and healthy'.
segments.s651-g51: خردمند is the noun 'wise man' (subject). وز here is an archaic/poetic form — effectively a contraction of و + ز — functioning like 'and/of' or 'even from' to connect the idea; in effect it marks the following noun as the thing he lacks. خوردنی means 'what is to be eaten' (food) and بی‌نیاز is an adjectival predicate 'without need / self-sufficient'.
segments.s651-g52: فزونی here carries the sense of 'being above / having superiority' (excess/abundance over). بر + ین (برین = بر + این) gives 'over/above these'. Rنج و دردست و آز is a nominal list of negatives — 'suffering, troubles at hand, and greed' — that he surpasses or is free from. Note the old contraction برین (بر + این) is common in classical Persian.
segments.s651-g53: وگر is the conditional 'if'. شاه is the subject. با governs an abstract quality: با داد و بخشایشست means 'with justice and mercy' — بخشایشست is the older poetic combination of بخشایش + است (the copula attached), so the clause says essentially 'if the king has/acts with justice and mercy'.
segments.s651-g54: جهان is the topic 'the world'. پر ز is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر = full, followed by the preposition ز). خوبی و آسایشست are the things filling the world: 'goodness and comfort/peace' — آسایشست again shows the poetic fusion of آسایش + است (predicate 'is peace/comfort').
segments.s651-g55: وگر is the conditional 'if'. کژی 'crookedness/wrongdoing' is the direct object of آرد 'he brings' (3rd-person singular present/subjunctive used in the protasis). بداد is a nominal meaning 'injustice/oppression' and اندرون specifies the place 'within / into the inner circle/household'. The clause reads 'if he brings crookedness/oppression inside'.
segments.s651-g56: کبستش is an archaic/obscure term (here the poet's word for his 'lot' or 'portion' / possibly 'cup' or 'consequence') with the possessive pronoun -ش attached. بود is the copula 'would be/was' giving the resulting state. خوردن is used in the literal sense 'eating' and is paired with آب خون 'water of blood' (a graphic image for bloodshed) to state the unhappy consequence: 'his portion will be eating and the water of blood.'
segments.s651-g833: شهنشاه (the subject) + گفتا‌گر is an archaic/poetic sequence: گفتا (said) followed immediately by گر (if). آید is a 3rd-person present/future form 'he/it comes'. رواست is the adjective روا 'fitting/permissible' with the copula -ست (= است) attached. Together this forms the protasis and apodosis: 'The shah said, "If he comes, it is fitting."'
segments.s651-g834: چنان دان is an imperative (or hortative) use of دان (literally 'know/consider') meaning 'consider it so / think so'. که introduces the subordinate clause. این خانه ما is a noun phrase 'this house of ours' (ما functions possessively in context). وراست is a fused form (ورا + است) meaning 'is for him / belongs to him'—here expressing 'be his'. The clause therefore: 'consider it such that this house is his.'
segments.s651-g835: فرستاده آمد is a simple past narrative: 'the messenger came.' زمین بوس داد is a light-verb construction where بوس (kiss) functions as the object/noun and داد ('gave') is the past verb — literally 'gave a kiss to the ground' = 'kissed the ground.' Word order places the object (زمین) before the nominal action (بوس) and light verb (داد).
segments.s651-g836: برفت is past tense 'he went away / departed'. و links the two actions. شنیده is a perfect participle 'having heard' (or 'what was heard') and functions here as the content of remembrance; همه کرد یاد is another light-verb construction: یاد کردن/کردن = 'to make/bring to mind' — so 'he caused everyone to remember.' The sequence says he departed and caused all to recall what had been heard.
segments.s651-g837: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. منذر is a proper name (Munḏir). که introduces the indirect question/nominal clause 'what Khosrow said' where چه 'what' + گفت 'said' form the content of hearing. The whole phrase = 'When Munḏir heard what Khosrow said...'.
segments.s651-g838: برخساره is a compacted/poetic sequence likely meaning 'upon his face' (بر + رخساره) in classical usage; خاک زمین را is the object 'dust of the earth' with را marking definiteness; برفت is past tense 'he went / he fell / he cast himself' in narrative contexts. Together the clause describes a ritual gesture of grief: 'he cast dust on his face and fell to the ground.'
segments.s651-g839: همانگه is an adverb 'at that very moment / immediately'. بیامد is the past form 'he came'. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the near(place) of the king' meaning 'near the king' or 'to the king.' The sequence is a simple past action: 'At that moment he came near the king.'
segments.s651-g840: همه مهتران is the plural subject 'all the chiefs/elders' (مهتران = great men/chiefs). برگشادند is 3rd-person plural past 'they opened' (بر-گشاد-ند), and راه is the object 'the way / the path' — together 'all the chiefs opened the way,' i.e. they made way or cleared a path for approach.
segments.s651-g377: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' here. The verb بگشاد (past) precedes its subject کسری — a common poetic inversion: 'بگشاد گوش' is an idiom meaning 'opened his ear' / 'lent an ear, paid attention.' شهنشاه کسری is the title + name 'King Khosrow.'
segments.s651-g378: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; دیوان here means the royal court/divan (office), and بابک names the court's master. برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is the noun 'cry/roar' which is the thing that issued forth.
segments.s651-g379: بخندید is a past-tense verb 'he laughed' and appears before its subject کسری (poetic V–S order). The coordinating و links the next clause; مغفر بخواست treats مغفر as the object/idea requested or sought — here rendered 'sought pardon' (مغفر = 'pardon/forgiveness') with بخواست as past 'he asked/desired'.
segments.s651-g380: درفش بزرگی is a noun phrase 'a banner of greatness / a great banner' (noun + modifier). برافراشت is a past tense verb 'he raised' (or 'was raised' in passive rendering). راست here functions adverbially, 'upright/straight' (i.e. raised up straight/rightwards). Word order places the adverb after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s651-g381: به + دیوان بابک marks direction 'into Babak's divan/court.' خرامید is the past tense verb 'strode/pranced proudly' and شاه is the subject 'the king'; again V–S order is poetic but means 'the king strode into Babak's court.'
segments.s651-g382: نهاده is a perfect/past participle 'having placed' or simple past 'placed.' ز آهن here denotes material 'of iron' (z = 'of/from' used poetically for material). به سر (on the head) and بر کلاه (upon the cap/hat) stack prepositional phrases: the iron piece is on the head, over the (existing) hat — i.e. an iron helmet placed over his cap.
segments.s651-g383: فروهشت is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'was spread out / was scattered' (poetic past). از introduces the material/source 'from the strips/shreds (ترگ) of the Rûm (Roman/Byzantine) — here 'ترگ رومی زره' describes pieces/strips of Roman armor being spread.
segments.s651-g384: زره زده is a compound participial construction meaning 'armour‑worn/armoured' (زده functioning as a past participle attached to زره). The repetition بر زره بر gives 'armor upon armor' (preposition + noun stacked for emphasis), and فراوان گره modifies that with 'many/abundant knots' — i.e. the armors bound/fastened together with many knots.
segments.s651-g633: چنین modifies کوه and precedes it (typical Persian word order: determiner/adverb before noun). The phrase links two coordinated noun phrases with و: 'چنین کوه' ('such a/these mountains') and 'این دشتهای فراخ' ('these wide plains'); فراخ is a postpositive adjective qualifying دشتهای (the plural marked by -های).
segments.s651-g634: همه is a resumptive/quantifying word 'all' applying to the preceding NPs; از در is a prepositional phrase 'from/around the gates' (در here = 'door, gate'). The sequence lists locations connected by و (باغ و میدان و کاخ). There is no overt copula; the prepositional phrase functions to locate the foregoing nouns.
segments.s651-g635: پر here is used adjectivally with از to mean 'full (of)'; the list following پر از names the contents (گاو و نخچیر و آب روان). آب روان is a tight collocation where روان is an adjective 'flowing' modifying آب.
segments.s651-g636: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'; دیدن is the verbal noun 'seeing' (masdar) governed by that preposition. همی is an archaic continuative particle signaling ongoing action; خیره is an adjectival/participial form 'staring/astonished' and گردَد is a classical present-tense verb 'becomes' (3rd sg.). روان is the patient/experiencer noun 'the spirit/soul' that becomes astonished.
segments.s651-g637: نمانیم is 1st‑person plural negative-subjunctive/imperative 'let us not remain' or 'we must not stay'. The sequence that follows (implicitly introduced by ک(ه) in classical usage) states the feared result: بوم 'homeland' + ویران 'ruined' + کنند (3rd pl.) 'they make/do' = 'they destroy the homeland'. The word کین here is irregular in form/context and in classical lines often functions as a contraction of 'که این' ('that this'); it is ambiguous in the provided senses.
segments.s651-g638: همی is the continuative particle 'continually/always'; غارت is the noun 'plunder' used with کردن to form a verbal idea ('to plunder'), and کنند is the 3rd‑person plural present 'they do' — together 'they plunder'. از marks source ('from the cities of Iran'), شهر ایران = 'the cities of Iran'.
segments.s651-g639: ز is the preposition 'from'/'out of'; شاهی means 'kingship' or 'royal power'. وز here is a poetic concatenation (originally و + ز) meaning 'and from', joining the next phrase. روی 'face/appearance' governs فرزانگی 'wisdom' (i.e. 'the appearance of wisdom'). The whole segment lists sources from which 'such a thing' should not be expected.
segments.s651-g640: نشاید is an impersonal present/subjunctive meaning 'it is not fitting/it should not be'. چنین 'such' is the demonstrative/adverb referring back to the previous idea; هم 'also/even' links the clause to what follows; ز is the preposition 'from'; مردانگی is a noun meaning 'manliness, bravery' (here: 'valor'). The clause states that this is not fitting even coming from bravery.
segments.s651-g993: This clause places the (definite) object before the verb and uses the enclitic object marker را after the object phrase (همه موبدان و ردان را). The verb بخواند appears at the end as a past narrative form; here it functions as ‘call/summon’ (an older/epic use of خواندن). موبدان and ردان are plural nouns coordinated by و.
segments.s651-g994: ازان نامه (‘from that letter’) is a prepositional phrase with ازان (archaic condensed form of از آن) introducing the source; نامه is the noun. چندی سخنها (‘several words/speeches’) is a quantified noun phrase where چندی means ‘some/several’. The verb براند follows and here carries the transitive/causative sense of ‘drive forth/utter’ (a poetic use meaning ‘utter’ or ‘set forth’).
segments.s651-g995: This clause gives a time span first (سه روز) followed by a locative/relational adverb (اندران = ‘among them’). بود is the past copula ‘was’/‘stayed’ and is used here to express duration. با رای‌زن (‘with the counselor’) marks accompaniment with the instrumental/prepositional با.
segments.s651-g996: Here چه functions as a coordinating/emphatic connector meaning roughly ‘and/also’ (classical usage), followed by با + plural noun پهلوانان (‘with the champions’). لشکر شکن is a compound attributive: لشکر (army) modified by شکن (‘breaker’), i.e. ‘army-breaker’ as an epithet for the champions. Word order is adjective-like apposition after the head (lshkr شکن).
segments.s651-g997: بران (‘upon that’/‘thereon’) links this clause to the previous days; راست شد is a two-word predicate meaning ‘became right/firm’ (راست used adjectivally + شد ‘became’). رای شاه (‘the king’s counsel/decision’) is the subject or theme made firm by that verb. The order places the temporal/ linking word before the predicate, a typical epic word order.
segments.s651-g998: The relative/complementizer که introduces the content of the resolved counsel. راند is the past verb ‘drove/led/sent’ (here ‘sent/led’ an army), followed by سوی (‘toward’) plus the goal phrase جنگ قیصر (‘Caesar’s battle/war’). سپاه (‘army’) appears as the direct object of راند and follows the goal phrase, giving a slightly inverted poetic order.
segments.s651-g999: برآمد (‘arose/issued forth’) is a verbal predicate placed before the source phrase ز در (‘from the gate’), a poetic order where the verb leads. ناله گاودم is a nominal phrase in which ناله (‘moan/lament’) governs گاودم (a compound or name; here treated as the object/qualification of the lament). ز is the preposition ‘from’ (poetic form) and در means ‘gate/door’ in this context.
segments.s651-g1000: This clause strings three nominal actions joined by و: خروشیدن نای (‘the ney’s crying/roar’) uses the infinitival/masdar-like verb خروشیدن with the instrument noun نای; و links to روینیه خم where روینیه is a cloak/cover and خم (‘bend/curve’) describes its state or movement. The verbless noun cluster after the first verb is a common poetic coordination: the first verb governs the sense of the following coordinated nouns.
segments.s651-g17: هر آنکس که introduces a free relative clause meaning “whoever/anyone who.” اندیشه بد کند is a present/habitual clause: اندیشه (the noun ‘thought/intention’) modified by بد (‘evil’), followed by کند (present/optative third-person singular of کردن used here as a general/habitual ‘does’ or ‘harbors’). The whole segment therefore means “whoever forms/harbors an evil intention.”
segments.s651-g18: به فرجام بد is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/into a bad end/outcome” (به + noun). با تن خود is an instrumental/reflective phrase literally “with his body himself,” i.e. “upon his own body.” The final کند repeats the verb 'do' and ties the two halves together: “brings/does (this) to a bad end with his own body” = “brings a bad fate upon himself.”
segments.s651-g19: ز ما means “from us/of us.” هرچ خواهند is “whatever they will want/ask” (هرچ = whatever, خواهند = 3rd pl. wanting/will ask). پاسخ دهیم is a 1st-person plural verb phrase (“we give an answer / we will answer”); classical Persian often uses این فعل forms for a courteous future/volitional sense. Together: “Whatever they ask of us, we will answer.”
segments.s651-g20: بخواهش گران is a noun phrase meaning “by/at the (their) weighty/important request” (بخواهش = by request/at asking, گران = weighty/precious). روز فرخ is a compound noun phrase “an auspicious/fortunate day.” نهیم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive of نهادن ‘to place/set,’ used here with volitional sense “we shall set/appoint.” So: “at their weighty request we will appoint a fortunate day.”
segments.s651-g21: از اندیشه دل is a genitive phrase meaning “concerning the thought(s) of the heart” (از = of/concerning, اندیشه دل = the heart's thought). کس آگاه نیست is an existential negative: کس here functions as ‘anyone/no one’ and آگاه نیست = ‘is not aware.’ Combined: “No one is aware of the heart's thoughts.”
segments.s651-g22: به تنگی دل literally “because of the narrowness/distress of the heart” (به + تنگی دل = due to the heart's anguish). اندر مرا is the locative + object: اندر = in/within, م + را here combine as the object marker forming مرا ‘me.’ راه نیست is an impersonal negative clause “there is no way/access.” So the clause reads: “Because of the heart's distress there is no way to me / no access to me.”
segments.s651-g23: اگر introduces a conditional “if.” پادشا را is the topical/object-marked noun “the king” (را topicalizes/marks definiteness). بود is the copula “were/was.” پیشه داد is a nominal phrase where پیشه = “profession/occupation” and داد = “justice”; word order (copula between topic and predicate) is stylistically classical: “if the king's occupation were justice.”
segments.s651-g24: The second بود gives the result clause (“then would be” / “would result”), modified by بی‌گمان “undoubtedly.” هر کس = “every person/everyone.” از داد شاد is a short prepositional phrase “glad from/because of justice” (از = from/because of, داد = justice, شاد = glad). Overall: “then surely everyone would be glad because of justice.”
segments.s651-g777: سراسر (front adverb) = 'throughout/everywhere' modifies the whole clause; به + noun here marks the means ('by/with'). شمشیر is the instrument 'sword'. بگذاشتند is an archaic/periphrastic past plural of گذاشتن (they placed/put) but in this context idiomatically means 'they laid waste/attacked' with the following instrument; the verb comes at the end as typical Persian word order (S… O … V).
segments.s651-g778: ستم کردن is a light-verb construction: ستم (noun 'oppression') + کردن (here functioning as a light/auxiliary verb) = 'to do oppression / to oppress'. و = 'and'. رنج برداشتند: رنج = 'suffering'; برداشتند is past plural of برداشتن ('they took/removed') but here used idiomatically to mean 'they caused/inflicted' or 'they brought (suffering)'; the clause lists the bad acts/results connected to the previous clause.
segments.s651-g779: ببود is an archaic past/existential form of بودن ('was' / 'there was') used here to introduce the state. ایمن از + noun = 'safe/protected from ...'; از marks the source/reason (ʼfromʼ), رنج = 'suffering/harm', شاه جهان = a title-like genitive 'king of the world' (شاه modified by جهان). The whole phrase says that no one was safe from that king's harm.
segments.s651-g780: بلوجی = 'a Baloch (person/people)'. نماند is past negative of ماندن ('did not remain/was not left'), so here 'not one remained'. آشکار و نهان = a fixed pair 'open/visible and hidden/concealed'; the coordination emphasizes that none remained either openly or in hiding.
segments.s651-g781: چنان بد که = a correlative clause 'so/that' meaning 'so bad that...'; که introduces the result clause. بر + کوه = 'on the mountain'. ایشان in this position refers back to the people (their) or 'them' and qualifies گله; گله here is the possessive noun 'flock/herd' (their flock on the mountain). Word order places the location and possessor before the noun.
segments.s651-g782: بدی here names the bad condition ('badness/ill state') of the flock. بی‌نگهبان = 'without a guard' = 'unguarded'. و = 'and'. کرده is the participle/verb 'made/done' (from کردن) modifying یله; یله = 'loose/unrestrained' (here describing the flock being let loose). Together the phrase describes the flock left unguarded and loose.
segments.s651-g783: شبان = 'shepherds'. هم = 'also/either' emphasizes that shepherds were likewise absent. نبودی is the past negative form of بودی ('were/was') meaning 'there were not' (negated past existence). پس = 'then/so' indicating consequence. گوسفند = 'the sheep' (subject of the consequence clause).
segments.s651-g784: به هامون = 'to/into the plain' (به marking direction/place). و = 'and'. بر تیغ کوه بلند: بر = 'on/upon', تیغ کوه is a poetic image 'the mountain's knife-edge/ridge' (تیغ used figuratively as a sharp ridge), and بلند = 'high'; the prepositional phrases list where the sheep went in the absence of shepherds.
segments.s651-g353: ز ایوان (prepositional phrase) = 'from the iwan/porch'. باسب is a fused (poetic) form meaning 'on horseback/by horse' placed immediately after the place. اندر آورد پای is a verbal phrase where آورد is the past-tense verb 'brought' and پای is its object; together ('اندر آورد پای') idiomatically means 'set foot down' or 'dismounted.' Word order is poetic (place and manner before the verb).
segments.s651-g354: بفرمودشان is a past tense verb 'he commanded/ordered them' with the clitic -شان marking the object. بازگشتن is an infinitive 'to return' serving as the content of that command. ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from their place/position.' The construction is 'he ordered them [to do X]': بفرمودشان + infinitive.
segments.s651-g355: برین نیز = 'over this (also)' with بر + این (here spelled ین) and نیز 'also' as an adverbial phrase. بگذشت is the simple past 'passed' (here conveying passing of time or movement). گردان سپهر is a noun phrase 'the revolving/turning sky/heavens' where گردان is adjectival/participial 'revolving' modifying سپهر. The verb follows the adverbial phrase in poetic order.
segments.s651-g356: چو introduces a temporal/simile clause meaning 'when.' خورشید تابنده is noun + adjective 'the shining sun.' بنمود is the archaic/poetic past 'showed/revealed' (ب‑ prefix is common in poetic verbs). چهر 'face' is the direct object ('showed its face').
segments.s651-g357: خروشی برآمد is a noun + past verb pattern meaning 'a cry/roar/commotion arose.' ز درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court/threshold of the king' (درگاه شاه = 'the king's court'). برآمد is past simple third person.
segments.s651-g358: که‌ای here functions as an exclamatory/vocative particle (classical/poetic 'که ای' = 'O!'). It introduces the address to those present: گرزداران ایران سپاه = 'mace-bearers, the army of Iran' (گرزداران 'mace-bearers', ایران qualifying سپاه as 'army of Iran'). Word order is poetic; this is direct address.
segments.s651-g359: همه = 'all/everyone' as the addressed group. با introduces accompaniment or instrument 'with' and is followed by a list of arms: سلیح (weapon/armament), کمان (bow) and کمند (lasso/snare), linked by و. This phrase describes how they are to be armed.
segments.s651-g360: بدیوان is a poetic contraction of به دیوان 'to the court' (directional phrase). بابک names whose court it is. شوید is the present-subjunctive/optative (2nd-person plural form of شدن) used here as a jussive/imperative 'become' or 'be' (i.e., 'be honoured'). ارجمند is the predicate adjective 'esteemed/esteemed ones' describing their desired state. The clause issues a commanding exhortation: go to Babak's court and become honoured.
segments.s651-g1009: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative copula (like نبود) meaning 'there was not'; it precedes the locative phrase. بر زمین is the locative 'on the earth'. پشه را uses را to mark the definite/ topicalized object 'the fly'. جایگاه is the noun 'place' that completes the sense 'there was no place for the fly.'
segments.s651-g1010: نه is the negative for this clause; اندر هوا is the locative 'in the air'. باد را is topicalized/marked (را) — 'as for the wind' — and ماند is the past verb 'remained' (here negated by نه). راه is the noun 'way/path'; word order is topicalized object (باد را) + verb (ماند) + complement (راه), so: 'nor in the air did a way remain for the wind.'
segments.s651-g1011: ز is the preposition 'from' or 'because of' introducing a causal source. جوشن سواران is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the cuirasses (joushan) of the horsemen' (noun + possessor). وز here functions as a coordinated continuation ('and/from' or a gust notion modifying the next noun) and گرد پل (گرد پیل) means 'the dust (گرد) of the elephants (پیل)'; the sequence lists two sources that affect the ground.
segments.s651-g1012: زمین is the subject 'the earth'. شد is the past of شدن 'became'. به + کردار is a classical simile/ comparison marker 'in the manner/likeness of', so به کردار دریای نیل means 'like the sea of the Nile' (دریای نیل = the Nile). Word order: Subject + شد + comparison phrase.
segments.s651-g1013: جهاندار is the title/noun 'sovereign' or 'world-ruler' (subject). با introduces accompaniment: با کاویانی درفش = 'with the Kayanian banner/standard.' کاویانی qualifies درفش (Kayanian banner). The preposition با connects the sovereign to that emblem.
segments.s651-g1014: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/continuous past 'was going' (همی- prefix marks ongoing action in classical Persian). با again marks accompaniment or what he wore: با تاج و زرینه کفش = 'with a crown and golden shoes.' زرینه is an adjectival form 'golden' modifying کفش. The coordination with و joins 'crown' and 'golden shoes.'
segments.s651-g1015: همی is the durative/emphatic prefix 'was/continued to'. برشد (بر + شد) = 'rose up' (past), here applied to آوازشان 'their voices/cry'. بر plus a measure (بر دو میل) gives an extent/height/distance sense — 'rose for/to two miles' (i.e. audible for two miles). Note: میل here denotes a distance (classical 'mile'); that particular sense is not among the supplied sense options, so it is marked accordingly.
segments.s651-g1016: به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'before/in front of the host/army' (به + پیش + سپاه). اندرون introduces what is inside or within the ranks: اندرون کوس و پیل = 'within (the ranks) [were] drums (کوس) and elephants (پیل).' The verb 'were' is omitted (classical poetic ellipsis), typical in Persian.
segments.s651-g25: 'از امروز' is a temporal prepositional phrase ('from today'); 'کاری' here names the work/task (object). 'به فردا ممان' is a prohibitive instruction meaning 'do not put (it) to/toward tomorrow' — ممان functions as a prohibitive/negative verb ('do not withhold/postpone'). Word order places the time phrase first, then the object, then the prohibition.
segments.s651-g26: The first 'که' introduces a clause often translated 'for' or 'since'; 'داند' is present tense 'knows' (a rhetorical 'who knows'). The second 'که' begins the content clause 'that what tomorrow will ...'; 'فردا' 'چه' + subjunctive 'گردد' together mean 'what will happen/come about tomorrow', with 'زمان' ('time') as the semantic subject placed after the verb (poetic/inverted order).
segments.s651-g27: This is a noun plus restrictive relative clause: 'گلستان که امروز باشد به بار' = 'the garden that today is bearing fruit'. 'که' introduces the relative clause; 'باشد' is the copula/optative verb 'is/may be' in an elevated/poetic form; 'به بار' here is part of the verbal phrase meaning 'to bear fruit' (i.e. 'in fruit').
segments.s651-g28: 'تو' is topicalized for 'you' ('for you'); 'فردا' 'چنی گل' = 'tomorrow such a flower'; 'نیاید' is a negative jussive/subjunctive meaning 'will not come / will not be of use'; the phrase 'نیاید به کار' (literally 'not come to work') is an idiom meaning 'be of no use'. Word order places the beneficiary/topic 'تو' before the temporal 'فردا' and the predicate after.
segments.s651-g29: 'بدانگه' is an adverbial 'thereupon/at that time'; 'که یابی' introduces a temporal/subordinate clause meaning 'when you find' (یابی is present/optative 'you find/you may find'); 'تن زورمند' is the predicate 'a strong body', with adjective following the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g30: 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') here means 'about/considering'; 'بیماری اندیش' is an imperative construction 'think about illness' where 'اندیش' is an imperative verb 'consider/ponder'; the following 'و درد و گزند' are coordinated objects ('and pain and harm') of that imperative.
segments.s651-g31: پس is a discourse adverb meaning 'so/therefore' that begins the line. زندگی یاد کن is an imperative construction: یاد (noun 'remembrance') + the light imperative کن = 'remember' (i.e., ‘remember life’), with زندگی as the direct object. روز مرگ is a temporal/genitive phrase 'the day of death' placed after the imperative to indicate when/with respect to what one should remember.
segments.s651-g32: چنانیم is chenan + -īm (we are thus), a poetic/contracted copular predicate meaning 'we are such' or 'such are we'. با مرگ is an adverbial/comitative phrase 'with (regard to) death' (i.e. 'in the face of death'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and باد و برگ are the two terms being compared ('wind and leaf'). Word order places the copular predicate up front in a poetic style and follows with the simile.
segments.s651-g361: برفتند is the past plural of رفتن (‘to go’), here rendered as 'they advanced'. The coordination با نیزه و خود و کبر lists instruments/qualities: با (with) + نیزه (spear) then و خود (and themselves/their own force) and و کبر (and pride). The sequence is a poetic accumulation of means and attitudes accompanying their departure.
segments.s651-g362: همی is a frequentative/adverbial marker indicating ongoing or emphatic action ('kept/continually'); گرد لشکر here uses گرد as a noun 'dust' (the dust of the army). برآمد is a past tense verb 'rose/appeared', and به ابر gives direction 'up to (the) cloud(s)'. The phrase therefore means the army's dust continually rose up into the clouds.
segments.s651-g363: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (did) = 'cast a look' (past, third-person singular). The phrase به گرد سپاه means 'around the army/host' (به + گرد + سپاه), with گرد as 'around'. Word order is Subject (بابک) after the verbal phrase, typical of Persian narrative inversion for emphasis.
segments.s651-g364: چو here means 'when'. پید ا + نبد is a negated stative: پیدا 'visible' and نبد (classical negative auxiliary) 'was not'. فر is the technical poetic term for royal radiance. The coordination 'فر و اورند شاه' links two items lacking visibility: 'the splendour and King Orund' (اورند is a proper name, followed by شاه 'king').
segments.s651-g365: چنین گفت is a conventional narrative formula 'thus he said'. کامروز (literary compound) means 'this very day' or 'today'. با مهر و داد is an instrumental/attitudinal phrase 'with kindness/affection and justice' modifying the speech or decree that follows.
segments.s651-g366: همه بازگردید is an address/declarative meaning 'let all return' or 'all returned' depending on context; here it functions as the content of the speech (imperative/optative sense is natural in direct speech). پیروز و شاد are predicative adjectives 'victorious and glad' describing the state in which they should return. Word order places the verbs before adjectives in the clause.
segments.s651-g367: به‌روز سه دیگر literally situates time: به‌روز (on the day) + سه دیگر = 'on the third day' or 'after three days'. برآمد خروش is a standard poetic phrasing 'a cry/roar arose' (برآمد = 'arose', خروش = 'clamour/roar'). The verb is past and the phrase announces the action.
segments.s651-g368: که‌ای here is a poetic vocative (״که ای״ = 'O ...') addressing listeners: نامداران 'renowned ones' is the vocative noun. با فر و هوش is a prepositional phrase 'with glory and understanding' qualifying those addressed. The syntactic order is vocative + qualification, a common classical rhetorical pattern.
segments.s651-g737: به راه اندر is a fronted locative prepositional phrase ('on/along the road'); اندر is an archaic/poetic preposition meaning 'in/within' or 'on'. آگاهی آمد is an impersonal/result clause 'information/news came' (آمد = past 'came'). به شاه marks the beneficiary/recipient ('to the king'), placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s651-g738: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). گشت is the past intransitive sense 'became/turned (to be)'. از indicates cause/reason here ('because of' / 'from'). بلوجی is the ethnonym 'Baloch' with the -ی adjectival/relational ending (here referring to the Baloch people). جهانی سیاه is a compact predicate: جهانی is an attributive/poetic modifier ('worldly/of the world') and سیاه is the predicate adjective 'dark/black' — together 'the world became dark'.
segments.s651-g739: ز بس is an idiomatic poetic preface meaning 'from so much / because of so much' (ز = 'from/because of', بس = an intensifier 'so much/enough'). The verbs کشتن، غارت، تاختن are verbal nouns/infinitives listed in coordination to describe the actions causing the result (killing, plundering, charging/raiding); تاختن here has the sense 'to charge/raid'.
segments.s651-g740: زمین را uses را as the definite object marker ('the land' as direct object). به آب اندر انداختن is an infinitival verb phrase 'to cast/throw into the water' where به + آب + اندر gives the goal 'into/to water' (اندر is an archaic locative 'in/into'), and انداختن is the infinitive 'to throw/cast' describing the action performed (subject omitted in this nominal clause).
segments.s651-g741: ز گیلان is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from Gilan'. تباهی is the noun 'devastation/destruction'. فزونست is an archaic/persianized verbal adjective meaning 'was greater / increased' (فزون + است fused as poetic past/present). ازین is the archaic/poetic form of این ('this') used after فزونست to express a comparative sense 'greater than this' (literally 'is more than this').
segments.s651-g742: ز introduces cause/source again ('from/because of'). نفرین is 'the curse'. پراگنده شد is a verb phrase 'became scattered/widespread' (پراگنده used adjectivally/adverbially with شد as 'became scattered'). آفرین follows the verb as the thing that spread; here آفرین functions as the exclamatory noun 'afarin' — an acclamation/praise/cry — so the clause means 'the cry “afarin” became widespread/was scattered.'
segments.s651-g743: دل شاه نوشین روان شد غمی is built around the past copula شد 'became'. دل شاه is the possessed noun phrase 'the king's heart' (word order: possessor after possessed is common in older Persian poetry). نوشینِ روان is a compound adjectival description 'sweet-souled/pleasant of spirit' modifying دل; شد takes the following noun غمی as a predicative: 'became sorrow (i.e. became sorrowful)'. The line omits the ezāfe linking the adjective and noun, a frequent poetic compression.
segments.s651-g744: برآمیخت اندوه با خرمی uses the past verb برآمیختن (3rd sg past) meaning 'was/were mingled' or 'mingled'. Here اندوه functions as the theme/subject 'sorrow' and با introduces the accompaniment 'with mirth/delight'; word order (verb before subject) is an instance of poetic inversion but the grammatical relations are verb + subject + adjunct.
segments.s651-g89: This clause places the subject جهاندار (the sovereign) before a prepositional phrase بر داوران ('over/above the judges') and finishes with the predicate داورست, an archaic copular-predicative form meaning 'is judge' (داور + -ست). In classical Persian poetry the prepositional phrase can interrupt the clause for emphasis; داورست is third-person singular and serves as the main verb/predicate.
