segments.s651-g90: ز here is the poetic form of از introducing the standard/comparison ('from/of'), followed by اندیشه هر کسی ('the thought of any person'). The clause ends with برترست, an older third-person singular predicate meaning 'is superior/above.' Word order is standard poetic Persian: comparator phrase + topic + predicative adjective/verb.
segments.s651-g91: مکان و زمان ('place and time') are listed before the verb آفرید ('created'), following classical Persian order where objects/accusatives often precede the verb. The conjunction و links سپهر, which begins the next coordinated clause and is the object of the following verb (بیاراست).
segments.s651-g92: بیاراست is a past-tense/poetic verb meaning 'adorned' (bi- + arast), followed by the direct objects جان و دل ما ('our soul and heart'). The phrase به مهر ('with/by love' or 'for love') expresses the instrument or benefit. Order here is verb then its objects and adjunct, typical of poetic flexibility.
segments.s651-g93: شما را here is a dative/emphatic topical marker ('for you' / 'your'), followed by دل ('heart') as the theme that is affected. از مهر ما ('from/through our love') gives the source, and برفروخت is a past poetic verb meaning 'was kindled/was set afire.' The construction is typical older Persian where the affected party is marked before the affected noun and verb.
segments.s651-g94: دل و چشم دشمن is the compound subject ('the heart and eye of the enemy'), به ما marks the direction/goal ('toward us'), and بربدوخت is a past poetic verb meaning 'turned toward/was directed to' (an archaic compound verb). Grammatically it is Subject + goal adjunct + past verb; the exact lemma senses provided do not match this verbal use.
segments.s651-g95: The phrase رای و فرمان یزدان ('the counsel and command of God') is a genitive construction where یزدان modifies the preceding nouns. شما is the addressed subject, and کنید (second-person plural/formal) is an exhortative/imperative or polite present-subjunctive meaning 'do/follow.' Word order places the (possessed) noun phrase before the verb, as is common.
segments.s651-g96: به چیزی ('in/with something' or 'regarding anything') introduces the relative clause که پیمان دهد ('which the covenant gives/decrees'); آن کنید places the demonstrative آن ('that') before the verb کنید ('do'), forming the imperative-like instruction 'do that which the covenant gives.' The verb دهد is present-tense/subjunctive third person 'gives/decrees.'
segments.s651-g497: 'که' is the relative pronoun 'who/that'. 'دارنده' is a participial noun 'holder/possessor' serving as the predicate. 'اویست' here functions as a fused copula construction meaning 'he is (his)' or 'is his', so 'دارنده اویست' = 'he is his protector/possessor'. The conjunction 'و' links the next nominal predicate 'هم رهنمای' = 'and also (his) guide' (no additional verb is needed because the copula is understood).
segments.s651-g498: 'همو' = 'that same one' (object/emphatic pronoun). 'دست گیرد' is a verbal phrase: 'گیرد' is 3rd-person singular present 'takes/seizes' and with 'دست' (hand) it carries the idiomatic sense 'takes (someone) by the hand' = 'helps/assists'. 'به هر دوسرای' = 'in/to both abodes' (هر = each/every; دوسرای = the two abodes/worlds). The clause states a present-time general truth about help extending to both realms.
segments.s651-g499: 'مترسید' here is negative admonition 'do not fear' (an archaic/poetic imperative-like form). 'هرگز' = 'ever/at any time' (used with negative to mean 'never/never at all'). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. 'تخت و کلاه' = 'throne and crown' (metonymies for royal power). Together: 'Do not ever fear the throne and the crown.'
segments.s651-g500: 'گشادست' = 'is wide/open' (poetic fused form of 'گشاد است'). The prepositional phrase 'بر هر کس' = 'toward/for every person'. 'این بارگاه' (this palace/court) appears at the end as the syntactic subject; Persian often places the predicate ('گشادست') before the subject for emphasis. The clause reads: 'This court is open to everyone.'
segments.s651-g501: هر آنکس که آید is a free relative 'whoever comes' (هر + آنکس + که + verb). آید is the present/subjunctive/present-tense form 'comes' used for a general or conditional statement. به‌روز و به شب are adverbial phrases 'by day and by night' (به + time-word) giving the temporal scope of 'whoever comes'.
segments.s651-g502: ز گفتار is a prepositional phrase 'concerning/from speech' (ز = 'from; regarding'). بسته is an adjective 'closed/shut' modifying لب. مدارید here is a hortative/imperative plural form telling the addressees to 'keep/maintain' (i.e., 'keep [them] closed'); classical Persian often uses such forms where Modern Persian would use نگه دارید. لب (lips) is the object. The literal order is 'from speech closed keep (you) lips' = 'keep your lips closed about speech.'
segments.s651-g503: اگر is the conditional 'if' introducing the clause. می‌گساریم is a first-person plural present/imperative-form with the prefixed mi-; here it functions poetically as 'we are (or would be) scattered/ruined' (the root گسار used in a defective, poetic conjugation). با انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'with/by gatherings' (انجمن = assembly/meeting) that marks the cause/agent of being ruined.
segments.s651-g504: گر is a variant conditional particle (like اگر) repeating the 'if' for the second clause. آهسته باشیم combines the adverb آهسته 'slow(ly)' with باشیم, here a present-subjunctive/optative form 'we be / we are' (used after a conditional to express 'if we are slow' or 'if we delay'). با رای‌زن is another prepositional phrase 'in/with counselors' (رای‌زن = advisor/counselor) specifying the sphere in which the slowness occurs.
segments.s651-g113: جهان را ببخشید بر چار بهر is a compact, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'he allotted the world into four parts'. جهان را uses the object marker را after the noun; ببخشید is a past-tense verb 'bestowed/gave' (here 'allotted'); بر چار بهر = 'into/over the four directions/quarters' with چار 'four' qualifying بهر (quarter/share). Word order is Subject omitted — the verb governs the phrase.
segments.s651-g114: وزو نامزد کرد آبادشهر is syntactically 'and to it he appointed Abadshahr.' وزو here is actually a fused sequence (و + زو = 'and to/for it'), نامزد کرد uses کرد as the light verb 'made/appointed', and آبادشهر is the place appointed. Note that نامزد in modern lists often means 'suitor/engaged', but here functions as an appointive/nominal element paired with کرد.
segments.s651-g115: نخستین خراسان ازو یاد کرد reads 'Khorasan first remembered/commemorated him.' نخستیِن 'first' modifies خراسان; ازو ('from/of him') is the object of یاد 'remembrance' and کرد is the past tense 'did/made'. The verbal noun یاد together with کرد forms the predicate 'made remembrance (of him)'.
segments.s651-g116: دل نامداران بدو شاد کرد = 'he made the hearts of the renowned happy for him.' دل (heart) is the direct object, نامداران qualifies whose hearts they are, بدو (= 'to him' / 'for him') shows beneficiary; شاد 'happy' is an adjective completed by کرد 'made'. Word order places the beneficiary before the adjective+verb cluster.
segments.s651-g117: دگر بهره زان بد قم و اصفهان means 'another share from that he gave to Qom and Isfahan.' دگر 'another/other' modifies بهره 'share'; زان 'from that' links back; the token بد here functions as a prepositional marker 'to/for' (a graphic variant of به), and then the place names follow. The poetic compression merges connective elements.
segments.s651-g118: نهاد بزرگان و جای مهان = 'he placed/established (it) for the great ones and a place for guests.' نهاد here is the past of نهادن 'placed/established' (not the abstract 'disposition'), followed by beneficiaries: بزرگان 'the great ones' and جای مهان 'a place/seat for guests.'
segments.s651-g119: وزین بهره بود آذرابادگان reads 'and this share was Azerbaijan.' وزین is a fused/written-together sequence (و + زین = 'and this') in context; بهره 'share/portion' is the subject and بود is the past copula 'was', followed by the place-name آذرابادگان (historic Azerbaijan).
segments.s651-g120: که بخشش نهادند آزادگان = 'which the free (آزادگان) placed/established as a gift.' که introduces the relative clause; بخشش is a nominal meaning 'grant/donation', and نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they placed/established', so together 'they endowed/made as a gift'; آزادگان is the agent 'the free/the nobles'.
segments.s651-g161: This phrase names the items at issue: خوردنیهای 'foodstuffs' modified by خردادماه 'of the month Khordad'. وزین here appears fused/poetic; none of the listed lexical senses fits well in context, so treat it as a connective/pointing word (poetic 'and/these'). Word order is determiner/pointing + noun phrase (pointing word + 'foods' + genitive month).
segments.s651-g162: نکردی is the simple past negative (2nd person singular) 'you did not do'. The remainder is an object/goal phrase: به کار 'to/for the work' and اندرون کس 'the inner/household affairs of anyone', followed by نگاه 'a look/concern' used as a noun. Together the phrase means 'you did not make a look/concern toward the inner affairs of anyone' → 'you did not concern yourself with anyone's household matters.'
segments.s651-g163: کسی 'someone' + the agentive suffix کش attached to درم yields a compound meaning 'one who takes/collects the dirham' (agentive formation). بود is the past copula 'there was'. The clause contrasts that a tax/dirham collector existed but دهقان 'peasant' نبود 'was not' (there was no peasant).
segments.s651-g164: ندیدی is past negative (2nd person) 'you did not see'. The following nouns are a paratactic list linked by و: غم 'sorrow', رنج 'hardship', کشت 'sowing/cultivation' and درود (here 'blessings' or 'good fortune'). The verb is placed before the list (typical poetic inversion): 'you did not see sorrow, hardship, sowing, or blessing.'
segments.s651-g165: بر اندازه is an adverbial phrase 'according to measure' or 'in proportion'. از + [amount] gives the range 'from ten dirhams' and تا introduces the other limit 'to four' or 'as low as four' (تا here = 'as far as/as low as'). The sequence sets the monetary scale that follows.
segments.s651-g166: بسالی 'per year' (adverbial) modifies the verb. ازو 'from/for him' indicates the beneficiary/affected party. بستدی is an older past form (2nd‑person past in this poetic register) meaning 'you bound/assigned/set' (i.e., you fixed or attached an officer). کاردار is the noun 'steward/agent' — the direct object: 'you appointed a steward for him at X per year.'
segments.s651-g167: کسی here functions as 'anyone' (indefinite) and بر takes the meaning 'against' in the sense of 'doing something to/against someone'. کدیور is a proper name; نکردی is the past negative 2nd‑person 'you did not do', and ستم is the noun 'oppression/injustice'. Together: 'you did not oppress anyone against Kadyūr' or more simply 'you did not wrong Kadyūr.'
segments.s651-g168: This is a short predicative clause giving the monetary rate: به سالی 'per year' (temporal/adverbial), به بهره 'at (so many) shares' — here به works like 'at/by' (adverbial marker) and بهره 'share/portion'. بود is the past copula 'was', and این درم 'this dirham' is the subject: 'this dirham was three shares per year.'
segments.s651-g913: که‌گر چند is a poetic concessive phrase: که‌گر (an older conditional particle) + چند (here acting as part of the concessive idiom 'though/even if'). من شهریار توام literally orders as 'I (من) — sovereign (شهریار) — of you (تو) am (ام)'; in classical Persian the copula is attached to the possessive pronoun, so 'شهریار توام' = 'I am your king.'
segments.s651-g914: برین is written as two pieces here: بر (preposition 'on/upon') + ین (an orthographic form of 'این' = 'this'), so برین کینه = 'this grudge.' The second بر before مایه‌دار functions as a preposition 'against/toward' and مایه‌دار means 'the wealthy'; توام repeats the copular ending attached to تو (see previous segment), so the full phrase says the speaker bears this grudge against the rich.
segments.s651-g915: فرستاده‌ای here is a noun 'a messenger/sent one' (formed from the verb stem); ما is the first-person plural subject 'we'; کنون = 'now'; چرب‌گوی is an adjective 'smooth-tongued, flattering' modifying the messenger. The whole chunk is a verbless nominal phrase functioning as the object/role to be sent.
segments.s651-g916: فرستیم is a hortative/jussive form 'let us send' / 'we will send' (classical present-subjunctive/jussive nuancing). با + نامه‌ای = 'with a letter' (نامه‌ای = 'a letter', indefinite by -ی). نزد اوی = 'to/into the presence of him' (نزد = 'in the presence of; near', اوی = 'him/he'), marking the addressee.
segments.s651-g917: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional sense 'unless.' خود 'himself' emphasizes the subject. نیاید is an optative/subjunctive-form in classical Persian meaning 'come' (negative shape in some usages; here part of the conditional clause — 'unless he himself comes'). تو را uses the object/topic marker را after the pronoun 'you.' زان = 'from that' and گزند = 'harm' or 'injury'.
segments.s651-g918: به روم و به قیصر = 'to Rome and to the Caesar' (به = 'to/for'). تو ما را پسند is a succinct verbless/existential clause: تو (addressing 'you') + ما را (us, with object marker) + پسند (approval/liking) — i.e. '(you) accept/approve us (before)'. In classical epic style word order is flexible and some verbs/nouns stand in place of full verbs.
segments.s651-g919: نویسنده‌ای is an indefinite agent 'a scribe/writer' (the -ای ending here = 'a certain / one'); خواست is the past tense 'requested/wanted' (he asked); از بارگاه = 'from the court/palace' (از = 'from', بارگاه = 'royal court'). The clause states who made the request and where.
segments.s651-g920: به قیصر = 'to the Caesar' (destination). یکی نامه = 'a certain letter' (یکی as an indefinite 'a certain'); فرمود here is the past tense 'he ordered/caused/sent' (classical polite verb used of a sovereign issuing something) and شاه 'the king' is the agent performing the action. Word order places the object phrase before the verb and the subject after the verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s651-g609: نماند is the negative past/stative of ماندن (‘to remain’) and here means ‘was left’ (nothing remained). ز introduces a partitive/source phrase (‘of’/‘from’) and links to بسیار و اندک (‘great and small’ = ‘much or little’). به جای is a fixed locative/idiomatic phrase meaning ‘in place’ / ‘left behind’ (literally ‘in/at place’), so the whole segment says that nothing, whether much or little, remained.
segments.s651-g610: ز is the partitive ‘of’ introducing the list of categories; پرنده، مردم، چارپای are three coordinated nouns (‘birds, people, four‑footed animals’). The conjunction و simply links the items; چارپای is a collective singular for ‘quadrupeds/animals’. The whole segment names what nothing remained of.
segments.s651-g611: گزندی (‘harm, injury’) is the head noun and is followed by a relative clause introduced by که (‘that/which’) — که آید به ایران سپاه = ‘that an army comes to Iran’. آید is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن used here for a general/future occurrence (‘comes’), and سپاه is the agent (‘the army’) placed after the locative phrase آید به ایران (poetic word order).
segments.s651-g612: ز کشور به کشور is a repetitive prepositional sequence meaning ‘from country to country’ (i.e. from realm to realm). جزین (‘except this’) combines with نیست راه (there is no way) in inverted poetic order: literally ‘from country to country, except this is not a way’ → ‘there is no route (of that kind)’. راه is the predicate noun and نیست negates its existence.
segments.s651-g613: بسی پیش ازین is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘long before’ / ‘many (a long time) ago’ (پیش ازین = ‘before this’). کوشش و رزم (‘effort/exertion and battle’) are the nouns that follow the temporal phrase, and بود is the past copula ‘there was’ — together: ‘long before there was much struggle and battle.’
segments.s651-g614: گذر ترک را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker on ‘the Turks’ pass/passage’ (گذر ترک). The predicate follows: راه خوارزم بود — ‘was the road to Khwarezm.’ This is an instance of inverted, poetic word order where the topic (گذر ترک را) is fronted and the copular clause (راهِ خوارزم بود) gives the identity of that topic.
segments.s651-g615: کنون means ‘now’. چون here functions causally — ‘since’/‘because’. ز introduces the source/agent phrase (‘because of/from the …’). دهقان و آزادگان names the responsible groups (‘the peasants/landholders and the free people’), with و linking them; this clause gives the reason or cause that will be continued by the next line.
segments.s651-g616: برین بوم = بر + این بوم contracted poetically, meaning ‘upon this land’ (بر = ‘on/upon’, ین = variant of این = ‘this’, بوم = ‘homeland’). و links the next phrase; بر پارسازادگان similarly means ‘upon (or against) the Parsā‑zādagān’ (a compound denoting Persian noble/virtuous descendants). The segment is locative/emphatic, placing whatever follows as directed ‘onto this land and onto the noble Persians.’
segments.s651-g305: بدین بارگاهش بلندی بود is a nominal clause describing state/place. بدین (ب + دین here = 'in/at this') introduces location or manner; بارگاهش (بارگاه + ـش) is the possessed noun 'his/that court/palace'; بلندی is an abstract noun 'loftiness/height'; بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order places the locative/qualifier before the copula, a common Persian poetic order.
segments.s651-g306: بر موبدان ارجمندی بود is another stative clause parallel to the previous one. بر introduces the target/recipient ('toward/with respect to'); موبدان is the plural of mōbed 'the priests'; ارجمندی is an abstract noun meaning 'honor/dignity/esteem'; بود is the past copula. The clause literally says 'there was honor toward the mobeds.'
segments.s651-g307: به نزدیک یزدان ز تخمی که کشت strings several prepositional/relative pieces. به نزدیک یزدان = 'near to God' (به + نزدیک + یزدان). ز تخمی = 'from the seed/offspring' (poetic 'ز' = 'from'); که کشت is a relative clause 'that he sowed/that was sown' with کشت here understood as the cultivation/seeding idea. The clause groups location (near God) then origin (from the seed he sowed).
segments.s651-g308: به باید بپاداش خرم بهشت uses an optative/modal construction. باید = 'must/ought' or 'let it be that'; بپاداش = 'as a reward' (بـ- + پاداش); خرم بهشت = 'joyful Paradise' (adjective before noun, poetic ordering). The literal sense: 'let the reward be a joyful Paradise.' The initial به here is connective/emphatic.
segments.s651-g309: که ما بی‌نیازیم ازین خواسته is a content clause introduced by که 'that'. ما بی‌نیازیم = 'we are free/of no need' (present tense/state); ازین خواسته = 'from this wish/request' (ازین = 'from this', خواسته = 'wish/request'). The clause states a wish to be rid of that request.
segments.s651-g310: که گردد به نفرین روان کاسته is a subordinate clause introduced by که with گردِد (subjunctive/optative use of گردیدن) meaning 'may it become/turn'. به نفرین = 'into/a curse'; روان کاسته = a compound where روان 'spirit/soul' is qualified by کاسته 'diminished', i.e. 'a curse that lessens the spirit.' The verb گردَد expresses a hoped-for or feared result.
segments.s651-g311: گر از پوست درویش باشد خورش is a conditional clause. گر = 'if'; از پوست درویش = 'from the skin/hide of a dervish' (possessive relationship indicated by juxtaposition); باشد is the subjunctive/present-form of 'to be' used here to set up the hypothetical; خورش = 'stew/dish' is the predicate. Word order (condition then predicate) is typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g312: ز چرمش بود بی‌گمان پرورش completes the conditional result. ز چرمش بود = 'from his leather there was' (z = 'from'); بی‌گمان = 'undoubtedly'; پرورش here functions as 'nourishment/upbringing/development' — in context it reads like 'would provide sustenance.' The past copula بود frames the hypothetical result as asserted by the narrator.
segments.s651-g169: گزارنده (an agent-noun) is the predicate nominal 'messenger/one who reports', followed by the past second-person copula بودی 'you were'. The prepositional phrase به دیوان شاه ('to/at the king’s divan') attaches to this clause and marks the office or place where the person served.
segments.s651-g170: ازین (از + این) = 'from this' begins a prepositional phrase indicating source. باژ is the noun governing what is taken from (here left as baž in translation). بهری (from بهر) functions as a noun/adverb 'a share/portion' or 'for benefit', followed by the measure phrase به هر چار ماه 'every four months' (هر + numeral + ماه). Word order is source → object/portion → frequency.
segments.s651-g171: This is a coordinated noun phrase: دبیر 'secretary' و 'and' پرستنده شهریار 'devoted follower of the king' (پرستنده governs شهریار as a possessive/attributive). The second noun phrase is a compound where پرستنده takes شهریار as its object/possessive 'of the king'.
segments.s651-g172: نبودی is the past negative (ن + بودی) meaning 'you were not'. The phrase به دیوان کسی 'in the divan/office of anyone' is an oblique locative; زین شمار ('of this number/rank') qualifies 'کسی' and limits the scope. Overall this clause denies that the addressee held those roles in any such court.
segments.s651-g173: گزیت و خراج is a coordinated object list ('Gazit and the kharaj/tribute'). آنچ = 'that which/whatever' introduces a relative clause modifying what was taken; بد نام 'ill‑named/ill‑reputed' is an adjectival phrase placed before نام, and برد is the past verb 'took/seized' acting on the preceding noun phrases.
segments.s651-g174: بسه here functions as a transitional particle/adverb ('then/after that' or 'sufficient/so') introducing the next action. روزنامه is 'the daily register/record', and به موبد سپرد is a verb phrase 'he entrusted (سپرد) to the mobed (the Zoroastrian priest)', with به marking the recipient.
segments.s651-g175: یکی آنک = 'one (a certain one) who/that' (یکی = 'a certain one'; آنک is the relative 'who/which'). بر دست گنجور بود is an idiomatic expression 'had the treasury in hand' meaning 'was in charge of the treasury' (بر + دست = 'on/at hand' → in control). بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s651-g176: This is a nominal predicate: نگهبان 'the guardian/keeper' + آن نامه دستور 'that letter (نامه) of instruction/order (دستور)' where نامه and دستور form a compound 'letter of instruction'. نبود is not present; here بود closes the clause: 'he was the keeper of that letter/order.' Word order is predicate → object → copula.
segments.s651-g1065: This is a nominal clause with the copula 'بود' (=was) at the end. 'سپهدار' (commander) is the head noun, modified by the epithet 'شیروی' (literally "lion-like/valiant") and followed by the proper name 'بهرام'. Word order is [title + epithet + name] + copula: "The commander, the lion-like Bahram, was."
segments.s651-g1066: 'که' introduces a relative clause describing that commander: 'who/that'. 'در جنگ' = 'in battle' (prepositional phrase). 'با رای و آرام' uses 'با' + two nouns linked by 'و' to characterize his manner: 'with counsel (رای)' and 'calm/serenity (آرام)'. Final 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. So: 'who in battle was (one) with counsel and calm.'
segments.s651-g1067: 'چپ' (=left) is an attributive adjective placed before the noun 'لشکرش' (his army/host). The object marker 'را' follows the noun. 'به فرهاد داد' is a ditransitive structure meaning 'gave to Farhad' (gave the left wing to Farhad). Past verb 'داد' = 'gave'.
segments.s651-g1068: 'بسی پندها' = 'many/abundant advices'. The sequence 'بر برو کرد یاد' is an older/poetic order: 'بر' here points toward the motion/target ('toward, forth'), 'برو' = 'go' (infinitive/verb-noun), and 'کرد یاد' is a compound meaning 'made remembrance' → 'reminded' or 'reiterated'. So the whole phrase means he uttered many counsels reminding (him) to go forward.
segments.s651-g1069: 'چو' = 'when'. 'استاد پیروز' is a nominal phrase 'the master, victorious' (epithet after the noun). 'بر میمنه' = 'upon the right (side/flank)' where 'بر' = on/upon and 'میمنه' = the right side. Together: 'When the victorious master took up the right flank...'.
segments.s651-g1070: The clause names whom he placed there: 'گشسب' (Gashasp, a hero) qualified by 'جهانجوی' (literally 'world-seeking', an epithet). 'پیش' = 'before/in front (of them)'. 'بنه' here is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'to place/put' (i.e. 'he put/placed [Gashasp] in front'). The verb is final; subject is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s651-g1071: This is a compact, somewhat archaic sequence: 'به قلب اندر' = 'into the inner heart' ('به' + 'قلب' + locative 'اندر' = in/within). The next item 'اورند' functions here as a past action 'brought' (classical/poetic form corresponding to 'آورد'/'آوردند'); 'مهران' is the agent (Mehran). 'به پای' = 'to the foot / at his feet' — the phrase indicates placement in a low or intimate position. The verb is expressed in an older form and the word order is object‑heavy (object phrases precede or surround the subject).
segments.s651-g1072: 'که' introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause 'for/because'. 'در کینه گه' = 'in a place/time of hatred' ('کینه' = hatred, 'گه' = place/occasion). 'داشتی' is 2nd person past 'you had' (i.e. 'you once had/harbored'). 'دل به جای' = 'placed your heart in that place' (literally 'the heart in the place'). The whole clause explains the motive: 'because you had set your heart in a place of hatred.'
segments.s651-g657: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain'; پیر موبد is a compound noun 'an old mōbed' (mōbed = Zoroastrian priest). بران is an adverbial/locative particle 'thereon/thereupon' linking this action to what was just mentioned. کار کرد is a simple past causal construction: کار (work/task) + کرد (did/made) = 'set about the work / did the work.'
segments.s651-g658: This is a causative construction with an omitted subject (referring back to the mōbed): بیابان 'the wilderness' is what is affected; همه 'entire/whole' modifies it; پیش + دیوار = 'in front of the wall / before the wall'; کرد is the past causative 'made / caused' (i.e. 'caused the wilderness to be before the wall' = 'drove/placed the wasteland up to the wall').
segments.s651-g659: دری here means 'door' (an older poetic form of در). برنهادند is a past plural 'they lifted/raised' (بر + نهادن); ز آهن بزرگ is an ablative/descriptive phrase 'of/from great iron' = 'a large iron (gate)'. The clause literally: 'They raised a great iron door.'
segments.s651-g660: رمه 'the flock/herd' is the affected group; یک سر is an idiomatic intensifier here meaning 'entirely/utterly' (literally 'one head/whole'); ایمن شد is past-state 'became safe/protected'; از بیم گرگ 'from the fear of the wolf' gives the source of danger. Overall: 'The herd became completely safe from fear of wolves.'
segments.s651-g661: همه modifies روی کشور and means 'all/throughout'; روی کشور is a locative phrase 'on the face/surface of the country' = 'throughout the land'. نگهبان is the noun 'guards', and نشاند is the archaic past of نشاندن 'set/placed/posted' (here 'he posted guards'). The verb places the guards over the country.
segments.s651-g662: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; ایمن شد is 'became safe/secure' (passive/state); از دشت indicates the source/area 'from the plain' or 'against threats from the plain'; لشکر is the object 'host/army' and براند (from راندن) is a narrative past 'he drove away/drove off' — so: 'When it was secured against the plain, he drove the army away.'
segments.s651-g663: ز ... کشید is a classical pattern 'he stretched/drew from ... to ...'. ز دریا 'from the sea', به راه الانان 'to/toward the Alans' road' (الانان = the Alans). کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to draw/extend/stretch' (the direct object—something like a boundary or line—is implied).
segments.s651-g664: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' or 'one'; مرز 'frontier/border' is the thing seen; ویران و بیکار are adjectival predicates 'ruined and deserted/idle'; دید is simple past 'he saw'. Together: 'He saw a certain border ruined and deserted.'
segments.s651-g105: ستایش همه زیر فرمان اوست: Noun phrase + modifiers with the copula at the end. ستایش (a noun) is the head, همه follows to quantify it (“all praise” / “praise in its entirety”), سپس the prepositional phrase زیر فرمان او (زیر + noun + possessive) marks subjection (“under his command”). The final ست is the contracted copula (poetic form of است) making the whole clause a predication (“is”). Word order is typical classical/poetic Persian: topic → modifier → predicate.
segments.s651-g106: پرستش همه زیر پیمان اوست: same structure as the previous clause. پرستش is the head noun (“worship”), همه quantifies it, and زیر پیمان او is a prepositional/relational phrase meaning “under his پیمان.” Again ست is the contracted copula. Here پیمان is used figuratively (a bond/covenant that governs worship).
segments.s651-g107: چو نوشین‌روان این سخن برگرفت: چو is a conjunction meaning “when.” نوشین‌روان (epithet/name) is the subject that immediately follows the conjunction; این سخن is the direct object (“these/the foregoing words”), and برگرفت is the past verb (‘took up’ / ‘took up and spoke’). برگرفت is a simple past (3rd person singular) of برگرفتن, so the clause reads “When Nushin‑ravan took up these words.”
segments.s651-g108: جهانی ازو مانده اندر شگفت: جهـانی here means “the whole world/the entirety of the world” and acts as the subject. ازو (‘from/of him’) indicates the target of the wonder. مانده is a past participial/past form meaning “remained/was left,” and اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase meaning “in astonishment.” Word order (subject – ‘from him’ – verb – locative phrase) is typical of Ferdowsi’s classical style.
segments.s651-g109: همه یک سر از جای برخاستند: همه is the universal quantifier (“all/everyone”). یک سر is an idiomatic phrase meaning “as one” or “with one head” (i.e. together); سر is used metaphorically. از جای is the locative phrase “from their places,” and برخاستند is the past plural verb “they rose” (3rd person plural).
segments.s651-g110: برو آفرین نو آراستند: آفرین نو is a noun phrase “a new acclamation/praise.” آراستند is the past plural verb “they fashioned/arranged/uttered” (here ‘offered’ or ‘pronounced’). برو here is poetic/elliptical; in context it functions like ‘for him / toward him’ (classical contraction of بر او) indicating the acclamation was directed at the person just mentioned. Word order places the small direction-word before the noun phrase and the verb at the end, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s651-g111: شهنشاه دانندگان را بخواند: شهنشاه is the subject (“the king/emperor”). دانندگان is the plural noun “the knowers/wise men/experts.” را is the definite object marker attached to دانندگان, and بخواند is a past action here meaning “he called/summoned” (3rd person singular). The verb is placed after subject and object as in classical narrative.
segments.s651-g112: سخنهای گیتی سراسر براند: سخنهای گیتی is a possessive/construct phrase “the sayings/words of the world.” سراسر (‘throughout/entirely’) modifies the extent of what follows. براند is an older/poetic third‑person verb form (from راندن) here used to mean roughly “he put forth / caused to be spread / set forth” (i.e., made them known or recited them everywhere). Grammatically this clause reports what was done regarding the worldly sayings.
segments.s651-g241: به جز is a fixed prepositional expression meaning “except.” نبد is an archaic negative past form (classical/Nāhīv) of نبود ‘was not,’ so the clause literally reads “except justice and goodness was not in the world” → “there was nothing in the world except justice and goodness.” Word order is topical: the excluded items (به جز داد و خوبی) come before the copular negation and location phrase.
segments.s651-g242: یکی بود = ‘there was one’ (یکی as an indefinite/ordinal 'one'); با links qualities or accompaniment. آشکارا نهان places two contrasting adjectives together (manifest and hidden), so با + [adj adj] means ‘one who was with/both manifest and hidden’ (a poetic way to say both apparent and concealed). بود is the past copula.
segments.s651-g243: نهادیم is the 1st‑person plural past of نهادن ‘to place/establish’ (we placed/established). بر روی گیتی is a locative prepositional phrase ‘upon the face/surface of the world’ (بر + روی + گیتی), and خراج is the direct object meaning ‘tribute/tax’ that was imposed.
segments.s651-g244: درخت گزیت is a noun phrase ‘the Gazit‑tree’ (درخت + proper name گزیت). از پی here means ‘after / following / in the wake of’ (classical word order places it before the thing it follows), so از پی تخت عاج links that tree to the ivory throne (تخت عاج = 'ivory throne').
segments.s651-g245: چو here is temporal ‘when.’ این نامه آرند uses آرند (classical 3rd‑person plural present/near‑past form of آوردن) ‘they bring’/‘bring,’ and نزد شما is a prepositional phrase ‘before you / in your presence.’ The clause is a temporal subordinate clause introducing what will happen when the letter is presented.
segments.s651-g246: که introduces an optative/blessing clause. فرخنده is an adjective ‘auspicious/fortunate.’ باد here is the optative particle (classical wish marker) meaning ‘may’ (not the noun ‘wind’). اورمزد is Ahura Mazda (the deity), and شما is the addressee; the phrase literally = ‘may Ahura Mazda make you fortunate.’
segments.s651-g247: کسی is an indefinite ‘someone / anyone.’ کو in classical usage often functions as a relative/particle (‘who/that’), introducing the subordinate clause; the supplied senses list does not include that sense, so it is treated as the relative particle here. برین is the fused form of بر این ‘on/for this’ (the pronoun comes after the preposition), یک درم is the object ‘one dirham,’ and بگذرد is a verb meaning ‘passes/pays’ (3rd‑person subjunctive/potential sense in context: ‘were to pay’).
segments.s651-g248: ببیداد is an adverbial/qualifying element meaning ‘with injustice / unjustly’ (بـ‑ prefix + بیداد), modifying the following verb. بر یک‌نفس is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase ‘as one breath / as a mere instant,’ and بشمرد (subjunctive/conditional 3rd person of شماریدن) means ‘he would count/consider.’ Together: ‘he would unjustly count it as a single instant.’
segments.s651-g193: به is the preposition marking time/place here ('on' or 'in'); بهرام روز = 'the day of Bahram' (بهرام is a proper name, here a calendrical/day name) + روز 'day'. و links the two time-words. بخرداد is a fossilized/prepositional form meaning 'in Khordad' (ب + خرداد) and شهر here means 'month' (شهر = month in older Persian). The whole segment is a temporal phrase 'on the day Bahram and in the month Khordad'.
segments.s651-g194: که is the relative/causal particle 'that / when / who' linking this clause to the previous time phrase. یزدانش is یزدان 'God' plus the enclitic -ش meaning 'to him' / 'his' (third-person clitic). داد is the simple past 'gave'. از جهان تاج = 'a crown from/of the world' (از = 'from/of', جهان = 'world', تاج = 'crown'). بهر at the end means 'for / as (to him)' — so the clause reads 'when God gave him the crown of the world.'
segments.s651-g195: This is a noun phrase: برومند is an adjective meaning 'flourishing/prosperous', modifying شاخ 'bough/branch'. از marks origin 'from' and درخت قباد = 'the tree of Qobad' (درخت = tree; قباد a proper name). Word order is adjective + noun + از + possessor phrase.
segments.s651-g196: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding شاخ ('which ...'). تاج بزرگی is the direct object 'a great crown' (تاج = crown; بزرگی = greatness/ greatness-sized = great). به سر is the prepositional phrase 'upon the head' (به used as 'on/to' with سر 'head'). برنهاد is the archaic/simple past of برنهادن 'to place on/raise', so the relative clause means 'which placed/raised a great crown upon the head.'
segments.s651-g197: سوی is a directional/prepositional particle 'toward/to/present before'. کارداران is plural 'administrators/agents/officials' (those who manage). باژ و خراج is a noun pair; خراج clearly 'tax, tribute'. باژ here is an older spelling/term (meaning a kind of levy/tribute) — the provided senses list doesn't give that meaning, so it is irregular in the sense list. The phrase names the officials responsible for levy/tribute.
segments.s651-g198: پرستنده functions as a postpositive adjective/participle 'worshipping / devoted' modifying the officials; in older Persian descriptive words often follow the noun. شایسته 'worthy' modifies whatever follows (i.e. worthy of فر و تاج). فر is the special royal concept 'farr' (divine royal glory) and تاج 'crown'. Together: '(those) devoted and worthy of farr and crown.'
segments.s651-g199: This is a formulaic salutation: بی‌اندازه 'immeasurably/excessively' (an adverb), از ما 'from us', شما را with the object/topic marker را gives the addressee 'to you' (شما = you), درود 'greeting/blessing'. Word order is emphatic: 'Boundless greetings from us to you.'
segments.s651-g200: هنر here means 'virtue/excellence' and serves as the subject/topic. با نژاد این = 'by/with this lineage' (با = 'by/with', نژاد = 'lineage/descendance', این = 'this'). بود is the past copula 'was'. با فزود (poetic order) contains فزود, the archaic past 'increased' (فزود = 'grew/was increased'); the construction means 'by this lineage excellence was increased' (the verb and auxiliary show a poetic inversion but the sense is 'it increased/was increased').
segments.s651-g1209: This is a prepositional/nominal phrase describing position: به پیش (towards the front) + اندرون (within/inside) + پهلوانی سترگ (a mighty hero/heroic quality). The phrase has no explicit verb here — in Persian epic style the verb of motion or appearance is often omitted and inferred from context (i.e. 'advanced' or 'stood forward'). Note that سترگ is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'great/mighty' modifying پهلوانی.
segments.s651-g1210: Another locative phrase: به جنگ اندرون = 'into/at the battle' (به + جنگ + اندرون) followed by هر یکی همچو گرگ = 'each one like a wolf'. Here هر یکی is a distributive 'each one' and همچو introduces a simile. The verb linking these descriptions is again omitted and understood ('was' / 'fought' / 'appeared').
segments.s651-g1211: به رومیش خوانند = 'in Roman (speech) they call (him)'. Here به + رومیش marks the language/register ('in Roman'), and خوانند is the archaic/poetic plural-present form meaning 'they call' (literary third-person plural used to report a name). فرفوریوس is a proper name being reported.
segments.s651-g1212: This is a nominal phrase describing that person: سواری سرافراز = 'a proud/triumphant rider/horseman', with با بوق و کوس = 'with trumpet and drum' (با + instruments). Adjective follows the noun in this order; با introduces accompaniment or instruments.
segments.s651-g1213: چو این گفته شد = 'when this was said' (چو = when; گفته شد is the passive/impersonal past 'it was said'). پیش بیدار شاه places the king at the front and characterizes him as بیدار (alert); the order is topical (time clause first, then the person described).
segments.s651-g1214: پدید آمد = 'appeared' or 'came into view' (simple past). از دور = 'from afar' (از = from, دور = distant). گرد سپاه literally 'around/about the army' but here functions as a compact way to indicate 'with the host' or 'the troop appearing' — گرد modifies سپاه to present the army as a mass following/around him.
segments.s651-g1215: بخندید = 'he laughed' (past tense, 3rd person). زان = 'at that / from that' linking to the preceding remark. شهریار جهان = 'the monarch/sovereign of the world' (title + epithet); Persian epic often stacks epithets after the noun.
segments.s651-g1216: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت here functions as 'said' but the provided sense list lacked the verb sense, so that is noted below). کین نیست از ما نهان = 'this hatred/vengeance is not hidden from us' — کین is 'vengeance/hatred', نیست = 'is not', از ما = 'from us' (i.e. 'to us'), نهان = 'hidden'. Word order follows Persian SOV tendencies but with predicate 'نیست' final in the clause.
segments.s651-g1033: ازو (from/of him) + خواست (asked/requested) takes the following noun phrase as the thing requested. Here the speaker 'asked of him' two nouns, پیروزی و دستگاه: 'victory and the establishment (of power/court)'. The object nouns follow the verb in this poetic order; ازو is the beneficiary/agent 'of him'.
segments.s651-g1034: نمودن is used as an infinitival/causative complement of خواست ('to cause/make/turn'), with دلش را as its definite object ('his heart') marked by را. سوی داد راه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the way/path of داد (justice)'; داد here is a noun 'justice' and راه 'way' together 'the path of justice'. The whole segment functions as the content requested: 'to make his heart turn toward the path of justice.'
segments.s651-g1035: پرستندگان را is the (definite) indirect object 'on/for the worshippers' (را emphasizes them), followed by ببخشید, a past-tense verb here meaning 'bestowed/gave' (bakhshidan). چیز is the thing given. Poetic order places the verb before its noun complement.
segments.s651-g1036: به جایی که is a locative relative construction 'to a place that / wherever', with درویش دیدند 'they saw a dervish' as the relative clause; نیز adds 'also/likewise'. The clause modifies جایی: 'and also to any place where a dervish was seen.' Note that دیدند is past tense; the implicit subject is the people present.
segments.s651-g1037: یکی 'a certain one/someone' + خیمه 'tent' + زد (light verb used with خیمه) = 'pitched/erected a tent'. پیش آتشکده 'in front of the fire-temple' is the locative. زدن with a noun like خیمه here functions idiomatically 'to pitch a tent'.
segments.s651-g1038: کشیدند is a past plural verb 'they drew/drew up' used with لشکر 'army' as its object: 'they drew up the army'. ز هر سو 'from every side' is a poetic prepositional phrase, and رده 'rows/ranks' specifies the formation: 'drew up in ranks from every side.' Word order (verb before object then locative) is typical of the poem's flexible syntax.
segments.s651-g1039: دبیر خردمند را is the definite object 'the wise secretary' (را marks him as definite/accusative). پیش خواند is a compound meaning 'called/summoned forward'—خواند here is 'to call/summon'. The order places the object before the verb (poetic inversion): 'he called the wise secretary forth.'
segments.s651-g1040: سخنهای بایسته 'necessary/fitting words' is the phrase describing the content; با او 'with him' indicates the person involved in the exchange. The verb براند (from راندن) is used figuratively here — literally 'to drive/bring forth' — and in context means 'uttered / addressed' (i.e., 'he gave/uttered fitting words with him' = 'he spoke appropriate words to him'). Word order places the noun phrase before the verb, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s651-g265: A prepositional noun phrase headed by the poetic preposition 'ز' (from/out of) that governs two coordinated nouns: 'تخم پراگنده' ('scattered seed') and 'مزد رنج' ('the wage/reward of toil'). Word order is typical Persian: modifier after noun ('تخم پراگنده' = 'seed that is scattered'); 'مزد رنج' is an attributive genitive-like compound meaning 'reward of hardship'.
segments.s651-g266: 'ببخشید' here functions as a past narrative verb meaning 'bestowed/gave' (third-person past in epic narration). 'کارندگانرا' is the direct object with the object marker -را ('the workers' as definite object). The final prepositional phrase 'ز گنج' ('from the treasury/treasure') indicates source; the clause order places the verb before the object-marked noun phrase for poetic rhythm.
segments.s651-g267: 'زمینی' is a demonstrative/nominal 'a land' (used as the head noun). 'که' introduces a relative clause. The sequence 'آن را خداوند نیست' is an existential/possessive-style construction meaning 'there is no lord for it' — Persian here uses 'آن را' (the place) with 'نیست' to express absence: literally 'that (place) a lord is not'.
segments.s651-g268: A continuation of the relative clause: 'به مرد' ('for/to the people') is a dative phrase; 'ورا' ('to it') resumes the place; 'خویش‌ش' (written here as 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش') means 'its kin/relationship'; 'و پیوند نیست' adds 'and there is no bond'. Overall: 'for the people there is no lord, and for it neither kinship nor tie exists.'
segments.s651-g269: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal meaning 'ought not / should not'; it governs the subordinate clause introduced by 'که'. 'آن بوم ویران بود' is a simple past clause ('that land was ruined') with predicate adjective 'ویران' and past copula 'بود'. Together: 'It ought not to be that that land was ruined.'
segments.s651-g270: 'که' resumes the explanatory clause. 'در سایه' is a locative prepositional phrase meaning 'in the shadow / under the protection of'; here 'سایه' carries the sense 'protection'. 'شاه ایران' is the genitive construction 'the King of Iran'. Final 'بود' places this situation in the past ('lay/was').
segments.s651-g271: 'که' introduces the reason/explanation. 'بدگو' is a noun 'slanderer' (one who speaks ill). 'برین کار' is a fused prepositional phrase: 'بر' ('on/upon') + the orthographic variant 'ین' = 'این' ('this') + 'کار' ('affair/matter') → 'upon this affair'. 'ننگ آورد' is a verbal phrase 'brought shame' (ننگ 'shame' as object, آورد 'brought' in past). The verb follows its object, as common in Persian poetic word order.
segments.s651-g272: 'که' again links the clause; 'چونین' ('thus/so') qualifies the manner; 'بهانه' is 'pretext/excuse'. The compound 'بچنگ آورد' (ب + چنگ + آورد) literally 'brought into the grasp' is idiomatic: 'seized' or 'made use of' — here 'he seized such a pretext'. The verb is past, completing the narrative sequence.
segments.s651-g625: This is a compact, poetic word order: the noun سرشک (teardrop) and the source phrase از دو دیده (from two eyes) come before the verb ببارید (fell), while the subject شاه (the king) is placed at the end for emphasis. ببارید is a past action verb (third-person singular) describing drops/tears falling.
segments.s651-g626: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is past tense 'he heard' (3rd singular) with its subject (the king) carried over from the previous clause; گفتار فریادخواه is a noun phrase 'the speech/words of the one crying for help' (an idāfa/possessive relation: گفتار = speech, modified by فریادخواه).
segments.s651-g627: به دستور is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by order' (به used adverbially), placed before the verb for topical emphasis. گفت here functions as the main past-tense verb 'said' (poetic word order puts the verb before its subject); آن زمان ('at that time') is a temporal modifier, and شهریار is the subject 'the sovereign' placed after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s651-g628: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. پیش آمد is a compound meaning 'came up/occurred' (پیش = before/forward, آمد = happened/came), and the noun phrase این کار دشوار خوار ('this affair, difficult and humiliating') follows the verb; adjectives دشوار and خوار postmodify کار as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g629: نشاید is an impersonal/formal negative 'it is not fitting' (classical Persian); کزین پس = 'from this henceforth' ('کز' = from, 'این پس' = after this). The verbs/words چمیم و چریم appear as a paired first-person plural form (–یم ending) meaning roughly 'we graze/roam'—the clause expresses that it is not proper that we henceforth live as grazers/herdsmen. The verbs are in a subjunctive/optative sense following the impersonal نشاید.
segments.s651-g630: وگر is a conditional 'but/if'; تاج را has the object marker را marking 'the crown' as definite object; خویشتن (ourselves) is emphatic; پروریم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive 'we should nurture/raise'. Together the clause reads 'and if we were to raise the crown ourselves...' — the verb is subjunctive/optative expressing a hypothetical.
segments.s651-g631: جهاندار is the subject 'the ruler/sovereign'; نپسندد is present (or timeless) negative 'does not approve / will not accept' (3rd person). از ما ستم is a noun phrase 'oppression from us' = 'our committing injustice' (از marks source/agent in this idiom).
segments.s651-g632: که introduces the content/result clause 'that...'. باشیم is a subjunctive/present-jussive 'we be' (used to express what would be objectionable), followed by the adjective شادان 'joyful' describing 'we'. The coordinated phrase و دهقان دژم places the noun 'the peasants' with the adjective 'sullen' but omits a repeating verb; the verb باشیم is understood to govern the whole clause so the sense is 'that we be joyful and (that) the peasants be sullen.'
segments.s651-g1273: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برزد is a poetic past verb (from برزیدن) meaning 'fell/struck/appeared' in past narrative. ز + خرچنگ تیغ آفتاب is a prepositional phrase 'from/of (the) ...': ز = 'from', خرچنگ here is a poetic image meaning a sidelong or crab-like movement, and تیغ آفتاب = 'the sun’s blade' (تیغ = sword/blade, آفتاب = sun); word order places the originating image after the verb for heightened poetic effect.
segments.s651-g1274: بفرسود is a past intransitive/causative verb here meaning 'was worn away/was consumed' (poetic past). رنج is the patient 'suffering'. و simply joins the two clauses. بپالود is another past verb meaning 'cleansed/cleared/dispelled' and خواب is its object 'sleep' (i.e. drowsiness); the two short verbs form a coordinated outcome: suffering ceased and sleep was driven off.
segments.s651-g1275: تبیره is the noun 'an announcement/interpretation/explanation' and is the subject. برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court of the king' (درگاه = royal court/presence, شاه = king); the clause describes something coming out of the royal presence.
segments.s651-g1276: گرانمایگان is the subject 'the illustrious/weighty ones (nobles)'. برگرفتند is a past transitive verb 'they took up / took on / took to' and here governs راه as its object; note the poetic word order: subject – verb – object (راه = 'road/way'), meaning they set out or departed by the road.
segments.s651-g1277: ازان is a compound demonstrative 'from that/therefrom'. دز in this context is best read as a place-name (Dez) rather than 'thief', and آن شارستان 'that town/city' continues the locative listing. مرد و زن is a simple coordinated subject 'men and women' who are the actors in the following clause.
segments.s651-g1278: به درگاه کسری is the prepositional phrase 'to the court of Kisra (Khosrow)'. شدند is the past plural verb 'they became / they were' used here in the sense 'they formed / came together as'. انجمن is a predicate noun 'an assembly/meeting' — so the clause means 'they assembled at the king’s court.'
segments.s651-g1279: که introduces a content/explanatory clause 'that/for'. ایدر = 'here'. ز جنگی is a causal/partitive phrase 'from/of the war' (ز = 'from', جنگی = 'military/war-like'). سواری is the affected noun 'horseman/rider' and نماند is the negative past 'did not remain / there remained none' — together meaning 'here no horsemen were left because of the war.'
segments.s651-g1280: بدین is a fused demonstrative/prepositional form (به + این) meaning 'in/at this'; شارستان = 'town/city'. نامداری is a noun 'renown / notable person' and نماند is the past negative 'did not remain'. The clause therefore states 'in this city no one of renown remained.' (Note: the segment is split into ب + دین in the token list, but they function together here as بدین 'in this'.)
segments.s651-g593: 'چنین گفت' is a short narration meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = thus; گفت is the past verb 'said'—here the sense list given doesn't match the verbal use). 'کای' is the vocative 'O', and 'روشن کردگار' is an adjectival epithet ('روشن' before the noun 'کردگار') addressing God as 'radiant Creator'. Word order places the manner phrase before the speech verb and then the vocative.
segments.s651-g594: A string of epithets joined by repeated 'و' in apposition to the vocative: 'جهاندار' (lord/sovereign), 'پیروز' (victorious), 'پروردگار' (Sustainer/Lord). The repetition of 'و' is a poetic coordination; these nouns are titles addressing the same addressee.
segments.s651-g595: 'تویی' is the emphatic second-person copula 'thou art/you are' placed before the predicate for emphasis. 'آفریننده هور و ماه' is an agent noun 'creator' followed by its coordinated objects 'هور' (poetic 'light/splendor') and 'ماه' (the moon) linked by 'و'.
segments.s651-g596: Two present-participle epithets coordinated: 'گشاینده' (one who opens/reveals) and 'نماینده راه' (literally 'one who shows the way'). The particle 'هم' (here 'also') emphasizes that the addressee fills both roles; word order keeps the participles after the predicate, continuing the address begun in the previous segment.
segments.s651-g597: 'جهان آفریدی' = 'you created the world' with 'آفریدی' a past 2nd-person form ('you created'); the prepositional phrase 'بدین خرمی' (= 'with this delight') follows to specify the manner. In older/poetic Persian 'بدین' functions as 'with this' (ب + this), and 'خرمی' is the noun 'delight/joy'.
segments.s651-g598: Relative clause introduced by 'که' ('that' / 'such that') qualifying the preceding manner: 'از آسمان نیست پیدا زمی' literally 'is not found/seen from the heavens on earth'. Word order places source 'از آسمان' before the negated predicate 'نیست پیدا' and the adjective 'زمی' ('earthly' / 'on earth'), yielding the sense 'nothing like it is visible in heaven or on earth.'
segments.s651-g599: 'کسی کو' functions as an indefinite/relative 'whoever' (کسی = anyone; کو here an archaic relative particle). 'جز از تو' = 'except from you' (i.e. other than you). The verb 'پرستد' is in a general/habitual sense 'worships', and the adverbial particle 'همی' marks ongoing/habitual aspect ('does/ever does').
segments.s651-g600: 'روان را' is the definite object ('the soul' + object marker 'را'), followed by the prepositional phrase 'به دوزخ' ('into Hell') and the verb 'فرستد' (3rd person singular present/habitual 'sends'). The final 'همی' again gives a general/habitual aspect: '(he) will have his soul sent to Hell.'
segments.s651-g1321: ز بس ... lists causal items: ز بس here (= 'because of/from so much') introduces the reason; بس functions as 'so much/many' modifying the following nouns. The nouns باغ، میدان، آب are simply coordinated by و ('and') in the usual order; روان is an adjective/participle after آب meaning 'flowing'.
segments.s651-g1322: همی تازه شد is an older/poetic verbal sequence: همی marks an ongoing or emphatic aspect and precedes the verb; تازه شد = 'became fresh/new'. پیر گشته جهان is a participial description: پیر گشته = 'grown old' (گشته as 'become/turned'), with جهان 'the world' as the noun being described/affected. The copula is condensed into شد and the participle pairs with it.
segments.s651-g1323: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said'; گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' (the senses list gives conversational senses but it is the verb). با موبدان indicates 'in the presence of / to the mobeds' (با = with/with the participation of), and شهریار names the speaker (the king) — the word order places the verb phrase before the agent phrase, a permissible poetic inversion.
segments.s651-g1324: که introduces the content of what was said ('that ...'). The clause has a slightly inverted, conditional arrangement: انطاکیه است این = 'this is Antioch' with the copula است, and the conditional اگر نوبهار ('if it is spring') follows, giving the sense 'this is Antioch, if it is spring' (i.e. 'if this is spring, then this is Antioch'). The copula is explicit (است) and the conditional marker اگر comes after the demonstrative.
segments.s651-g1325: کسی کو ... ندیدست is an archaic relative/negative construction: کسی کو functions like 'anyone who' or in effect yields a negative existential when combined with the negative sense of ندیدست ('has not seen' / 'is not seen'), producing 'no one has seen'. خرم بهشت is the object/phrase 'a joyful paradise' (خرم modifying بهشت). The verb form ندیدست is poetic/past perfective in sense 'has not seen/was not seen'.
segments.s651-g1326: This segment lists attributes of that paradise using z-ablative and conjunctions: ز + مشک + اندرو = 'from/made of musk within it' (اندرو = 'in it/within'). Then خاک وز زر خشت strings together features of the ground and bricks; وز here is an archaic/poetic conjunction joining خاک and زر خشت (functionally 'and'), so the phrase reads 'its soil (is) [of] dust, and its bricks (are) of gold.' The copula is omitted (nominal apposition) as is common in poetry.
segments.s651-g1327: درختش ز یاقوت = 'its tree(s) of/from ruby' uses ز as a poetic 'of/from' indicating material or figurative constitution. The second half, آبش گلاب, is a nominal equative without an explicit verb: 'its water (is) rosewater.' The conjunction و links the two parallel image-clauses.
segments.s651-g1328: This is a compact appositional sequence: زمینش سپهر = 'its earth (is) the firmament/heavens' and آسمان آفتاب = 'its sky (is) the sun.' There is no copula; nouns are placed in apposition to produce striking, condensed images (common in epic/poetic description).
segments.s651-g505: This is a string of prepositional phrases coordinating places: به چوگان (‘to/for the polo [ground or game]’) and بر دشت نخچیرگاه (‘on/upon the plain of the hunting‑ground’). Classical/poetic Persian freely repeats short prepositions (به / بر) before each noun phrase; نخچیرگاه is a compound noun ‘hunting‑ground’.
segments.s651-g506: Main clause with topical/directional phrase first: بر ما (‘toward us’) gives direction, شما را is the beneficiary/reciprocal object marked by the object particle را, and راه گشادست (‘the road is wide/open’) is the predication. گشادست is an adjectival predicate ‘is wide/open’; word order places direction/beneficiary before the predicate, typical in older Persian.
segments.s651-g507: A series of contrasted states introduced by repeated به for emphasis: به خواب (‘in sleep’) و به بیداری (‘and in wakefulness’) و رنج و ناز (‘and in hardship and in indulgence/comfort’). The repeated preposition gives the sense ‘whether in X or in Y’. ناز here means pampering/indulgence rather than coquettishness.
segments.s651-g508: ازین بارگه = از این بارگه (‘from this court/palace’); کس is the indefinite/anyone; مگردید is an archaic negative imperative/prohibitive ‘do not return’ and باز here reinforces the sense of 'back/again'. The verb comes at the end, as is normal in Persian clauses.
segments.s651-g509: یک تن ز من تافته = ‘a single person of mine (set apart/distinguished)’. یک تن is ‘one person’; ز is the poetic form of از (‘from/of’); تافته (participle/adjective) means ‘set apart’ or ‘distinguished’. مخسبید here is a verb form placed before the noun phrase (poetic inversion); in context it functions as a prohibition/statement about sleeping / being idle (archaic usage).
segments.s651-g510: مگر introduces the exception ‘unless’; آرزوها همه یافته is a verbless/result clause with یافته (past participle ‘having found/attained’) expressing the condition: ‘unless (he) has found/acquired all his wishes’. The implied subject of یافته is the یک تن referenced earlier.
segments.s651-g511: بدان گه (= به‌دان‌گاه, ‘at that time’) is a temporal adverbial; شود is the verb ‘become’; شاد و روشن describe the resulting state ‘happy and bright/clear’. دل + م is the heart with the enclitic (my), so the clause means ‘my heart will become happy and clear’. Word order places the time phrase first, then the verb and predicate, as common in poetic diction.
segments.s651-g512: که introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason/purpose: ‘that/when’. رنج ستم‌دیدگان = ‘the suffering of the oppressed’ (ستمدیدگان = those wronged). بگسلم is a 1st‑person verb (ب‑ prefix + گسلم) meaning literally ‘I break/loose’ and here figuratively ‘I break apart/relieve’ their suffering — an archaic verb form but used transparently as ‘I will undo/relieve’. The verb appears at the end of the clause.
segments.s651-g889: ور‌ای is an archaic/poetic opening here: ور (conditional 'if') + ای (vocative 'O'), addressing someone as 'O ...'. دون is a vocative adjective 'low/ignoble (person)'. که introduces the following clause ('who/that'). از دشت نیزه‌وران is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain of spearmen' where دشت (plain) is modified by the following plural noun نیزه‌وران ('spearmen') by simple juxtaposition.
segments.s651-g890: نبالد is a third-person verbal form of بالیدن used here in a conditional/hortative sense with the preceding ور — 'should boast / take pride'. In this poetic word order the verb comes before its indefinite subject کسی ('anyone'). از کران تا کران is an idiomatic prepositional span 'from edge to edge / from one end to the other' (از…تا = from…to).
segments.s651-g891: زمین is the noun 'land' (subject). آنک is an archaic relative/'that' introducing the following descriptive clause. بالا + ست is the adjective 'high' with the copula ست ('is'): 'that is high'. پهنا is the object 'breadth/expanse', and کنیم is first-person plural ('we make / let us make') used here as a hortative/future-like verb — 'we will make/spread'.
segments.s651-g892: وزان = و + زان, 'and from that', linking the next clause to the previous. دشت بی‌آب is a noun + adjective sequence 'plain (دشت) that is waterless (بی‑آب)'; note the adjective follows the noun. دریا کنیم repeats کنیم ('we will make') with دریا as its object: 'we will make (it) a sea.'
segments.s651-g893: فرستاده is the agent 'the messenger'. بشنید is a past tense 'he heard' (here describing what the messenger did). و آمد 'and came' joins the action. چو + گرد is a simile 'like dust' i.e. 'like a whirlwind/dust‑storm' (چو = 'as/like', گرد used as 'dust/whirlwind').
segments.s651-g894: شنیده modifies سخنها: 'the words (that he) had heard' — شنیده is a participial/adjectival use meaning 'heard'. همه 'all' emphasizes the totality. یاد کرد is a compound verbal idea 'he recalled / mentioned / recounted' (یاد = remembrance, کرد = did) in the simple past: 'he recounted/remembered all the heard words.'
segments.s651-g895: برآشفت is a past tense verbal form 'he grew angry / became enraged'. کسری is the proper name 'Kisra' (Khosrow) as subject. بدستور means 'by order' or 'in an ordered/official manner' and here marks that what follows was said as an ordered statement. گفت functions as 'said' (past), but the listed senses do not include the simple past 'said' so its sense index is unavailable.
segments.s651-g896: که introduces the reported clause 'that ...'. با مغز قیصر = 'with the brain/mind of Caesar' (با = 'with', مغز = 'brain/mind', قیصر = 'Caesar'). خرد نیست جفت is a compact predication: خرد 'wisdom' + نیست 'is not' + جفت 'a match/pair' — literally 'wisdom is not a match', i.e. 'wisdom does not pair/attach to Caesar's mind' meaning 'Caesar lacks matching wisdom.' The word order is typical poetic inversion with the predicate elements grouped at the end.
segments.s651-g1081: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). یک تن is an indefinite expression 'one person' (تن used as 'person') that precedes the relative phrase از راه من 'from/my way' (راه + possession من). بگذرند is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative (بـ- prefix + گذر + -ند) used in conditions: 'should pass' / 'pass'.
segments.s651-g1082: دم خویش = 'their own breath/life': خوی (root) + possessive ش (written separately here) yields 'one's own'. بی‌رای من is a negative prepositional phrase modifying how they should reckon it ('without my consent' / 'as belonging to me'); بی‌رای is a compound negative. بشمرند is again 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative of شمردن ('to count/reckon'), linking back to the conditional introduced by اگر.
segments.s651-g1083: بدرویش is an adjective/noun phrase describing those ill‑favored or unlucky (to/for whom things are bad). مردم is the direct object 'people'. رسانند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive/optative of رساندن used here causatively to mean 'they bring/inflict' and رنج is the object 'suffering'.
segments.s651-g1084: وگر ('and/but if') sets up an alternative conditional clause. بر here means 'upon/against' (motion/impact on someone). بزرگان is 'the great ones' (nobles/important men). که introduces a relative clause 'who', دارنـد is present plural 'have/possess', and گنج is 'treasure'—together 'the great ones who have treasure'.
segments.s651-g1085: وگر 'if/but if' again begins a conditional. کشتمندی is a noun/agent 'a slayer' or 'murderous one'. بکوبد is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive/optative of کوبیدن 'to strike' (here 'strikes'). به پای means 'at the foot/feet' (به + پای), indicating the target/place of the blow.
segments.s651-g1086: وگر again introduces another conditional: پیش لشکر 'in front of/the head of the army' (پیش = before/in front of, لشکر = army). بجنبد is 3rd-person singular subjunctive/optative of جنبیدن 'to move/shift' (here 'should move/shift'), and ز جای 'from his place' (ز = from, جای = place). The clause describes someone leaving his appointed position before the army.
segments.s651-g1087: ور is an archaic conditional particle equivalent to 'if'. آهنگ here means 'intention/aim'; آهنگ کردن = to intend or set one's aim. بر is the preposition 'toward/on', میوه‌داری (literally 'fruit-bearing' or 'fruitful enterprise') functions as the object/goal of that intention, and کند is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن 'to do/make' (so 'he directs his aim toward fruitfulness' / 'he seeks to be fruitful').
segments.s651-g1088: وگر again marks a conditional. ناپسندیده is an adjective 'unacceptable/unseemly'. کاری here means 'an action/deed' (object of کردن), and کند is the present/subjunctive of کردن 'to do' — 'if he does an unseemly deed'.
segments.s651-g1281: همه (all/everyone) is the patient group; کشته و خسته are two participial/adjectival predicates linked by و and governed by the single past auxiliary شد, so the clause means ‘were killed and became exhausted.’ بی‌گناه stands after them as a qualifying adjective ‘the innocent.’ The verb شد is the past/resultive marker here, giving the sense ‘were made/killed and were left weary.’
segments.s651-g1282: گه آمد is an impersonal/time expression 'an occasion came' (lit. 'a time came'); که introduces a subordinate clause. بخشایش آید uses آید in the present/future/optative sense 'may/should come' to express that pardon would be granted. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' and شاه is the source 'the king.'
segments.s651-g1283: This segment is a coordinated list of social groups: زن (women) و کودک خرد (a small child / little child) و برنا (the youthful) و پیر (the old). خرد here qualifies کودک and means 'small/young.' The conjunction و simply links the items in a typical enumerative order.
segments.s651-g1284: نه خوب آید is an impersonal negative construction 'it is not good'; آید here means 'happens / is fitting.' از داد یزدان اسیر is a prepositional phrase 'captives/from the justice of God' where از = 'by/from' and داد یزدان = 'God's justice'; اسیر is a predicative/adjectival noun 'captive.' The whole says it is not proper that these groups be made captives by divine justice.
segments.s651-g1285: چنان شد is a resultive phrase 'thus it became/so it happened.' The following nouns دز و باره و شارستان are presented in a verbless series describing the outcome—'thieves, plunder/load, and the city'—with شد supplying the resultive force for the list. The nouns are simply enumerated rather than fully inflected by a verb for each.
segments.s651-g1286: کزان 'from which' serves as a locative/relative preposition referring back to the ruined place; پس here means 'afterwards'; ندیدند is past plural 'they did not see'; جز 'except' introduces the sole thing they could see; خارستان is the object 'a thorny thicket/wasteland.' The clause literally: 'from which afterwards they saw nothing except a thorny waste.'
segments.s651-g1287: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; قیصر is the subject 'Caesar'; گنهکار is a predicate adjective 'sinful/guilty'; شد is the past 'became.' ما که‌ایم combines the pronoun ما 'we' with که‌ایم (که + ایم), where که functions like a relative/interrogative 'who/what' combined with the present of بودن 'ایم' to form a rhetorical question 'what are we?' The whole is a complaint: 'When Caesar has sinned, what are we (to do)?'
segments.s651-g1288: بقالینیوس is a personal name; اندرون is the locative 'inside/inner quarters'; بر here combines with چه‌ایم to form the idiomatic question 'بر چه‌ایم' = 'what are we (to do/in what state are we)?' — بر is the prepositional/locative element 'on/upon/at' and چه‌ایم (چه + ایم) is 'what are we.' Together the phrase conveys 'we are inside Bāqālinius's house — what can we do?'
segments.s651-g945: نهادند is 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن, used here to mean 'they placed/affixed/appointed'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon/to' marking the target of the action. نامه‌بر is a compound noun 'letter‑bearer / envoy' and مهر شاه is a genitive phrase 'the king's seal' (literally 'seal of the king') — together giving 'they affixed the king's seal on the envoy'.
segments.s651-g946: سواری is the noun 'rider/horseman' (object), گزیدند is the past 3pl of گزیدن 'they chose'. زان is the old/poetic contraction of 'از آن' meaning 'from that/there', and بارگاه is 'the (royal) court / palace' — 'they chose a rider from that court.' Word order has the chosen noun before the verb (typical in Persian narrative lists).
segments.s651-g947: چنانچون is a comparative/introductory particle 'as/just as' or 'as was fitting'. ببایست is an impersonal modal ('it was necessary / was fitting'); here it governs the implied choice. چیره‌زبان is an adjectival compound 'eloquent' used as a noun phrase ('an eloquent one').
segments.s651-g948: This is a string of adjectives describing the chosen man. جهاندیده is 'worldly‑wise' or 'well‑travelled'; و is the coordinating 'and'; گرد here functions adjectivally (poetic) as 'active/going about, well‑versed' (derived from the verb sense 'to go about'); روشن‌روان is a compound 'bright‑soul / clear‑minded' (cheerful, lucid in spirit). The adjectives pile up to qualify the implied person.
segments.s651-g949: فرستاده is the subject 'the envoy/messenger'. با + نامه denotes accompaniment or instrument 'with the letter' (i.e. bearing the letter), and شهریار is the possessor 'of the sovereign / the king' — 'the king's letter.'
segments.s651-g950: بیامد is simple past 'he/it came'. بر here marks motion toward someone and should be read 'to/unto'. قیصر نامدار is the prepositional object 'the famed Caesar' (قیصر = Caesar/Emperor, نامدار = renowned).
segments.s651-g951: آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase 'he praised/clapped (said "well done")' with کرد as the light verb. The initial برو is an archaic/poetic particle here (not the literal imperative 'go'); it functions as a discourse particle and does not mean literal motion in this line (sense not listed among the provided options). نامه بداد in context means 'he gave the letter', but the provided sense list for بداد only lists 'oppression' and so does not match the actual verbal use 'gave' (see senses mapping). The clause order places the praise, then the act of handing over the letter.
segments.s651-g952: همان رای is 'the same counsel/plan' (همان = that same, رای = counsel/plan). کسری is the proper name 'Kisra/Khosrow' (genitive relation: 'Khosrow's counsel'). The particle برو again appears as an archaic connective ('thereupon/then') rather than literal 'go' (so the literal senses listed do not apply). کرد is the past 'he did/acted', and یاد here is a noun used verbally 'took to mind / remembered / kept in mind' — together: 'he followed Khosrow's exact counsel and took it to heart.'
segments.s651-g409: This is an inverted, compressed classical order: literally 'dirham-more made one‑day king'. درمی (dirham) is the object/thing affected, فزون کرد is a verbal phrase 'made more/increased' with کرد as the past tense verb, and روزی شاه places the time and subject ('one day the king') in postposed order for poetic effect.
segments.s651-g410: به دیوان is the prepositional phrase 'to/into the divan/court' (به + destination). خروش آمد is an intransitive verbal expression 'a cry/uproar came', with آمد in the past tense; از بارگاه is a source adjunct 'from the palace', placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s651-g411: که introduces reported speech/relative content here ('that'/'(said) that'); اسب سر is a genitival compound where سر means 'chief/foremost' ('the foremost steed'), جنگجویان is the plural genitive 'of the warriors', and بیار is the imperative 'bring' (second‑person command) — a compressed command framed by که.
segments.s651-g412: This is an appositive or identifying tag: سوار جهان نامور شهریار strings together titles/epithets. سوار 'rider/horseman' + جهان 'of the world' (modifier), نامور 'renowned' (adjective) and شهریار 'the shah/king' — a sequence of nouns/adjectives identifying the speaker or addressee; word order is adjectival and titular, typical of epic style.
segments.s651-g413: فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly/heartily' modifying the past tense verb بخندید 'laughed'. نوشین روان is a compound epithet ('noshin‑ravan' = 'sweet/pleasant‑soul' or a proper epithet/name), functioning as the subject that performs the laughing.
segments.s651-g414: که here is causal 'for/because'. دولت جوان بود is a simple past copular clause 'the state's fortune/prosperity was young' with بود as the past copula, و links the second clause خسرو جوان 'and Khosrow (was) young' — the second 'was' is understood from the first copula.
segments.s651-g415: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. برخاست is the past tense 'rose/stood up', and بابک is the subject. ز دیوان is the ablative/prepositional phrase 'from the divan' (ز = from), and شاه names whose presence follows (the genitive relation is established by word order and the following phrase).
segments.s651-g416: بیامد is the past 'came'. بر here marks direction 'to/toward' (came to), نامور 'renowned' qualifies پیشگاه 'presence/royal audience' — together 'came before the renowned presence' (i.e., entered the shah's audience). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s651-g1185: بفرمود is a past, honorific/polite verb meaning ‘he commanded/said’. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/command (‘that’/‘so that’) rather than temporal ‘until’. تیرباران is a compound noun meaning ‘a shower/volley of arrows’ and functions as the action being commanded. کنند is the third-person plural subjunctive/present used here to report the commanded action (‘they should/do’), so the whole clause literally reads ‘he commanded that they shower with arrows.’
segments.s651-g1186: هوا is the noun ‘the air/sky’ (subject of the clause). چون is the simile preposition ‘like/as’. تگرگ is the literal ‘hail’ used here metaphorically to describe the falling arrows. بهاران qualifies تگرگ as ‘spring hail’ (seasonal image). کنند repeats the third-person plural verbal form ‘they make/do’, here meaning ‘they made the air be like…’ (resultative use).
segments.s651-g1187: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral in a negative/totalizing sense (‘not one’/‘not a single’ in this narrative idiom). تاجور is ‘merchant/trader’. خود is an emphatic/possessive ‘his/own’ emphasizing the subject. به لشکر indicates association/placement ‘with the army’. نماند is past third-person singular of ماندن ‘to remain’ (here conveying that not one stayed with the army).
segments.s651-g1188: بران is an archaic compound (= از + آن) meaning ‘on/at that’ or ‘from that’; here it points back to the place just mentioned. بوم is ‘homeland/soil’. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’. خار is ‘thorn’ (here likely ‘thorny ground’ or ‘affliction’ as a place-image). خاور is ‘the East’. بماند is a form of ماندن (present/subjunctive form used in narrative as a past resultative here) meaning ‘he remained/stayed’. The clause lists places where someone remained.
segments.s651-g1189: همه + گنج قیصر is a genitive sequence meaning ‘all the treasure of Caesar’. به تاراج داد is a light-verbal phrase: به تاراج (‘to plunder/for pillage’) modifies داد (‘gave’), so the sense is ‘he gave [it] up to plunder’ or ‘he let it be pillaged’. داد is the simple past ‘gave’. Word order is typical Persian: object phrase followed by the verbal phrase.
segments.s651-g1190: سپه را uses the postposed را (definite/object marker) with سپه ‘the army’ to indicate the recipient/target of the giving. همه بدره و تاج داد: همه ‘all’, بدره (‘full moons’ used poetically = beauties), و تاج ‘and crowns’, داد ‘gave’ (past). The clause therefore states what was distributed to the army. Note the poetic use of بدره as a metaphor for beautiful persons/objects.
segments.s651-g1191: برآورد here is a past narrative verb meaning ‘(there) arose/was raised’ or ‘brought forth’ in context (the sense closest in the supplied options is ‘accomplished/brought about’). زان is an archaic contraction of از + آن = ‘from that’. شارستان means ‘city’ or citadel. رستخیز is a lofty/poetic noun that can mean ‘resurrection’ or ‘uprising’; here it denotes an uprising/uprising from the city. The construction describes that an uprising sprang up from that town.
segments.s651-g1192: همه ‘everyone/all’ is the subject. برگرفتند is the past plural of برگرفتن (بر + گرفت + ند) meaning ‘they took up’ or ‘they adopted/assumed’; with راه it means ‘they took the road’. راه is ‘road/way’. گریز is ‘flight/escape’. Together: ‘all took the road of escape’ (they fled).
segments.s651-g489: جهانی here functions as a collective subject ('all the world' / 'the whole world'). به درگاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the court/presence'. بنهاد is the past form of نهادن ('placed/turned'); in classical/poetic Persian it often means 'set' or 'turned'. روی ('face') is the object of بنهاد, so the literal order is 'the world placed/turned (its) face to the court'.
segments.s651-g490: هر آنکس که is a relative phrase 'every one who / whoever'. بد here is an adjective 'bad, wicked' qualifying the person. بر زمین is a locative 'on the earth' (بر + زمین). راه‌جوی is a substantive participial compound 'one who walks/seeks the road' (راه 'way' + جوی 'seeker/walker'), so the whole clause means 'every person who walks (i.e. acts) wickedly on the earth.'
segments.s651-g491: خروشی (a noun from خروش) 'a shout/clamor' is the subject; برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from', so ز درگاه شاه = 'from the king's court/presence'. The clause is a straightforward past narrative: 'a clamor arose from the king's court.'
segments.s651-g492: که introduces the reported content ('that' / 'saying that'). هر کس که 'whoever' is a free relative. جوید is a classical present (or jussive) form of جستن 'to seek' and here means 'seeks'. سوی داد راه is a noun phrase 'toward justice's path' (سوی 'toward', داد 'justice', راه 'way/path'): 'whoever seeks the way to justice.' The clause is open-ended and reported by the preceding خروشی.
segments.s651-g493: بیاید is an optative/subjunctive classical form meaning 'let him come' or 'may he come' (often used in narrative to admit someone). بدرگاه 'into the presence / to the court' combines the preposition بِـ with درگاه. نوشین روان is a compound epithet: نوشین 'pleasant/delightful' modifies روان 'soul/spirit' (here a poetic epithet, 'pleasant-souled' or 'delightful in person'), describing the place/person receiving the visitor.
segments.s651-g494: لب شاه خندان is an idiomatic phrase 'the king's smiling lip' i.e. 'the king's smile' (لب 'lip' used for an expression). و links the coordinated noun phrases. دولت جوان groups دولت 'state/fortune/prosperity' with جوان 'young' as an epithet 'youthful prosperity' or 'the young sovereign's bounty'. The whole segment names the favors to which the seeker is admitted.
