segments.s651-g495: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'aloud / with a voice' where به takes an adverbial sense ('by / with'). گفت here is the past tense 'spoke/said' (the provided sense list is lexicalized but in context it is the verb 'said'). آن زمان 'at that time' (آن = 'that/then', زمان = 'time'). شهریار is the speaker 'the sovereign/king'. The word order is typical: adverbial + verb + temporal phrase + subject.
segments.s651-g496: که introduces the reported speech. جز 'except' negates alternatives. پاک یزدان 'the Pure God' (پاک 'pure', یزدان 'God/divine'). مجویید is a negative jussive/imperative form 'do not seek' (addressing listeners). یار 'companion/friend' is the object: 'seek no companion except the pure God.' The clause is an explicit moral injunction from the king.
segments.s651-g577: 'که‌ای' is a poetic vocative/attention marker (roughly 'O' or 'O you'); 'زیردستان' is the plural noun 'subjects/vassals' addressed; 'شاه جهان' is a genitival phrase 'king of the world' (شاه governing جهان). Word order is vocative → addressee → title.
segments.s651-g578: The clause is a relative/continuative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that'). 'دارد' is present third-person singular of داشتن used here to mean 'holds/harbors'. 'گزندی' (from گزند) is the object 'harm/hostility'; 'ز ما' ('from/against us') marks the target/source relationship; 'در نهان' is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s651-g579: 'مخسبید' is a negative exhortation (classical/poetic) addressing the addressees: 'do not sleep' / 'do not slumber'. 'ناایمن' is a predicate adjective 'unsafe/insecure' describing the addressed people; 'از شهریار' ('with respect to/from the king') specifies the source of danger (i.e. you are not safe from the shah).
segments.s651-g580: 'مدارید' here functions as an injunction meaning roughly 'do not follow / do not accept' (poetic imperative addressing the group). 'ز اندیشه' is a prepositional phrase 'from the thought' (i.e. 'be not influenced by'); 'دل نابکار' is a noun phrase 'the wicked/treacherous heart', the source of the bad thought to be rejected.
segments.s651-g581: 'ازین گونه' ('از' + 'این' written as 'ازین' + 'گونه') is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus'. 'لشکر' is the subject 'the army/host'. 'بگرگان' (ب + گرگان) is a poetic prepositional phrase 'to/among the wolves'. 'کشید' is past tense 'drew/led/carried' — here meaning the army was driven/led into the wolves (metaphorical destruction).
segments.s651-g582: 'همی' is an adverbial particle adding continuity/emphasis ('also/then/indeed'). 'تاج و تخت بزرگان' is the coordinated object 'the crowns and thrones of the great ones'. The repeated 'کشید' is the same past verb 'carried off / took away', saying that crowns and thrones were likewise seized/removed.
segments.s651-g583: 'چنان دان' is an imperative formula 'know/consider it so' (چنان = thus; دان = consider/know). 'که' introduces the result or stipulation clause. 'کمی نباشد' is a negative jussive/subjunctive phrase 'let there be no lack/shortage' (نباشد = 'not be'). 'ز داد' ('of/from justice') specifies that what must not be lacking is justice.
segments.s651-g584: 'هنر' here means 'virtue/excellence' or the right qualities of rule. 'باید' is the modal 'must/ought'. 'از شاه و رای و نژاد' lists the sources: 'from the king, from counsel (rāy), and from lineage' — i.e. true excellence must originate in these three.
segments.s651-g369: مبادا is a negative precative/optative ('may it not be', 'lest') introducing a clause via که. از لشکری is a prepositional phrase 'from an army' (از + لشکری), and یک سوار is the (indefinite) subject 'a single horseman'. The main verb is omitted here (an elliptic construction common in older/poetic Persian): the sense is 'may it not be that a single horseman (comes/goes)...'.
segments.s651-g370: نه negates the following instrumental phrases: 'not with ...'. The structure links two 'with' phrases joined by و ('and'), so the negation scopes over both items (i.e. 'neither with a rag nor with a cuirass'). کارزار ('battle') is placed after these phrases to indicate the purpose/place (to fight/on the battlefield). Note the verb still remains omitted and is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s651-g371: بیاید is a jussive/optative third-person form ('let him come' / 'may he come'). برین here is the adjective 'exalted/supreme' formed from بر + the adjectival suffix ین, modifying بارگه ('court, royal hall'). بگذرد is a second verb in the same jussive sequence ('let him pass'); both verbs share the same understood subject.
segments.s651-g372: عرض گاه is a compound meaning the place of presentation/audience ('audience-chamber' or 'approach to the throne'); و links this noun to ایوان او ('his iwan/porch'). بنگرد is the jussive/subjunctive 'let him look/regard' (third person), completing the imperative sequence 'come... pass... and see'.
segments.s651-g373: هر آنکس که باشد is a standard relative clause 'whoever is/whosoever is'. که introduces the clause; باشد is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person 'is/shall be'. به before تاج indicates possession or being 'in/with' the crown (i.e. 'having the crown'), and ارجمند is an adjectival attribute 'esteemed/precious' modifying تاج.
segments.s651-g374: This segment lists the attributes or honors associated with that person using repeated به to mark accompaniment/possession: 'with فر (royal glory) and بزرگی (greatness) and تخت بلند (a lofty throne)'. بلند is a postpositive adjective modifying تخت ('lofty throne'). The repeated و connects the items in a parallel series.
segments.s651-g375: بداند is a prescriptive/narrative third-person form ('let him know' / 'he should know' / 'know that...'). که introduces the subordinate content clause. بر عرض آزرم literally 'upon/at the presentation of Azram'—بر + عرض (here 'presentation/audience') + آزرم (a proper name) — meaning 'in Azram's presence'. نیست is the copula 'is not / there is not', negating what follows.
segments.s651-g376: سخن ... نیست states the negated predication: 'there is no speech/words with ...'. با محابا ('with deference') is an adverbial phrase; the second coordinated phrase با شرم ('with shame/modesty') is linked by و. The final نیست repeats (or continues) the negation: 'there is no deference in speech, nor modesty.' Note the idiomatic use: 'شرم' here reads as 'modesty' rather than moral shame.
segments.s651-g97: نگهدار here is a nominal title 'the Preserver' (a name/epithet of God) acting as the subject; تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'); جست is a poetic/archaic perfect/past-stem of جستن used here with the sense 'spring up / be raised' or 'be so' and belongs to the same clause as تخت بلند (= 'the lofty throne'). Note the adjective بلند follows the noun تخت, as typical in Persian.
segments.s651-g98: تو را is a marked (definite/emphatic or dative) object meaning 'for you'; بر + noun expresses 'for/upon/in the matter of' (here 'in/for worship'); بود is the past copula 'was' linking to the predicate یارمند, which here functions as a predicative noun/adjective 'helper, aid'. Word order is somewhat inverted (object before predicate) as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g99: همه quantifies the noun تندرستی ('all well‑being/health'). به + فرمان here means 'by/through the command of' (instrumental/authoritative sense), and اوست is the fused pronoun + copula ('he is' / 'is his'). The clause states that well‑being comes by his decree.
segments.s651-g100: Parallel structure: همه نیکویی ('all goodness') is the subject; زیر here means 'under / subject to' (i.e., governed by) and پیمان اوست is the predicate 'is under his covenant/pledge'. The copula ست (= است) closes the clause in the usual classical poetic form.
segments.s651-g101: ز (poetic 'from') marks the lower bound 'from chaff' (خاشاک). تا introduces the upper bound 'as far as / up to' (هفت چرخ بلند = 'the seven high heavens'). Note the numeral/adjective phrase follows the head noun (چرخ بلند = high heaven).
segments.s651-g102: همان ('the same' / 'those very') picks out the listed elements; the coordinated nouns آتش و آب و خاک ('fire and water and earth') are the subject, and نژند is a predicate adjective meaning 'base/low/worthless'. Classical Persian allows this noun-list + predicate order.
segments.s651-g103: به introduces the indirect object/target 'to' — here 'to the being/existence of God' (هستی یزدان is a genitive-like sequence 'the existence of Yezdan/God'). گوایی is a noun meaning 'testimony, eloquence, or giving voice' and دهد/دهند (plural) is the verb 'they give' — a present/plural or generic poetic usage meaning 'they show/give'. The clause means that things testify to God's existence.
segments.s651-g104: Parallel clause: روان 'the soul' is the theme, تو را is the marked object 'your', را marking definiteness/emphasis, آشنایی 'familiarity / acquaintance' is the thing given, and دهند is the same plural verb 'they give'. The two clauses form a balanced pair ('they bear witness to God's being; they give knowledge/familiarity to your soul').
segments.s651-g185: همه روی گیتی is a noun phrase meaning “the whole face/surface of the world” (همه before the head for ‘whole’, روی = ‘face/surface’, گیتی = ‘world’). پر از داد is an adjectival phrase ‘full of justice’ (پر + preposition از + noun داد), and کرد is the 3rd‑person past verb ‘did/made’ that binds the predicate to the subject implied earlier in context. The order is typical poetic Persian: determiner + head phrase, then predicate ‘پر از داد کرد’.
segments.s651-g186: بهرجای is an adverb meaning ‘everywhere’; ویرانی (noun) names what is being affected (‘desolation/ruins’) and آباد is the resultative adjective ‘prosperous/inhabited’ used with کردن to mean ‘to make prosperous/rebuild’. کرد is again the past 3rd‑person verb. The sequence ‘ویرانی آباد کرد’ is a poetic inversion of the common verb phrase ‘آباد کردنِ ویرانی’ (to make ruins prosperous).
segments.s651-g187: بخفتند is the 3rd‑person plural past ‘they slept/lay down’ (the initial ب is an archaic/preverbal marker common in epic Persian). بر + دشت = ‘on the plain’, and خرد و بزرگ form a coordinated noun phrase ‘the small and the great’ (i.e. low and high, young and old). Word order places the location immediately after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s651-g188: به آبشخور آمد is ‘came to the watering‑place’ (به = to/into; آبشخور = watering‑place; آمد = past ‘came’). همی is an archaic preverbal particle adding emphasis or iterative/continuous sense; it often appears in Ferdowsi before verbs. میش و گرگ is the compound subject ‘the ewe and the wolf’ placed after the verb (poetic inversion) and linked by و.
segments.s651-g189: یکی here means ‘a certain’ (introducing an indefinite/particular item). نامه is ‘letter’. فرمود is a polite/past third‑person verb used for ‘composed/sent/said’ (in epic diction it often covers ‘wrote’ or ‘dictated’). بر پهلوی indicates the medium/language (‘in Pahlavi’), with بر + language following the verb.
segments.s651-g190: پسند آیدت is an optative formula ‘may it please you’ (پسند = liking/approval; آیدت literally ‘may it come to you’ with آید in a jussive/optative sense). چون ز من بشنوی is a subordinate clause ‘when/if you hear it from me’: چون here functions as a conjunctive ‘as/when’, ز = ‘from’, من = ‘me’ (object), and بشنوی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive ‘you hear / you be told’. The clause specifies the circumstance in which the wish applies.
segments.s651-g191: نخستین سر نامه کرد = ‘he made/wrote the first head/heading of the letter’ (نخستین = first; سر = head/top used here as ‘heading’; نامه = letter; کرد = did/made/wrote). از introduces the following formula ‘from …’, and مهست is given as the word/epithet used in that heading (here treated as an epithet/name with the sense ‘moonlike/beautiful’).
segments.s651-g192: This is a sequence of titles/names in apposition that completes the reported heading: شهنشاه = ‘Shahanshah’ (emperor), کسری = the royal name ‘Khosrow/Kisra’, and یزدان‌پرست is an epithet ‘God‑worshipper’ (a compound adjective). Together they function as the sender’s titulary after از (‘from’).
segments.s651-g9: کزویست is an archaic fused expression (کز + ویست) meaning 'from him is/comes'; it introduces a verbless predicate. نیک و بد are coordinated predicate nouns 'good and bad' following that copular sense. بدویست should be read as بد + ویست ('and bad is/from him') with the same copular idea, and کام is a separate noun 'desire/wish' governed by that copula; the clause lists what originates from him.
segments.s651-g10: ازو is the prepositional phrase 'from him'; مستمندیم is a first‑person plural predicative (مستمند + -یم) 'we are needy/dependent'. وزو here is a poetic contraction (و + زو) 'and from him' used to link the next predicate, and شادکام is an adjective 'fortunate/joyful' serving as the second predicative describing the same subject.
segments.s651-g11: ازویست is again the fused 'from him is'; فرمان is the noun 'command/order' serving as the predicate. و links the coordinated clause; زویست repeats the 'is from him' formula and مهر is the noun 'love/kindness' as the second predicate — a parallel list of what proceeds from him.
segments.s651-g12: به here functions instrumentally as 'by' (i.e. 'by the command of'), فرمان is the governing noun 'command', and اویست is a fused form corresponding to 'او است' ('it is his' / 'is by him'); بر چرخ is a locative phrase 'upon the wheel/sky', and مهر is again 'love/kindness' — the clause says that by his command love rests on the heavenly wheel. Word order places the instrumental phrase before the locative.
segments.s651-g13: ز is the poetic preposition 'از' ('from' or 'by'); رای here means 'counsel/plan' and وز is the sequence 'و ز' ('and from'), so تیمار او is 'his care/solicitude'. نگذریم is first‑person plural negative present/subjunctive 'we do not pass/go beyond' (ن‑ negates the verb). The clause says we do not go beyond his counsel and care.
segments.s651-g14: نفس here is the idiomatic 'a breath' (a single life‑beat or self) and جز is the exception particle 'except'. به فرمان او is the instrumental/agent phrase 'by his command', and نشمریم is first‑person plural negative 'we do not count/ reckon'. The clause literally says 'we do not count even a breath except by his command.'
segments.s651-g15: به تخت مهی is a prepositional phrase 'on/to the moonlike (radiant) throne' (مهی = moon‑like/beautiful). بر هر آنکس که is a relative construction 'upon every one who...' and داد is the noun 'justice' which takes the verb کند in the relative clause — together: 'upon every one who does justice'.
segments.s651-g16: کند is the third‑person singular verb 'does/makes' referring back to 'he who does justice'. در دل او باشد is an existential/subjunctive construction 'there shall be/in his heart be' (باشد as a subjunctive/optative 'be'), and از داد شاد is an ablative phrase 'joyful because of justice' (شاد = happy/joyful). The whole says that in the heart of such a person there will be joy from justice.
segments.s651-g457: سوی is the preposition 'toward/to' placed before its noun (prepositional phrase). پهلوانان is the plural 'champions, heroes'. The coordinating و simply links two parallel prepositional phrases; the second سوی repeats the preposition for emphasis. ردان is a plural noun placed as the object of the second سوی; it is an older/poetic noun (see senses) and here functions as a target group in parallel with پهلوانان.
segments.s651-g458: هم begins the clause as an adverb meaning 'also/too' and links this clause to the previous phrase. از is the preposition 'from/of' introducing the source of the following noun پند 'advice, admonition'. بیداردل is a compound adjective 'wakeful‑hearted' describing the source or tone of that advice. بخردان is the contracted poetic form of بهِ خردان (به + خردان) meaning 'to the wise/prudent' and functions as the indirect object.
segments.s651-g459: نبشتم is an older/poetic first‑person verb form (here taken as 'I wrote/sent'); it is irregular/rare in modern Persian. The short form بخ in poetry stands in for به ('to') before the following noun phrase. هر کشوری is an indefinite genitive phrase 'every/each country' and نامه‌ای (indefinite with -ی) is the direct object 'a letter'.
segments.s651-g460: به is the standard preposition 'to/for' marking the indirect object. هر again marks 'every/each'. نامداری here denotes a person of renown/notability ('a notable'); the trailing -ای on خودکامه‌ای makes خودکامه an indefinite noun 'an autocrat/self‑willed one'. The coordinating و links the two recipients.
segments.s651-g461: The initial که introduces a relative/conditional clause 'that/for/who'. هر کس که is the stock phrase 'whoever' (هر + کس + relative که). دارید is the present tense 'has/possesses' used generically here (3rd‑person sense). هوش و خرد are coordinated nouns 'intelligence and wisdom' acting as the possessed qualities.
segments.s651-g462: همی is an archaic/adverbial marker here giving a habitual/continuous sense ('will always' / 'continually'). کهتری را: کهتری 'the lesser/lowly one' is the definite object, and را is the definite object marker. پسر پرورد is a verbal phrase 'to raise/nurture as a son' — here پرورد is an older form of 'پروریدن' and follows the object, yielding 'raises the lesser as a son.'
segments.s651-g463: به میدان is a prepositional phrase 'to the field/arena' with میدان as the destination. فرستید is a past/poetic 2nd‑person or narrative past form of 'to send' (in epic style it can narrate actions of people as 'they sent' or address the addressee); context and the following verb make the clause purposive. با ساز جنگ is an instrumental phrase 'with the war‑instrument/tune' (ساز 'instrument' + جنگ 'of war') describing how they were sent.
segments.s651-g464: بجویند is a 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative form 'that they might seek' (shows purpose/result after فرستید). نزدیک ما is an adverbial phrase 'near us' (نزدیک + pronoun ما). نام و ننگ are coordinated nouns; نام 'name/reputation' and ننگ in epic usage can mean 'honor/glory' or 'stain/shame' depending on context — here treated as paired goals sought in battle. The clause expresses purpose: they were sent so that they might seek name and n(ong) close to us.
segments.s651-g281: بزرگان is the head noun 'the great ones / great men' (subject). که introduces a relative clause modifying بزرگان. شاهان پیشین is the predicate nominal 'former kings' and بدند is the archaic past plural copula/verb 'they were' (بُدَند → 'had been' / 'were'). The order is noun + relative + predicate (typical of classical Persian: the relative clause follows its head).
segments.s651-g282: ازین = از + این, 'from this / out of this' (a prepositional phrase). کار is 'deed/affair'. بر here functions as a directional preposition 'to / toward' and introduces the goal آیین (custom/practice). بدند again is the past plural 'they were / they became' used here to mean 'they turned / changed (to)'; the clause says they moved from this affair toward another custom.
segments.s651-g283: بد و نیک 'the bad and the good' (a coordinated phrase: both bad and good people/things). با + کارداران marks the instrument/agent 'by/with the administrators or officers'. بدی is a nominal predicate 'evil/harm' used elliptically (the verb 'کردند' is implied): 'they did harm to the good and the bad by their officials.'
segments.s651-g284: جهان is the affected domain 'the world'. پیش + اسب‌سواران is a locative/relative phrase 'before/in the presence of the horsemen' (پیش = 'in front of / in the sight of'). بدی again functions as a nominal predicate 'evil/harm' (verbal sense: 'they harmed / did evil to the world'), with the verb understood from context—classical Persian often uses a noun as the resultive predicate.
segments.s651-g285: خرد را: خرد 'wisdom/reason' marked by را as the definite/direct object. همه 'all/everyone' quantifies the scope. خیره is an adjectival/adverbial element 'astonished / agape' modifying the action, and بفریفتند is the past plural verb 'they deceived/led astray'. Together: 'they deceived wisdom / they bewildered all minds.' Note the object-before-verb word order characteristic of the verse.
segments.s651-g286: بافزونی گنج is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'with the increase of treasure / as the treasure grew'. نشکیفتند is a past plural verbal form from the root شَکفتَن (to open, to blossom) meaning 'they blossomed / swelled / opened' — here applied to the hoard (گنج) to mean the treasury expanded. In classical poetry a noun-subject may follow or be close to the verb; the sense is 'the treasure grew/blossomed.'
segments.s651-g287: مرا is the dative 'to me' (indicating the beneficiary). گنج is 'treasure' and دادست is the archaic perfect/present-resultive 'has given' (gave/has been given) with the subject omitted in the line. و links to دهقان سپاه, here 'a peasant host / peasant army' (دهقان = peasant/dihqān; سپاه = army/host). The clause reads: 'Treasure has been given to me, and a peasant host (has been given).'
segments.s651-g288: نخواهیم is the negative present/future first‑person plural 'we will not / we do not wish' (ن- negates خواهیم). بدینار is a prepositional phrase 'for a dinar' (به + دینار contracted: 'for a dinar'). کردن is the light verb 'to make/do' often forming verbal compounds (here part of the phrase 'بدینار کردن' = 'to make/settle/measure by a dinar' / 'to put a price of a dinar on something'). نگاه is 'regard/view/keeping' (here 'regard' or 'stewardship' — i.e., what one values). Together the clause says 'we will not set our regard/value for a dinar' — idiomatically: 'we will not sell our esteem / we will not value it for a dinar.'
segments.s651-g1193: خروش آمد is a common poetic expression meaning 'a shout/uproar arose' (آمد here = 'came/arose'); از introduces the source ('from'). The list 'کودک و مرد و زن' is a simple coordinated NP ('children and men and women') following Persian order (source phrase after the verb).
segments.s651-g1194: همه ... شدند انجمن: همه 'everyone' is the subject, پیر و برنا is a coordinated adjective phrase ('old and young'), شدند is the past-plural copular/verb 'became/assembled' and انجمن is the predicate noun 'assembly/meeting'—so literally 'everyone, old and young, became an assembly' = 'everyone gathered.'
segments.s651-g1195: به پیش + NP means 'into the presence of / before' someone; گرانمایه is an epithet 'esteemed/noble' placed before شاه, and آمدند is the past plural 'they came'—so 'they came before the noble king.'
segments.s651-g1196: This is a simple coordinated subject phrase followed by آمدند ('they came'). غریوان (here) names one class of people (likely 'wailers' or mourners; the provided lemma senses don't match the poetic use, so sense is left unspecified), و links the two nouns, and فریادخواه is 'one who cries out/requests redress' (a petitioner or supplicant).
segments.s651-g1197: که here introduces reported content ('that' / 'who said that'). دستور و فرمان و گنج is a series of direct objects (orders, commands, treasure). آن تست is an archaic poetic clause meaning 'that is yours' (تست corresponds to archaic 'توست' = 'it is yours'), so the whole clause reports 'that ... are yours.'
segments.s651-g1198: بروم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form of رفتن ('I will go' / 'that I go') used in reported or volitive context; اندرون رزم و رنج is the prepositional phrase 'into the midst/inside of battle and hardship.' The final آن تست repeats the emphatic clause 'that (too) is yours' with the same archaic تست = 'توست'.
segments.s651-g1199: به جان ویژه is an oath-like prepositional phrase 'by (my) special/beloved life' (به + noun + adjective). زنهار is an interjection/imperative 'beware / take heed / I implore you', خواه is the lexical verb 'to want/ask' used here in a pleading sense, and توایم is an archaic first-person predicative form meaning 'I am yours / I am with you'—so the clause is an emphatic plea 'by my life, I beg you / take heed; I am yours.'
segments.s651-g1200: پرستار is the noun 'guardian/keeper' used as predicate; فر + کلاه form a compound 'فرکلاه' meaning the royal glory/crown (فر as the royal radiance modifying کلاه 'hat/crown'); توایم again is the 1st-person predicative 'I am (yours/with you),' so the phrase states the speaker's role: 'I am the guardian of the royal crown / at your service to guard the crown.'
segments.s651-g1249: ز introduces source/origin ('from the Romans'); رومی specifies who (Rûm/Roman/Byzantine). سپاهی بزرگ is a noun phrase 'a great army' (سپاهی used collectively here), and اندروی is an adverb/locative 'inward/to the interior' indicating direction or location. The clause is elliptical (no finite motion verb is required in the line) and presents origin + description + destination in a compact poetic order.
segments.s651-g1250: همه quantifies the following noun phrase ('all/every'), نامداران is the plural noun 'the famed/renowned ones', and پرخاشجوی is an adjectival descriptor 'quarrelsome/aggressive' placed after the noun in the poetic/classical Persian order; together they qualify the army's fighters. This is a nominal phrase attached to the previous clause.
segments.s651-g1251: دو فرسنگ پیش = a distance expression 'two parasangs away/ago' where پیش functions adverbially for distance. اندرون بود شاه is inverted poetic order for 'شاه بود اندرون' — 'the king was inside'; بود is past 'was' and شاه (subject) appears after the verb for emphasis/poetic effect.
segments.s651-g1252: سیه گشت = 'became black/dark' with گشت (become) following the adjective (poetic order). گیتی is the affected noun 'the world', and ز + گرد سپاه gives the cause: z here 'because of' and گرد is best read as 'dust' (the army's dust); thus 'the world became dark because of the army's dust.'
segments.s651-g1253: خروشی (a roar/commotion) is the nominal subject and برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'; ز قالینیوس specifies the source ('from Galinius'). Word order is subject + verb + source phrase, a straightforward past-tense clause.
segments.s651-g1254: کزان = 'from which' (referring back to the roar). نعره names that 'shout'; اندک شد is a predicate 'became small/faint' (اندک as predicative adjective + شد 'became'). آواز کوس 'the sound/voice of the war‑drum' is the affected noun phrase, placed after the verb in inverted/poetic order: 'From that shout, the drum's sound became faint.'
segments.s651-g1255: بدان is a locative compound 'ب + دان' meaning 'at/to that' (here 'at that city'). شارستان = 'city/town'. در here is the noun 'door/gate' and نگه کرد is the compound verb 'kept watch' (نگه = watch/keeping + کرد = did/made), with شاه (the king) placed at the end as subject in the customary poetic inversion: 'The king kept watch at that town's gate.'
segments.s651-g1256: همی is an adverb 'still/continually', هر زمانی = 'at every moment' (هر quantifier + زمانی 'time/moment'), فزون شد = 'became greater/increased' (فزون 'increasing' + شد 'became'), and سپاه (army) is placed at the end as the subject. The phrase describes continual growth of the army.
segments.s651-g1025: بفرمود is a third‑person past, honorific/epic verb meaning “he commanded/said.” تا introduces the subordinate (purpose/that‑clause) describing what he ordered. نامه is the direct object “letter/book.” زند here is the noun Zand (a commentary/book) rather than a verb, and و است is the coordinating copula phrase (“and it is/was”) that links this clause to what follows; the whole segment sets up what was to be prepared.
segments.s651-g1026: بواز is an instrumental/prepositional phrase “by means of/through this.” برخواند is a past‑tense causative/action verb meaning “had (someone) read/recite (it).” موبد is the animate object/agent “the mobed (Zoroastrian priest).” درست is an adverb/adjective “properly/correctly,” modifying how the mobed should perform the reading.
segments.s651-g1027: This verbless, poetic clause lists actors and their motion: the first word is uncertain/ambiguous in isolation (here acting as a nominal element or name), و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” هیربد is the title “chief priest,” پیش means “before/in the presence of,” غلتان is a present‑participle/gerundial adjective “rolling/rolling about,” and به خاک is a prepositional phrase “to/onto the ground.” The whole phrase describes them rolling prostrate toward the earth.
segments.s651-g1028: همه is a universal quantifier “all/everyone.” دامن is the noun “hem/skirt/robe.” قرطها is plural “scraps/fragments” (here indicating the garment in tatters). کرده is the light perfect/past participial form “having made/done,” and چاک is the result noun/verb “(torn) into rags/gashes.” Together this describes everyone having rent their hems into shreds.
segments.s651-g1029: بزرگان is the plural subject “the great ones/nobles.” برو is a small adverbial/particle here meaning roughly “thereupon/then” (poetic usage), and گوهر is the direct object “gems/jewels.” افشاندند is a past transitive verb “they scattered/sprinkled.” The clause describes the nobles throwing gems (as an offering).
segments.s651-g1030: به + زمزم is a prepositional phrase “to/into the Zamzam (water) / with Zamzam water” (Zamzam as sacred/pure water). همی is an older/prosodic particle meaning “then/so/thereupon” or an ongoing aspect; آفرین is a noun “acclamation/praise,” and خواندند is the past verb “they sang/uttered/recited.” Grammatically this is a coordinated clause saying they raised acclamations at/over the Zamzam.
segments.s651-g1031: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” نزدیکتر is the comparative adverb/adjective “nearer/drawn nearer” (here figurative ‘as he drew near’), شد is the past auxiliary/verb “became/became the case,” نیایش is the noun “prayer,” and گرفت is a light verb “took/held” used here in the sense “began (to take up) prayer.” The clause means that upon approaching, he began to pray.
segments.s651-g1032: جهان‌آفرین is the compound noun “world‑creator / the Creator.” را is the definite object/marker that flags סטایش as the direct object; ستایش is the noun “praise/worship,” and گرفت again is the light verb “took up/began.” Grammatically this is an object phrase: he took up praise directed to the Creator.
segments.s651-g1097: به خشکی = 'to/onto the dry land, ashore' (به as preposition + خشکی 'dry land/shore'). روم‌گر is the poetic/archaic conditional construction: روم (1st‑person subjunctive/optative 'I go') + گر 'if' → 'if I go'. بدریای آب is a contracted/poetic form referring to 'the sea' (literally 'the sea's water'); in normal prose this would be به دریای آب or دریای آب. The whole segment sets up the conditional protasis: 'If I go ashore (and) to the sea's water...'.
segments.s651-g1098: نجویم is the verbal form 'جویم' (subjunctive/present) with the negative prefix نـ, giving 'I do not seek / I will not seek' here; the verb closes the conditional introduced earlier. برزم is an archaic nominal form (برز + possessive -م) meaning 'my camp/battle‑place' (locative idea) and so functions as the location affected; this lexeme is archaic/poetic. اندر = 'in/within', آرام = 'calm/peaceful', و = 'and', خواب = 'sleep' — together 'in calm and sleep', describing the state the speaker will take up in his camp.
segments.s651-g1099: منادیگری is a deverbal noun ('the act/office of heralding' or shorthand for 'the herald's action'); نام او رشنواد = 'his name, Rishnavad' where نام (name) is followed by the pronoun او and then the proper name in apposition. Word order (name + pronoun + apposition) is typical of Classical Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g1100: گرفت here is the simple past 'took' / 'seized' used idiomatically with به یاد to mean 'took into memory' → 'remembered' or 'held in mind'. آن سخنهای کسری = 'those words/speeches of Khosrow' (آن 'those/the'), so the clause means he remembered or registered Khosrow's words.
segments.s651-g1101: بیامد is the past 'he came'. دوان is an adverb/participle 'running, hastening'. گرد لشکر = 'around/about the army/host' (گرد with a following noun). بگشت here functions as a verbal element meaning 'went about/returned/turned' (poetic usage) — the whole segment describes his swift movement round the army.
segments.s651-g1102: به هر خیمه و خرگهی = 'to/at every tent and stall/hut' (به = to; هر = every; خیمه = tent; خرگهی an archaic word for a shelter/stall/hut). برگذشت is past 'passed by' or 'passed through', so the clause says he passed by each tent and shelter in turn.
segments.s651-g1103: خروشید is the past/plural 'they roared/cryed aloud'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' (often used in poetry), addressing the following noun phrase. بی‌کرانه سپاه = 'the boundless/endless host/army' (adjective after the vocative and before the noun), so the segment reports the host's loud cry.
segments.s651-g1104: چنینست = 'thus is' / 'such is' (copular statement). فرمان بیدار شاه = 'the command of the awake/vigilant king' (in poetry the ezāfe that would link these nouns is often omitted; بیدار qualifies شاه or the king's command). The whole clause quotes or reports the content of the outcry: that this is the king's alert command.
segments.s651-g1017: پس is an adverb meaning 'then/therefore' linking this action to the previous scene. پشت و پیش is a fixed pair meaning 'back and front' or 'all around' and is placed before اندر آزادگان, so the whole phrase means 'among the nobles, on every side.' اندر is the poetic preposition 'in/among,' and آزادگان is the plural noun 'the free/nobles.' Word order is topical: adverb + spatial pair + preposition + group.
segments.s651-g1018: همی‌رفته is the older continuous/imperfect form (همی- prefix + رفتن) meaning 'was going' or 'kept going.' تا here means 'as far as/until' and introduces the limit of motion, followed by the place-name آذرابادگان (historic Azerbaijan). The verb precedes the limit phrase, typical of Persian motion clauses where تا marks extent.
segments.s651-g1019: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when.' چشمش is the possessed noun 'his eye' (the sight of him). برآمد here means 'appeared/rose' or idiomatically 'fell (into view)' — 'when his eye fell upon…'. بآذرگشسب is the preposition به fused with the proper name (به + آذرگشسب), meaning 'upon/toward Azar‑Gushasp.'
segments.s651-g1020: پیاده شد is a light verb construction: پیاده ('on foot') + شد ('became') = 'he dismounted' or 'he became on foot.' از دور means 'from a distance' / 'at a distance.' و is the coordinating 'and.' بگذاشت is the past of گذاشتن with the -ب prefix (classical poetic form) meaning 'he put/left,' and اسب is 'horse' (the object he left).
segments.s651-g1021: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here introducing source or in the sense 'in accordance with'). دستور پاکیزه is a genitive phrase 'the pure/orderly ritual' (dastūr used for ritual/ceremony). برسم is an infinitive/attainment sense of رسیدن 'to reach/attain' and here combines with بجست (past of جستن) so the compound means 'he sought/strived to reach/perform' the pure rite; بجست functions as the main past verb 'sought/leapt toward.'
segments.s651-g1022: دو رخ 'two cheeks/faces' is the direct object marked by را. به here marks the instrument or means 'with' (literally 'by the water of his two eyes'), آب دو دیده is a genitive construction 'the water of the two eyes' (i.e., tears). بشست is the past tense 'he washed.' The result is idiomatic: he washed his face with his tears.
segments.s651-g1023: The initial به in به باژ functions adverbially ('by/with') to mark manner: 'with a cry.' باژ is 'cry/noise.' اندر آمد is the compound 'entered/ came in' using poetic اندر for 'in/into.' The following به before آتشکده is the directional 'to/into' and آتشکده is 'fire‑temple.' Together: 'he came into the fire‑temple with a cry.'
segments.s651-g1024: نهاده is the past participle/past form 'placed/put' (here presenting a secondary action: 'having placed' or 'placed [it/him]'). به introduces the goal/location 'at/to' and درگاه is 'threshold/entrance' (often temple threshold or court). جشن سده is a genitive phrase 'the festival of Sadeh' (سده here refers to the Sadeh festival, not a century). The phrase indicates that something was set down at the temple threshold for the Sadeh celebration.
segments.s651-g481: چو + past verb introduces a temporal clause: چو بشنید = 'when he heard'. سخنها (plural) is the direct object placed before the verb (typical Persian topical order). موبد is the subject; ز شاه is a short prepositional phrase 'from the king' (ز is the poetic form of از) that attaches to بشنید to specify source.
segments.s651-g482: آفرین خواند is a light past construction: خواند (past 'read/said') takes آفرین (an exclamation/acclamation) as its object — 'he pronounced acclamations'. بسی here intensifies (much/many). بر + noun marks the target ('upon/over the crown'); و links تاج and گاه. گاه, a short noun, follows تاج in a compound two-term phrase — both are objects/targets of خواند.
segments.s651-g483: Again چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خورشید is the subject and بنمود is the past transitive 'displayed/showed' (classical/poetic past form). تابنده is a participial adjective 'shining/radiant' modifying چهر (face/appearance); the modifier follows the verb in this elevated poetic order but still attaches to چهر.
segments.s651-g484: در باغ is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/over the garden'. بگشاد here is a poetic past/preterite sense 'opened' (the prefix ب- appears in older/poetic past forms), and گردان سپهر is a noun phrase 'the revolving/turning sky' with the adjective/participle گردان preceding the head سپهر; the whole phrase is the subject/agent of بگشاد or a nominative phrase describing what opened.
segments.s651-g485: پدید آمد is a compound past meaning 'appeared' (پدید as root + آمد 'came' = 'came into view'). آن توده is a demonstrative + noun 'that mass/heap' and شنبلید modifies it as a noun (a botanical/ear-like term); the whole phrase is the thing that 'appeared'.
segments.s651-g486: This clause strings predicates: دو زلف (two locks of hair) + شب تیره (the dark night; شب as noun, تیره as adjective) form the subject phrase; شد is the past 'became' (linking verb) and ناپدید is a predicative adjective 'vanished/hidden' that follows شد to complete the resultive idea ('became vanished'). The sequence is idiomatic poetic compression: subject → شد → result adjective.
segments.s651-g487: نشست is the simple past 'sat' (here used intransitively). The prepositional cluster از بر تخت is an older/poetic locative phrasing meaning 'upon/on the throne' (از بر is a classical combination indicating relation to the throne). نوشین روان are two adjectival epithets ('sweet/delightful' and 'flowing/graceful') that modify تخت and follow it in the poetic sequence.
segments.s651-g488: This is a nominal apposition: a string of epithets before the head noun — خجسته (auspicious) and دلفروز (heart-illuminating, here an epithet rather than a proper name) qualify شاه (king), and جوان ('young') follows as a final attribute. In Persian poetry multiple adjectives commonly cluster around the noun without additional particles.
segments.s651-g553: This is a prepositional/instrumental phrase listing two items. 'به' here works like a marker meaning 'with' or 'bearing' (instrumental), placed before each noun phrase. Adjective + noun order is regular: 'زرین عمود' = 'golden staff/standard' and 'زرین کلاه' = 'golden hat/helmet'. The repeated 'به' stresses the instruments borne by the envoys.
segments.s651-g554: Full clause: 'فرستادگان' is the subject ('the envoys'), followed by the past-tense verb 'برگرفتند' (3rd person plural past) meaning 'they took up' or 'they set out/took on' in this context; the object 'راه' (road/path) follows the verb — a common classical word order where the verb appears before its object. The sense is that the envoys took the road bearing the named items.
segments.s651-g555: 'به' marks direction/goal ('to'), followed by 'درگاه' meaning the royal court or audience-hall. 'شاه جهان' is a genitive construction 'the King of the World' (king + world), and 'آمدند' is the 3rd person plural past 'they came'. Word order is prepositional phrase + noun phrase + verb.
segments.s651-g556: 'چه' is exclamatory here ('what/with what'), introducing an emphatic phrase. 'با' is instrumental 'with', followed by 'ساو' and 'باژ' joined by 'و' — here treated as names/sounds or elements accompanying their arrival — then 'مهان' (guests/nobles). The clause ends with the repeated past verb 'آمدند' (they came); the second 'آمدند' reiterates and emphasizes their arrival. The construction emphasizes the manner/noise and the distinguished status of the guests.
segments.s651-g557: 'بهشتی' is an adjective 'heavenly, paradisiacal' placed before the noun. 'بد آراسته' is an older/poetic compound: 'آراسته' = 'adorned', and 'بد' here functions idiomatically to intensify or qualify the participle (classical usage), so the phrase means 'beautifully/remarkably adorned'. 'بارگاه' is the noun 'court/palace'. The whole phrase is a nominal description of the palace.
segments.s651-g558: The preposition 'ز' ('from' / 'because of' in poetry) introduces a causal/explanatory list introduced by 'بس' ('much/abundant' here). The nouns 'برده و بدره و بارخواه' are coordinated occupants/attendants of the palace: 'برده' = slaves, 'بدره' = beauties (poetic), 'بارخواه' = petitioners or those seeking favor. The sequence explains the cause of the palace's ornamentation/appearance.
segments.s651-g559: 'برین' = بر + این = 'upon/after this' (a temporal/discourse pointer) with 'نیز' 'also/too' adding continuity. 'بگذشت' is past singular 'passed' used impersonally about time; 'چندی' means 'a while' and 'سپهر' (the heavens) is the poetic subject/agent. The clause states that some time elapsed after the preceding events, in a literary inverted ordering.
segments.s651-g560: 'همی‌رفت' is an imperfective/past-continuous form ('was going on, continued'), the 'همی-' prefix signalling repeated/continuous action. 'با شاه ایران' = 'with the king of Iran' (instrumental/associative), and 'به مهر' means 'into/unto favor' or 'in favor/affection' — together conveying that fortune/kindness (the heavens) continued to accompany the Iranian king. Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrases, a poetic/classical pattern.
segments.s651-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کسری is the king's name (Kisra). نشست is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'sat'. The preposition pair از بر functions in classical Persian to mark location with بر as 'on/upon' (so از + بر together frame the place), followed by تخت 'throne' and عاج 'ivory' as its modifier.
segments.s651-g2: به here marks direction/place 'onto/on'. سر is 'head'. برنهاد is past tense of نهادن with the prefix بر- meaning 'placed on / set upon' (3rd-person singular). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. دل‌افروز is a compound adjective 'heart-delighting' (poetic). تاج is 'crown'. The phrase literally 'placed that heart-delighting crown on the head.'
segments.s651-g3: بزرگان گیتی is a nominal phrase 'the great ones of the world' (subject). شدند is the past plural of شدن 'became' used here to mean 'assembled / formed' and takes انجمن 'assembly' as its predicate noun after the verb, a common classical ordering.
segments.s651-g4: چو again 'when', followed by the verb بنشست 'sat down' (past 3rd singular). Note the verb precedes its subject سالار 'the commander/chief' — an older/poetic word order. با رای‌زن means 'with the counselors' (با = 'with').
segments.s651-g5: سر here functions as 'head' or 'chief/foremost' (the leading figure among the named). نامداران is 'the renowned/notables' (plural), so سر نامداران = 'the foremost of the illustrious'. زبان برگشاد is an idiom 'opened (his) tongue' = 'began to speak' (زبان + past participle برگشاد 'opened').
segments.s651-g6: ز is the classical preposition 'from'. دادار is 'the Creator' (God). نیکی دهش is a genitive phrase 'gift/grant of goodness' (دهش as 'gift'). کرد is the past of کردن 'did/made' used as a light verb here, and یاد functions as 'remembrance/mention' — together کرد یاد = 'made mention' or 'recalled'.
segments.s651-g7: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus'; گفت here functions as the verb 'said' though the provided lemma senses do not list the verbal sense). کز introduces the content clause (classical conjunction roughly 'that / from which / that from'), followed by کردگار سپهر 'the Maker of the heavens' (کردگار = Creator/Maker, سپهر = sky/heaven).
segments.s651-g8: دل ما = 'our heart(s)'. پر ... از is the predicate 'full (پر) of (از) ...'. آفرین here means 'praise/acclamation' (poetic noun). باد is the optative particle 'may' (archaic/poetic subjunctive marker, not the noun 'wind'). و مهر = 'and love/affection' (مهر). The construction yields 'may our hearts be full of praise and love.'
segments.s651-g521: نخواهند is a 3rd-person plural negative/future-like form (classical usage of nـ + خواهند) meaning 'they will not / they do not want'. نیز is an adverb 'also / too' placed after the verb here for emphasis. از شما marks the source ('from you'), and زر و سیم ('gold and silver') is the coordinated direct object following the prepositional source phrase (a common word order in poetic/classical Persian).
segments.s651-g522: مخسبید is a negative imperative (plural/formal) from خسبیدن 'to sleep' formed with the prohibitive مـ- + verbal form, here meaning 'do not sleep / do not rest'. زین پس is the fixed adverbial phrase 'from now on' (زین + پس), with پس giving the temporal sense 'after / henceforth'. ز من is a prepositional phrase 'from me'. دل is the direct object 'heart / loyalty', and ببیم is an archaic jussive/imperative-like form (not the modern ببینم spelling); in context it functions as an instruction/request meaning roughly 'give/entrust (me) [your] heart' (the listed dictionary senses don't match this exact archaic use, so that form is irregular/poetic).
segments.s651-g523: برآمد is a past/preterite verb 'arose / issued forth'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and ایوان is the place 'iwan / porch' (source of the cry). یکی here means 'someone / a certain one' (an indefinite subject) and is followed by آفرین, an exclamation/acclamation ('afarin!' = 'bravo!' / 'well done!') as the content of that outcry.
segments.s651-g524: بجوشید is a past-tense or perfective verb from جوشیدن used here in a vivid/poetic sense 'gushed / burst forth'. تابنده is a present-participle/adjective 'shining / resplendent' modifying the following noun phrase. روی زمین is a locative phrase: روی as 'upon / on' (not 'face' here) and زمین 'earth/ground', so the clause says that a shining presence poured/gushed upon the earth.
segments.s651-g525: که here introduces a wishing/optative clause (rendered in English with 'may'). نوشین is an adjective 'pleasant / delightful' describing روان 'soul / spirit'. باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical use of باد to express a wish). با + فرهی ('with nobility') is an instrumental phrase specifying what the wished-for condition should be accompanied by.
segments.s651-g526: همه‌ساله is an adverb 'every year / yearly' modifying the whole wish; با تخت is 'with the throne' (با = 'with/by', تخت = 'throne'); شاهنشهی is an adjective/noun 'imperial / of kingship' qualifying تخت, so the phrase places the wished blessing annually on the imperial throne.
segments.s651-g527: مبادا is a classical negative optative/prohibitive 'may it not be / lest'. ز تو is a causal/source phrase 'because of you / on your account' (ز here = 'because of / from'). تخت پردخت is a compound: تخت 'throne' and پردخت (from پرداخت) here as a noun/adjective meaning 'adorned/embellished' (i.e., 'the ornamented throne'). و گاه links 'and time/occasion' as a coordinate noun; گاه is a polyvalent noun meaning 'time / occasion / season'.
segments.s651-g528: مه here is the poetic image 'the moon' (a common metaphor for splendour) modifying the following noun phrase. این نامور خسروانی کلاه is a sequence of modifiers: این 'this', نامور 'renowned/famous', خسروانی 'royal', and کلاه 'cap/hat' (here metaphorically the crown). Grammatically the group is a nominal phrase apposed to or further specifying the preceding 'گاه مه' phrase; the line is phrased as a wish that such splendid royal insignia not be jeopardized 'because of you'.
segments.s651-g1153: برآورده‌ای is an archaic/participial expression meaning “(one) raised” or “lifted up” used nominally here; دید is the past tense “he saw”; سر بر هوا literally ‘head toward the air’ = ‘raised into the air’ (سر here = top/summit). The phrase as a whole states what he saw — a raised top/extremity thrust up into the air.
segments.s651-g1154: پر از … is an adjectival phrase ‘full of …’; از here marks the content (‘full of’); مردم = people; ساز = instruments (here musical/war instruments) joined with جنگ و نوا (‘war and melody’) to describe what fills the raised top. The conjunction و links the nouns.
segments.s651-g1155: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; خارا here is the rocky/flinty ground (a noun); پی افگنده is a participial cluster with افگنده ‘thrown/cast’ — together indicating things ‘cast from’ or ‘sent off from’ the rock; در قعر آب gives the destination ‘into the depths/bottom of the water.’
segments.s651-g1156: کشیده is a past participle ‘drawn/pulled’; سر باره is a tight noun phrase: سر (‘top/head’) + باره (‘load/burden’), i.e. the loaded head or the projectile’s top; اندر سحاب = ‘into the clouds.’ The phrase describes another class of projectiles whose (loaded) heads were pulled up into the clouds.
segments.s651-g1157: بگرد here is an archaic/poetic adverbial use meaning ‘around’ (rather than an imperative); حصار = rampart/fortress; اندر آمد سپاه = ‘the army came in/approached within.’ Word order is literary: the prepositional/adverbial phrase precedes آمد to set the scene.
segments.s651-g1158: ندیدند is past negative ‘they did not see’ (ندیدن = did not see); جایی here = ‘any place/anywhere’ (no place was found); به درگاه راه = ‘to/at the gate there was no way’ — the phrase means they found no passage through the gate. The clause is compact and typical of epic Persian: verb + negative + existential phrase.
segments.s651-g1159: برو here functions as an adverbial/temporal particle ‘at once/thereupon’ (not a literal imperative in this context); ساخت is past ‘made/built’; از چار سو = ‘on/from four sides’ (چار سو = four directions/sides); منجنیق = siege engine/catapult. The order places the adverbial before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s651-g1160: به پای آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase ‘came on foot/approached (to the foot/base of something)’ with به + پای + آمد; آن باره = ‘on that occasion’ or ‘at that moment’; جاثلیق is the title ‘Catholicos’ (a senior cleric). Together: ‘at that moment the Catholicos came (on foot/approached).’
segments.s651-g297: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بیداردل is a compound adjective 'wakeful-/vigilant‑hearted' describing کارداران 'stewards/managers'. من is a possessive in context ('my') attached after the noun (typical older word order: noun + possessor).
segments.s651-g298: به here marks direction ('into/to'); دیوان موبد = 'the court/office of the mōbed (Zoroastrian priest)' with the genitive relation implied by adjacency. شدند (third‑person plural past of شدن) is used here in the sense 'came/assembled' or 'became', and انجمن (assembly) records the resulting gathering (word order is poetic: verb + noun).
segments.s651-g299: پدید آید is a verbal phrase meaning 'comes to light / appears' (آید = 'comes'); از گفت = 'from the saying/words of'; یک تن = 'one person' (classical diction for 'a single man'); دروغ = 'a lie'. Together this creates a conditional result clause ('if a lie appears from a single person's words').
segments.s651-g300: ازان پس = 'after that / thereafter'. نگیرد is present/subjunctive/negative formation of گرفتن here used actively 'does not take / will not seize'; بر ما فروغ = 'radiance/uplift upon us' (بر = 'upon', ما = 'us', فروغ = 'radiance/splendour'). The clause says that after such an event no radiance will come to us.
segments.s651-g301: The clause uses a classical Persian order: به بیدادگر = 'for/to the tyrant (the unjust one)'; مرا مهر نیست literally 'there is no love for me' but functions as 'I have no affection'—so together 'I have no affection for the tyrant.' Note that مرا is split orthographically here (م + را) but functions as the experiencer/object marker, and نیست negates existence ('is not').
segments.s651-g302: پلنگ و جفاپیشه = 'the leopard and the oppressor/tyrant' joined by و 'and'; مردم functions as the viewpoint/experiencer ('to the people' / 'in the people's eyes') and یکیست is a copula 'are one / are the same'. The line states that, for the people, the predator (پلنگ) and the cruel doer are indistinguishable.
segments.s651-g303: هر آنکس که = 'whoever / every one who'; او is the emphatic third‑person pronoun 'he' (classical poetic usage); راه یزدان بجست uses بجست (past tense 'sought') to mean 'sought/followed the way of Yezdan (God)'. The relative clause sets up a general moral statement.
segments.s651-g304: بآب = 'with the water' (instrumental/prepositional form). خرد here = 'wisdom' (so بآب خرد = 'with the water of wisdom'); جان تیره = 'the darkened/obscured soul' (تیره modifying جان); بشست is past 'he washed'. The clause means he cleansed his dark soul by the purifying water of wisdom.
segments.s651-g1217: کجا introduces a rhetorical question meaning 'when/where (ever)'. جنگ را is the direct object (را marks the definite object) with جنگ meaning 'war/battle'. پیش ازین is the archaic/poetic sequence meaning 'before this' (پیش = 'before', ین = orthographic variant of این). ساختیم is the simple past 'we made/caused' (ساختن used here figuratively for 'wage/undertake' a war). The whole segment asks when we had ever engaged in such a war previously.
segments.s651-g1218: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/through/by'. اندیشه is 'thought/intention' and functions as the instrument or medium ('in/through thought'). هرگونه means 'in every way / every kind', modifying what follows. پرداختیم is past 'we attended to / worked out / carried out' (پرداختن in classical usage can mean to treat/attend to or to complete), so the segment says that they examined/arranged every possible plan by thought.
segments.s651-g1219: کی here functions as an interrogative/adverbial 'when' (it can be ambiguous with old proper-name use, but context reads as 'when'). تاجور is the common noun 'merchant/trader' used as the agent. بر لب آورد is a light-verb phrase 'brought (it) to his lip' (بر = 'to/on', لب = 'lip', آورد = 'brought'), and کف is 'palm' (the object moved to the lip) — together describing a gesture (cupping or putting the hand to the mouth/lip).
segments.s651-g1220: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he said/commanded' and here functions as 'he ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. برکشیدند is past plural 'they raised/lifted' (here 'raise the ranks/lines'), and صف is 'row/line' specifically 'battle line/formation'. The clause thus reports his command that the formation be raised or drawn up.
segments.s651-g1221: سپاهی is a military noun here meaning 'a force / troop' (derived from 'سپاه'). بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/in front of the army' (به = 'to/toward', پیش = 'before/in front of', سپاه = 'army'), so the segment says a force came up before the army.
segments.s651-g1222: بشد here is the past/preterite 'it became / it happened'. بسته is the adjective 'closed / shut'. The sequence بر گرد (literally 'on/around' + 'around/about') and the following parallel بر باد combine as poetic locutions to indicate enclosure on every side; راه is 'way/road'. The whole segment thus means the way became closed in all around (i.e., the route was blocked on every side).
segments.s651-g1223: شده is the past participle/verb 'has become / became'. نامور is the adjective 'renowned/famed'. لشکری here qualifies as 'military' or 'army-like' and combines with انجمن 'assembly/gathering' to form a noun phrase 'a renowned military assembly'. Word order is verb then predicate noun phrase (typical in Persian: verb + complement).
segments.s651-g1224: یلان is the plural of یل 'heroes' and functions as the head of this appositive phrase. سرافراز is an epithet 'proud / triumphant / lofty' modifying یلان. شمشیرزن is a compound noun 'sword‑wielder / swordsman' further specifying the kind of heroes; together the phrase characterizes the assembly as composed of proud, sword‑wielding heroes.
segments.s651-g73: همه مردمی is the subject phrase 'all/the people' (literally 'all people'). باید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it must be' or 'there must be' that takes the following noun as its complement; و is the coordinating 'and'; راستی is a noun meaning 'truth/uprightness' (here 'upright behaviour'). The clause literally says 'There must be among all the people honesty/uprightness.'
segments.s651-g74: نباید is the impersonal negative 'there should not be / must not (exist)'. به کار اندرون is a prepositional locution 'in the inner affairs' (به = 'in/to', کار = 'affair/business', اندرون = 'within/inner [matters]'). کاستی is the noun 'lack/deficiency' and is the thing being negated. Together: 'There must be no lack in the internal affairs.'
segments.s651-g75: هر آنکس که باشد is a relative clause 'whoever is' or 'anyone who is'; هر + آنکس gives the indefinite 'whoever/anyone'. باشد is the third-person verb 'be' in a relative/subjunctive sense ('may be' / 'is'). از ایرانیان marks the group 'from among the Iranians' (از = 'from/of', ایرانیان = 'Iranians/countrymen').
segments.s651-g76: ببندد is a third-person optative/subjunctive verb here meaning 'let him bind/attach (himself)' or 'should bind (himself)'. The sequence بدین (here split into ب + دین) functions as 'to this' (i.e. 'to this court'); بارگه is 'the royal court'; برمیان means 'in the midst/among'. The whole segment instructs that such a person be attached/committed to this court.
segments.s651-g77: بیابد is a third-person verb ('he will find' / 'may find'); ز ما = 'from us' (poetic z for az); گنج 'treasure' is the first direct object; و connects the second object; گفتار نرم 'soft/gentle speech' is the second object (گفتار = speech, نرم = gentle). The clause states what such a person will receive from the speaker(s).
segments.s651-g78: چو باشد is a conditional 'when/if he is'; پرستنده is a participial noun 'one who is devoted / a worshipper / a devoted follower' and serves as the subject of the condition. با introduces accompanying qualities 'with'; رای here means 'counsel/wise opinion' and شرم means 'modesty/shame' (i.e. modest behaviour). So: 'If he is a devoted one, with counsel and modesty...'.
segments.s651-g79: چو is the conditional 'when/if'; بیداد is the abstract noun 'injustice/oppression' and is the subject; جوید is the verb 'seeks' (3rd person); یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' modifying the following noun; زیردست is 'an underling / subordinate' and is the object of جوید. The phrase means 'if injustice seeks a certain subordinate...'.
segments.s651-g80: نباشد is a negative third-person copula meaning 'is not / will not be' referring back to that 'one underling'. خردمند 'wise' and خسروپرست 'one who adores/worships the king' are two coordinated predicates joined by و. The clause thus states that the underling will be neither wise nor a royal‑worshipper.
segments.s651-g937: وگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'); زیردستی is a noun meaning an underling/subordinate and is the subject of the clause; بود is the past/correlative copula 'was/there was' used here in the conditional sense; بر + منش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'against (or over) disposition/character' (بر marks opposition toward the noun منش). The phrase thus says 'if there was an underling against (your) disposition/standing'.
segments.s651-g938: به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the sword' (به marking instrument); یابد is the classical present/subjunctive/optative of یافتن used here in the sense 'will find/shall meet' (a literary future-like sense); ز من places the source/agent 'from me'; سرزنش is the direct object 'reproach' (i.e. blame or rebuke). The whole segment means 'he shall find reproach from me by the sword.'
segments.s651-g939: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; زان مرز = 'from that border' (زان = 'that/from that'); یک رش is a poetic measure phrase 'a single small amount/step' (رش = small quantity/measure); مپیمای is the classical form built on پیما(دن) with the prohibitive/optative prefix م- (here giving the sense 'do not/let not — you should not traverse'), so it functions as an hortative/negative imperative 'do not cross'; پای here means 'foot/pace' and ties to the idea of stepping. The segment therefore: 'do not take even a single step beyond that border.'
segments.s651-g940: چو خواهی introduces the reason/condition 'if/when you wish'; که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'; پیمان is 'the covenant/treaty/agreement' and بماند is the present subjunctive/optative 'remain/last'; بجای is an adverbial 'in place' or 'in force.' Together: 'if you want that the covenant remain in place.'
segments.s651-g941: وگر sets a conditional 'if'; بگذری is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of گذرِیدن meaning 'you pass/you go beyond' (here 'pass over/ignore'); زین سخن (literary order) = 'over/through these words' (زین = 'this/of this' in archaic/poetic usage, here marking the object phrase); بگذرم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of the same root and here means 'I will pass (it) over / I will forgive/let it go.' The pair expresses reciprocity: 'If you pass (i.e. disregard) these words, I will let them pass (forgive).' Note: زین as a demonstrative is an archaic poetic form.
segments.s651-g942: سر و گاه is a compact poetic coordination ('head and station/occasion' — here roughly 'head and position' or 'head and life'); تو is the possessor 'your'; زیر پی is a locative phrase 'under the foot' (زیر = beneath, پی here 'foot/track' used as 'foot'); بسپرم is the 1st-person present subjunctive/optative of سپردن 'to entrust/consign' meaning 'I will consign/commit.' So the clause says 'I will consign your head and station under the foot' — a poetic way of saying I will place you utterly at my mercy/underfoot.
segments.s651-g943: درود is a vocative/blessing 'greetings/blessings'; خداوند here functions as 'lord/sovereign' (a title) rather than exclusively the divine name in this phrasing; دیهیم is an epithet 'of the countryside/land' (an adjective/noun indicating territorial lordship) and و زور links 'and might/strength.' The phrase is a short benediction: 'blessings to the lord of the land and (to) strength.'
segments.s651-g944: بدان is the compound of the directional prefix ب- + demonstrative/relative دان here functioning as 'to that (one)'; کو is an archaic relative 'who/which' introducing a relative clause; نجوید is the negated present stem of جستن 'to seek' (نـ‑ marks negation: 'does not seek'); ببیداد is a prefixed phrase meaning 'with oppression/cruelty' or 'to act unjustly' (ب‑ indicating intensive or instrumental) and شور is 'tumult/strife.' Altogether: 'to him who does not seek tumult through injustice.' Note: some particles here are archaic/poetic in order and morphology.
segments.s651-g1073: طلایه (the vanguard/standard) is the thing given; به marks the recipient (to/for). هرمزد خراد here works as a personal name composed of two elements (Hormozd + Kharad), so داد is a simple past transitive verb 'gave' meaning 'assigned/gave (the vanguard) to Hormozd‑Kharad' — i.e., he appointed him commander of the vanguard. The word order is SOV-ish with the recipient prepositional phrase before the verb, normal in poetic Persian.
segments.s651-g1074: بسی here functions adverbially ('much', 'at length') modifying گفت (spoken); گفت is used as the act of speaking ('spoke / talked') and با او ('with him') gives the interlocutor. ز introduces the topic/concern ('about, concerning') and the coordinated nouns بیداد و داد mean 'injustice and justice.' The whole clause means 'he spoke much with him about injustice and justice.'
segments.s651-g1075: به هر سوی is a fronted prepositional phrase 'to every direction/side.' رفتند is the past plural verb 'they went,' and کارآگهان is the plural subject 'the knowing/informed ones' (those who investigate or bear knowledge). The word order places the adverbial/prepositional phrase before the verb, typical in poetry.
segments.s651-g1076: بدان is composed of the prefix ب- + دان (to know) and here means 'to ascertain / in order to know.' تا introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that') and نماند is a negative perfective/past-form used here with resultive force 'may not remain / did not remain.' سخن در نهان = 'a word/saying in secret' = 'no word remained hidden.' Together: 'they went to find out, so that no word remained hidden.'
segments.s651-g1077: ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' لشکر is 'the army,' and جهاندیدگان is a plural noun 'those who have seen the world' = 'the worldly‑wise / veterans.' را is the definite object marker attached to the phrase, and بخواند is a past tense verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (بخواند = he caused them to be called). The whole clause: 'He summoned the worldly‑wise from the army.'
segments.s651-g1078: بسی quantifies the following nouns ('much / many'), پند و اندرζ are two synonyms for 'advice / counsel,' نیکو qualifies them as 'good.' براند is a past tense poetic verb meaning 'he put forth / set before / offered' (here: offered advice). The clause thus means 'he offered many good counsels.'
segments.s651-g1079: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'), introducing reported speech. کین here carries the sense 'vengeance/host of vengeance' modifying لشکر 'army,' and بی‌کران is an adjective 'endless / boundless.' Word order places the comment before the explanation of composition.
segments.s651-g1080: ز again is the preposition 'from,' introducing the elements composing that army: بی‌مایگان 'the penniless / insignificant.' وز here functions as an archaic connector meaning 'and/from' in the poetic listing, and پرمایگان are the 'great/wealthy ones' or nobles. The line lists the army's members 'from the poor and from the great.'
segments.s651-g1369: پس is a discourse adverb meaning “then” that links this sentence to what came before. آگاهی is the noun “news/information” and آمد is the past tense verb “came”; the order here is noun + verb (typical Persian). ز + proper name (فرفوریوس) marks the source (“from Ferforius”) using the poetic preposition ز.
segments.s651-g1370: بگفت is the past tense 3rd‑person verb “(he) said” (the b- prefix is the classical form). آنچ is the relative/indefinite “that which / what,” and آمد here is the verb of the reported clause (“came / happened”). The proper name بقالینیوس follows the clause as the speaker (inverted word order: verb + clause + name).
segments.s651-g1371: به قیصر marks the indirect object/recipient (“to Caesar”). چنین گفت is the formula “thus he said.” The following کآمد is ک + آمد (که آمد) — the subordinating particle که reduced to ک plus the verb آمد — introducing the content clause “that the army came.” سپاه is the subject of that content clause (“the host/army”).
segments.s651-g1372: This nominal phrase names the arriving force: جهاندار کسری = “the world‑ruler Khosrow” (an epithet + name). ابا here functions in classical usage as a connective meaning roughly “with / having” (archaic), introducing companions of the host; پیل is “elephants.” و گاه adds another element — the exact nuance of گاه in this martial list is idiomatic/elliptical (an additional contingent/element). Word order places epithets before the name, and coordinated elements follow.
segments.s651-g1373: سپاهست is the copular/nominal assertion “it is a host” (noun + -ست, archaic copula). چندانک is an adverbial conjunctive meaning “to such an extent / so that,” introducing the consequence clause. دریا و کوه are the affected entities (“sea and mountain”), listed in parallel.
segments.s651-g1374: همی‌گردد is a poetic imperfective verb here meaning something like “are swept/are turned/are rolled” describing the effect on sea and mountain; Persian often uses a singular verb even when two nouns are coordinated. از گرد = “from the dust” with گرد used as “dust.” اسبان is the genitive/possessive plural “of horses,” and ستوه is a predicative adjective “weary,” meaning the horses (or the forces) are exhausted by that dust.
segments.s651-g1375: بگردید is a past form of گشتن here meaning “turned / was affected / changed” (i.e., Caesar’s mood or stance changed). قیصر is the subject. ز گفتار خویش = “because of / from his own words” (ز as causal/ablative + گفتار “speech” + خویش “his own”), explaining the cause of his change.
segments.s651-g1376: بزرگان فرزانه is a noun phrase “the great, wise ones” (buzurgān + adjective). را is the definite/object marker marking them as the object of خواند; خواند here means “he called/summoned” (one of its classical meanings). پیش is an adverb meaning “before / in the presence of,” so خواند پیش = “called before (him).”
segments.s651-g649: This is a pre-verbal set of oblique phrases that mark means and source: دو repeated 'به' here means 'by/with' (instrumental), followed by the materials سنگ and گچ; باید is the impersonal modal meaning 'must/it is necessary'; از قعر آب is a prepositional phrase 'from the bottom of the water' serving as the source of what is to be raised. Word order places instruments and source before the main verbal phrase that follows.
segments.s651-g650: برآورده is used here as the verbal complement of باید, meaning 'be raised/bring up' (a past-participle/verb form filling the necessity expressed by باید). تا introduces the extent/goal 'up to' (تا = 'until/up to'), and چشمه آفتاب is the goal phrase 'the spring/source of the Sun' (a poetic name). No explicit subject is given; the construction is impersonal/passive in effect.
segments.s651-g651: هر آنگه که is a temporal/concessive subordinating phrase meaning 'whenever/whensoever'; که introduces the subordinate clause. سازیم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive ('we make' / 'we should make') and takes زین گونه بند ('a bond of this kind') as its object (زین گونه = 'of this sort', بند = 'bond/fetter/fastening').
segments.s651-g652: ز (poetic = from) دشمن marks source 'from the enemy'; به ایران marks the target 'to Iran' (به = 'to/for'); نیاید گزند is a negative impersonal/result clause 'harm will not come' (نیاید = 'will not come/shall not come', گزند = 'harm'). Persian here places the negated verb before the noun denoting the undesired event.
segments.s651-g653: نباید + که + آید is an impersonal prohibition construction: 'it should not be that someone comes' = 'no one should come'. یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/anyone'. زین here functions as an archaic demonstrative/compound (roughly 'to/into this' or 'from this') and is followed by به رنج 'into hardship' where به indicates movement/tendency 'into' or 'to' + رنج 'suffering'.
segments.s651-g654: بده is an imperative 'Give!' addressing the addressee; هرچ خواهند is the object clause 'whatever they want' (هرچ = 'whatever', خواهند = 3rd pl. 'they want' or 'they will wish'). The coordinating و links to بگشای گنج, another imperative 'and open the treasure' (بگشای = 'open', گنج = 'treasure').
segments.s651-g655: A simple enumerative noun phrase listing social types: کشاورز (farmer), دهقان (peasant/land-worker), مرد نژاد (literally 'man of lineage' = 'man of noble birth' or 'of distinguished family'); the nouns are coordinated by و. مرد نژاد is a genitive/compound meaning 'a man characterized by (good) lineage'.
segments.s651-g656: نباید که introduces an impersonal prohibition 'should not'; آزار یابد is a passive-like verbal phrase 'receive/experience harm' (یابد here functions as 'receive'/'come upon'); ز داد 'from justice' indicates the agent/source (i.e., 'by/through the action of justice' or 'on account of judicial action'). Together: 'must not be harmed by justice.'
segments.s651-g1449: This clause is subject + intransitive verb + prepositional phrase: 'تبیره' is the subject (here a banner or banner-name) and 'برآمد' is the past/perfective intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth.' The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') introduces the source 'درگاه شاه' = 'the king's court' (درگاه + شاه in a genitive/appositive relation).
segments.s651-g1450: This is a topical/prepositional fronting plus verb + noun phrase: 'سوی اردن' (toward Jordan) is the directional phrase placed at the start for emphasis, 'آمد' is the past/polite form 'came,' and 'درفش سپاه' is a genitive phrase 'the standard/banner of the army' with 'سپاه' qualifying 'درفش.' Poetic word order puts the direction before the verb.
segments.s651-g1451: 'جهاندار کسری' is a title plus name (apposition): 'جهاندار' (world-ruler) modifies the proper name 'کسری.' 'چو' is the simile particle 'like/as,' 'خورشید' is the comparison term 'the sun,' and 'بود' is the past copula 'was.' The clause reads 'The world-ruler Khosrow was like the sun.'
segments.s651-g1452: 'جهان را' places 'the world' as a topical/definite object (را marks definiteness/emphasis). 'ازو' means 'from him'/'because of him.' The coordinate 'بیم و امید' ('fear and hope') is the compound predicate, and 'بود' is the copula/existential 'there was'—together: 'For the world, because of him, there was fear and hope.'
segments.s651-g1453: 'برین سان' is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'in this manner' (composition: بر + این/این variant + سان). 'رود' here functions as a verbal form 'proceeds/goes' (poetic present/imperative flavor); it governs the following nominal phrase 'آفتاب سپهر' = 'the sun of the sky/heavens' (آفتاب governed by سپهر in a genitive or descriptive relation). Note: the provided lemma-senses list treats 'رود' as a noun, but in context it is the verb 'rūd' = 'goes/advances.'
segments.s651-g1454: 'به' introduces an instrumental/possession phrase 'with/in (one) hand': 'به یک دست شمشیر' = 'a sword in one hand' and the parallel 'و یک دست مهر' = 'and (in) one hand kindness/affection.' The construction lists two coordinated noun phrases describing what the subject (the sun/king) holds.
segments.s651-g1455: This clause begins a correlative negative with 'نه' ('neither'). 'بخشایش آرد' = 'he brings/grants forgiveness' where 'آرد' is a present/past-form verb 'brings/dispenses' used here as general/presentive. 'به هنگام خشم' is a time phrase 'at the time of anger.' The sense: 'He does not grant forgiveness when he is angry.'
segments.s651-g1456: The second half continues the negative pair: 'نه خشم آیدش' = 'nor does anger come to him' where 'آیدش' is the verb 'comes' plus the enclitic pronoun '-ش' = 'to him.' 'روز بخشش' = 'the day of forgiveness/mercy' (temporal phrase), and 'به چشم' literally 'to the eye' meaning 'into sight/appear.' Together: 'nor does anger come to his eye on a day of forgiveness' (i.e., anger does not appear on days of mercy).
segments.s651-g313: This is a compact comparative clause with omitted copula: پلنگی (a leopard / leopard‑skin) + به از + شهریاری چنین (better than such kingship). به از is the fixed comparative pattern 'better than'. Word order is topical: the thing judged superior (پلنگی) comes first; 'چنین' ('such') modifies 'شهریاری'.
segments.s651-g314: که introduces a relative clause describing 'شهریاری': 'which has neither ... nor ... nor ...'. نه ... نه ... is a threefold negation listing the absent qualities; دارد is the present (possessive) verb 'has'.
segments.s651-g315: گشادست is a poetic fused form (گشاد + است) meaning 'is open/wide'. بر ما = 'for/to us', and در راستی here uses در with the 'door/gate' sense: 'the gate of uprightness'. The clause means 'the gate of righteousness is open to us.'
segments.s651-g316: چه is a rhetorical 'why/what (exclamatory)'; کوبیم is 1st‑person‑plur. present subjunctive/hortative of کوبیدن ('we should/shall knock/strike'), here 'knock' (as on a door). خیره در کاستی = 'staring at / fixed on lack' (خیره modifies the following prepositional phrase 'در کاستی'). The clause asks why we continue to beat/knock while fixed on deficiency.
segments.s651-g317: A short imperative/instructional sequence: نهانی 'secretly' (adverb) + بدو 'to him' (indirect object) + داد دادن 'to give' (periphrastic infinitive/verb). بروی is an older 2nd‑person imperative/subjunctive 'go' (برو/بروید → بروی in poetic diction).
segments.s651-g318: بدان here is the fused 'بِدان' = 'with that / thereby'; تا introduces purpose/result 'so that/in order that'; رسد is a jussive/subjunctive 'may arrive / reach'; نزد ما = 'to us / in our presence'; گفت و گوی is the compound phrase 'talk / conversation' (گفت و گو). The whole: 'with that, so that the conversation may reach us.'
segments.s651-g319: به نزدیک یزدان is an idiomatic locution 'in the sight/nearness of God' (به + نزدیک = 'in the view of'); بود is the past/copular verb 'was' (classical style), and ناپسند is a predicate adjective 'displeasing / unacceptable'. Together: 'It would be displeasing before God.'
segments.s651-g320: نباشد is a negative jussive/subjunctive ('may it not be', literally 'let it not be'). بدین is the fused prepositional/adverbial 'in/at this' (ب + دین here function as one unit 'بدین' = 'in this'); بارگه ارجمند = 'the noble/august court'. The clause prays/commands that such a thing not occur in this honored court.
segments.s651-g793: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative formula meaning “thus said” (چنین = thus; گفت = said, past). کایدر appears immediately after گفت as the (expressed or implied) speaker/name or vocative; ز introduces the following scope ('from' / 'regarding') and is followed by the two nouns خرد و بزرگ — here 'خرد' and 'بزرگ' are paired as contrasting classes ('small/young' vs. 'great/old' or 'low' vs. 'high'). Word order is typical Persian: adverb + verb (said) + speaker/name, then preposition + noun phrase.
segments.s651-g794: نیاید که is a negative impersonal/jussive construction meaning 'it should not be / it does not happen that'; که introduces the subordinate clause. ماند is a present/optative 3rd-person form 'remain' (here subordinate to نیاید). یکی میش و گرگ is the coordinated predicate of the subordinate clause: یکی 'one' (indefinite/one of them), میش 'ewe/sheep', و 'and', گرگ 'wolf' — literally 'that one remain a sheep and (the) wolf', i.e. one be a sheep and the other a wolf.
segments.s651-g795: چنان شد is an idiom 'so it happened / thus it came to pass' (چنان = so/thus; شد = became/happened). ز کشته is a prepositional cause phrase 'from/because of the slain'. همه کوه و دشت is the affected domain: 'all the mountains and plains' (همه quantifies the following nouns); word order places the cause phrase before the affected places.
segments.s651-g796: که introduces a result clause: 'such that ...'. خون is the noun 'blood' (the subject of the result clause). در همه روی کشور is a locative phrase meaning 'on every surface/face of the country' (در = in/on; همه = all/every; روی = surface/face/upon; کشور = country/realm). بگشت is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'turned/spread/flowed' (the exact sense is poetic; the form is a past/resultative).
segments.s651-g797: ز بس here is a causal phrase 'from so much / because of so much' (بس = 'so much' / 'enough' used adverbially). It governs a coordination of verbal nouns: کشتن و غارت و سوختن — 'killing and plunder and burning' (verbal nouns in Persian frequently form noun phrases linked by و).
segments.s651-g798: خروش آمد is a common structure meaning 'a cry/uproar came' (noun + آمد 'came' = 'there arose ...'). و links to the next coordinated noun phrase ناله مرد و زن — 'the moaning/laments of men and women' (ناله = lament, مرد و زن = 'men and women'). The verb آمد governs the whole coordinated result.
