segments.s651-g540: This clause gives the action: به داد 'with justice' (داد here noun 'justice'), و 'and', به لشکر 'with an army'. بیاراست is the verb 'arrayed / adorned' (past tense, 3rd person) and شاه (the king) follows it in inverted poetic word order — so 'the king adorned/arrayed (it) with justice and with an army.'
segments.s651-g541: کسی ... نداند is a classical negative existential construction meaning 'no one knew' (کسی 'anyone/no one' and نداند 'knew' used negatively). آن سپه را is the definite object 'that host' (را marks it). شمار placed after the verb is the thing not known: 'the number/count.'
segments.s651-g542: به گیتی = 'in the world' (prepositional phrase). مگر here means 'except' and introduces the sole exception: نامور شهریار 'the famed king/sovereign' — the one who did know the count. Word order places the exception after مگر.
segments.s651-g543: همه 'everyone/all' is the subject. با دل شاد is a prepositional phrase 'with a joyful heart' (با + noun دل modified by adjective شاد). و connects to با ساز جنگ: با ساز (with instrument/tune), جنگ qualifies ساز as 'martial/war music' — 'with the music/instruments of war.'
segments.s651-g544: The repetition همه resumes the subject; گیتی‌افروز is a compound adjective 'world‑illuminating' (گیتی 'world' + افروز 'illuminating'), describing those who make the world shine. با نام و ننگ 'with name and nang' is a stock phrase meaning 'with fame and renown' (nang here in epic use carries an honor/glory sense). The verb is omitted (typical of poetry); the clause characterizes the same 'all.'
segments.s651-g257: به جایی که باشد زیان ملخ is a prepositional relative clause: به جایی 'to/at a place' + که introduces a relative clause; باشد is the classical subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used here as 'there is' or 'where there is'; زیان ملخ (noun phrase) is the thing being present in that place ('the damage of the locust'). Word order is typical Persian: locative phrase precedes the relative clause, and the predicate (باشد) comes before the noun that names the harm.
segments.s651-g258: وگر تف خورشید تابد به شخ is a conditional clause introduced by وگر 'if'. تف is a poetic metaphor (a small burst/blow or 'spit'); خورشید is the agent (sun); تابد is the present/subjunctive of تابیدن 'to shine' (here 'should shine' / 'fall'); به + شخ marks the target ('on/against the shakh'). The whole clause means 'or if the sun's little blaze/spit were to strike the shakh.'
segments.s651-g259: دگر تف باد سپهر بلند strings several modifiers before the head noun: دگر 'another' modifies تف 'a puff/blow'; تف + باد together specify 'a blow of the wind'; سپهر بلند is a postmodifier 'of the high sky/heaven' (classical word order often stacks modifiers). The phrase is a poetic nominal group 'another blow of the high heaven's wind.'
segments.s651-g260: بدان کشتمندان رساند گزند is the clause saying what that blow does. بدَان here functions as a prepositional/directional phrase 'to/onto those' (in older Persian بد(ه)+ـان/بدان = 'to that/ those'); کشتمندان is the addressee/affected group (poetic/compound agent noun — here contextually 'cultivators/tillers'); رساند is the past form 'brings/delivers' used as a simple past/presentive; گزند is the object 'harm'. Word order is verb-final in the clause's older poetic diction: [prepositional phrase] [patient] [verb] [object].
segments.s651-g261: همان‌گر نبارد به نوروز نم is another conditional clause: همان‌گر 'if likewise' (a classical conditional particle), نبارد is the negated present/subjunctive of باریدن 'to fall/rain' meaning 'does not fall' (i.e. 'no moisture falls'), and به نوروز نم places the time/occasion 'on Nowruz (the moisture)'. The negation is built into نبارد (ن- + بارَد).
segments.s651-g262: ز خشکی شود دشت خرم دژم: ز خشکی 'from/due to the dryness' (ز is the poetic form of از), شود is the present/subjunctive/impersonal of شدن 'become' (here 'becomes'), and دشت خرم دژم is the resulting predicate nominal where two adjectives (خرم 'lush/pleasant' and دژم 'sullen/bleak') jointly qualify the plain. Classical Persian often places the verb before the main noun phrase; here the clause emphasizes result ('the plain becomes ...').
segments.s651-g263: مخواهید باژ اندر آن بوم و رست is an admonition: مخواهید is an imperative/prohibitive 'do not (you) want / do not ...'; باژ 'cry/noise' is the action being forbidden; اندر آن بوم 'in that land' gives the location; و رست (رست = رستن) is a verb meaning 'sprout/grow' (here intransitive perfective/past sense 'and (so) it would grow' or an expected consequence). The clause thus connects prohibition with a hoped-for result (don't make noise there and expect growth).
segments.s651-g264: که ابر بهاران به باران نشست is a causal/explanatory clause introduced by که 'for/that'. ابر بهاران 'the clouds of spring' is the subject; به باران نشست uses the verb نشست (here 'settled' / 'condensed into') with به marking result 'into rain'. The clause explains why one should not make noise: the spring clouds have already settled into rain.
segments.s651-g129: ببخشید is a past/plural literary verb meaning “they bestowed/gave” (here not ‘forgive’). آگنده گنجی is a noun phrase with آگنده (filled, laden) modifying گنجی (treasure). بر + ین here function together as برین 'on this' (بر = on/upon; ین = the enclitic/old spelling of این meaning 'this'), so the whole segment says they bestowed a treasure full upon this (world).
segments.s651-g130: جهانی is adverbial/nominal meaning 'throughout the world' or 'in the world'. برو here has the literal sense 'went' / 'went about' and is used with خواندند to indicate movement or spreading of the action; خواندند is past plural 'they called/sang/exclaimed' and آفرین is the interjection 'Afarin!' (an acclamation). Together: 'throughout the world they cried “Afarin!”'.
segments.s651-g131: ز شاهان introduces the domain 'of the kings' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register; شاهان = 'kings'). هرآنکس که is a relative 'whoever who/that'. بد is an adverb/adjective here meaning 'badly/ill', and پیش ازوی (پیش = before/in the presence of; ازوی = از + وی = 'of him'/'before him') means 'in his presence' or 'before him'. The clause: 'Of the kings, whoever behaved badly before him...'.
segments.s651-g132: This is a conditional/contrast clause introduced by اگر 'if'. کم here means 'a little/insufficient' (i.e. 'a little harm'); بدش is a fused phrase where بد = 'harm/badness' and ش (short for 'ـش') is the enclitic 'his/to him' ('bad to him' or 'harm done to him'). گاه functions adverbially 'at times/sometimes'. The second اگر + بیش ازوی repeats the conditional with بیش = 'more' and ازوی = 'than him' (or 'from him' in comparative use). Grammatically the line offers alternatives: whether the harm was small sometimes or greater than that.
segments.s651-g133: بجستند is past/plural 'they sought' or 'they took' (classical past). بهره ز is a prepositional object 'a share/from' (بهره = share/benefit; ز = 'from'). کشت و درود are coordinated nouns ('kisht' = cultivation/harvest; درود here is best read as 'blessings' or beneficent greetings). Grammatically this says they took a share from cultivation and blessings.
segments.s651-g134: نرستست is an archaic/poetic perfective form (obscure in Modern Persian) functioning as the main past assertion meaning roughly 'no one had gone' or 'none reached/was' in this context. کس = 'anyone/no one' (context makes it negative: 'no one'). پیش ازین = 'before this' (پیش = before; از + ین = از این = 'this'). نابسود is an adjective 'unfruitful / unrewarded'. The clause negates experience prior to this: 'no one before this had been unrewarded.'
segments.s651-g135: This gives fractional shares. سه یک here functions as 'one of three' i.e. 'one-third'; یا = 'or'; چار یک = 'one of four' i.e. 'one-quarter'. بهر شاه = 'for the king' (بهر = for/to the benefit of; شاه = king). The copula بود ('was') links the fraction to its recipient.
segments.s651-g136: قباد آمد is simple past 'Qobad came'. و is the coordinating 'and'. ده یک is another fractional expression 'one of ten' (one-tenth). آورد is past 'he brought' (آورد = he brought/produced) and راه is the noun 'road/way' (here likely indicating 'on the road' or 'as a road-levy/tribute' depending on context). Grammatically it states that Qobad arrived and brought a tenth (for the road/way).
segments.s651-g465: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it should not be' (expressing prohibition or undesirability). که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. اندر فراز و نشیب is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ('in the highs and lows' / 'in ups and downs') giving the circumstance; note the poetic preposition اندر (in) before the paired nouns فراز (the high point) و نشیب (and the low point).
segments.s651-g466: ندانند is a third-person plural form meaning 'they do not know' (here used in the subordinate clause introduced by که). The three nouns چنگ و عنان و رکیب are the objects/fields of knowledge listed in a row (harp, reins, companion), with و as the simple coordinating conjunction; the verb appears before these objects because of poetic inversion.
segments.s651-g467: The repeated preposition به ('with/by') marks instruments and is used before the first two items for emphasis; the list گرز و شمشیر و تیر و کمان names weapons (mace, sword, arrow, bow). Word order is a simple coordinated list of instruments introduced by به.
segments.s651-g468: بدانند is again a third-person plural verb 'they know' (or 'they are skilled in'); پیچید is a past-form of پیچیدن ('to twist/turn') used here as an action verb meaning 'twist/brandish' (poetically used alongside or as a consequence of 'بدانند'). با بدگمان is an adverbial phrase 'with suspicion / ill-disposedly' modifying پیچید. The object (the weapons) is carried over from the preceding segment.
segments.s651-g469: جوان بی‌هنر is a noun phrase 'young man without skill' (بی‌هنر = unskilled). سخت is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'very' or 'severely' and modifies ناخوش ('displeased/unhappy'). بود is the past-tense copula 'was', so the whole segment is a past-state description.
segments.s651-g470: اگر چند here functions concessively ('although/even if') rather than as a plain conditional; several classical Persian passages use 'اگر چند' to introduce a concession. فرزند آرش بود is a past copular clause 'he was (a) child/son of Arash', so the whole segment means 'though he was a son of Arash.'
segments.s651-g471: عرض شد is a passive/light-verb expression 'a petition/presentation was made' (عرض = petition/presentation, شد = 'was' as auxiliary). ز در is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the court/door' (ز = from, در = gate/court); سوی هر کشوری means 'toward each province/realm' (سوی = toward, هر کشوری = every country/realm).
segments.s651-g472: درم برد here conveys that coin/tribute (درم = dirham/money) was taken or gained (برد used in the sense of gain/collection). نزدیک هر مهتری frames the source/target of the collection as 'from/near each chief/notable' (نزدیک = near/at, هر مهتری = every chief/notable), so the clause says money was collected from each notable.
segments.s651-g665: به آزادگان (prepositional phrase) marks the indirect object/recipient: 'to the free (men)'. گفت here is the past-tense verb 'he said' placed before the quoted/clausal predicate. ننگست این is a predicative phrase ('this is shameful') where این anticipates the subordinate clause introduced by که.
segments.s651-g666: که introduces the subordinate content clause ('that ...'). ویران بود is a past-state construction: ویران (ruined) as predicate adjective with copula بود (was). بوم ایران‌زمین is a noun phrase 'the land/soil of Iran' (بوم modified by the following Iran-phrase).
segments.s651-g667: نشاید is an impersonal modal 'it is not proper/fit'. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. باشیم is a subjunctive/present-form 'that we be' (first-person plural subjunctive), and همداستان is predicate/adjective 'of one mind' or 'in agreement.'
segments.s651-g668: که starts another explanatory subordinate clause ('that ...'). دشمن is the subject 'the enemy'. زند is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning roughly 'will make/compose' or 'will take (as)', here indicating the enemy's action (sense not present in the supplied list). زین functions as 'from this' (poetic equivalent of از این). نشان داستان is the object phrase 'a tale/story from this sign/token' (نشان 'sign', داستان 'story').
segments.s651-g669: ز لشکر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the army'. فرستاده‌ای is a participial/noun form 'one sent' i.e. 'a messenger' (a person sent out). برگزید is the past tense 'he chose/selected'. The sequence places the source first, then the noun, then the selecting verb.
segments.s651-g670: سخن‌گو و دانا are appositive epithets modifying the messenger: 'a spokesman and a wise (man)'. چنان چون introduces a comparative/qualifying phrase 'such as' or 'just as', and سزید (past of سزا in this context) is a verbal/adjectival use meaning 'was fitting/deserved', so the whole means 'such as was fitting'.
segments.s651-g671: بدو is the clitic/prepositional phrase 'to him' (to/unto him). گفت is the past-tense 'said' (verb). شبگیر is the agent 'the night-sentinel/night-watchman' (poetic noun). ز ایدر is a compound meaning 'from here' (ز = from, ایدر = here). بپوی is an imperative 'go/seek' (2nd-person imperative), instructing movement/search.
segments.s651-g672: بدین is a dative demonstrative 'to these' (preposition+demonstrative form). مرزبانان لشکر is a nominal group 'the frontier-guards of the army' (مرزبانان = border guards, لشکر = army, here in close apposition/genitive relation). بگوی is the imperative 'tell' (say to), giving the order to inform those guards.
segments.s651-g785: همه is a determiner 'all' placed before the noun. رختها is the plural noun 'garments/possessions' and خوار is an adjectival predicate meaning 'worthless/despised' that modifies رختها; together they form the object phrase. بگذاشتند is the past-tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they left/abandoned' (here 'left aside, regarded as worthless'), so the whole segment means 'they abandoned all their garments as worthless.'
segments.s651-g786: This is a coordinated object phrase (در و کوه را) followed by the verb پنداشتند 'they considered/thought'. را is the definite/object marker attached to the second coordinated item but scopes over the whole phrase. خانه is the predicative complement 'home/house', so خانه پنداشتند = 'they considered ... a home.' Word order is object(s) + predicate verb in the past tense.
segments.s651-g787: وزان is a compound prepositional/temporal marker 'and/from that' introducing movement from the previously mentioned place. جایگه 'place' names the origin; سوی گیلان is a prepositional phrase 'toward Gilan'. کشید is a past-tense verb 'they moved/advanced (marched)'; here it takes the preceding prepositional phrase as its complement. Note the subject is omitted (understood from context) and the verb concludes the clause.
segments.s651-g788: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. رنج آمد is 'hardship came' (چو + noun + آمد = 'when trouble came'). از + noun phrase ('از گیل و دیلم') marks the source or cause ('from the Gil and the Daylamites'). پدید is the intransitive verb 'appeared/manifested' placed at the end; together the clause means 'when hardship appeared from the Gil and the Daylamites.'
segments.s651-g789: ز دریا is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the sea' modifying سپه 'army' (subject). بود is the past copula 'was' linking the subject to the following extent phrase. تا marks extent 'as far as / up to' (here تا تیغ کوه = 'up to the mountain's ridge/crest'). The clause thus states the army's extent: 'an army was from the sea up to the mountain's crest.'
segments.s651-g790: This is a pair of verbless nominal phrases that describe the scene: هوا پر درفش = 'the air full of banners' and زمین پر گروه = 'the ground full of troops.' پر here means 'full (of)' and follows the noun it modifies; درفش 'banner' and گروه 'troop/company' are the nouns filling those roles. The two phrases are coordinated with و.
segments.s651-g791: پراگنده is an adjective/participle 'scattered/widespread' placed before the location phrase. بر گرد گیلان is a prepositional phrase 'around Gilan' (برگرد = around/about). سپاه is the noun 'army' placed at the end of the noun phrase; the whole chunk reads 'a dispersed army around Gilan.'
segments.s651-g792: بشد is the past form of 'شد' meaning 'it happened/it became' (here 'there came' or 'arose'). روشنایی is the subject 'brightness/light' and ز خورشید و ماه is a source phrase 'from the sun and the moon'. The verb comes before the subject in this poetic inversion but functions as a normal past intransitive clause: 'brightness came from the sun and the moon.'
segments.s651-g1089: به یزدان is an oath-like prepositional phrase: به + یزدان = “by Yazdān/God.” که is a relative pronoun linking this clause to what follows (“who/that”), او is the resumed personal pronoun “he,” and داد is a past tense verb “gave.” دیهیم is an archaic/poetic noun (here the speaker’s domain/estate or rural holding); زور is a noun “strength/power.” The whole segment functions as an introducer: “By God, who gave (us) dīhīm and strength….”
segments.s651-g1090: This is a noun phrase enumerating divine/astral figures. خداوند is used as “lord/sovereign” (an epithet), followed by three proper names/epithets (کیوان, بهرام, هور) invoked as powerful patrons. The coordinating و simply links the three names.
segments.s651-g1091: که is the relative/concessive link “who/that”; در پی (a fused prepositional expression) = “in pursuit of” or “while pursuing.” میانش = میان + ـش (possessive suffix) = “his middle/midsection.” ببرم is first-person present-subjunctive/optative of بردن/بُرْدن in poetic usage here meaning “I would cut / I should cut” (the verb form places the action as potential/willed). به is instrumental “with/by,” and تیغ means “sword.” The clause thus reads “that, if I pursue, I would cut his midsection with the sword.”
segments.s651-g1092: وگر = “and/or if / but if.” داستان here means “the affair/turn of events” (poetically: the matter or the story). را is the definite object/emphasis marker attached to داستان. برآید is a present/subjunctive form meaning “arise / be brought about / be decided.” به introduces the instrument again (“by/with”), and میغ is an archaic synonym for blade/edge. Together: “or if the affair is to be decided by the blade.”
segments.s651-g1093: به پیش سپه = “at the front of the army” (به + پیش + سپه = locative, an adverbial phrase). در طلایه = “in the vanguard/forefront” (در marking location). منم is the emphatic copula “I am,” placed at the end for poetic emphasis (subject + predicate inversion common in classical Persian).
segments.s651-g1094: جهانجوی is a compound adjective/noun “world-seeking” or “ambitious/conqueror of the world.” و simply coordinates the next phrase. در قلب = “in the heart/core.” مایه منم is a predicative construction: مایه “source/substance” + منم “I am,” i.e. “and in my heart I am the source/whole substance.” Word order (predicate before enclitic pronoun) is typical poetic inversion.
segments.s651-g1095: نگهبان is a noun meaning “guard/guardian.” This head noun takes a coordinated series of complements: پیل (elephant), سپاه (army), و بنه (and the camp/encampment—here بنه is an older/poetic word referring to the camp or group). The sequence lists things the speaker guards: “guardian of the elephant, the army, and the camp.”
segments.s651-g1096: گهی = “sometimes.” بر + میان + گاه = a locative phrase “upon/in the middle place/position” (بر as locative/on, میان = middle, گاه here ‘place/spot’ in poetic sense). برمیمنه is a fixed poetic/archaic adverb meaning “on the right / on the favoured/auspicious side.” The clause reads: “sometimes I am upon the middle place on the right (i.e. I stand at the right hand in the center of the formation).”
segments.s651-g601: This is an initial nominal group identifying the actor. ازیرا is an archaic/poetic temporal or discourse particle here (an old form of 'then' or similar) placed before the name; فریدون is the proper name (subject); یزدان‌پرست is a postpositive epithet meaning ‘God‑worshipper’ and modifies the name. The whole group functions as the subject of the following verb phrase.
segments.s651-g602: This is the verb phrase with its locative: بدین بیشه = 'in/into this grove' (بدین = prepositional + demonstrative), برساخت is a past, perfective verb 'he built/erected', and جای نشست is a small noun phrase 'a place/seat' followed by نشست used as a past action 'sat/settled (himself)'. Word order is typical Persian narrative: locative/adjunct + verb + object + result/action.
segments.s651-g603: بدو is a dative clitic/phrase 'to him'; گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the supplied lemma list gave a nominal sense, but context is verbal past); گوینده is the agent 'the speaker' and appears after the verb (inverted narrative order). کای is a vocative 'O' addressing the addressee, and دادگر is an epithet 'just / just ruler' attached to that vocative.
segments.s651-g604: This is a conditional protasis: گر = 'if'; ایدر = 'here' (locative); ز = 'from' (poetic preposition introducing the agent/source); ترکان = 'the Turks' (agent of movement); نبودی is the past negative copula/auxiliary 'there was not / would not be' used in a conditional; گذر = 'passage/way'. Together: 'if here there were no passage from the Turks...' — word order reflects older/Persian epic style.
segments.s651-g605: ازین = 'from this' (prepositional demonstrative), مایه‌ور is an adjective 'wealthy/rich (in resources)' modifying جا 'place'. بدین here functions as a directional 'to this' (preposition + demonstrative), and فرهی is a postposed adjective 'noble / exalted' qualifying the destination. The sequence gives a source (ازین ... جا) and a destination (بدین فرهی).
segments.s651-g606: دل ما = 'our heart(s)' (possessive construction). ز is the ablative/causal 'of / from', رامش = 'tranquility/solace', نبودی is the past negative copula 'was not' and in combination with تهی ('empty') yields 'was not empty (of)'. The clause states that their hearts were not lacking in tranquility.
segments.s651-g607: نیاریم is a first‑person plural negative/modal verb form meaning roughly 'we will not / we ought not / we do not wish to' (an archaic/unitary negative form) and governs the following verbal noun. گردن برافراختن is an infinitival/verbal noun phrase 'to raise the neck' = an idiom for 'to be proud' or 'to raise (one's) head/pride'. Together they express refusal to act proudly.
segments.s651-g608: ز بس = 'because of so much' (ز = 'from/because of', بس = 'much/excess'), and the coordinated nouns کشتن و غارت و تاختن list the actions 'killing and plunder and raiding/charging'. This whole phrase is an adverbial of reason explaining why they will not raise their necks.
segments.s651-g217: نفرمود is the negative past (3rd singular) of فرمودن 'to say/tell' (here meaning 'he did not say / he told us not otherwise'); ما را is the indirect object 'to us' where را is the object marker attached to ما; جز ... introduces the exception ('except'); از راستی literally 'from truth' = 'but the truth' (جز از = 'nothing except'). Word order is verb — object — exception phrase, typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s651-g218: که is the subordinate conjunction 'that' introducing reported content; دیو is the subject 'the demon'; آورد is past 'brought/caused' (simple past third singular); کژی و کاستی are the direct objects 'crookedness and deficiency/shortcoming', linked by و. The clause reports what was told to 'us' in the previous segment.
segments.s651-g219: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); بهر من is a beneficiary/goal phrase 'for me'; زین سرای is a genitive noun phrase 'of this house' (زین = this, سرای = house) placed before the head noun سپنج, which here is the noun governed by the verb in the next segment. The whole phrase is the conditional topic: 'if, for me, (in) this house ...'.
segments.s651-g220: نبودی is a negative past/conditional form meaning 'there would not be / there were not' (used under the conditional اگر); جز از introduces the exception 'except for'; باغ و ایوان و گنج are the listed items ('garden, iwan/porch, and treasure'). Together they mean 'if there were for me in this house nothing except garden, iwan and treasure.'
segments.s651-g221: نجستی is the past of جستن used here as 'sought' or 'desired' (past, 3rd/1st-person sense determined by context); the phrase دل من is the agent/experiencer 'my heart' (inverted word order for emphasis: verb before subject); به جز introduces the exclusion 'except'; داد و مهر list the two things sought: 'justice/gift' and 'love/affection' (داد here functions as an abstract noun 'justice' or 'generous giving').
segments.s651-g222: گشادن is the infinitive 'to open'/'to reveal' and here expresses purpose or complement to the previous verb (what the heart sought to do/produce); بهر کار is a purpose phrase 'for the sake of work/action'; بیدار چهر is a descriptive noun phrase 'awake-faced' or 'with an alert visage' describing the manner or attendant appearance. The infinitive construction links intention/purpose to the desires named earlier.
segments.s651-g223: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; روی بوم زمین is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'on/upon the surface (canvas) of the earth' (روی = surface/upon, بوم = surface/canvas, زمین = earth); سر به سر is an idiomatic expression 'from end to end / all over' reinforcing the thoroughness of the action. Word order places time first, then the location phrase and the adverbial intensifier.
segments.s651-g224: ز خاور برو is an imperative: ز 'from' + خاور 'the east' + برو 'go' (imperative), instructing movement starting in the east; تا marks the endpoint 'as far as / to' and در باختر 'in the west' gives that endpoint (در = in/within, باختر = west). The clause thus gives the route: go from east to west.
segments.s651-g433: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. دست و عنان is an idiomatic pairing meaning 'hand and reins' — i.e. power or control — placed before the vocative تو‌ای شهریار ('O king'), where تو‌ای is the poetic 'you' plus the vocative marker and شهریار names the addressee.
segments.s651-g434: به ایوان is a locative/directional phrase 'to/at the iwan'; ندیدست is an archaic/perfective form meaning 'was not seen' (passive sense) and applies to the following noun پیکرنگار, which is the entity that 'was not seen.' Word order is poetic (preposed locative + verb before subject).
segments.s651-g435: به کام تو is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to your desire' (i.e. 'according to your wish'). گردد (from گشت/گرد) here has an optative/hortative sense 'may ... become' or 'may ... turn out,' and سپهر بلند ('the lofty sky/heavens') is the subject that follows the verb — a common poetic inversion.
segments.s651-g436: دلت is the possessed noun 'your heart' (دل + 2nd-person suffix ت). شاد بادا is a conventional optative formula 'may [it] be glad' (شاد = happy; بادا = 'may it be'). تنت بی‌گزند literally 'your body unharmed' is a coordinated wish where بادا is understood to govern both clauses (i.e. 'may your heart be glad and your body be safe').
segments.s651-g437: به موبد literal 'to the mobed' is the indirect object marked by the preposition به. چنین گفت is 'thus said' or 'said so'; the speaker's name نوشین‌روان comes after the verb — a stylistic inversion common in classical Persian (verb before subject). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said.'
segments.s651-g438: که introduces the reported content ('that'). با داد ما is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by our justice' (با = 'by/with', داد = 'justice'). پیر گردد جوان is a simple predication 'the old becomes young' where گردد is 'becomes' (here reported-future/optative sense) and جوان is the result predicate. Word order is subject (پیر) + verb (گردد) + predicate (جوان).
segments.s651-g439: به گیتی ('in the world') sets the domain. نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not/ought not' and is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that'). از شهریار ('of/from the king') marks the theme about which the clause speaks. The whole phrase expresses a general judgment or rule.
segments.s651-g440: بماند is the present/jussive 'remain' (here used impersonally: 'nothing should remain'). جز = 'except' introduces the sole survivor, and از راستی یادگار is a genitive phrase 'a memorial of righteousness' (از راستی = 'of/from uprightness/justice' modifying یادگار). The clause thus restricts what may remain to a single, moral remembrance.
segments.s651-g689: This is a relative clause introduced by که (that/who). سپاهی (soldiery) is the head noun; شان is a possessive here ('their') modifying the infinitive تاختن (to charge). پیِشه بود (was a profession) places the copula بود (was) at the end in typical Persian SOV order, so the clause says literally ‘an army whose charging was their profession.’
segments.s651-g690: وز is an archaic/poetic connective (here meaning something like ‘and/of’); آزادمردی is the abstract quality (nobility/generosity) and کم‌اندیشه is a compound adjective ‘short of thought’ (thoughtless, lacking reflection). The copula بود (was) follows, so the clause means ‘and (they) were lacking in noble-mindedness.’
segments.s651-g691: از ایشان introduces the agent/cause (‘because of them’). بدی شهر ایران is a noun phrase ‘the harm/damage to the city of Iran.’ ببیم is an exclamatory interjection (‘behold!’) inserted for emphasis; the full predication is completed in the next segment.
segments.s651-g692: نماندی is the past existential verb ‘was left / remained’ used here to indicate that nothing remained (context yields a negative sense: ‘no one remained’). بکس literally ‘a person’ is used in such negative existential constructions to mean ‘no one.’ The following nouns جامه, زر (robe, gold) are listed with و linking them; word order (verb before noun phrase) is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s651-g693: A simple coordinated noun phrase: زن و مرد (women and men). با introduces accompanying parties (با کودک = with children), and چارپای (literally ‘four-footed’) refers to pack/beast animals. The list sets up who moved together.
segments.s651-g694: به هامون رسیدی means ‘(they) arrived at/into the plain.’ Note the epic/archaic 3rd‑person plural ending -ی in رسیدی rather than modern -ند. نماندی again serves existentially (‘there remained’) and, with بجای (‘in place’), yields ‘there remained no place’ (i.e. no free spot/place was left).
segments.s651-g695: This is a genitive/attributive sequence: فرستاده پیغام شاه جهان can be read as ‘the messenger (bearing) the message of the king of the world’ or more simply ‘the king-of-the-world’s messenger.’ Persian often stacks nouns to show possession or role without a separate particle.
segments.s651-g696: بدیشان is the prepositional phrase ‘to them.’ بگفت is the narrative past form ‘he said / told’ (the prefixed ب- is a classical/epic feature). آشکار و نهان are adverbial complements ‘openly and secretly,’ placed after the verb to indicate manner.
segments.s651-g1049: درفشِ مرا is the noun + possessive: درفش (standard/banner) with the enclitic ـم (مرا/ـم) ‘my’ (the object marker را appears as a separate word here). تا introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'until'; نبیند is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'see' used with تا to mean 'until (it) be seen' and کسی with the negated verb yields 'no one' (i.e. 'until no one sees it'). Word order: [object + possessor] + temporal clause.
segments.s651-g1050: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / one must not'; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. ایمن functions adverbially/predicatively 'safely/securely', and بخسبد is the present/subjunctive form of 'sleep' (archaic form used in narrative subordinate clause after نباید). بسی is an intensifier 'much/at all' modifying the verb phrase. Overall: 'one must not sleep securely at all.'
segments.s651-g1051: از آتشکده is a prepositional phrase 'from the fire-temple'. چون here is a temporal conjunction 'when/as', and بشد is an archaic/narrative past (شد with b‑prefix) meaning 'he set out / it happened that he went'. سوی روم is the directional phrase 'toward Rum (Byzantium)'. Word order is typical poetic: prepositional phrase + temporal conjunction + verb + directional phrase.
segments.s651-g1052: پراگنده شد is a verbal phrase 'became widespread / spread' (پراگنده as adjective/adverb + شد 'became/happened'). زو is the poetic combination of ز + او 'from/of him' (here 'news of him'); خبر is the noun 'news'; گرد بوم is a locative phrase 'around/about the land' (گرد 'around' + بوم 'land/home'). Together: 'news of him became widespread around the land.'
segments.s651-g1053: به پیش آمد is an idiomatic narrative phrase 'came forward/approached (in the presence of someone)'. آنکس که فرمان گزید is a relative clause 'the one who chose/took the command' (که introduces the relative clause; گزید is past 'chose/took'). The clause as a whole identifies the person who came forward.
segments.s651-g1054: دگر زان here functions adverbially as 'thereafter/from that time on' (دگر used in the temporal/transitional sense + زان 'from that'). بر و بوم is a fixed pair 'land and dwelling/realm' (the domain in question). شد ناپدید is the verb phrase 'became invisible/vanished' (شد = became; ناپدید = vanished). Literal flow: 'thereafter, from that land/realm became vanished.'
segments.s651-g1055: جهاندیده is an epithet 'worldly-wise / well-travelled' modifying the subject. با هدیه و با نثار are two prepositional phrases 'with a gift and with offerings' (با repeated for emphasis/parallelism). نثار here functions as a noun 'offering/tribute'. The sequence describes how he came (bearing gifts/offerings).
segments.s651-g1056: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'abundantly/plentifully' modifying the verb; بیامد is the past narrative 'he came'. بر شهریار is the prepositional phrase 'to the king' (بر used as 'to/toward' in classical usage). Word order (adverb + verb + prepositional complement) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s651-g1057: 'به هر بوم و بر کو فرود آمدی' is a locative clause in which 'به' (+noun phrase) marks direction/toward, 'هر بوم و بر' is a fixed paired phrase meaning 'every land and domain,' and 'کو' (= 'where') introduces the place clause. The verb 'فرود آمدی' is simple past, 2nd person singular ('you alighted/descended'), so the whole segment means 'wherever you alighted upon any land.' Word order is poetic (the interrogative/relative 'کو' appears before the verb).
segments.s651-g1058: 'ز هر سو' = 'from every side/direction' (the preposition 'ز' = 'from' is the poetic form of 'از'). 'پیام و درود آمدی' literally 'messages and greetings came' but the verb 'آمدی' is here addressed to the addressee (past 2nd person) in a poetic idiom meaning 'messages and salutations came to you from every side.' Note the coordination 'پیام و درود' (message and greeting) as the things that arrived.
segments.s651-g1059: 'ز گیتی' = 'from/throughout the world' (poetic 'ز' for 'از'). The phrase 'به هر سو که لشکر کشید' is a relative clause introduced by 'که' ('that/where'), meaning 'to every side that an army drew/marched.' The compound 'لشکر کشیدن' is an idiom meaning 'to move/march an army' (here past 'کشید').
segments.s651-g1060: 'جز از بزم و شادی' = 'except for feasting and joy' (جز = except). The verb phrase 'نیامد پدید' ('did not come/appear') is negation + verb 'آمد' with the adverb/verb 'پدید' ('into view/manifest'), so the construction implies 'nothing else appeared' — read with the preceding 'جز' it yields 'nothing appeared except feasts and rejoicing.' The word order (negative verb followed by 'پدید') is a poetic sequence.
segments.s651-g1061: 'چنان ... که' is a correlative/result expression: 'so ... that.' Here 'چنان' = 'so/such,' and 'که' introduces the result clause. 'هر شب' = 'every night.' 'ز گردان هزار' = 'a thousand from the ranks/troops' (ز = 'from', گردان = 'troop/unit'); the numeral 'هزار' quantifies 'گردان.'
segments.s651-g1062: 'به بزم آمدندی بر شهریار' = 'they came to the feast for the king.' 'به بزم' marks movement 'to the banquet,' 'آمدندی' is the archaic/persianate past plural ('they came'), and 'بر شهریار' specifies the feast belonged to or was held before the king ('بر' here = 'before/unto' or 'to' in the sense 'for the king'). The verb agrees with the plural collective implied by 'گردان هزار.'
segments.s651-g1063: 'چو نزدیک شد' = 'when it became near / when [the battle] drew near' ('چو' = 'when', 'نزدیک شد' = 'became near/approached'). 'رزم را ساز کرد' uses the object marker 'را' with 'ساز کرد' meaning 'he made preparations (for the battle)' — 'ساز' here is figurative 'means / preparations' and 'کرد' is the past-tense 'did/made.'
segments.s651-g1064: 'سپه را درم دادن آغاز کرد' = 'he began to give dirhams (pay) to the army.' 'سپه را' is the definite object ('the army'), 'درم دادن' is the infinitive 'to give dirhams' (i.e., to distribute pay), and 'آغاز کرد' ('began') + infinitive expresses the start of that action ('he began to give the army money').
segments.s651-g177: دگر here means "others/another group" (an adverbial/nominal sense) and introduces a new set of agents. تا + فرستد: فرستد is the classical Persian 3rd-person form (present-subjunctive used in narrative) functioning as a past/action verb meaning "sent." به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase (به + noun) meaning "to every country/realm," with the verb placed at the end of the clause as in Persian word order.
segments.s651-g178: This repeats the dative/recipient structure: به هر ... means "to every ...". هر is the distributive "every/everyone," نامداری denotes a person of renown ("man of renown") and مهتری here means a chief/elder; the preposition به governs both nouns.
segments.s651-g179: سه دیگر = "three others." که introduces a relative clause "who/that." نزدیک موبد برند literally places the adverbial/locative phrase before the verb: نزدیک موبد = "near the mōbed/priest," and برند (بردن, 3pl) is again the classical narrative form (present-subjunctive used for past) meaning "they took/brought" or "they placed/stood."
segments.s651-g180: گزیت is a proper name or item brought (direct object). و links the next phrase. سر باژها بشمرند: سر functions as "heads" (i.e., countable units) and باژها is plural; بشمرند (3pl subjunctive used narratively) means "they counted/numbered." The verb again comes at the end, governing the enumeration.
segments.s651-g181: به فرمان او is a prepositional phrase meaning "by his command/at his order." بود is the past copula "there was/it was," and کاری که بود = "a task/work which was/consisted of..."; که introduces the relative clause that links this clause to the following list of items.
segments.s651-g182: The repeated poetic preposition ز (= از) introduces items "from/of": ز باژ و خراج و ز کشت و درود enumerates the things encompassed by that work. ز governs each noun in the list; خراج = land-tax/tribute, کشت = cultivation, درود = greetings/blessings (here enumerated as types of dues or matters under his charge).
segments.s651-g183: پراگنده is an adjective meaning "scattered/widespread" placed before the noun it modifies. کاراگهان is a proper name (Kara-gakhān / Kara-Khan) or title; در جهان is a locative phrase "in the world" specifying the scope. Adjective + name + locative follow normal Persian order.
segments.s651-g184: که تا introduces a result/consequence "so that/so much so that." نیک و بد = "good and bad." زو = ز + او (from him/of him). نماند is the negative past/optative form meaning "did not remain/was not left," and نهان is the predicate adjective "hidden." The verb precedes the adjective predicate (نماند نهان = "remained not hidden").
segments.s651-g1433: This is a pre-verbal noun phrase functioning as the object(s) of the following verb. وزان (a fused preposition + demonstrative) = 'from that' (locative), جایگه = 'place', ناله = 'a lament/moan' and گاودم is the noun attached to ناله (the thing making the moan). The whole phrase is fronted (before the verb), so the actual hearing-verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s651-g1434: شنیدند is simple past (3rd pl.) 'they heard' and takes the preceding noun phrase(s) as its object(s). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' connecting two heard sounds. آواز is a second noun 'voice/sound', modified by the adjective رویینه ('armoured, made of iron') which precedes the noun خم here; خم is the head noun 'bend/curve' (used poetically as a concrete, ‘curved/metal object’), so آواز رویینه خم = 'the sound/cry of the iron-clad curve/arch'.
segments.s651-g1435: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' (literally 'world-holder'). بیدار is an adjectival/epithetic word 'awake/alert' placed after the subject. لشکر براند is a verbal clause where براند (from راندن) functions as a past-tense narrative verb 'drove/led' and لشکر is its object — 'he set the army in motion / led the army'.
segments.s651-g1436: به + شام = 'into/at evening' (the preposition به with شام 'evening'), آمد is past 'came' (so: 'evening came' or 'he arrived at evening'). و connects the clauses. روزگاری is an adverbial noun 'for a while / for some time' and بماند (here third-person past narrative) means 'he remained / stayed'. Together: 'evening came and he stayed for some time.'
segments.s651-g1437: بیاورد is a past narrative verb 'he brought' (3rd sing.). چندان is a quantifier 'so much/so many' modifying the following nouns. سلیح و سپاه are coordinated direct objects 'weapons and army/troops' (note the noun + conjunction + noun structure).
segments.s651-g1438: همان here resumes/enumerates the spoils 'also/those same', and a string of nouns follows as additional objects of بیاورد. برده in this context is best read as 'the taken' = 'captives', بدره is a poetic word for 'the full-moon' used metaphorically for 'beautiful ones' (i.e., beauties), تاج = 'crown', and گاه is a general noun 'occasion/time' but here functions as part of the catalogue of items — 'and (other) possessions/occasions'; the sequence is an asyndetic list of spoils.
segments.s651-g1439: که is the relative 'which/that' linking this clause to the previous enumeration. پشت زمی را = 'the back of the earth' with را marking it as definite/direct object. همی‌داد is an iterative/presentational past-form 'kept giving/caused' (here functioning as 'caused to be' or 'made to'), and خم is the result-object 'a bend' = 'a bowing' — together 'which caused the earth's back to bend' (i.e., bowed the earth).
segments.s651-g1440: ز (poetic variant of از) is the ablative 'by/from' marking cause. پیلان (pl. of پیل) = 'elephants'. وز here is a noun 'weight/burden' (not a preposition) and links to the following genitive 'گنجهای درم' = 'treasures of dirhams' (wealth in dirhams). The two causal phrases together explain the reason for the earth's bending: 'by the elephants' weight and by treasures of dirhams.'
segments.s651-g865: This short clause names the addressee and sets up a conditional: ز (a poetic preposition here used vocatively/prefixing a name) + منذر (the name Munḏir), followed by تو‌گر (an older fused form meaning “if you”). دادیاب(ی) functions as a predicate/noun ‘dispenser/finder of justice’ describing the addressee, and بسست (an older/poetic form with copular sense) means ‘it is enough’ or ‘that suffices’. Word order is emphatic/poetic: the vocative/name comes first, then the conditional particle + person, then the predicate and the comment “enough.”
segments.s651-g866: که introduces a reason/explanatory clause (“for”); او is the 3rd-person pronoun ‘he’; را here plays a topical/emphatic role rather than marking a direct object; نشست is the verb ‘sit/seat’ (here ‘is seated’); از بر is a prepositional combination giving the sense ‘on/upon’ or ‘over’; هر کسست is a poetic fusion meaning ‘every person’ (کس + -ست where -ست = is). Overall: “for he is seated above every man.”
segments.s651-g867: Imperative construction: چپ خویش (here خویش is split into خوی + ش) means ‘your left’ (literally ‘left of yours’), with خوی used in its kinship/possessive base and ش the clitic possessive ‘his/your’. پیدا کن is the imperative ‘find!’ (پیدا = found/manifest, کن = do), and از دست راست = ‘from the right hand’ (از = from; دست = hand; راست = right). The clause orders the object phrase before the imperative for emphasis: ‘find your left out of/right of the right hand.’
segments.s651-g868: چو here = ‘when’ (temporal/conditional), followed by the verb complex پیدا کنی ‘you find’ (پیدا = found, کنی = 2nd-person subjunctive/optative form of کردن used in dependent clauses). مرز جویی is a noun phrase ‘seeking the border/frontier’ (مرز = border, جویی = search/pursuit), and رواست is a fused predicate (روا = permissible/fitting + ست = is). The clause thus reads: ‘When you find it, it is proper to seek the border.’
segments.s651-g869: چو begins a concessive/temporal clause here (‘when’ or ‘as’), بخشنده is an adjective/noun ‘generous, giver’, and بوم و کشور = ‘homeland and country/realm’. منم is the emphatic copula ‘I am’ (من + -م). The phrase means roughly ‘when/as I am the generous one of the land and realm.’ Word order places the descriptive role before the self-identification ‘I am.’
segments.s651-g870: به گیتی here gives the domain ‘in/throughout the world’ (به used in classical Persian to indicate realm/setting, followed by گیتی ‘world’). سرافراز ‘lofty/honored’ and مهتر ‘chief/distinguished man’ are predicate adjectives/nouns joined by و ‘and’, and the final منم repeats the copula ‘I am.’ Together: ‘I am honored and chief in the world.’
segments.s651-g871: همه آن کنم = ‘I do all that’ (همه = all, آن = that, کنم = I do). کار = ‘deed/affair’ and کز introduces a relative clause ‘which/that’; من = ‘me/I’ is the logical subject of the relative, and سزد is the impersonal/archaic present meaning ‘it befits / it is proper’. So: ‘I perform all those deeds which befit me.’ Word order follows a standard classical pattern where the relative clause follows the head noun.
segments.s651-g872: نمانم is the negative 1st-person form ‘I will not remain / I will not stay’ (ن- prefix for negation + مانم). که here introduces an explanatory or consequential clause (‘for/that’). بادی = ‘a wind/blast’ (adjectival/noun form), بدو = ‘to him/unto it’ (bi + 3rd-person clitic), and بروزد is a poetic/older verb form meaning ‘comes upon / happens to / befalls’ (here passive-like sense). The clause explains the reason the speaker will not remain: ‘for a wind may befall him.’
segments.s651-g873: تو is the explicit second‑person subject (emphatic). با تازیان is a prepositional phrase 'with the Arabs' that states the party involved. دست یازی is a compound noun/idiom meaning 'laying hands on; reaching out (in attack or alliance)' and بکین is the archaic/poetic 2nd‑person imperative of کردن (بکن), so the whole clause is an imperative: 'you (do) reach out to the Arabs.'
segments.s651-g874: یکی here functions adverbially ('once' / 'for a moment' / 'one time') introducing an instruction; در نهان 'in secret' is a locative phrase; خویشتن را uses the reflexive noun with the object marker را (definite/direct object) and ببین is the 2nd‑person imperative 'look/see'. Word order is adverbial + locative + object + verb: 'once, in private, see yourself.'
segments.s651-g875: و دیگر که 'and another (thing) that' is a connective introducing a new remark. آن پادشاهی is 'that kingship' (demonstrative + noun). مراست here functions as the predicate 'belongs to me / is mine' in an archaic or poetic form (not the modern separate مرا + است); this sense is not among the provided lemma senses, so it is marked outside the given options.
segments.s651-g876: در ... تا ... establishes a range: در گاو تا پشت ماهی = 'from the ox to the fish's back' (در = from/in, تا = as far as/to). پشت ماهی is a genitive or noun‑noun combination 'the back of the fish.' The final مراست repeats the predicate 'is mine' applying to that whole range (again the archaic poetic form, mapped to -1 below).
segments.s651-g877: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if'. من is the explicit subject. سپاهی فرستم is a verb phrase in the present/subjunctive 'I send an army' (فرستم is the subjunctive/optative form often used in conditional protases). بروم is a second verb coordinated after فرستم and likewise in the subjunctive sense 'and I go' — the pair gives 'if I dispatch troops and go'.
segments.s651-g878: تو را places possession/emphasis before the noun (classical Persian structure meaning 'your ...'). تیغ پولاد is the noun phrase 'sword of steel' (تیغ = sword, پولاد = steel, postposed attribute). گردد is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of گشتن here meaning 'become' or 'will turn into'. چو introduces a simile 'as/like' and موم is 'wax' — together: 'your steel sword will become like wax.'
segments.s651-g879: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. از نزد is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'from the presence of' or 'sent from' (از = from, نزد = in the presence of). نوشین‌روان is an epithet/name meaning 'sweet‑souled / pleasant‑minded' and functions as the source (the person who sent the messenger).
segments.s651-g880: بیامد is the simple past 'came/arrived'. به کردار is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/shape of'. باد دمان is a poetic image: باد = 'wind' and دمان = 'hem/skirt', so 'باد دمان' pictures a breeze at the hem (i.e., a light, swift breeze) — the whole phrase modifies بیامد: 'came like a breeze at the hem.'
segments.s651-g1161: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth' placed before its locative phrase (poetic word order). ز هر سوی = 'from every side/direction' (ز = poetic 'from', هر = 'every', سوی = 'side/direction'). دز here names the place 'Dez' and رستخیز is a metaphorical noun 'uprising' (literally 'rising/resurrection'), so the whole chunk says 'an uprising rose from every direction at Dez.'
segments.s651-g1162: ندیدند is the 3rd-person plural past negative 'they did not see' (used here as 'they found'). جایی functions as direct object 'any place' (no preposition). گذار و گریز are two coordinated nouns 'passage and flight/escape' describing what they lacked; the verb precedes its object in this poetic line.
segments.s651-g1163: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید تابان = 'the shining sun' (noun + postpositive adjective). ز گنبد = 'from the dome' (poetic 'from'). بگشت is a past-form verb 'turned/returned' (here 'turned away/rose'); the exact lemma provided doesn't match this sense in the metadata, so the tagger sense is unknown for this instance.
segments.s651-g1164: شد is the past 'became' (or 'it happened that'). آن باره = 'that instant/occasion' (temporal phrase). دز is the place-name 'Dez' (subject). به کردار دشت = 'in the likeness/form of a plain' (به = 'into/in', کردار = 'form/manner', دشت = 'plain'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: verb + time phrase + subject + simile phrase.
segments.s651-g1165: This noun phrase chains possessive/attributive relations: خروش سواران = 'the cry/uproar of the horsemen' and گرد سپاه = 'the dust/cloud of the army'. و links the two coordinated NPs. گرد here means the physical 'dust' (not the preposition).
segments.s651-g1166: دود و آتش ('smoke and fire') is the compound subject; برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'. به ماه = 'to/toward the moon' (به = 'toward', ماه = 'moon')—a poetic way to say 'up into the sky'. The initial token ابا is unclear/odd in this line (doesn't match the usual sense 'to be reluctant') and likely a scribal/reading variant or interjection; its sense in the provided list does not fit the context.
segments.s651-g1167: همه حصن = 'the whole/entire fortress' (همه as 'entire'). بی‌تن is a compound adjective 'without bodies/disembodied' modifying the following nouns سر و پای ('heads and feet'). بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase is a terse, poetic description: the fortress was full of disconnected heads and feet, lacking bodies.
segments.s651-g1168: تن بی‌سرانشان = 'their bodies without heads' (تن = 'body'; بی‌سرانشان = 'headless ones of theirs', the possessive -شان attaches to the adjective phrase). دگر جای = 'another place' / 'elsewhere' (دگر = 'other/another', جای = 'place'). بود is again the past 'was' — together: 'their headless bodies were elsewhere.'
segments.s651-g1305: به شهر اندر آمد is an archaic poetic order meaning 'entered the city': به + place + اندر + آمد ('came into'); اندر here functions as an older preposition/adverb 'in/into' and آمد is the past 'came/entered'. سراسر precedes سپاه as an adjective 'entire/throughout' (the entire army). The phrase thus reads literally 'into the city came the whole army.'
segments.s651-g1306: پیی را is the object phrase 'trace/pursuit' marked by the object marker را; نبد is an archaic negative/existential 'was not / there was not' (older form of نبود). بر زمین ('on the ground') is a locative prepositional phrase; نیز راه is appended as 'nor any path/way' — the clause means 'there was no trace of pursuit on the ground, nor any road.'
segments.s651-g1307: سه جنگ گران is a numeral + noun phrase 'three heavy/serious battles' (گران here = 'grave/intense'). کرده شد is a passive/perfect-like construction 'were done/was fought' (کرده + شد = 'were carried out'). در سه روز is the temporal adverbial 'in/over three days.'
segments.s651-g1308: چهارم is an adverbial 'on the fourth (day)'. چو here means 'when' (archaic/poetic). بفروخت is past tense 'sold/parted with' — in context it functions as 'relinquished' or 'gave up.' گیتی‌فروز is the proper name (the ruler) and follows the verb in inverted poetic order, serving as the subject.
segments.s651-g1309: گشاده شد is a verbal phrase 'was opened/became open' (گشاده = 'open', شد = 'became/was'); آن مرز آباد بوم is a compounded noun phrase 'that prosperous border‑land/home' where آباد qualifies مرز (prosperous) and بوم ('land'/'homeland') completes the phrase. Word order is adjective/qualifier before the head noun in poetry.
segments.s651-g1310: This is a negative-perceptual clause: سواری ندیدند = 'they saw no rider(s)'. جنگی is an adjective 'warlike/for war' modifying سواری (though separated for poetic effect). بروم is a verbal form 'go/going forth' (used here as the action seen or not seen), so together 'they saw no rider going to war.'
segments.s651-g1311: بزرگان 'the great ones/nobles' is the head noun; که introduces a relative clause 'who'. با تخت و افسر = 'with (their) throne and helmet/regalia' (با = 'with', تخت = 'throne', افسر here = 'helmet' as part of regalia). بدند is an archaic past plural of بودن 'they were' used here as the verb of the relative clause. The clause thus says 'the nobles who were with throne and helmet.'
segments.s651-g1312: هم is an additive particle 'also/likewise'. آنکس 'that one/person' is followed by a relative clause with که + predicate. گنجور قیصر reads as 'Caesar's treasure/treasury' used adjectivally or as an office ('the one of Caesar's treasury'); بدند (past plural of بودن) predicates the clause: 'and also the one who was Caesar's treasurer/held Caesar's treasure.' Note classical Persian often uses plural verb forms and flexible word order in such appositions.
segments.s651-g809: که here introduces a subordinate clause and functions like English "so that/that" (a purpose/result clause). ما بازگشتیم is a past form (بازگشتیم = “we returned/withdrew”) used here with an intentive or optative flavor: "that we might withdraw/turn back." زین is the fused poetic form (ز + این) meaning "from this," and بدکنش (literally "bad-doer/bad-action") names the evil source or bad conduct from which they propose to withdraw.
segments.s651-g810: مگر here means "unless" (introducing the single exception). شاه is the king; گردد (present/jussive of گشتن) is "becomes" or "may become" (used prospectively). زِ ما literally "from/of us," here timed as "toward us" (i.e. in relation to us), and خوش‌منش is an adjectival phrase meaning "kind‑hearted / well‑disposed." The clause therefore means "unless the king becomes well-disposed toward us."
segments.s651-g811: اگر introduces a conditional "if." شاه را دل is an instance of the object/ethical marker را attaching to شاه so that دل belongs to/concerns the king — idiomatically "the king's heart." زِ گیلان here gives the object of the king's desire or intent ("toward Gilan"), and بخست (past of بستن) literally "shut/closed" is used idiomatically: "his heart is fixed/attached/sets (on)" — i.e. "the king's heart is set on Gilan."
segments.s651-g812: ببریم is the 1st person plural present/subjunctive form with the prefix بـ giving an intentional/volitional sense "we will take/carry/take off." سرها is the plural "heads." ز here is the poetic preposition "from/with/by" linking to the instrument or source phrase; تنها بدست is an instrumental phrase meaning "by (our) hands alone" or "with our own hands only." The whole segment means "we shall take the heads by our own hands alone."
segments.s651-g813: دل شاه = "the king's heart" (noun + possessor). خشنود گردد uses خشنود (pleased/content) with گردد (subjunctive/present of گشتن) to mean "will become pleased" or "is/shall be pleased." مگر in this line functions as a discourse particle with a rhetorical sense (not the restrictive "unless") linking to the following temporal clause — roughly "indeed/when" in context.
segments.s651-g814: چو is a temporal conjunction "when." بیند (present/subjunctive of دیدن) = "he sees." بریده is the past-participle adjective "cut off / severed," modifying the noun phrase that follows. یکی توده سر = "one single heap/pile of heads" (یکی = one/a, توده = mass/heap, سر = head). Word order is poetic but straightforward: "when he sees a single heap of severed heads."
segments.s651-g815: چو here is a temporal conjunction "when." چندان modifies خروش and means "so much/so great" (intensifier). خروش آمد = "a cry/uproar came" (آمد = past "came/occurred"). از بارگاه (= "from the court/palace") gives the source of the noise. The clause is a temporal clause: "When so great an uproar came from the palace..."
segments.s651-g816: وزان is a fused form (و + ز ان) meaning "and/from that" or "from that (kind)." گونه here means "kind/manner" and combines with آواز "voice/sound" to yield "that kind of sound/voice." بشنید is past "he heard," and شاه is the subject placed after the verb (typical epic word order): "the king heard such a sound."
segments.s651-g1145: This is a conditional protasis introduced by اگر (if). شیر و پیل is a coordinated noun phrase 'lions and elephants'. آمدندیش is an archaic past plural form 'they came' with the enclitic -یش (= 'to him' / 'before him'), so the whole segment means 'if lions and elephants came before him' (i.e., faced him). پیش reinforces the sense 'in the presence of / before'.
segments.s651-g1146: نه is the negation placed before the verb. برداشتی here is a past-tense verbal form meaning 'would bear / would sustain / would carry on' (classical/poetic use of برداشتن for 'to undertake or endure'); it addresses the addressee ('you'). جنگ یک روز بیش is the duration phrase 'war more than one day' (روز = day; بیش = 'more, beyond'), so the clause reads 'you would not have borne the war for more than a day.'
segments.s651-g1147: سپاهی is a noun 'a host/force' used here as the head of a relative clause introduced by که (that/which). با خود = 'with its own (men/resources)'. و خفتان is an older/poetic phrase — خفتان derives from the root 'to sleep' and here functions as a plural substantive ('the sleepers' or camp-followers/men who lay down), coordinated by و; جنگ is the noun 'battle/war' tied to the clause context. The whole segment is a relative phrase describing the army.
segments.s651-g1148: به پیش سپاه = 'to/in front of the host' (به + پیش + سپاه). آمدی is an archaic past form 'came' (here third‑person narrative/past). به یدرنگ is an adverbial phrase 'at once / immediately' (به + یدرنگ), placed after the verb for emphasis. The clause as a whole: 'came before the host immediately.'
segments.s651-g1149: Another conditional started by اگر (if). کشته بودی is a past perfect/conditional rendering 'you had been killed' (کشته = slain; بودی = 'you were / you had been'). و‌گر is an alternative conditional 'or if'. بسته is a past participle meaning 'bound / tied / taken captive' and زار is an adjective 'wretched / pitiable', so together 'or bound and wretched.'
segments.s651-g1150: This small clause names the agent/result: پیروزگر شهریار = 'the triumphant/victorious sovereign/king' (compound noun phrase, adjective before noun). بزاندان in the manuscript here reads oddly (lemma given as 'to beget / to produce'), but in context classical variants often have بَ زندان ('into prison') or a verb meaning 'would have put/handed over'. The line is elliptical: it implies that the victorious king would have taken action (e.g. put you in prison or otherwise taken you).
segments.s651-g1151: چنین 'thus/so' opens the clause. تا + بیامد here has a temporal sense 'when / once he came' (classical use of تا to introduce a temporal clause). بران is an archaic locative/relational particle 'to/on that' and شارستان means 'city/town'; together the phrase reads 'thus, when he came to that town.'
segments.s651-g1152: که is a relative/explanatory conjunction 'for/which'. شوراب literally 'saline water / brine' here likely refers to a brackish stream or the salty water of the locality (possibly a place-name). بد نام is a compound adjective 'bad-named' = 'ill‑famed / notorious'. آن کارستان is 'that place / that district' (کارستان in poetry can name a place or a feat); together the clause explains that the town had an ill reputation because of its salty water.
segments.s651-g953: سخنهاش (sukhan-hâsh) is 'his words' — plural noun + enclitic possessive (-ش). بشنید is a past 3rd-person form 'heard' (simple past). The conjunction و links the two past verbs: the subject (understood 'they' or 'those present') first 'heard his words' and then 'read the letter' (نامه + بخواند, where خواند is past 'read/recited').
segments.s651-g954: بپیچید is a past-form of پیچیدن; here used figuratively to indicate being turned, thrown into disorder or bewilderment. The conjunction و continues the narrative. اندر شگفتی بماند combines the locative/poetic اندر 'in' + شگفتی 'astonishment' (noun) + بماند, past/subjunctive-looking form meaning 'remained' or 'was left.' Together: 'and (they) remained in astonishment.'
segments.s651-g955: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (introducing cause). گفتار کسری is a genitive construction 'the speech/words of Khosrow' (کسری = Kisra/Khosrow). سرافزار modifies مرد (poetic adjective + noun order) and describes the man as exalted/noble; this whole phrase gives the cause and subject: 'From Khosrow's speech, the noble man...'.
segments.s651-g956: This clause describes the effect on that man. پر ز چین کرد uses پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/from' + چین 'wrinkle' with کرد 'made' (i.e. 'became full of wrinkles' or 'grew wrinkled'). The following و links the second result: رخساره زرد 'his complexion/face became pale' (رَخساره = face/complexion, زرد = pale), with the previous کرد understood to apply to both results.
segments.s651-g957: نویسنده را uses the object marker را on 'the scribe' (definite direct object). خواند here is used in the sense 'called/summoned' (one common literary use), not merely 'read.' The conjunction و links the next past verb: پاسخ نوشت 'wrote an answer' (پاسخ = reply, نوشت = wrote).
segments.s651-g958: پدیدار کرد is a causative construction composed of پدیدار (manifest/visible) + کرد 'made', meaning 'made appear' or 'revealed.' اندرو = اندر + او → 'in it/within it' (referring to the letter or what was written). خوب و زشت lists the contents revealed: 'the good and the ugly/vile.'
segments.s651-g959: سر خامه is a classical compound 'the head (سر) of the pen/quill (خامه)' (noun + noun). چون here functions as 'when/as' (temporal), and کرد is the past 'made/caused.' رنگین بقار is an adjectival phrase 'colourful/bright dignity/solemnity' — together the clause means something like 'when he set the pen's nib, he endowed it with bright dignity' (a poetic description of beginning to write).
segments.s651-g960: نخست is 'first/first of all.' آفرین here is an acclamation/praise noun ('praise, blessing') and together with کرد ('he made/did') forms 'he gave praise.' بر کردگار uses بر in the sense 'to/toward' and کردگار = 'the Creator/God.' So: 'first he praised the Creator.'
segments.s651-g825: 'به ره بر' is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'on the road / on the way' (به + ره + بر). 'یکی لشکر' uses 'یکی' as the numeral/indefinite 'one/a single' modifying 'لشکر', and 'بی‌کران' is a postpositive adjective ('boundless') describing that host; the whole phrase is an adverbial/nominal phrase placed before the verb.
segments.s651-g826: 'پدید آمد' is a two-part literary predicate meaning 'appeared/emerged' (پدید + آمد). 'از دور' is the source/adverbial 'from afar', and 'نیزه‌وران' is the noun 'spearmen' that completes the clause; word order is literary but straightforward: verb + adverbial + noun.
segments.s651-g827: 'سواری بیامد' is a simple past clause 'a rider came'; 'به کردار' is 'in the manner of / in the guise of' (به + کردار), and 'گرد' here characterizes that manner as roaming/going-about (a literary use meaning 'roving'); together 'به کردار گرد' = 'in the fashion of a wanderer.'
segments.s651-g828: The 'که' here gives the reason ('for/because'); 'در لشکر' = 'in the army'; 'گشن' is an adjective 'hungry'; 'بد‌پای' is a compound adjectival phrase literally 'bad-footed' = 'with sore/ill feet', and 'مرد' is the affected noun 'men'. The phrase explains why the rider acted as he did: hunger had made the men's feet sore.
segments.s651-g829: پیاده شد از اسب و بگشاد لب is a sequence of two past actions. پیاده شد literally 'became on foot' = 'dismounted'; از اسب marks the source 'from the horse'. و links to بگشاد لب, where بگشاد is the past form 'opened' (poetic prefix b- is retained) and لب 'lip' (= 'mouth', metonymically 'spoke').
segments.s651-g830: چنین گفت کاین منذرست از عرب: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said'. که introduces the content clause; کاین = 'that this' (the demonstrative + enclitic particle). منذرست is a predicative copular phrase 'is a warning' (stative), and از عرب = 'from the Arabs' marks the source. The main verb گفت here introduces reported speech.
segments.s651-g831: بیامد که بیند مگر شاه را: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. که introduces a purpose/result clause 'that/in order that he might see' and بیند is the present/subjunctive form 'may see' used for purpose. مگر here is a modal/parenthetical ('perhaps'/'maybe') modifying the clause; شاه را is the object marked by را (definite/accusative) — 'the king' is the intended object of seeing.
segments.s651-g832: ببوسد همی خاک درگاه را continues the purpose clause: ببوسد is the subjunctive/jussive 'that he might kiss' (dependent on the previous که for purpose), همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (here strengthening/continuing the action), and خاک درگاه را is the definite object 'the dust of the threshold/royal court' with را marking it as object — a conventional humble gesture 'to kiss the dust of the threshold'.
segments.s651-g1361: بدو (prep + enclitic) = 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (reporting speech). کاین is the literary/poetic fusion of که + این, introducing the content clause 'that this ...'. The noun phrase زیب خسرو تو راست places the possessed noun خسرو before the possessive pronoun تو (a typical Persian order: noun + possessor), followed by راست as a stative/predicative word meaning 'is true/is so' serving as the copula-like predicate.
segments.s651-g1362: This clause is coordinated with the previous by an implied continuation: غریبان functions as a predicate/noun ('a stranger/strangers') describing the addressee; و links this predicate to the following noun phrase. این خانه نو is a demonstrative + noun + adjective 'this new house'; تو after the noun marks the possessor 'your'; راست again is the predicative 'is so/true' that applies to the coordinated items.
segments.s651-g1363: به سان is a fixed literary simile 'like/as' (به + سان). درخت is the noun 'tree' and برومند is an adjective meaning 'flourishing/prosperous/tall'; باش is the second-person singular imperative 'be!' (imperative verb comes at the end here). The whole is an imperative simile: 'Be like a flourishing tree.'
segments.s651-g1364: This line strings imperatives: پدر باش = 'be a father' (پدر noun + باش imperative). گاهی 'sometimes' modifies the following clause; چو = 'as/like' introduces the simile; فرزند 'child' and the final باش repeats the imperative 'be.' Word order is straightforward: adverbial (گاهی) + simile (چو فرزند) + verb (باش).
segments.s651-g1365: ببخشش is the verb ببخش with the object-clitic -ش (him/it) and here functions as an imperative or hortatory infinitive 'forgive/grant him.' بیارای is an imperative-form verb 'adorn/beautify' (here used transitively with the same object understood); و links to the negative command زفتی مکن, where زفتی is an adjective/adverb 'foul/nasty' and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do.' Together: 'Forgive him and adorn him, and do not be foul.'
segments.s651-g1366: بر اندازه باید places the preposition/locative بر + اندازه ('in/with moderation') before the modal باید ('must/ought to be'), so literally 'ought to be according to measure.' ز هر در سخن is a poetic phrasing meaning 'from every gate/door of speech' where ز = 'from,' هر = 'every,' در = 'door/gate' (metaphorical), and سخن = 'speech/word.' The whole clause means 'in every case (from every gate), speech must be measured'—i.e., speak with moderation appropriate to each situation.
segments.s651-g1367: ز انطاکیه is the poetic preposition ز = 'from' + place name 'Antioch.' شاه is the subject 'the king'; لشکر is the object 'army/host.' براند is the past tense/archaic form of راندن 'to drive/send (forth),' so the clause reads 'from Antioch the king sent/led forth the army.' Word order puts the place first for emphasis.
segments.s651-g1368: جهاندیده is an adjective 'worldly‑wise, experienced'; ترسا (ترسا/ترسا) here denotes 'a pious man' (a religiously devout person). نگهبان is the noun 'guardian/keeper' and نشاند is an archaic past tense of نشاندن meaning 'he set/installed/placed' (appointed). The clause means 'he appointed the worldly‑wise pious man as guardian.'
segments.s651-g1417: گر here is the conditional 'if'. رستخیز is a poetic noun meaning an uprising/resurrection; the prepositional phrase از پی (از + پی) means 'as a result of / in pursuit of' and is followed by خواستست, an archaic past form meaning 'he wished' (third person past). The clause literally orders as: 'If this uprising from/after (in pursuit of) [was] wished.'
segments.s651-g1418: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that…'. آزرم is treated as a proper name here, joined by و 'and' to دانش 'knowledge'; بدو is the dative/clitic meaning 'to him/for him'. کاستست is a past passive/impersonal form meaning 'was lessened / diminished'.
segments.s651-g1419: بیاوردم is first‑person past 'I brought' (بیاورد + ـم). اکنون 'now' and همه 'all' modify گنج روم 'the treasure(s) of Rome/Byzantium' (روم as a genitive/identifier). Word order is subject‑verb omitted + verb phrase 'I brought now all the Roman treasure(s)'.
segments.s651-g1420: که here gives the reason/explanatory clause 'for/that'. روشن‌روان is a compound epithet 'bright‑souled' (used as a substantive), and بهتر از is the comparative 'better than' followed by the two items compared: گنج 'treasure' and بوم 'homeland'. The comparator uses از after بهتر in standard Persian order.
segments.s651-g1421: چو is the temporal 'when'. بشنید is simple past 'he heard'. The sequence ز + و represents the poetic contraction زو = 'from him' (ز preposition + enclitic و = 'him/her'). این سخن شهریار is 'this speech/these words of the king' (سخن + possessor شهریار). The verb بشنید governs the following noun phrase 'from him this speech (of the king)'.
segments.s651-g1422: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive clitic ـش). گشت is past 'became' (intransitive), so دلش گشت خرم = 'his heart became joyful'. چو here introduces a simile 'like/as' and باغ بهار 'a spring garden' is a poetic nominal phrase (garden of spring) serving as the image of joy.
segments.s651-g1423: پذیرفت is past 'he accepted'. Again the preposition + enclitic appears as ز + و (زو) = 'from him'. هرچ آورده بود is a subordinate phrase 'whatever he had brought' (هرچ = whatever; آورده بود = past perfect/pluperfect sense 'had brought'). بود is the past auxiliary indicating the preceding perfective action.
segments.s651-g1424: اگر introduces a conditional/alternative 'if / whether'. بدره here is poetic 'a beauty' (figurative; literally 'full‑moon' imagery) and is qualified by زر 'gold' (golden/precious). و‌گر is the alternative 'or if', followed by برده 'slave' (noun) and the copula بود 'was' in past — the whole line lists alternatives of what was brought.
segments.s651-g65: هر آنکس که آید بدین بارگاه is a relative clause meaning “whoever comes to this court.” هر آنکس = “anyone/whosoever”; که introduces the relative clause; آید is 3sg present (classical) “comes”; بدین is the prepositional phrase (به + این) “to this/into this”; بارگاه is the place noun “court/palace.” Word order places the relative phrase before the result clause, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s651-g66: ببایست کاری نیابند راه: ببایست is the archaic/modal verb ‘must/ought’; کاری here functions as an object meaning ‘(any) deed/action’ (lexical entry given is mismatched, so sense is unspecified); نیابند is negative present/subjunctive or jussive 3pl of یافتن ‘to find’ (used impersonally here: ‘they/one does not find’); راه is the object ‘way/method.’ Together: ‘there must be no way found (to do any deed).’
segments.s651-g67: نباشم ز دستور همداستان: نباشم is the first-person negative present/subjunctive or jussive of بودن ‘to be’ — ‘I will not / I am not’; ز دستور = ‘from/by reason of the order’ (ز = poetic preposition ‘from/because of’); همداستان here = ‘accomplice / one of the same mind’ (literally ‘of the same story’). The clause says the speaker refuses to be complicit because of an order.
segments.s651-g68: که بر من بپوشد چنین داستان: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ‘who/that’; بر من = ‘upon me’ or ‘to my account’; بپوشد is 3sg present subjunctive of پوشیدن here used in the sense ‘to put on / to cover / to clothe’ (i.e. ‘to ascribe or fasten onto someone’); چنین داستان = ‘such a tale/story’ (چنین = ‘such/thus’, داستان = ‘story/tale’). So: ‘who would ascribe/put such a story upon me.’
segments.s651-g69: بدرگاه بر کارداران من: بدرگاه is an adverbial/place noun ‘at the court/in the presence (of the king)’; بر is a preposition ‘on/upon/at’ linking to the following noun; کارداران من = ‘my managers/officials’ (کارداران = stewards, officers). The phrase lists who is present at court: ‘at court, my officers.’
segments.s651-g70: ز لشکر نبرده سواران من: ز لشکر = ‘from the army’; نبرده is the negative perfect/participial sense ‘has not taken / were not taken’ (verb lacks an explicit subject and is understood from context); سواران من = ‘my horsemen/riders.’ The clause therefore states (elliptically) that the horsemen have not been taken from the army.
segments.s651-g71: چو روزی بدیشان نداریم تنگ: چو = temporal conjunction ‘when’; روزی here is the noun ‘sustenance/daily provision’ (the supplied lexical senses do not show this meaning, so sense is unspecified); بدیشان = ‘for them/to them’; نداریم = 1pl present negative ‘we do not have’; تنگ = ‘scarce/tight’ (literally ‘narrow’ but used idiomatically of scarcity). Together: ‘When we have little/no provision for them…’
segments.s651-g72: نگه کرد باید بنام و به ننگ: نگه کردن = ‘to keep/hold/retain’; here the verb is inverted (poetic order) as نگه کرد باید = ‘one must keep/hold (them)’ or ‘we must keep them tightly’; بنام و به ننگ is idiomatic: بنام = ‘by name/in name’ (i.e. only titularly) and به ننگ = ‘in shame/in disgrace.’ So: ‘they must be kept — by name only and in shame.’
segments.s651-g585: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the point of origin; the place names گرگان, ساری, آمل are coordinated with و. بخ is an archaic/poetic spelling of به (direction 'to') placed before ساری; شدند is the past plural/copular verb 'they became/they came' used here to mean 'they arrived' or 'they were (there)'. The phrase as a whole expresses motion from one place to others.
segments.s651-g586: به هنگام is a fixed prepositional phrase 'at the time of'; آواز بلبل is a genitival phrase 'the song/voice of the nightingale' (نِگارشی noun + noun), and شدند again is the past copula/verb 'they were/they arrived'. Together this gives 'they arrived at the time of the nightingale's song.'
segments.s651-g587: در و دشت lists two locales ('the court/house (در) and the plain (دشت)') joined by و. یه کسر here functions as the poetic compound یک‌سر meaning 'altogether' or 'entirely' and modifies the predicate همه بیشه بود 'was all a grove'; بود is the past copula. So word order is locatives → adverbial 'entirely' → predicative noun → copula.
segments.s651-g588: دل شاه ایران is a genitive chain 'the heart (دل) of the king (شاه) of Iran (ایران)'; پراندیشه is a compound adjective 'full of thought / pensive' placed after the noun, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The construction states a mental state of the king in simple past.
segments.s651-g589: ز هامون 'from the plain' (ز = from, هامون = plain) marks origin; به indicates motion toward a place (here 'to' a mountain), کوهی is an indefinite/collective reference to 'a mountain' or 'mountainous place', and برآمد is the past verb 'rose/ascended'. بلند follows and modifies the verb as an adverb/adjective 'high' (i.e. 'ascended high').
segments.s651-g590: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'one/a certain' introducing an unspecified person; تازیی is a descriptor (here an ethnic/qualifying word in the text, 'Tazi') placed after it; برنشسته is a participial compound 'mounted/having sat upon' (بر- + نشسته) and functions attributively; سمند is the head noun 'steed/warhorse'. The sequence places modifiers before the head, describing 'a certain Tazi mounted on a steed.'
segments.s651-g591: سر کوه و آن بیشه‌ها is a coordinated subject 'the summit of the mountain and those groves'; سر here means 'top/summit'. بنگرید is a classical poetic past form of 'to look/gaze' (literary perfect/past), so the clause reads 'he looked at / gazed over the mountain-top and those groves.'
segments.s651-g592: A series of direct objects is listed (گل و سنبل و آب و نخچیر) 'flowers and hyacinths and water and game' followed by دید, the simple past 'saw' — typical Persian order: objects then verb. دید is the past-tense head verb applying to the preceding coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s651-g1121: جهانی here functions as a nominal phrase 'a world' (poetic use of an otherwise adjectival form) and is the theme of the clause; بدو is a dative ('to him'); مانده is a perfect/past participial/predicative form meaning 'remained' or 'was left'; اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment' that modifies the state expressed by مانده. Word order (Nominal — dative — predicate — prepositional phrase) is typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g1122: که introduces a subordinate (explanatory) clause 'for/that'; نوشین روان is an epithet 'the sweet‑souled (one)' with نوشین as adjective before the noun روان; آن بزرگی is the object phrase 'that greatness' and گرفت is a simple past ('took/assumed'); the clause explains why the world remained in wonder.
segments.s651-g1123: به هر جایگاهی is a prepositional phrase 'to/at every post/place'; که introduces a relative/clausal modifier 'where/when'; جنگ آمدی is 'you came in battle' (آمدی is 2nd‑person past 'you came'), so the whole segment means 'whenever/at whatever post you came to fight.'
