segments.s651-g1124: This is a coordinate list of qualities all governed by the final verb آمدی (shared verb): ورارای (archaic/poetic: 'beyond/undaunted'), و هوش ('and awareness/cleverness'), و درنگ ('and (without) delay' — درنگ is 'pause/delay'); with آمدی ('you came') the sense is 'you came/appeared undaunted, with awareness, and without delay.' The nouns are linked by واو and relate back to the subject implied in the previous clause.
segments.s651-g1125: فرستاده‌ای is used as 'an envoy / one to be sent' (from فرستادن) and functions as the object of خواستی ('you wanted/wished for'); راستگوی is a postpositive adjective 'truth‑speaking, truthful' modifying فرستاده‌ای. The verb خواستی is 2nd‑person past 'you wished/desired.'
segments.s651-g1126: که introduces a relative clause modifying the envoy; رفتی here is a past form 'went' used in the clause 'who went' (poetic morphology can differ from modern prose), بر denotes direction/target 'against/to', and چاره‌جوی is an agent noun 'seeker of remedy/solution' describing the envoy's purpose. The clause thus says the envoy would go toward the enemy in search of a solution.
segments.s651-g1127: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); یافتندی is a past plural 'they found' (used here in the protasis); سوی داد راه is a poetic word order for 'a path/way toward justice' (سوی = toward, داد = justice, راه = way) with راه as the head noun.
segments.s651-g1128: نکردی is a negated past 'did not do' (the verb precedes its patient/object, a stylistic V–O word order common in poetry); ستم خود = 'his own oppression' (خود marking possession 'own'); خردمند شاه is an appositive 'the wise king' that supplies the subject — so the clause reads 'the wise king did not inflict/commit his own injustice.' Note the archaic/poetic endings (‑i) that occur in narrative past forms.
segments.s651-g425: تن خویش را (object) comes first: 'your body' (تن + possessive خویش). چون محابا is an adverbial phrase 'in such a way / with deference'; کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of کردن used here in a conditional sense ('if you do / if you treat'). Word order is object → manner → verb; the conditional sense is carried by the verb form (چون ... کنی = 'if you treat ...').
segments.s651-g426: دل راستی را is a doubled noun phrase with را marking the definite object ('the heart of uprightness/truth'). همی- is a classical prefix indicating ongoing or emphatic action and بشکنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of شکستن ('you break' / 'you will break'), so together همی‌بشکنی functions as a warning/result ('you will break' / 'you keep breaking').
segments.s651-g427: بدین ارز is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'by/with this worth' (بدین = 'by this'; ارز = 'value'), placed before the subject. تو is the subject 'you'; نزد من = 'in my regard / before me' (i.e. 'in my eyes'); بیش گشت is a verbal phrase with بیش 'more' and گشت 'became' = 'became greater / increased'. The verb (گشت) appears at the end as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s651-g428: دلم is 'my heart' (دل + possessive suffix); سوی + اندیشه خویش is the directional complement 'toward its/one's own thought' (سوی = 'toward(s)'); گشت is 'turned / became'. The clause thus reads literally 'my heart turned toward its own thought.'
segments.s651-g429: که introduces the content/question clause here ('that/which' or beginning a rhetorical question). ما is the subject 'we'; در صف کار = 'in the battle-line/row of work' (در صف = 'in the ranks/line', کار = 'deed/duty'); ننگ و نبرد are nouns joined by و 'and' ('shame and battle'). The sequence lists what would be brought into the ranks.
segments.s651-g430: چگونه = 'how'. برآریم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive of آوردن in a hortatory/interrogative sense ('shall we bring' / 'can we bring'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; آورد is the stem 'bring' (here noun-like or verbal root used in the verbal phrase); گرد is the adverb/particle 'about/around' often used to form the idiom 'ز آورد گرد' = 'how could it be brought about'. Together the phrase asks rhetorically how such a thing could come to pass.
segments.s651-g431: چنین داد ('thus he gave') + پاسخ ('answer') is a fixed pattern meaning 'thus replied'; به پرمایه شاه is the indirect object 'to the stalwart/substantial king' (پرمایه = 'rich, substantial, valiant' as an epithet). Word order places the manner/adverb قبل of the verb and then the indirect object.
segments.s651-g432: که introduces the content of the reply ('that'). چون = 'when/as'. نو modifies the following nouns 'new'. نبیند is the 3rd-person present form of دیدن as used here in classical/poetic syntax to mean 'he sees' (in older/persian poetic prose the initial n- can appear in forms that are not a simple negative), and نگین و کلاه are the direct objects 'gem and hat'. The clause therefore reads 'that when he sees a new gem and hat...'.
segments.s651-g753: ز here introduces the ground/cause ('on account of' or 'concerning') and attaches to the following noun phrase. کار بلوج = 'the affair/work of the Baloch' (کار governing بلوج), and ارجمند اردشیر is the epithet + name ('the noble Ardashir'); the phrase as a whole means 'Ardashir, the noble, regarding the Baloch matter'. Word order is poetic: preposition + noun phrase then epithet+name.
segments.s651-g754: بکوشید is the past tense 3rd-singular of کوشیدن ('he strove/endeavored'). با introduces accompaniment ('with'), and کاردانان پیر is a noun phrase 'the skilled/experienced elders' (کاردانان = experts; پیر = old/elderly, here 'experienced'). The whole segment: 'he strove with experienced experts/elders.'
segments.s651-g755: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative form (equivalent to نبود) meaning 'there was not'. سودمندی is the nominal subject 'usefulness/benefit'. به + افسون و رنگ links the domain ('in/for enchantment and appearance'); رنگ here means 'appearance/complexion' rather than literal 'color'.
segments.s651-g756: نه از introduces a negated source ('not from'). بند here means 'fetter/bond' and the following items enumerate things that yield no benefit. وز in this context is an archaic connective (effectively 'and/from'); it functions to continue the list rather than its dictionary senses. The list continues with رنج (suffering/hardship), پیکار (battle/struggle) and جنگ (war).
segments.s651-g757: اگرچند is a concessive conjunction 'although'. بد modifies سخن ('an ill/unfavorable speech/remark') and ناگزیر is a postpositive adjective meaning 'inevitable/compelled' — together: 'although this utterance was unfortunate and unavoidable.' Word order places the concessive clause before the main clause.
segments.s651-g758: بپوشید (past 3rd sing.) from پوشیدن here means 'to cover/conceal' rather than 'to wear'. بر خویشتن is a reflexive phrase 'upon himself / within himself' (بر = on/upon; خویشتن = oneself), and اردشیر is the subject placed after the object phrase in poetic order: 'Ardashir concealed it in himself.'
segments.s651-g759: ز again marks cause ('because of'). گفتار دهقان = 'the speech/words of the dehqan' (دهقان here understood as the dehqan/landed-gentry/farm-honcho). برآشفت is a past form meaning 'became angry/agitated' and شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb (poetic order): 'the king was angered by the dehqan's words.'
segments.s651-g760: به سوی = 'toward' (direction). بلوج = 'the Baloch' (the people being approached). اندر آمد ز راه is a verbal phrase 'came/approached by/along the road' (اندر آمد = 'came in/approached'; ز راه = 'by/from the road' — here ز has the sense 'from/by' indicating the route).
segments.s651-g857: ز لشکر زبان‌آوری برگزید is literal word order: ز + noun phrase 'لشکر' = 'from the army'; 'زبان‌آوری' (a substantive meaning 'an eloquent one / spokesman') is the thing selected; 'برگزید' is a past tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'he chose/selected.' The preposition ز comes before the source noun as in classical Persian.
segments.s651-g858: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'زبان‌آوری' ('who...'). 'گفتار ایشان' = 'their speech/words' (possessive order). 'بداند' here is an older/poetic present/subjunctive form of دانستن meaning 'be able to know/understand', and it pairs with the following verbal idea 'شنید' ('he hears') — two related verbal notions: 'could understand and hear (their speech)'.
segments.s651-g859: 'بدو' is the dative pronoun phrase 'to him' (classical order: به + او compressed), and 'گفت' is the past 'he said' introducing the reported speech. 'ز ایدر برو' = 'go from here' (ز = 'from', ایدر = 'here', برو = imperative 'go'). 'تا بروم' = 'until I go/leave' where 'بروم' is 1st‑person subjunctive/present used for 'I go/leave'.
segments.s651-g860: 'میاسای' is a verbal form meaning 'be at rest / settle' (here jussive/imperative sense 'do not rest' in combination with 'هیچ'). 'هیچ' with the verb gives the sense 'not at all / in no (place)'; 'اندر آباد بوم' is a locative phrase 'in any inhabited land' (اندر = 'in', آباد بوم = 'inhabited dwelling/land'). Word order is classical: verb + negator + locative phrase.
segments.s651-g861: 'به قیصر' is the dative 'to/for Caesar.' The surface 'بگو‌گر' should be read as the imperative 'بگو' plus the conditional particle 'گر' ('say / tell — if ...'), so the phrase introduces a conditional address: 'Tell Caesar: if you do not have...'. 'نداری' is 2nd‑person present negative 'you do not have / you lack', and 'خرد' is the noun 'wisdom' as the thing lacking.
segments.s651-g862: 'ز' = 'because of / on account of' here links cause ('because of your counsel') to the following result. 'رای تو' = 'your counsel/opinion' (رای = counsel/plan). 'مغز تو' = 'your brain/mind' (possessive). 'کیفر برد' is an idiomatic classical sequence meaning 'to receive / incur punishment' — برد here means 'to take/receive' (so the mind receives punishment). The clause states causation: because of your counsel, your mind will bear punishment.
segments.s651-g863: 'اگر' = 'if' introduces a conditional. 'شیر جنگی' is a compound noun: 'شیر' (lion) qualified by the adjective/epithet 'جنگی' ('warlike, a fighter'). 'بتازد' (بـ + تازد) is a classical 3rd‑person present/future 'he rushes/attacks.' 'بگور' is an adverbial phrase 'to the grave / unto death' indicating the deadly result of the charge.
segments.s651-g864: 'کنامش کند' literally 'he makes (it) his bosom/side' where 'کنامش' = 'his bosom/near his side' and 'کند' = 'makes/does.' Here the verb takes two complements: 'گور' = 'a grave' (i.e. he makes his resting‑place a grave) and, by ellipsis of the second verb, also applies to 'آب شور' = 'water salty/brackish' (شور = saltiness/brine). Thus the construction uses one verb ('کند') to govern both objects connected by 'و هم' ('and also').
segments.s651-g1313: This clause uses the preposition به + recipient (به شاه جهاندار) followed by the past plural verb دادند and then the direct object گنج. دادند is 3rd-person plural past (“they gave”) with an omitted subject; word order here places the recipient before the verb (a common classical/poetic ordering). جهاندار is an epithet modifying شاه: “the world-ruling king.”
segments.s651-g1314: به چنگ آمدش گنج is a small clause where به چنگ (“into the grasp/into the hold”) is an adverbial phrase, آمدش is the past verb آمد with the clitic -ش “to him” (came to him), and گنج names what came. The following چون دید رنج uses چون as a temporal/concessive “when/as,” دید is 3rd-singular past with an implicit subject (the king), and رنج is the object meaning “suffering/trouble.”
segments.s651-g1315: A noun phrase listing the things sent: اسیران و آن گنج قیصر (the prisoners and that treasure of Caesar). آن is the demonstrative ‘that’ modifying گنج; قیصر follows as a postpositive genitive/attributive (‘Caesar’s’). به راه is an idiomatic locative/heading phrase meaning ‘on the road’ or ‘out on the way.’
segments.s651-g1316: فرستاد is the 3rd-person singular past (“sent”) with its subject expressed after the verb (شاه, a common poetic order). The prepositional phrase به سوی مداین marks the destination: به سوی = ‘toward’ and مداین = Madāʾin (Ctesiphon).
segments.s651-g1317: وزیشان here functions as an archaising connective meaning roughly ‘and/of them’ (the exact gloss is archaic); هران کس که introduces a restrictive relative clause ‘each one who…’; جنگی is an adjective/noun ‘warlike/warrior’ and بدند is 3rd-person plural past (‘they were’ / ‘were such’), so the whole segment means ‘and of them every person who was a warrior.’
segments.s651-g1318: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن ‘to place/set’ (‘they placed’). بر پشت پیلان is a prepositional phrase ‘upon the backs of the elephants’ (بر = on, پشت = back, پیلان = plural of پیل ‘elephant’). ببند is an imperative or hortative ‘bind/fasten’ addressed to attendants — here compressed into the narrative to indicate the action taken to secure the captives/treasure.
segments.s651-g1319: This clause uses دید in a perceptual/impersonal sense (‘was seen/appeared’) with زمین as the subject: زمین دید رخشان‌تر از چرخ ماه = ‘the earth appeared more radiant than the moon of the heavens.’ رخشان‌تر is the comparative ‘more radiant,’ and از introduces the standard of comparison (‘than’).
segments.s651-g1320: بگردید here is a past/plural verbal form (from گشتن) meaning ‘they turned/went about.’ بر گرد is the prepositional phrase ‘around/about,’ and آن شهر شاه is a noun phrase ‘that city of the king’ (شهر modified by the genitive/attributive شاه). Together: ‘they turned about that king’s city.’
segments.s651-g881: بر قیصر آمد is a prepositional phrase + past verb: بر (toward/to) precedes قیصر (Caesar) and آمد is the simple past 'came' (3rd sing.). پیامش is پیام + enclitic -ش 'his/its', 'his message'. بداد here is a classical past-form verb (بَداد = 'gave' / 'delivered') used to say the message was delivered; the dictionary senses provided did not list this verbal sense, so the exact lemma-sense pairing is not in the given list.
segments.s651-g882: بپیچید is a past action (3rd person singular) meaning 'turned/turned away' (the prefixed ب here is part of older verbal morphology). بی‌مایه is an adjective 'without means / worthless' modifying قیصر (Caesar). ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' and داد is the noun 'justice' — together ز داد gives 'from justice' or 'away from justice'.
segments.s651-g883: نداد is the negated past of دادن ('did not give'); here it functions 'he did not give'. ایچ is an older/poetic form meaning 'anything / at all' (negated: 'not at all'). پاسخ is the direct object 'reply/answer', and ورا (و + را fused) means 'to him' or 'toward him' (the clitic marking indirect object). جز is the preposition 'except' and فریب is the noun 'deception' — together: 'he gave him no answer at all except deception.'
segments.s651-g884: همی is the archaic/poetic continuous particle (here 'continually' / 'kept on'), preceding the verb دید 'saw' (past of دیدن). دور is an adverb 'far, at a distance'. از is the preposition 'from', بلندی (here intended 'height, high place') governs نشیب 'descent' (نشیب = the downward slope or the act of descending). The literal order is 'kept watching from the height the descent.' Note: the provided senses for بلندی did not include 'height', so that sense is not available in the list.
segments.s651-g885: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). کز is the fused compound of که + از used here as 'that'/'which', introducing a subordinate clause. منذر is a proper name (Munḏir) or a noun 'warner' — here treated as the name. کم خرد is an adjectival phrase 'of little understanding' (کم 'insufficient/little' + خرد 'wisdom/understanding'). Note: the entry for گفت in the provided senses list did not include the verbal past 'said', so that verbal sense is not among the given senses.
segments.s651-g886: سخن باور آن کن is an instruction: سخن 'the words', باور ... کن is 'believe' (باور 'trust/credence' with the imperative کن 'do' → 'believe'), and آن 'that' refers to the proposition; که introduces the following clause. اندر خورد is a fixed verbal phrase where خورد (here sense 'befall / happen') means 'that which comes to pass' — so 'believe that speech which will happen / come to pass.' Grammatically, 'سخن' is the object of the verb compound 'باور کن'.
segments.s651-g887: اگر is the conditional 'if'. خیره (lit. 'staring, fixed-eyed') modifies the following noun; in this poetic word order the adjective precedes or immediately follows the name: 'staring Munḏir' (منذر = Munḏir). بنالد is a present/habitual form (بـ + root + د) meaning 'moans/laments' (3rd person), and همی is the continuous particle marking repeated or ongoing action ('keeps on').
segments.s651-g888: برین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus'. رنجش is the noun 'resentment / annoyance' and ببالد is a 3rd person present subjunctive/optative from بالیدن meaning 'to foster / let grow / be proud of' — here idiomatically 'to harbor or let resentment grow'. همی again marks continuous aspect ('will keep on'). The clause says that, if Munḏir keeps lamenting thus, his resentment will grow/continue.
segments.s651-g1041: This clause is a compact verb phrase in past tense: 'یکی نامه فرمود' literally places an indefinite/quantifying word before the noun (یکی = 'one/a certain'), then the object (نامه = 'letter') and the past verb فرمود ('he said/ordered/sent' — here used to mean 'sent/wrote'). The following prepositional phrase با آفرین ('with praise') modifies the action and is attached after the verb; آفرین is a noun meaning 'acclamation/praise'. Note the poetic, slightly elliptical style where 'فرمود' carries the sense of composing/sending a letter.
segments.s651-g1042: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward/to' and introduces the indirect object phrase; the direct object of that motion is مرزبانان ('marzbans', plural). ایران‌زمین is a compound noun 'land of Iran' qualifying whose marzbans they are — in epic poetry the ezafe linking particle is often omitted, so this reads 'the marzbans of Iran-lands'. Word order is preposition + plural noun + nominal qualifier.
segments.s651-g1043: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the substance of the command ('that...'). ترسنده is an adjective 'alarmed/fearful' used predicatively; باشید is the second-person-plural present subjunctive/imperative 'be' (a polite or formal command: 'be X'). The conjunction و links a second admonition. بیدار is the adjective 'awake/alert' and the following بید is an archaic/poetic imperative-like form of 'be' (parallel to باشید) used for emphasis: 'be awake'.
segments.s651-g1044: This clause gives the concrete duty: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as the thing to be guarded; ز + دشمن is a prepositional phrase 'from the enemy'. نگهدار here functions as an imperative/verb 'keep/guard' (the lexical entry lists nominal senses, but in context it acts verbally), and the final بید again is the poetic 'be'/emphatic copula, reinforcing the command to remain vigilant in guarding the army.
segments.s651-g1045: کنارنگ is an adverbial/locative element 'at the side/edge/flank' placed up front for emphasis. با پهلوان means 'with a champion', and هرک هست is an indefinite relative 'whoever is (there)' — هرک is an archaic 'whoever' and هست is the copula 'is'. The whole phrase specifies positioning: 'at the flank, with whatever champion there is.' Poetic word order places the location first and the relative clause after.
segments.s651-g1046: همه داد جویید is a verbal phrase meaning 'seek/dispense justice for all' — داد ('justice') is the object/goal and جویید is a second-person-plural present-subjunctive/imperative verb 'seek' or 'make a quest for' (here: 'see to'). با زیردست literally 'with your subordinates' indicates agency or the sphere in which this should be done (i.e., using or among the underlings). The clause is an imperative instruction to ensure justice among subordinates.
segments.s651-g1047: بدارید is a second-person-plural imperative/subjunctive 'keep/hold/maintain' addressed to the governors. Poetic inversion places چندانک ('so long as' / 'to that extent') and باید ('it is necessary / must') before the noun سپاه ('army'): the sense is 'keep the army there as long as necessary.' Note the compact, somewhat inverted order typical of epic diction.
segments.s651-g1048: بدان here functions as a locative phrase 'to/at that (place)' (in the text the prefix and element are split). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that/in order that' and governs the subjunctive; نیابد is the negative third-person singular subjunctive ('he may not find' / 'let him not find'), expressing the desired prevention. بداندیش is an adjective 'ill‑minded/malicious', and راه is the noun 'way/path'. Together: '...so that the ill‑minded may not find a way (to it).'
segments.s651-g1337: شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became' (intransitive). آن is the demonstrative/determiner 'that'. زیب خسرو functions as a named epithet ('Zib‑Khosrow' / 'the beautiful Khosrow') with زیب acting as a descriptive element before the name. چو is the simile conjunction 'like/as', and خرم بهار is the comparison target 'the joyful/lush spring' (adjective خرم modifying بهار).
segments.s651-g1338: بهشتی is an adjective meaning 'heavenly' or 'paradisal'. پر از is the common prepositional phrase 'full of' (پر followed by از + noun). رنگ و بوی و نگار is a coordinate list: 'color, scent, and ornament/beauty' — و joins the nouns.
segments.s651-g1339: اسیران is the plural subject 'prisoners'. کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which/from whom' linking to شهرها ('from those cities'). بسته بود is the past-state 'were bound/tied' (بسته participial/adjectival, بود = past copula). Word order places the relative phrase after the head noun, a common Persian construction.
segments.s651-g1340: ببند گران here functions as a noun phrase 'heavy bonds/fetters' (ببند is a poetic/variant form related to بند 'fetter', modified by گران 'heavy'). دست و پا is a coordinated noun phrase 'hands and feet', and خسته بود is the past-state predicate 'were weary' (خسته = exhausted; بود = past copula). The whole line describes their condition: heavy fetters on tired hands and feet.
segments.s651-g1341: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'so that / in order that' (here 'that' in the sense of directing an action). بند is the object 'the fetters/bonds', and برداشتند is past 'they removed/took away' (برداشتن, third-person plural). The construction = 'He ordered so that they removed the bonds.'
segments.s651-g1342: بدان is composed of the prefix ب- + دان (here دان acts like 'to consider/regard'), so the phrase reads 'considering/regarding those cities...' (بدان modifies the following noun phrase). شهرها is 'those cities'. خوار is an adjective 'despised/lowly'. بگذاشتند is past 'they left/placed', so the clause means 'they left those cities despised' or 'they left those cities in disgrace.'
segments.s651-g1343: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech. گفت is the verb 'said' (here used with reported clause کاین ... جای) — none of the supplied noun senses fit the verbal use, so use -1. کاین = که + این 'that this' (introduces the content clause). نوبر is 'first fruit / new produce / choice gift' brought as an offering, and آورده is the perfect participle 'brought'. جای is 'place' and functions as the head of the clause 'this brought thing has made the place...'.
segments.s651-g1344: همش is 'all of it' (i.e. the whole place). گلشن و بوستان و سرای is a coordinate list 'garden and orchard and dwelling/house' describing the place's character; و links the items. سرای here means 'house/dwelling' in context (the supplied senses do not match that meaning), so its supplied senses are inapplicable.
segments.s651-g897: من (I) is the subject, او را is the direct object phrase ('him' + object marker را) placed before the verb in classical word order. نمایم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive form ('I show' / 'I reveal') used here to introduce what follows; که begins a subordinate clause ('that'). فرمان is the noun 'command/order', and کراست is an archaic/poetic past-form functioning as the finite verb of the subordinate clause meaning 'did / issued' (i.e. 'he issued the command').
segments.s651-g898: This is a coordinated series of verbal nouns/infinitives headed by a final finite verb (کراست in the previous segment). جهان جستن is 'to seek the world' (infinitive + object), followed by coordinating و 'and', then جنگ 'war' used with the infinitival sense, and پیمان 'pact/treaty' likewise; the final finite verb (کراست) applies to the whole series.
segments.s651-g899: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', introducing a cause. بیشی here means 'excess/surplus' (i.e. overabundance, arrogance) and وز (here) functions as a noun ('weight'/'burden' or part of a poetic collocation); گردن افراختن is a fixed verbal phrase 'to lift the neck', idiomatically 'to become proud/haughty' (گردن object + light verb افراختن 'to raise').
segments.s651-g900: This is a continuation: وزین is ambiguous here (it can be read as a fused 'و زین' = 'and from this' or as the adjective 'weighty'), followed by three coordinated infinitives/nouns that denote consequences: کشتن 'killing', غارت 'plunder', و تاختن 'and charging/raiding' (تاختن here has the martial sense 'to charge/raid' rather than mere 'to gallop'). The coordination lists results that follow the pride mentioned earlier.
segments.s651-g901: پشیمانی 'regret/remorse' is the noun subject/topic; آنگه 'then' is an adverbial temporal marker. خورد here is the verbal light-form 'befell/struck' (i.e. something happened to someone), so the clause reads literally 'regret then befell the drunken man' — مرد مست is the experiencer ('the drunk man').
segments.s651-g902: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'مرد مست' ('who...'). شب is an adverbial ('at night'), زیر آتش is a prepositional phrase 'under the fire', and کند is the (classical) present/present-subjunctive form 'he does / he puts' used to describe the man's action habitually or characteristically. هر دو دست = 'both hands' (هر دو preceding دست).
segments.s651-g903: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/said' (often used of rulers giving orders). تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause ('so that' / 'that'), and برکشیدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they lifted' (بر- prefix + کشیدن). نای is the object 'ney'/'pipes' (a wind instrument used here as a signal); the clause means 'he commanded that the pipes be raised/sounded.'
segments.s651-g904: سپاه is the subject 'the army/host'. اندر آمد is the past 'entered / came in' (اندر = 'into/among', آمد = 'came'). ز هر سو = 'from every side/direction' and ز جای = 'from their places/positions' — two successive ablative phrases giving source/direction; the word order places the motion verb before these adverbial phrases as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s651-g1353: This is a short nominal phrase: a proper name (بقالینیوس) followed by a locative/possessive phrase اندرون خان من = "the inner part/quarters of my khān." Word order is Name + location + possessor (خان + من), where من is the possessive 'my' attached postpositively to خان.
segments.s651-g1354: This clause states the situation: یکی 'someone' (indefinite subject) + تود 'a heap/mass' (object/thing placed) + بد 'bad/foul' (adjective before its noun) + پیش بالان من 'before my upper parts/rooms' (prepositional phrase with پیش = 'before/in front of' and بالان = 'upper parts/wings'). The verb is implied (there is/has been), typical of older Persian where placement is descriptive rather than a full finite verb phrase.
segments.s651-g1355: A full clause with a negative existential construction: ازین (از + این) 'of/from this' + زیب 'beauty/fair one' + خسرو (name) — then مرا سود نیست literally 'for me profit is not' meaning 'brings me no profit.' The sequence مرا سود نیست is an object/beneficiary (مرا) + noun سود + copula/negator نیست; note م here is segmented oddly in the list, but syntactically this is the standard older Persian order 'for me profit is not.'
segments.s651-g1356: A subordinate clause introduced by که meaning 'because/for': که بر پیش درگاه من تود نیست = 'for/because there is no tūd (heap/crowd) before my threshold.' Note the prepositional string بر پیش درگاه = 'on/in front of the threshold' and the negation is carried by نیست at the end.
segments.s651-g1357: بفرمود is a courtly/honorific verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded' here; تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that' and بر در شوربخت sets the location and target: بر در = 'at/on the door' and شوربخت = 'the unfortunate/unlucky (one).' Together this frames the clause that follows as what he commanded to be done at that place/person.
segments.s651-g1358: بکشتند here takes the sense 'they planted/cultivated' (older Persian allows بکشتند = either 'killed' or 'planted'); contextual object is درخت 'trees'. شاداب چندی = 'some fresh/healthy' (شاداب adjective before چندی 'several/some'), so the whole clause is 'they planted some fresh trees.' The verb is plural past (they).
segments.s651-g1359: An inverted/basic narrative order typical of classical Persian: یکی 'a certain' + مرد ترسا 'pious man' (noun + adjective) + گزین کرد 'chose' + شاه 'the king' (agent placed after the verb here for emphasis). So the sense is 'The king chose a certain pious man.'
segments.s651-g1360: بدو داد = 'gave to him' (بدو = 'to him' + داد = 'gave'). The list فرمان و گنج و کلاه enumerates what was given: فرمان 'a command/post/rank', گنج 'treasure', کلاه 'a hat' (all joined by و 'and'). This is a compact parataxis typical of epic diction.
segments.s651-g1393: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. مهراس is a proper name (the agent); داننده‌شان is داننده 'knower' with the enclitic pronoun -شان 'their', forming 'their knower'. پیش رو is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in front of / before', placed after the subject to mark that he approaches or stands before someone.
segments.s651-g1394: گوی here is a poetic/present-tense or narrative form meaning 'he speaks/said' (used as the verb of the clause). در introduces the indirect locution/scene ('in/at the presence of'); خرد پیر is a compound meaning 'wise old man' (خرد 'wisdom' modifying پیر 'old/elder'); و links the two titles to سالار نو, 'the new commander/master' (سالار = leader, نو = new). The whole phrase marks to whom or in whose counsel he spoke.
segments.s651-g1395: ز is the poetic variant of از 'from'. هر چیز = 'every thing / of every sort'. گنجی is the direct object 'a treasure' (indefinite). به پیش اندرون is a motion/goal phrase: به + پیش + اندرون together indicate 'into the inner rooms / into the presence inside' — i.e. he carried the treasures into the inner chamber.
segments.s651-g1396: شمارش 'the counting/numbering' is the noun subject of the following verbal phrase; گذر کرده is a compound: گذر (passage/passing) + کرده (perfect participle/auxiliary) yielding 'has passed over / has gone through' (i.e. reviewed). بر takes the objects of that review; چند و چون is a fixed pair meaning 'how many (quantity) and how (manner/types)'. Together: 'he had gone over/checked their number and manner.'
segments.s651-g1397: بسی here functions as an intensifier meaning 'many / much' (classical usage). لابه in this context is 'pleading/entreaty' (a poetic word for supplication), followed by the coordinating و 'and' and پند 'advice' and نیکو سخن 'good/fair speech' (نیکو modifying سخن). The series lists the kinds of speech he used.
segments.s651-g1398: پشیمان is an adjective 'regretful/repentant'. ز here is the poetic form of از and means 'because of / at' (introducing the cause). گفتارهای is گفتار 'utterances/sayings' in the plural with the possessive/relational suffix -های, and کهن 'ancient/old' modifies it: 'regretful because of ancient sayings.'
segments.s651-g1399: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent / dispatched'. با is the preposition 'with' introducing companions or accompaniment. باژ and ساو appear together after that preposition; in context they function as accompanying names or terms (the line lists them as the partners of the sending). گران immediately following qualifies what is sent: 'valuable / heavy' — an adjective that will attach to گروگان in the next segment.
segments.s651-g1400: گروگان is the direct object 'hostage(s) / pledges'. ز again is the poetic from/among marker meaning 'from'. خویشان 'relatives' is the first source; و links to کنداوران, the second source 'excavators' (people who dig, here likely miners or tunnelers). Together: 'hostages from among his relatives and the excavators.'
segments.s651-g1425: فرستادگان را is the direct object marked by the postposition را (definite/object marker). ستایش گرفت is a light‑verb construction: ستایش (praise, noun) + گرفت (past of گرفتن 'to take'); here گرفت functions like 'bestowed/took (praise)' → i.e. 'praised' (he gave praise to the envoys). Word order is Subject (implicit) – Object – Noun+Verb.
segments.s651-g1426: بران is an adverbial/relational particle here meaning 'on/for that' (referring back to the praised qualities). نیکویها (plural) names the 'good/excellent things' and is the target of the following phrase. فزایش گرفت again uses the noun فزایش 'increase' with گرفت to mean 'took/increased (the praise)' – i.e. 'he increased the praise regarding those virtues.'
segments.s651-g1427: بدو (to him) is the dative pronoun placed before the verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' (here reported speech). کای is a vocative particle 'O', followed by the epithet مرد روشن خرد — مرد 'man' modified by روشن خرد 'clear/enlightened-wisdom', i.e. 'man of clear understanding.'
segments.s651-g1428: نبرده is a negative perfect/past form (literally 'has not taken' / 'was not taken'); here it participates in an idiomatic negative-existential sense ('there is no one...'). کسی is used in the sense 'no one' (classical/poetic use). کو is an archaic relative particle (here 'whom'/'who') linking to the following predicate. خرد پرورد is a compound meaning 'wisdom‑nurtured' or 'reared by wisdom' (پرورد from پروریدن 'to nurture'). The whole clause reads 'there is no one whom wisdom has not reared.'
segments.s651-g1429: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). زر is the subject/target 'gold'. گردد is the present subjunctive/presentive of گشتن 'become' used in the protasis ('were to become'). همه modifies the following noun phrase خاک روم 'the whole soil of Rome' (همه precedes the noun phrase it quantifies).
segments.s651-g1430: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. سنگی‌تری is the comparative adjective ('more stony' / 'harder‑hearted') formed with the ˈ‑تریˈ comparative. زان is an ablative/relative 'than that/from that' introducing the standard of comparison. سرافزار بوم is a noun phrase 'that glorious, honor‑bringing homeland' (سرافزار modifies بوم). The clause states a comparison: 'you are more stone‑hearted than that glorious land.'
segments.s651-g1431: نهادند is past plural 'they placed/sent' (here used as 'they dispatched/sent'). The preposition بر before each place gives the destinations: بر روم ('to Rome'), بر باژ و ساو ('to Baz and Sav'). In this context باژ and ساو should be read as place‑names (thus the lexical sense 'cry' for باژ does not fit here).
segments.s651-g1432: پراگنده is an adverb/adjectival participle 'scattered' or 'distributed'. Classical Persian often places the numeral after the counted noun, so دینار ده reads as 'ten dinars.' چرم گاو is a noun phrase 'cowhide' (چرم 'leather/skin' + گاو 'cow'). Together the phrase lists the dispersed gifts: 'ten dinars and a cowhide.'
segments.s651-g153: بنا کشته اندر نبودی is a poetic noun phrase + predicate with an omitted ezāfe: بناِ کشته = “the monument/tomb of the slain.” اندر = “in/within.” نبودی is the past form (ne + budi) meaning “was not” (there was not). The final سخن is the subject of the following verb (it is not governed here but carries over into the next segment: “the word/rumor …”).
segments.s651-g154: پراگنده شد is a two-word predicate: پراگنده (adjective ‘scattered/widespread’) + شد (past ‘became’). In poetry the verb precedes or is separated from its nominal subject, here رسمهای کهن, so the whole means “the ancient customs became scattered/widespread.” کهن postmodifies رسمهای (old/ancient).
segments.s651-g155: In short poetic notation گزیت رز بارور شش درم, گزیت names a variety or proper name placed before رز without an ezāfe (common in poetry): “the Gazit rose.” بارور (‘fruitful’/‘in blossom’) modifies it; شش درم is the price/amount “six dirhams.” The copular verb (was/amounted to) is omitted but implied.
segments.s651-g156: به خرما ستان is a dative/locative phrase (به + خرماستان = “to/for the date-grove/estate”); note the poetic separation of خرما + ستان (compound). بر همین بد رقم is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning “at this same (unfavorable) rate/figure”: بر…رقم = “at the rate/figure,” with بد qualifying رقم as ‘poor/unfavourable.’ The clause states that the same poor rate applied to the date-lands.
segments.s651-g157: ز = ‘from’ (poetic form). The list uses و ‘and’ between items: زیتون (olive) و جوز (walnut) و ز هر میوه‌دار = ‘and from every fruit-bearing [tree]’; poetry often omits the ezāfe between noun and adjective (هر میوه‌دار = ‘every fruit-bearing one/tree’).
segments.s651-g158: که introduces a relative clause ‘which/that.’ در مهرگان = ‘at/in Mehregan’ (the festival/month). شاخ بودی is a past descriptive clause: شاخ (‘bough/branch’) + بودی (past ‘was/had’), i.e. ‘that had boughs.’ ببار here is a verb meaning ‘bear/produce’ (in context: they bore fruit); in poetry the verbal forms can be terse and the tense/aspect may carry an optative or habitual sense (they bore/produced fruit in that season).
segments.s651-g159: ز ده بن درمی رسیدی is a measure-expression: ز ده بن = ‘from ten (roots/trees/bushes)’ (poetic omission of connectors), درمی = ‘one dirham’ (indefinite monetary unit), رسیدی is 2nd-person past ‘you (would) obtain/arrive at’ used impersonally here to mean ‘one would get.’ به گنج marks the recipient/destination ‘to the treasury/treasure-vault.’ The clause states a tax or yield rate: from ten trees one dirham went to the treasury.
segments.s651-g160: نبوید جزین is a negative existential/poetic phrasing: نبوید (poetic negative form) + جزین ‘except this’ = ‘there was nothing but this.’ تا سر سال رنج is a temporal phrase ‘until the head/top of the year (i.e. through the year)’ qualified by رنج ‘of hardship/toil,’ so the whole means ‘nothing else existed but this until the year’s period of hardship.’ Note that نبوید is an archaic/poetic negative form and functions like ‘there was not.’
segments.s651-g1201: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning “he said/ordered” placed initially as a narrative formula. پس is a simple adverb “then.” تا here introduces the condition/clause meaning “so long as / provided that,” and نکشتند is the negated past plural verb (ن + کشتند) “they did not kill,” forming the conditional content of تا. نیز is an adding particle “also,” linking this clause to the following action.
segments.s651-g1202: برای + شان = برایشان is the preposition + possessive clitic “for them.” ببخشود is an older/classical past form meaning “he granted/forgave” (here ‘granted’ in the sense of giving favors). بسیار is a quantifier “many” modifying the noun چیز “things,” which follows in typical Persian noun phrase order.
segments.s651-g1203: وزان (poetic contraction of از آن) begins the phrase “from that,” followed by جایگه “place.” لشکر is the noun “army/host,” and اندر + کشید forms the verbal phrase where اندر is a locative particle “in/into” and کشید is the past verb “drew/moved” (verb at or near the end, as is common in Persian). The phrase means “from that place he drew in his army.”
segments.s651-g1204: از introduces a prepositional phrase “from/out of,” آرایش here means “array/arrangement” and روم refers to the Romans/Byzantium. برتر is a comparative/adverbial “superior/above,” modifying کشید (the past verb again) to indicate the army was advanced or drawn out in superiority relative to the Roman array.
segments.s651-g1205: نوندی is a proper name/rare term (here functioning as the person’s name). ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from” or “by,” گفتار “speech/words” is the source, and کارآگهان (an adjectival/participial form) means “made aware/informed.” Together this cluster reads “Nawandī, informed by the words...” with the participial phrase following the name.
segments.s651-g1206: بیامد is the simple past “came.” به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase “to near/near to” and شاه جهان is a kenning/title “the king of the world” (a poetic way to say the king). Word order is normal: verb then directional prepositional phrase.
segments.s651-g1207: که here introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause “that / for,” and قیصر is the subject “Caesar.” سپاهی (troops/army) is the object, and فرستاد is the past verb “sent.” پیش following the verb means “ahead / before” (i.e., sent ahead), forming the verb phrase “sent troops ahead.”
segments.s651-g1208: ازان is an archaic contraction of از آن / از آن‌ها “from/of those,” introducing the source group. نامداران are the famed/renowned men (here a plural substantive), و is the conjunction “and,” and گردان خویش = “his own troop/unit” with خوی + ش the older split form of the possessive clitic (خوی = kin/own, ش = his/her). The whole phrase specifies the troops’ composition: from those famed men and his own unit.
segments.s651-g545: دل شاه هر کشوری is a genitive sequence meaning 'the heart (دل) of the king (شاه) of every country (هر کشوری)'. خیره گشت is a past verbal phrase where خیره is a predicative adjective 'astonished' and گشت is the past of شدن 'became' — so literally 'became astonished'. Word order (possessive phrases before predicate) is typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s651-g546: ز introduces the cause here ('because of'); نوشین‌روان is a compound adjective/noun 'sweet‑souled'. رایشان is functioning as 'their counsel/opinion' (the provided lemma/senses seem to mismatch the actual word form). تیره گشت repeats the copular pattern above: تیره 'dark/ominous' + گشت 'became'.
segments.s651-g547: فرستاده آمد is subject + past verb 'a messenger/envoys came'. The prepositional phrases ز هند و ز چین use the poetic ablative ز 'from' twice for rhythm: 'from India and from China'. Word order is standard: verb follows subject and is followed by adverbial/ablative phrases.
segments.s651-g548: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; شاه را is the definite object marked by را ('the king' as direct object). خواندند آفرین is a compound verbal phrase: خواندند (here 'they chanted/recited/sang') and آفرین is the acclamation they uttered. The verb is plural to agree with 'همه'.
segments.s651-g549: ندیدند is past negative 'they did not see'. با خویشتن ('with themselves') is an emphatic adjunct meaning 'with their own selves' or 'with their own eyes'; تاو او places the noun 'radiance' (تاو) followed by the pronoun او 'his' — a poetic order for possession. The whole segment means 'they themselves did not see his radiance'.
segments.s651-g550: سبک شد is 'became light' (سبک here = light in feeling/weight) with an implicit subject carried from context. به دل is a locative/experiential phrase 'in/into the heart' (به + دل). باژ ('a cry' / noise) followed by با + ساو او is an adverbial accompaniment: 'with the cry connected to Sāv (ساو او)'. The clause therefore describes an internal change of feeling caused/triggered by that cry; the personal pronoun is built into ساو او ('Sāv's' or 'his Sāv').
segments.s651-g551: همه کهتری را is the object phrase 'all the lesser/lowly ones' with را marking the definite object. بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they ornamented/arrayed/adorned' (bi‑arastan), so the clause means 'they adorned all the lesser ones.'
segments.s651-g552: بسی here functions as an intensifier 'much/many' (poetic use). بدره (literally 'full moon') is used metaphorically for beautiful persons; و برده‌ها 'and slaves' is conjoined. خواستند is past plural 'they wanted/asked for/sought'. Together: 'they sought many beautiful ones and slaves.'
segments.s651-g1409: This is a speech-introduction phrase. به کسری (beh Kasrī) is a prepositional phrase 'to Khosrow' placed before the verb; چنین گفت (chenīn goft) literally 'said thus' (چنین modifies گفت). کای شهریار is a vocative: کای is an archaic vocative particle 'O' and شهریار 'sovereign/king' addresses the addressee directly.
segments.s651-g1410: This is the content of the admonition. جهان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the world' as the thing being spoken about. بدین ارجمندی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with such esteem' (بدین = 'in this way/so', ارجمندی = 'dignity/preciousness'). مدار here is a poetic/archaic imperative/prohibitive form meaning 'do not consider/regard' (the prohibitive sense is carried by this finite verb form in the context of direct address).
segments.s651-g1411: برومی تو اکنون has an inverted, poetic word order: the verbal form برومی ('go' / exhortative) appears before the subject تو 'you', so it reads 'you go now' / 'go now'. و links this command to the following clause: ایران تهیست is a present/stative clause 'Iran is empty/void' with تهیست 'is empty' as the predicate.
segments.s651-g1412: همه مرز is a generalizing nominal phrase 'all the frontiers/borders'. بی‌ارز و بی‌فرهیست is a paired predicative description using the privative prefix بی‑ 'without': بی‌ارز 'without worth / worthless' and بی‌فرهیست 'without refinement/learning'. The clause comments on the condition of the country introduced in the previous clause.
segments.s651-g1413: هران گه که is a temporal subordinating phrase 'at that time / whenever' (چو/هران گه که = 'when/whenever'). قیصر نباشد is a subordinate negative clause 'if/when Caesar is not (present)'; نباشد is a subjunctive/negative present form. بروم follows as a consequence/action 'I go' (بروم is the 1st‑person-subjunctive/optative form used narratively), so the whole clause sets the timing: 'whenever Caesar is not here and I depart...'.
segments.s651-g1414: This clause states the result in inverted order. نسنجد is a poetic/archaic predicate meaning roughly 'befit' or 'measure up to' (here used negatively: 'will not suit / will not be fitting'). به یک پشه is a dative/measure phrase 'even to a single gnat' (meaning 'not fit even a tiny creature'). این مرز و بوم 'this border and land' is the affected domain. Word order (verb first, then dative, then object) is typical of classical poetic diction.
segments.s651-g1415: همه سودمندی ز مردم بود is a simple past/state sentence: همه سودمندی 'all benefit/prosperity' is the subject, ز مردم 'from the people' indicates source, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole means 'all the good came from the people' or 'all usefulness was from the people.'
segments.s651-g1416: چو او گم شود is a temporal/subordinate clause 'when he became lost / when he was gone' (چو = 'when', شود = 'becomes/is' used narratively). مردمی گم بود follows as the main clause with inverted order: مردمی (of the people/the people's) + گم 'lost' + بود 'was' — 'the people's prosperity/standing was lost.' The repetition of گم (lost) emphasizes the collapse after his disappearance.
segments.s651-g1105: که‌گر is an archaic conditional 'if'. جز به ... links to the whole list that follows and means 'except by/without', so داد و به مهر و خرد are the instruments/qualities required ('by justice and by affection and by wisdom'). The repeated به is a poetic prepositional repetition ('by … and by …').
segments.s651-g1106: کسی is an indefinite subject 'anyone'. سوی is the direction preposition 'toward'. خاک سیه is the noun + adjective 'dark earth/soil' (ironically of the grave or humble dust). بنگرد is the classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of 'to look/turn one's gaze' (بـ-نگرد), here rendered as 'looks' in the conditional.
segments.s651-g1107: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'upon/thereon' referring back; تیره qualifies خاک as 'dark', and خاکش ('his/its dust') is possessive, here 'his dust/earth'. بریزند is a classical 3rd-person plural (present/subjunctive or narrative future) 'they pour/scatter', and خون is the (definite) object 'blood' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s651-g1108: چو is temporal 'when'. آید is the 3rd-person present/ subjunctive 'comes' (here meaning 'occurs' or 'goes forth'). ز is the poetic genitive/from ('from/by virtue of'). فرمان یزدان is 'the command/decree of God', and برون 'out' completes the motion 'comes forth'.
segments.s651-g1109: به بانگ منادی is an adverbial phrase 'at/by the cry of the herald' (به + noun). نشد is past negative of 'become' ('did not become'), شاه is the subject 'the king', and رام is a predicative adjective 'tame/obedient' — together 'the king did not become tame at the herald's cry.'
segments.s651-g1110: به‌روز سپید 'by/at the bright day' (به‌روز = 'by day' or 'in the daytime', سپید = 'bright/white') and شب تیره‌فام 'and the night of dark hue' — a paired temporal/adjectival phrase describing 'neither by day nor by night'. The adjective tacked after the noun is normal in Persian poetic order.
segments.s651-g1111: همی is a habitual/continuous adverb 'constantly' or 'used to'. گرد لشکر is a phrase where گرد functions with لشکر to indicate 'about/around the host' or the act of turning/leading the host; بگشتی is 2nd-person past-habitual 'you turned/led' (بـ-گشتی), and به راه 'onto the road' uses به + spatial noun 'راه' ('towards/into the road').
segments.s651-g1112: همی‌داشتی is another habitual past form 'you used to keep/you would have', followed by the coordinated object نیک و بد 'the good and the bad' marked by را (definite/object marker). نگاه here is a noun 'watch/gaze/keeping watch' so the clause means 'you would keep watch over good and bad.'
segments.s651-g569: تو گفتی is a 2nd-person past (تو + گفتی) meaning “you said”; what follows is reported content. بکان is an archaic/uncertain item here (possibly a place-name or an old compound) and stands before اندرون; اندرون زر is an internal prepositional/noun phrase (“within/on the inside, gold”), and نماند is the past negative verb ‘remained/not left’ placed at the end (classical SOV order: subject/phrase + object + verb).
segments.s651-g570: همان is anaphoric/adverbial here (“likewise”/“the same”); در introduces the following item(s) (در خوشاب و گوهر = “in/among sweet drink and jewels”), خوشاب is a poetic noun for pleasant drink/wine, و links گوهر (jewel) as a second coordinated item, and نماند again is the final past-negative verb (‘there was none left’). The whole clause repeats the predicate pattern of the previous clause.
segments.s651-g571: تن آسان is a compact description: تن (‘body/person’) modified by آسان (‘lightly/gently’), functioning adverbially to describe manner. بسوی خراسان is a prepositional phrase ‘toward Khorasan’ placed before the final verb; کشید is the past tense of کشیدن (‘drew/moved/advanced’) and comes at the end in classical SOV order. The verb here describes setting out or turning toward the east.
segments.s651-g572: سپه را is a definite direct-object phrase (را marks the object: ‘the army’). به آیین ساسان is an adverbial phrase ‘in the custom/manner of Sasan’ (آیین = custom/practice; ساسان = Sasanian). کشید appears again as the final past-tense verb ‘he led/drew’ (classical usage: to marshal or move an army). The structure is object + adverbial + verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s651-g573: به هر بوم آباد is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘at/to every inhabited/prosperous land’; کو is a poetic relative/adverb meaning ‘which/where/that’ (introducing the clause), and بربگذشت is an archaic past verb ‘he passed by/passed over’ (compound form with the prefix بَر-). Word order places the locative phrase up front, then the relative particle, then the verb.
segments.s651-g574: سراپرده و خیمه‌ها is a coordinated noun phrase (‘the inner curtains/privy-chambers and the tents’). زد is the past verb ‘he struck/set up’ used idiomatically with خیمه to mean ‘pitched (tents)’, and به دشت is the directional/locative phrase ‘onto the plain’. Note the verb زد precedes the locative phrase here — a flexible order common in poetic narrative.
segments.s651-g575: چو is the conjunction ‘when’. برخاستی is a past/perfective verb meaning ‘rose/was raised’ (classical epic morphology places the verb directly after the conjunction); ناله کرنای is a genitive/attributive phrase ‘the cry/lament of the trumpet’ where ناله (lament/cry) is qualified by کرنای (of the horn/trumpet). Together they mean ‘when the trumpet’s cry rose’.
segments.s651-g576: منادیگری is a deverbal noun ‘heralding’ or ‘the act/office of a herald’; پیش کردی is a verbal phrase ‘you put/send forward’ (پیش as adverb ‘before/forward’, کردی = 2nd-person past ‘you did/you caused’). به پای is a prepositional phrase ‘on foot’ (manner). The clause reads literally ‘you set the heralding before (you) on foot’, i.e. you sent a herald forward on foot.
segments.s651-g473: چهل روز بودی literally places a period of time ('it was forty days'); بودی is the past copula used impersonally to state a duration. درم را places 'dirham' (here topicalized by را) as the thing affected, and درنگ (‘delay/hesitation’) names the action — word order is topical object (درم را) after the time expression and before the noun درنگ which describes the delay.
segments.s651-g474: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they departed/went away' and introduces the action that follows the delay. از شهر is the prepositional phrase 'from the city', با ساز جنگ is an instrument/means phrase 'with (the) war-instrument(s)/music' (با + noun), where ساز جنگ functions as a compound indicating martial music.
segments.s651-g475: ز دیوان چو is a temporal clause: ز (poetic 'from') + دیوان ('the bureau/royal office/treasury') and چو 'when' introduces the subordinate clause. دینار برداشتند is the main verb phrase of the clause: دینار 'dinar(s)' is the object and برداشتند is the past plural 'they took'.
segments.s651-g476: بدان here is the contracted locative/relational form (به + آن) meaning 'in/at that' — it links to خرمی 'merriment' that follows. روز is the noun 'day' and بگذاشتند is past plural 'they spent/they passed (the day)'; the verb بگذاشتند literally 'they placed' but in this idiom means 'they passed (time)'. Word order puts the locative (بدان) before the phrase describing how the day was spent.
segments.s651-g477: کنون 'now' sets the time; لاجرم is an adverb 'necessarily/inevitably' qualifying the statement. روی گیتی combines 'روی' (face/surface) with گیتی (the world) as a poetic expression 'the face of the world'. بمرد is a subjunctive/jussive third-person singular form meaning 'may (it) die' / 'it will perish' in this context; the verb follows the nominal phrase as a predicate.
segments.s651-g478: بیاراستم is a first-person singular past tense 'I adorned/I prepared' (بی- as an intensifying prefix + آراستن root). تا کی functions as 'until when' (تا = until, کی = when), introducing a question about duration, and آید نبرد is a nominal + verb clause 'the battle comes' (آید 'come' in present/subjunctive sense, نبرد 'battle' as subject/object in poetic order).
segments.s651-g479: مرا is the beneficiary/possessive marker 'for me' (here topicalizing what belongs to or concerns the speaker). ساز و لشکر is a coordinated noun phrase 'instruments (means) and army'. ز شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'from the kings' modifying ساز و لشکر, and پیش here means 'in front / at hand / on my side' — word order places the beneficiary first, then the possessed items and their source.
segments.s651-g480: فزونست is an impersonal/predicative form meaning 'is greater/exceeds' and serves as the main comment about the foregoing possessions. و هم 'and also' links the next pair of nouns, دولت و رای ('fortune/power and counsel/plan'), while بیش is the comparative adverb 'more' modifying دولت و رای. The clause therefore asserts an increase/advantage in both forces and in fortune/counsel.
segments.s651-g849: اگر introduces a counterfactual conditional ('if') and precedes the protasis. شاه is the subject ('the king'). برتخت is an adjectival prepositional compound (بر + تخت) meaning 'upon the throne' and follows the noun it qualifies in the older Persian word order. قیصر is the genitival possessor of تخت (Caesar's throne). بود is the past-tense/copular verb used here in a subjunctive/counterfactual sense ('were' / 'would be').
segments.s651-g850: سزد is an impersonal present/subjunctive-like verb meaning 'it befits' or 'ought' and heads the apodosis. کو here is an archaic relative/particle meaning roughly 'that' or 'who' (not well captured by the provided senses), introducing the phrase describing who should be present. سرافراز is an adjective 'lofty/ honored'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. مهتر is a noun/adjective 'foremost/chief' that pairs with سرافراز. بود repeats the copula in the subordinate clause and matches the counterfactual mood started by اگر.
segments.s651-g851: چه here functions as an indefinite/relativizing particle meaning 'whatever' or 'whatsoever' and introduces 'whatever order'. دستور is the noun 'order/command'. باشد is the subjunctive/presumptive form of the copula (here expressing 'may be' or 'there be'); its form is archaic/poetic rather than the modern indicative. گرانمایه is an honorific adjective 'noble/esteemed' modifying شاه, which is the head noun 'king'.
segments.s651-g852: نبیند is a negative present/subjunctive form of دیدن meaning 'he does not see' or 'he will not see'; here it predicates what the king 'will not behold'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among' marking source. ما is the pronoun 'us'. نیز means 'also' or 'even'. فریادخواه is a compound noun 'one who cries for help' or 'a petitioner' and is the object of نبیند — 'he will not see a petitioner among us.'
segments.s651-g853: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' (subject). دشتی is an adjectival/noun form meaning 'of the plain' or 'plain-dwelling', here qualifying the horsemen. چو is a comparative/temporal particle meaning 'when' or 'as/like'; in this line it introduces the circumstance ('when they meet/like the Roman riders'). رومی is the ethnic/territorial adjective 'Roman/Byzantine'. سوار repeats 'rider' and can be appositional to رومی to mean 'a Roman rider.'
segments.s651-g854: بیابند is a present/subjunctive form of یافتم/یافتن meaning 'they find' or 'they may find' and here functions with the sense 'when they meet/encounter' (resulting clause). جوشن is the noun 'jacket of mail/armor' and is the patient of the clause. نیاید is the negative form of آمدن used impersonally here to mean 'does not come' or idiomatically 'is of no use/does not avail.' به is the preposition 'for/to' in the idiom به کار آمدن ('to be of use'). کار is the noun 'use/function' completing that idiom: 'armor will not be of use.'
segments.s651-g855: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' marking cause. گفتار is the noun 'speech/words' and is the cause of the emotion. منذر here is the proper name Munḏir (not the common noun 'warner') and is the agent of the گفتار. برآشفت is a past-tense, intransitive verb meaning 'became angry/was agitated' (بر‑ prefix intensifies the verb). شاه is the subject 'the king' who experiences the anger; note that older Persian frequently places the verb before or after the subject depending on emphasis.
segments.s651-g856: که introduces a causal/explanatory subordinate clause here ('for/that'). قیصر is the subject 'Caesar' of the following verb. همی‌برفرازد is a prefixed present/future-like verb: همی- is an older continuous/licitive prefix ('keeps/continually') and برفرازد (بر + فرارزد/فراز + زدن) means 'raises/exalts' — together 'will exalt/raise.' کلاه is the direct object 'hat' — literally 'that Caesar will raise his hat' (an idiomatic image of boasting/pride).
segments.s651-g209: مرا داد فرمان و خود داورست is composed with an older word order: the object (مرا) appears before the verb داد ('gave') and the noun فرمان ('command') completes the idea — literally 'gave me command'. خود is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun 'himself' placed before the predicate داورست. داورست is a poetic/copulative form built on داور + ‑ست (like ‘is judge’/‘is arbiter’); the clause means 'and He himself is the judge'.
segments.s651-g210: ز هر برتری جاودان برترست uses ز (poetic form of از) to introduce a scope or source: 'from/above every superiority'. هر برتری 'every superiority' is modified by جاودان 'eternal'. برترست is a predicate with the old copula ‑ست meaning 'is superior' — literal: 'is superior above every eternal excellence.' The sentence uses poetic inversion and compactness typical of Shahnameh.
segments.s651-g211: به یزدان سزد is an impersonal/fixed phrase: به + یزدان ('before/for God') plus سزد ('it befits' or 'it is fitting'), so 'it is fitting before God'. ملک و مهتر یکیست coordinates two nouns 'kingdom/sovereignty and chief' and concludes with یکیست 'is one' (one and the same). The verb سزد is present/impersonal — 'it is fitting'.
segments.s651-g212: کسی را جز از بندگی کار نیست uses the existential construction with کسی را ... نیست ('there is no one / no person ...'), where را functions as a topical/emphatic marker rather than a simple definite object marker. جز از 'except by' introduces the limiting condition, بندگی 'servitude/devotion' governs the manner, and کار نیست 'there is no work/standing' asserts the lack of status except by that condition. Overall: 'No one has any place except by servitude.'
segments.s651-g213: ز مغز زمین تا به چرخ بلند is a range expression: ز ... تا ... ('from ... to ...'). مغز زمین literally 'the core/heart of the earth' (مغز here = 'core'), تا به introduces the other bound 'to the wheel/sphere (چرخ) high (بلند)' — a poetic image for the high sky. The construction marks the vertical extent from earth's core to the lofty heavens.
segments.s651-g214: ز افلاک تا تیره خاک نژند repeats the range pattern: ز افلاک ('from the firmaments/heavens') تا تیره خاک ('to the dark soil'). نژند is a predicative adjective meaning 'base/worthless' placed at the end; the copula is omitted (poetic inversion), so the clause asserts something about that whole extent (here translated as 'there is no baseness' or 'they are not base' depending on context).
segments.s651-g215: پی مور بر خویشتن برگواست is compact and partly obscure in this recension. پی + مور can mean 'following the ant' or 'the ant that follows'; بر خویشتن ('upon itself') is a reflexive phrase. برگواست is a corrupted/uncertain reading in the manuscript — its exact lexical reading is doubtful, so the line is best understood loosely as 'the ant (acts) upon/boasts of itself' or 'the ant goes about itself.'
segments.s651-g216: که ما بندگانیم و او پادشاست is a reporting/explanatory clause introduced by که ('that'/'for'), stating two nominal predicates: ما بندگانیم ('we are servants') uses the plural copula ‑یم, and او پادشاست ('he is king/sovereign') uses the poetic copula ‑ست. The structure explains the reason: 'for we are servants and He is the sovereign.'
segments.s651-g1177: وزان is a fused poetic prepositional phrase (و + زان) meaning 'and/from that (place)'. جایگاه is the location noun 'place/position'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. اندر is the older/prepositional form meaning 'in/within' (here marking movement relative to the place). کشید is the past tense verb (from کشیدن 'to draw/move'), giving the motion 'drew/moved' — the provided lemma senses do not include the normal sense 'to pull/move', so that sense is marked separately.
segments.s651-g1178: This clause gives what happened next. آمد پدید is a doubled verbal phrase (آمد 'came' + پدید 'appeared/manifested') functioning as a single predicate 'came into view/appeared'. بر is the preposition 'toward/on' governing the following noun; دیگر modifies the noun as 'another/other'. Several lexical items here are ambiguous in the supplied sense lists (see senses); the overall structure is: [subject phrase] + آمد پدید (predicate).
segments.s651-g1179: که introduces this explanatory/relative clause ('for/that'). در بند او is an idiom meaning 'in his custody/prison' with بند functioning as 'fetter/confinement' or 'custody'. گنج قیصر is a genitive noun phrase 'Caesar's treasure'. The final word بدی here behaves as an archaic/predicative element (effectively 'was/there was'); the senses supplied do not match this grammatical usage, so it is marked as -1.
segments.s651-g1180: نگهدار is a noun meaning 'guardian/keeper' and here names the person who holds custody. آن + دز = 'that Dez/that fort' (or less likely 'that thief' — the manuscript context favors a place/fortress reading). توانگر is an adjective 'wealthy/rich' modifying the keeper; the closing بدی again functions as an archaic predicative/auxiliary 'was' in the clause and does not match the provided lexical senses (marked -1). Word order places the noun 'keeper' before its modifiers in a compact, poetic sequence.
segments.s651-g1181: که again introduces an explanatory clause. آرایش روم is a genitive phrase 'the array/arraying of Rome' (i.e. the Roman/Byzantine deployment). بد modifies نام (بد نام = 'bad-named' = 'ill-reputed') so بد نام اوی = 'his bad name' or 'his bad reputation', with اوی the possessive 'his'. The sequence describes cause/effect: the Roman array resulted in his ill repute.
segments.s651-g1182: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کسری is the proper name 'Khosrow/Kisra'. برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. به فرجام اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to/for his end' (فرجام = end/outcome) with اوی = 'his'. The clause therefore states the origin and result: 'his (bad) end/fate came from Khosrow.'
segments.s651-g1183: This clause describes the king's immediate posture. بدan دز is a fused locative expression 'at/unto that Dez' built from the prepositional prefix ب (b-) + a demonstrative/fused element; the supplied segmentation breaks it into ب + دان + دز but the sense is locative 'at that Dez'. نگه کرد is a light-noun + verb construction where نگه 'watch/keeping' + کرد 'did' = 'kept watch'. بیدار شاه is 'the king awake/alert' (adjectival predicate following the subject).
segments.s651-g1184: هنوز is the adverb 'still/yet'. اندرو (poetic form of اندر + او/رو) functions as 'there/in it' (i.e. at that place). نارسیده is a past-participial adjective 'not-yet-arrived' modifying سپاه 'the army'. Word order is inverted (adjective phrase before noun) as is common in poetry: literally 'still there not-yet-arrived the army' → 'the army had not yet arrived there.'
segments.s651-g641: نخوانند is a third‑person plural negative present/formal-subjunctive form (ن‑ + خوانند) meaning “they do not / will not say.” The verb appears first (verb‑initial, poetic order). بر ما is a prepositional phrase “upon/us,” کسی is the (indefinite) subject which, under the negation, functions as “no one,” and آفرین is the object/complement meaning the exclamation “bravo.”
segments.s651-g642: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” ویران is a predicate adjective “ruined/desolate,” and بود is the past of “to be” (was). بوم ایران‌زمین is a genitival noun phrase where بوم “homeland” is modified by ایران‌زمین “land of Iran.” Word order is standard for a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو.
segments.s651-g643: به دستور is an adverbial phrase “by/under the order,” where به here marks the manner/means. فرمود is the past tense/honorific verb “he commanded/said.” کز is the subordinating particle “that/from which” introducing the following indirect/interrogative clause (classical usage).
segments.s651-g644: This is a subordinate interrogative/optative clause. کجا is the interrogative “where,” نام is “name/reputation,” and باشد is the present/subjunctive third‑person “may be / should be” (here indicating where a name should exist). به آباد بوم is a locative phrase “in (the) prosperous/inhabited land,” with به marking direction/location in this classical context.
segments.s651-g645: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” هر کشوری is “each/every country” (کشوری is adjectival/noun form). مردم بیش literally “people more” means “many/more people” (بیش as quantifier follows the noun in classical style). بین is the imperative “look/seek!” (second person singular imperative of دیدن/بینش).
segments.s651-g646: که introduces a purpose/result clause “so that/that.” استاد بینی uses بینی as 2nd‑person present/subjunctive “you may see/find” (i.e. ‘if/when you find a master’). برین is the superlative/foremost form (classical formation with بر‑ + ‑ین) meaning “the best/foremost,” and برگزین is the imperative “choose/select!” The sequence gives a purpose and an instruction to pick the best when a master is found.
segments.s651-g647: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase “at once / in one stroke / suddenly.” از آب is the prepositional phrase “out of/from the water.” برکش here functions as a verbal/nominal element meaning something drawn up from the water (classical/poetic form of ‘to draw up’/object drawn up) followed by بلند “tall/high,” the adjective describing what was drawn up; the adjective follows the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s651-g648: برش here refers back to that object (its side/section/“it”); پهن is the adjective “broad/wide.” و connects the clauses. بالای او means “on its top/above it,” and کمند is “lasso/snare.” (The verb ‘ده’ = “put/place/give” that actually occurs in the line is an archaic imperative meaning “place,” but that specific token was not listed among the segment’s word entries.)
segments.s651-g417: بدو گفت is a compact expression meaning "he said to him"; بدو is the prepositional/benefactive pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb, and گفت is the past tense 'said' (poetic/narrative past). کای is the vocative particle 'O' used with titles, so کای شهریار بزرگ is the address "O great king."
segments.s651-g418: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. امروز من بنده گشتم is a past conditional clause: امروز 'today', من 'I', بنده 'a servant' and گشتم is the past of 'become' (I became/have become). سترگ here is an attributive adjective after the clause (poetic word order); its exact nuance is archaic/poetic and functions as an attribute modifying the state just asserted.
segments.s651-g419: همه در دلم راستی بود is literal word order: همه 'all' + در 'in' + دلم 'my heart' (دل + enclitic م) + راستی 'uprightness/truth' + بود 'was' (past). The clause states a past fact about the speaker's inner state; داد immediately follows in coordination as a noun 'justice'.
segments.s651-g420: درشتی نگیرد ز من شاه یاد is an optative/negative wish construction: درشتی 'roughness/rudeness' is the object, نگیرد is a negative present-subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may (he) not take' or 'let him not take', ز من 'from me', شاه 'the king' as agent/experiencer, and یاد 'remembrance/notice' (here 'take to heart' or 'notice'). Word order is compactly poetic: object + verb + source + experiencer + noun.
segments.s651-g421: درشتی نمایم چو باشم درست: درشتی 'harshness' is the direct object/topic, and نمایم is a first-person present/subjunctive form of 'to show/appear' ('I may show / I appear'). چو here means 'when', and باشم is the subjunctive/present form 'I am' (classical/subjunctive usage after چو); درست is a predicate/adverb meaning 'right/upright/just'. The clause says 'I may appear harsh when I am in the right.'
segments.s651-g422: انوشه کسی کو درشتی نجست is compressed classical word order. انوشه is a proper name (Anoushe/Anush), کسی کو functions like 'there was no one who...' (کسی ... کو is an older relative/indefinite pattern), درشتی 'harshness' is the object, and نجست is past of جستن used here in the sense 'sought' or 'looked for'. The clause expresses that Anoushe (or in his time) there was no one who sought/embraced harshness.
segments.s651-g423: بدو گفت again 'he said to him' (بدو 'to him' + گفت 'said'). شاه‌ای هشیوار مرد is a string of vocatives/adjectives: شاه‌ای 'O king', هشیوار 'alert/watchful', مرد 'man' (addressing him as a person). The sequence is vocative and descriptive — poetic direct address.
segments.s651-g424: تو هرگز ز راه درستی مگرد is an admonition: تو 'you', هرگز 'never', ز راه 'from the path/way', درستی 'of righteousness/honesty' (genitive), and مگرد is an imperative/negative jussive meaning 'do not turn aside / do not depart' (classical form). The whole means 'You must never leave the way of righteousness.'
segments.s651-g761: چو آمد به نزدیک آن مرز و کوه is a temporal clause: چو (archaic conjunction) = 'when', آمد = past tense 'he came'. به نزدیک = 'to/near', here a prepositional phrase marking approach; آن مرز و کوه is the goal 'that border and mountain'. Word order is typical older Persian: temporal word + verb + prepositional phrase + noun phrase.
segments.s651-g762: بگردید گرد اندرش با گروه: بگردید is an older/perhaps poetic perfective/intransitive form meaning 'went around' / 'circled' (verb applies to the subject understood from context). گرد here is an adverb/particle 'around', اندرش = اندر + ش ('in/within' + third‑person enclitic 'his/it') = 'around it', and با گروه = 'with (his) company/troop'. The verb precedes the adverbial phrase in the poetic word order.
segments.s651-g763: برآنگونه گرد اندر آمد سپاه: برآنگونه = 'in that manner / thus', گرد = 'around' (adverbial), اندر آمد = 'came in' (compound preposition + past verb), سپاه is the subject 'the army'. The clause states manner first, then movement, then the subject — common in epic diction.
segments.s651-g764: که بستند ز انبوه بر باد راه: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/that'. بستند is past plural 'they closed/blocked'. ز انبوه functions as 'because of/from (their) multitude' (ز = 'from/because of', انبوه = 'multitude'). بر باد راه is a terse poetic word order for 'راه بر باد' — 'the road' qualified by باد ('wind') used literally; the whole line means they blocked the road by sheer numbers. This is an archaic, compressed phrasing rather than a literal wind action.
segments.s651-g765: همه دامن کوه تا روی شخ: همه 'all/entire' quantifies the following noun. دامن کوه = 'the skirt/foothills of the mountain' (compound noun). تا = 'up to / as far as' links to روی شخ, where روی = 'face/surface' and شخ is a poetic/obscure word for a crest/face (here used as a topographic poetic element). The phrase sets the spatial extent.
segments.s651-g766: سپه بود برسان مور و ملخ: سپه (subject) + بود (copula, past 'was'). برسان here functions as a comparative/extent marker in classical style ('amounting to / as many as'), introducing the simile that follows. مور و ملخ = 'ants and locusts' is the simile comparing the host's multitude to swarms. The clause is a simple copular simile in epic diction.
segments.s651-g767: منادیگری گرد لشکر بگشت: منادیگری is a deverbal noun 'heralding / the herald's call'. گرد لشکر = 'around the army'. بگشت is an archaic past verb 'went/turned about' (here 'went round'); the verb follows the noun phrase in classical/poetic word order. Altogether: 'the heralding went round the host.'
segments.s651-g768: خروش آمد از غار وز کوه و دشت: خروش آمد is a common construction where a noun (خروش 'cry/roar') + آمد = 'a cry arose'. از غار = 'from the cave'. وز کوه و دشت is an older compressed coordination: وز = وَ + ز (and + from) = 'and from', so the phrase lists sources 'and from the mountain and the plain'. The sequence is a series of sources from which the roar came.
segments.s651-g1233: بشد is the classical past-form of شدن meaning “became/was”; here it comes first (verb‑initial narrative order). خسته از جنگ is a predicate phrase “weary from/because of battle” (از = from). The proper name فرفوریوس follows as the subject (postverbal subject), so the whole segment reads “Farforius became/weary from battle.”
segments.s651-g1234: دریده is a past participle/resultative used as a verbal action here (“torn” / “he tore”), applied to درفش (the banner/standard). The coordinating و links a second noun phrase نگونسار کوس: نگونسار is an adjective “mournful/woeful” modifying کوس (“war‑drum”). The clause implies two actions (he rent the standard and (he) struck the doleful drum), with the second verb omitted by poetic ellipsis.
segments.s651-g1235: سواران ایران is a genitive construction “the horsemen of Iran” (i.e. Iranian horsemen). بسان is a classical simile preposition meaning “like/as,” and پلنگ is the simile’s head noun “leopard.” Word order is straightforward: [noun + specifier] + بسان + comparison noun.
segments.s651-g1236: به هامون = “onto/onto the plain” (به + place). کجا is the interrogative “where.” غرمش is غرم (wrath) + enclitic pronoun -ش “his/its,” so “his wrath.” آید is the narrative/presentive verb “comes/would come.” بچنگ (ب + چنگ) is an adverbial phrase “into the grasp/in the clutches,” so the rhetorical question asks where his wrath could take hold of them on the plain; syntax is interrogative with verb after subject phrase.
segments.s651-g1237: پس is the narrative temporal adverb “then.” رومیان is the subject “the Romans/Byzantines.” در همی‌تاختند is a compound verbal phrase: همی‑ (the old continuous narrative prefix) + تاختند (they charged/rushed), with در indicating mutual or intense contact — so “they charged down/they clashed.”
segments.s651-g1238: در و دشت is a coordinated locative/scene phrase “at the gates and on the plain” (در here = ‘gate/entrance’). ازیشان is از + ایشان “from/by them/them” and bپرداختند (past tense) is used in the sense “dealt with / fell upon / set about” (poetic/older usage). Together the clause means they attacked/dealt with the enemy in gate and plain; the verb takes the coordinated place phrase and the pronoun phrase as its complements.
segments.s651-g1239: چنان هم همی‌رفت = “thus/so they were going” with همی‑ indicating continuous/habitual past action; هم adds emphasis (“also/indeed”). با ساز جنگ is an instrumental phrase “with the tune/strain of war” (ساز = melody/tune here), so the whole means they advanced to the war music.
segments.s651-g1240: همه introduces a list “all (of them).” The sequence نیزه و گرز و خنجر lists weapons (spear, mace, dagger). به چنگ is an idiomatic phrase meaning “in hand / in their grip” (به + noun of grasp); چنگ literally “claw/griff” but here denotes hold/grip. The phrase thus describes everyone armed with spear, mace and dagger in hand.
segments.s651-g1225: همه جنگ را تنگ بسته میان is built as a noun phrase + participial predicate. همه modifies جنگ ('the whole/entire battle'). را is the definite/object/topic marker attached to جنگ. تنگ بسته is a compound participial/adjectival phrase (بسته, past participle of بستن, 'closed/tied', modified by تنگ 'tight/narrow') describing the state of the battle. میان is the preposition 'between', which introduces the following list of parties.
segments.s651-g1226: A simple coordinate list of the groups that fill the prepositional complement of میان. Each noun is a plural/collective: بزرگان (great ones/nobles), فرزانگان (the wise/sages), کیان (the kings/royal house). The conjunction و links them in a row; there is no extra inflection here because they are objects of میان.
segments.s651-g1227: به خون آب‌داده همه تیغ را is a nominal phrase: به خون آب‌داده is a participial prepositional modifier meaning 'watered/bathed in blood' (آب‌داده = past participle 'watered/soaked' with به marking the instrument/medium). همه تیغ را is 'every/all the swords' with را marking the definite/object noun phrase. Together they form the object clause of the following verb (بدان).
segments.s651-g1228: بدان is an imperative construction: ب‑ is the verbal prefix and دان is the root دانستن 'to know' used imperatively here ('know that ...').
