The clause that follows is the object of this imperative. تیغ برنده is a noun + adjective phrase 'that/this sharp sword' (برنده as adjective 'sharp'). مر میغ را is an archaic possessive construction: مر = 'me/my' and میغ = 'blade/edge', with را marking the definite object; thus the clause says 'know that that sharp blade is my blade' (in practice: 'know that that sharp sword is mine').
segments.s651-g1229: سپه را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker on 'army' (را can mark topic in poetic/classical usage). نبد is an archaic negative past form (نبود → 'was not' / 'did not exist'). بیشتر here means 'more' or 'longer'. زان is a compound 'from that'/'because of that' (زان = ز + آن). درنگ = 'pause/delay'. The phrase thus says essentially 'the army did not (remain) longer because of that delay.'
segments.s651-g1230: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('by/which' or 'that'). نخچیر is 'prey/booty'. گیرد is present (3rd sg.) of گرفتن 'to take/seize' (here a general/punctual present: 'seizes'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', بالا is 'above/from above', and پلنگ is the subject 'the leopard'. Notice the poetic inversion: the verb گیرد appears before ز بالا پلنگ (the doer), a common epic ordering.
segments.s651-g1231: به هر سو is an adverbial phrase 'to/at every side' ('in every direction'). ز رومی indicates the group 'of the Romans' (ز as poetic genitive/from). تلی کشته is a noun phrase 'a heap/mound of slain [men]' (تلی = pile/heap, کشته = slain). بود functions as the past auxiliary here (کشته بود = 'had been slain' / 'had slain' depending on voice), giving a past perfect sense.
segments.s651-g1232: دگر here means 'others'/'another' (i.e., other men). خسته 'tired, weary' describes those others. از جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'from the war/fighting'. برگشته بود is the compound past ('had returned'), where برگشته is the past participle and بود is the auxiliary, producing a past-perfective meaning.
segments.s651-g985: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. مهر here is a proper name (or deity) functioning as the subject. از بر نامه بنهاد is a compact classical phrasing: از بر (literally 'from the breast/possession') + بنهاد (an older past-tense form of نهاد, 'put/placed') means 'put the letter down/aside'. گفت is the following main verb 'he said' introducing reported speech.
segments.s651-g986: که introduces the content clause 'that/that which' (reported speech). با تو is a prepositional phrase 'with you' marking accompaniment. صلیب و مسیحست is a coordinated subject ('the Cross and (the) Christ') plus the archaic copula -ست attached to مسیح meaning 'is (Christ)'; جفت is the predicate noun 'a pair/paired'. Word order places the accompaniment before the noun phrase and the predicate follows in typical classical Persian.
segments.s651-g987: فرستاده is the subject 'the envoy'. با او is an accompaniment phrase 'with him'. نزد + هیچ دم is an idiomatic adverbial: نزدِ here 'in the presence of/at' and هیچ دم 'no moment/never' together meaning 'not even for a moment' or 'at no time'. The whole segment sets up that the envoy, despite being with him, never obtained a favorable instant.
segments.s651-g988: دژم دید literally places the adjective 'dژم' (sullen/grim) before the past verb دید 'he looked/was seen', a common poetic order meaning 'he looked grim'. پاسخ بیامد is a simple past 'an answer came'. The final دژم repeats the adjective to qualify that the reply itself was 'sullen' — i.e. the answer came in a grim tone.
segments.s651-g989: بیامد is the past-tense verb 'he came/arrived'. بر شهر ایران is the prepositional phrase 'to/upon the city of Iran' (بر + place). چو گرد is a simile 'like a swarm/whirl' (چو = 'like/as', گرد here indicating a mass/whirl or movement); together this colors his arrival as sudden or turbulent.
segments.s651-g990: سخنهای قیصر is the genitive phrase 'the words/speeches of Caesar' functioning as the object. همه is an emphasizing quantifier 'all'. یاد کرد is the compound verb 'to recall/relate' (یاد + کردن) in past tense; here it means 'he recalled/related all of Caesar's words.'
segments.s651-g991: چو is 'when'. برخواند is an older/perfective verb here meaning 'read aloud' or 'recited' (برخواندن). آن نامه را is the definite object 'that letter' with را marking the object, and شهریار is the subject 'the king'. The order (چو + verb + object + subject) is a classical/poetic inversion used for emphasis.
segments.s651-g992: برآشفت is a past-tense intransitive verb 'he became angry/was enraged'. با گردش روزگار is a prepositional phrase giving the cause or object of anger: با 'at/with' + گردش روزگار 'the turn/whirl of time' — an idiom meaning 'the turn of fate' or 'fortune's change'. Together they mean 'he was angered by the turn of fate.'
segments.s651-g249: به یزدان is an oath-like prepositional phrase: به here functions as a particle meaning “by” (an invocation, not literal motion) and یزدان = God. The following که introduces a relative clause describing God/what he did (که او داد ...). داد is the verb “gave” (past); فر is the substantive “farr/royal glory.” دیهیم is an older/poetic noun placed between داد and و فر as another thing given; و simply links that item to فر.
segments.s651-g250: که begins a new clause meaning “that,” introducing the speaker’s comment on the previous gift. من خود is emphatic “I myself.” میانش (میان + ـش) means “in/among it/them” (the -ش = “it/him/them”). ببرم is a present/subjunctive form (first person singular) of بردن meaning “I may take/carry” or “I should take”; it expresses the speaker’s hypothetical/optative action. به ار is a conditional phrase: به here is the usual preposition/particle, and ار = “if/supposing,” marking the conditional sense.
segments.s651-g251: برین is the fused form of بر + این = “concerning/for this.” نیز = “also/likewise.” بادافره is a poetic adjectival compound meaning “wind‑borne / fleeting”; it modifies کردگار, which is a name/title for God (the Creator). The whole chunk addresses or invokes God with a qualifying epithet.
segments.s651-g252: نباید is an impersonal modal negative (“it must not / should not be”). The clause introduced by که reports the unwanted result: چشم بد آید به کار literally “an evil eye comes upon the work.” چشم بد is the idiom “evil eye / harmful glance,” آید (subj./present) = “comes/falls,” and به کار = “to the affair/work.”
segments.s651-g253: همین is the demonstrative intensifier “this very.” نامه و رسم = “letter and custom.” بنهید is the verb from نهاد in an imperative/hortative form meaning “place/set (down), establish” addressed to the addressee. پیش here means “before” or “in front of” (i.e., keep it before you / keep it in view).
segments.s651-g254: مگردید is a negative imperative/hortative (“do not turn away / do not depart”). از این = “from this”; فرخ آیین خویش = “your fortunate custom/practice,” where فرخ is an adjective “fortunate,” آیین = “custom/rite,” and خویش + ش is the possessive “your/his/its.” The clause thus forbids abandoning the stated custom.
segments.s651-g255: به هر چار ماهی = a time expression “at every four months / every four months” (چار = four; ماهی here = monthly / month). یکی بهر ازین = “one for/from this” (یکی = one, بهر = for/toward, از این = of these). The phrase sets a periodic allocation or request.
segments.s651-g256: بخواهید is a 2nd-person (plural/formal) imperative/subjunctive form of خواستن meaning “ask/request/seek.” The prepositional phrases با داد و با آفرین qualify how this should be done: با داد = “with justice/fairness” (داد as abstract “justice”) and با آفرین = “with praise/acclamation” (آفرین here = praise/acclamation rather than the verb “to create”).
segments.s651-g1241: سپه را is the direct object (سپه 'army' + postpositive object marker را). بهامونی اندر is a locative phrase 'into/among Bahamuni' (اندر = 'in/into'). کشید is the past tense of کشیدن used here in the sense 'to draw/lead' (classical usage); the verb follows its object and locative phrase, as common in older Persian.
segments.s651-g1242: برآورده functions like a participial/adjectival form meaning 'that has risen/been brought forth' and modifies the following clause; دیگر is 'another/other'; آمد is the simple past 'came'; پدید is an intransitive verb 'to appear/come into view.' The sequence gives: 'having been raised/appeared, another came into view.'
segments.s651-g1243: دزی is the subject noun here (a personal/agent noun in the text); بود is the past copula 'was' used to state existence; با introduces accompaniment 'with', followed by a list (لشکر و بوق و کوس) 'an army and trumpets and drums.' The constituents are listed after با to indicate what accompanied him.
segments.s651-g1244: کجا here is a particle introducing the following clause ('where' / 'at which place' in narrative style). خواندندیش is a past plural verb + enclitic third-person object ('خواندند' they called/summoned + -یش 'him') meaning 'they called/summoned him' (classical use of خواندن = 'to call/name/summon'); قالینیوس is the name being attached as the object of that calling.
segments.s651-g1245: This is a compact comparative phrase: سر here = 'head/top/crest' and باره forms a compound notion with سر implying a prominent head/crest; برتر ز = 'higher/superior than'; پر عقاب = 'the eagle's feather/plume.' Word order is noun + qualifying phrase + comparative 'برتر ز' + genitival phrase.
segments.s651-g1246: یکی is an indefinite 'one'/'a certain one' as subject; کنده‌ای is a descriptive participial/adjectival form (here from کندن: 'plucked/off/torn' in older usage) that modifies the subject — the precise nuance is pictorial/poetic; گردش is 'a turning/circuit' and اندر پر آب is a locative phrase 'over/within the surface of the water' (اندر = 'in/over/in the midst of', پر = 'on/over' or 'full of' in compounds). Together: 'one, (so) plucked/taken, made a circuit over the water.'
segments.s651-g1247: یکی = 'one'/'another' introduces an item; شارستان is 'city/town' (the noun core); گردش اندر فراخ is a descriptive phrase: گردش 'circuit/extent' + اندر 'in' + فراخ 'wide/spacious' giving 'having a broad/wide circuit' — an adjectival locution describing the town's spread.
segments.s651-g1248: پر here is used as 'full (of)' or 'filled with' followed by a list of items joined by و 'and' (ایوان 'porticoes', پالیز 'orchards', میدان 'squares/arena', کاخ 'palaces'). This is a concise enumerative predicate: 'full of A and B and C and D.'
segments.s651-g441: چرا begins the question ('why'). باید is a modal auxiliary ('should/must') that anticipates an infinitival clause to follow. "این گنج و این روز رنج" is a coordinated noun phrase: two definite noun phrases joined by و ('and'), where این is the demonstrative 'this' placed before each noun; روز رنج functions as a genitival/compound idea 'the day's hardship' or 'this day's suffering'.
segments.s651-g442: روان بستن is the verbal infinitive phrase 'to bind/fasten the soul' (روان = soul/object comes before the verb, a normal poetic order), and this infinitive is governed by the earlier باید. اندر is an archaic/prepositional form meaning 'in/within' and introduces the locative phrase سرای سپنج. سرای here functions as 'house/inn/place' (a house-noun) and سپنج specifies it; note the edition's sense list lacks the common meaning 'house', so the lexical item is being used as a locative noun.
segments.s651-g443: چو is the conditional conjunction 'if'. ایدر is an archaic locative 'here'. نخواهی is the second-person negative/future-like form 'you do not want / you will not', setting up the conditional; it governs the following verb. همی‌آرمید is an imperfective/present-habitual-form with the enclitic prefix همی- 'continuously' meaning 'rest(s)'/ 'would rest' — together the clause reads 'if you will not rest here'.
segments.s651-g444: بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (here repeated for emphasis) followed by verbal stems to state obligations. چرید is a verbal past/stem form 'graze/browse/roam' used after بباید to mean 'you must graze/browse'. و links the two obligations. The second بباید repeats the modal and is followed by چمید, a verb-form whose listed senses don't fit the lexical use here (see senses field); syntactically, both verbs are governed by the modal 'بباید'.
segments.s651-g445: پراندیشه is a predicate adjective/compound adjective meaning 'full of thought / pensive' and is followed by the past-tense auxiliary بودم 'I was'. ز is the preposition 'because of/about' linking to the genitive phrase کار جهان ('the affairs/work of the world'), so the clause means 'I was pensive because of the world's affairs.'
segments.s651-g446: سخن را places 'speech/words' as a definite object (را marks definiteness/emphasis). همی‌داشتم is an archaic/periphrastic imperfective 'I kept/held' (همی- marks continuous/habitual aspect) and در نهان is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in secret' or 'hidden'; together: 'I kept my words in secret.'
segments.s651-g447: که تا here introduces a causal/temporal clause 'for/as long as'. تاج شاهی is a genitival/attributive compound 'the crown of kingship' (تاج + شاهی). م (part of the original مرا) is the clitic pronoun 'me' attached to را in the manuscript (the edition splits it) and together with را marks the object 'me'; دشمنست is the predicate 'is enemy/hostile'. Word order places the object (مرا) before the predicate, a common construction in classical Persian.
segments.s651-g448: همه گرد بر گرد is an emphatic, partly reduplicative locative phrase meaning 'all around / everywhere about'; گرد here means 'around/about' and بر is the prepositional element 'upon/around' reinforcing that meaning. آهرمنست is the predicate 'is Ahriman' (Ahriman, the evil spirit) concluding the sentence: 'all around is Ahriman.'
segments.s651-g289: شما را uses را as the definite/object marker attached to the pronoun 'you' (شما را = 'you' as object/topic). جهان is the logical subject here and بازجستن is the infinitive complement ('to seek'); in older/poetic Persian the past verb بداد (بَداد) follows the infinitive, so the clause literally reads 'you the world to-seek gave' = 'the world gave you to seek (the world)'. This is an archaic word order (object — subject — infinitive — verb) and بداد is the poetic/past form of دادن ('gave').
segments.s651-g290: نگه‌داشتن is the verbal noun / infinitive 'to keep; to preserve' and here functions as the purpose/result of the previous clause. ارج is the thing preserved ('honour'); مرد نژاد is a noun phrase 'a man of lineage' (نژاد = 'lineage'). Word order is noun after modifier (مرد نژاد = 'man of lineage').
segments.s651-g291: گرامی‌تر از جان is a comparative: adjective + -تر and از introduces the thing compared ('dearer than life'). بدخواه من is the noun phrase 'my ill-wisher' (بدخواه = 'one who wishes ill'); من follows the noun as a possessive/owner ('of me' = 'my'). The whole phrase sets up a subject that the following relative clause will modify.
segments.s651-g292: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'بدخواه من'. جوید is the archaic/poetic present ('seeks'); همی is an old continuous/emphatic particle ('still/always') that often appears with verbs in classical Persian and here indicates habitual action. کشور و گاه من are the direct objects: 'my realm and my time' (connected by و), with من again marking possession.
segments.s651-g293: سپهبد is the head noun 'spahbed' (a high military rank). که begins a relative clause 'who...'. مردم is the object 'the people' and فروشد here functions as the verb 'sells' (archaic/poetic present form). به زر is the prepositional phrase 'for gold' (price/consideration). Word order in the relative clause is typical poetic inversion but corresponds to 'who sells the people for gold'.
segments.s651-g294: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'must not / should not'. بدین is a contracted prepositional expression (به + این = 'to/into this') marking direction/location; in the provided tokenization it is split (ب + دین) — here it functions as 'to this'. بارگه 'court/palace' is the destination, and برگذر ('let pass / admit / allow through') is the verbal idea 'be allowed in' with an implicit subject; the whole clause means '(such a man) should not be permitted into this court.'
segments.s651-g295: کسی را is the definite/object-marked phrase 'someone' (کسی را = 'to someone' / 'someone (as object)'). کند is the present/simple verb 'gives/does' (here general statement: 'one grants' or 'one makes'); ارج این بارگاه = 'the honour of this court' (ارج modified by this bargeh). The clause thus says that honour in this court is given to someone.
segments.s651-g296: که introduces the relative clause describing the person who merits honour. با داد و مهرست should be read as 'with justice and with مهر است (love/affection)' — مهرست here is a poetic contraction of مهر است (مهر + است). با + nouns list the qualities: داد = 'justice', مهر(ـاست) = 'affection/kindness'. و با رسم و راه adds 'and with custom and conduct' (رسم = custom, راه = way/manner). The relative clause thus lists the moral and customary qualities required for honour.
segments.s651-g337: بیاراست is a past (perfective) verb meaning 'he/they adorned or arranged' and here introduces the clause; it precedes the noun phrase جایی فراخ و بلند (‘a wide and tall place’), a normal poetic inversion where the verb comes before its object. جایی فراخ و بلند is a descriptive noun phrase (noun + two adjectives joined by و), literally 'a place wide and high/lofty.'
segments.s651-g338: This clause states a comparison: سرش (سر + enclitic ش ‘his’) is the subject/possessor phrase 'his head'; برتر is a predicative/adjectival comparative 'higher/superior'; از introduces the comparison 'than'. تیغ کوه پرند is a poetic collocation 'the ridge/point (تیغ) of the mountain (کوه), with پرند adding a simile/epithet ('bird/winged one'), so the whole means 'his head higher than the mountain's ridge, like a bird.'
segments.s651-g339: بگسترد is a past transitive verb 'he/they spread' and takes فرشی ('a carpet/rug') as its object. برو شاهوار functions as an adjectival/epithet cluster modifying the carpet (poetic phrasing meaning 'regal/lofty'); word order (verb → object → modifiers) reflects poetic inversion and compression.
segments.s651-g340: نشستند is the past plural of نشستن 'they sat (down)'; the following relative construction هرکس که بود او به کار modifies who sat: هرکس که بود = 'whoever/each person who was' and او به کار is a short predicate 'he was at (the) work / of service' (literally 'he was to the work'), i.e. 'each person who had business/duty sat down.' Note the elliptical verb in the relative clause and the adjective-like use of به کار.
segments.s651-g341: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; دیوان بابک is a genitive/possessive group 'Babak's diwan / court'; برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is the nominal subject/complement 'a cry/uproar'—literal order 'From Babak's court arose a cry.'
segments.s651-g342: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they put/placed/caused' (here best rendered as 'they lifted/turned'); یک سر literally 'one head' is used idiomatically for 'their heads' (they raised their heads), برآواز 'toward the sound' (بر + آواز) and گوش 'ear/attention' complete the phrase: 'they raised (their) head(s) to the sound,' i.e. they turned to listen. The clause has an idiomatic, compressed poetic word order.
segments.s651-g343: که‌ای is a vocative/old poetic address ('O you...') calling the addressees; نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones'; جنگ آزمای is a compound epithet where جنگ ('battle/war') combines with آزمای (the combining form of 'test/try') to mean 'battle‑testing' or 'trial in war' — together 'renowned testers of battle,' i.e. celebrated warriors.
segments.s651-g344: سراسر is an adverb 'all through/entirely' or 'altogether'; به اسب اندر آرید پای is an archaic idiom meaning 'put your foot into the stirrup / mount onto the horse': به + اسب 'to/on horse', اندر (old preposition) 'into/on', آرید here is an imperative/subjunctive 'put / mount' (poetic 2nd‑person plural), and پای 'foot' names the thing put into the stirrup—so the whole is an imperative call 'mount up onto your horses.'
segments.s651-g321: This short clause supplies the source(s) and the greeting: ز یزدان = 'from God' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from' before the noun یزدان). وز ما is a contracted/poetic link meaning 'and from us' (وز here functions as a fused conjunction+preposition rather than a literal noun), and بدان کس is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto that person' where ب is the preposition 'to' and دان functions as a demonstrative 'that' (an archaic form). درود is the noun 'greeting/blessing' — the whole clause reads 'from God and from us, greeting to that person.'
segments.s651-g322: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'کس' (i.e. 'who/that'). از داد و مهرش is a genitive/partitive phrase 'made of / from justice and his love' (مهرش = مهر + -ش 'his'). بود is the past copula 'was' linking to the predicate تاروپود ('fabric, warp-and-weft, metaphor for the person's makeup'). The order places the copula after the genitive phrase in a poetic, nominal predicate construction: 'who was (in) fabric of justice and love.'
segments.s651-g323: This is the protasis of a conditional: اگر = 'if'. دادگر is an adjective 'just' or 'just-ruling'. باشدی is an archaic/poetic second-person form of the verb 'to be' — here giving the conditional/optative sense 'if you are / should you be' (it addresses the king: شهریار follows as a vocative/title 'O king').
segments.s651-g324: This is the apodosis: بماند is a subjunctive/optative or future-oriented third-person form 'may he/it remain' or 'he will remain.' به گیتی is locative 'in the world' (به + گیتی), بسی is an intensifier/adverb 'long/very' in poetic use, and پایدار is the predicate adjective 'lasting/steadfast.' Together they mean 'he will remain long/firm in the world.'
segments.s651-g325: که again introduces a relative clause 'whom/that'. جاوید is an adverb/adjective meaning 'eternal/forever' modifying the following predicate. هر کس is an indefinite subject 'everyone/anyone' and کنند is the plural verb 'they do/make' (used here generally for people). آفرین functions as 'praise/acclamation' (i.e. 'they utter acclamation'). The clause thus says 'whom everyone praises forever' or 'whom everyone will acclaim for eternity.'
segments.s651-g326: بران شاه is a demonstrative + noun: بران here points back to that person ('that king'); this is followed by the descriptive clause کآباد دارد زمین where کآباد ('flourishing') qualifies the object زمین ('the land'). دارد is the present/gnomic verb 'has/keeps' so کآباد دارد زمین literally 'he keeps/has the land flourishing.' Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'that king who has the land flourishing.'
segments.s651-g327: ز شاهان 'of kings' (ز = poetic 'of/from' + شاهان 'kings'). که introduces a relative clause 'who' and با تخت و افسر 'with throne and helmet/crown' is a comitative/instrumental phrase describing the kings. بدند is an archaic plural copula/verb form ('they were' / 'they had' in sense of possessing or being characterized by), so the clause reads 'of those kings who, with throne and crown...' (poetically describing their outward trappings).
segments.s651-g328: This continues the description: به گنج و به لشکر 'in/with treasure and in/with an army' (the repeated به marks the spheres in which they are strong), توانگر is an adjective 'wealthy/powerful' modifying the nominal phrases, and بدند again is the plural copula/verb. Taken together: 'they were wealthy in treasure and in troops.'
segments.s651-g1001: به آرام is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in calm' (به + noun). اندر نبودش درنگ is an old-fashioned word order: نبودش (نبود + ش enclitic) is the past negative 'there was not' or 'he had no', and درنگ is the noun 'pause/delay' that is negated — together 'there was no pause'. The enclitic ش refers back to the implied subject; the clause therefore states there was no pause in calm.
segments.s651-g1002: همی is a habitual/continuous marker ('continually/ever'), and جست is the past tense 'leapt/sprung'. The sequence همی ... جست gives a sense of repeated or continual action in the past ('kept leaping'). از پی is a compound preposition 'after/in pursuit of' and راستی is its object 'truth'; جنگ at the end is the subject ('the battle') — classical inversion where the verb precedes or is separated from its subject.
segments.s651-g1003: سپه is the subject 'the host/army'. برگرفت is a past transitive verb 'took up/raised' and is followed by the coordinating و 'and' which links a second verb phrase. بنه here is a noun ('a small heap / bundle' — here idiomatically 'camp' or 'encampment') and برنهاد is the past 'raised/placed', so together the two coordinated verbs describe successive actions by the host.
segments.s651-g1004: ز یزدان نیکی دهش is a noun phrase 'the gift/grant of goodness from God' (ز is the poetic 'from' marking source). کرد یاد is the verbal phrase 'made remembrance / remembered' (کرد = 'did/made', یاد = 'remembrance'). The whole clause thus means 'he remembered the gift of goodness from God.'
segments.s651-g1005: یکی گرد برشد is an intransitive clause 'a certain dust/cloud rose' (یکی = 'a/one', گرد = 'dust/cloud', برشد = 'rose'). که گفتی here introduces the following statement; in context گفتی functions as a speech-verb ('as you said' or 'that you said'), though the provided sense list lacks the verbal sense (see senses mapping). سپهر then names the topic 'the sky/heavens'.
segments.s651-g1006: به دریای قیر اندر اندود چهر is a descriptive prepositional phrase: به دریای قیر (into/a sea of pitch) and اندر اندود (coated in) modify چهر ('face/appearance'), so literally 'the face (of the sky) was coated in a sea of pitch.' اندر is the locative 'in/within' and اندود is a verbal/adjectival form 'coated/smeared'.
segments.s651-g1007: بپوشید is a past-form verb 'covered/clothed' (here 'it covered'). روی زمین را is the direct object 'the surface/face of the earth' (روی = 'surface/face', زمین = 'earth', را marks the definite object). به نعل is an instrumental/comparative phrase 'with/as a horseshoe' (نعل = 'horseshoe'), indicating the manner of covering. The implied subject (from previous clause) is the sky/air.
segments.s651-g1008: هوا is the possessor/topic 'the air/sky'. یک سر از پرنیان is a genitive phrase 'one edge/side of the silken robe' (یک = 'one', سر = 'end/side', از = 'of/from', پرنیان = 'silken cloth/robe'). گشت is the past copular/transformative 'became', and لعل is the complement 'ruby' — 'one edge of the silken robe (of the sky) became ruby.' Word order is poetic: possessor (هوا) appears before the predicate.
segments.s651-g121: وز here is a poetic/archaic form functioning like از (from); ارمینیه is the place-name ‘Armenia’; تا is the extent marker 'as far as'; در is the locative preposition 'in/at' placed before the place name اردبیل (Ardabil). The phrase literally gives the span ‘from Armenia as far as Ardabil’ with the preposition and extent marker framing the destination.
segments.s651-g122: بپیمود is the classical past (3rd sg.) of پیمودن ‘to traverse/cover’, used here as the main verb. بینادل is an uncertain/obscure toponym or textual reading (left untranslated as a proper name). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; بوم گیل is a noun phrase ‘the homeland/land (بوم) of Gīl’ (Gilan) where گیل names the people/place. Note the verb appears before its objects, a common ordering in classical Persian verse.
segments.s651-g123: سیوم is an archaic/poetic ordinal here ('the third' rather than the literal 'thirtieth'), followed by a list of regions: پارس (Pars/Persia), اهواز (Ahvaz), and مرز خزر (the Khazar/Caspian frontier). The conjunction و links the items in the enumerated place-list; the whole phrase names the third district/region.
segments.s651-g124: ز is the poetic/prepositional form (like از) meaning 'from'; خاور is 'the east'; ورا is an adverb meaning 'thither/there' or marking direction/extent; بود is the past copula 'was/lay'; تا is the extent marker 'as far as'; باختر is 'the west'. The clause states the territorial extent: 'it was from the east (to) the west.'
segments.s651-g125: چهارم is the ordinal 'the fourth' introducing the next region. عراق آمد literally 'Iraq came' — آمد serves as the predicate/verb in listing the region (a poetic way to say 'the fourth was Iraq'); و links the next phrase بوم روم 'the land of Rome' (بوم + روم forming a genitive or appositive pair).
segments.s651-g126: چنین 'such/thus' modifies the following nouns: پادشاهی 'kingship/kingdom' and آباد بوم 'a prosperous/inhabited land' (آباد as adjective). The construction piles descriptive nouns/adjectives after the region-name in the concise, elevated style of epic verse.
segments.s651-g127: وزین is a contracted/poetic form corresponding to از این 'from these'; مرزها is the plural 'frontiers/borders'. هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone who'; درویش بود is the predicate 'was a dervish' with بود as the past copula. The prepositional phrase sets location/scope and the remainder gives the character of people there.
segments.s651-g128: نیازش is 'his/her need' (نیاز + enclitic ش); به is the preposition 'to/for'; رنج تن خویش = 'the suffering (رنج) of his body' where تن is 'body' and خوی + ش together form خویش 'his own' (classical splitting of خویش into خوی + ش). بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause states an equivalence: 'his need was the bodily hardship.'
segments.s651-g1129: اگر introduces a conditional clause (“if”). جنگ جستی is an SOV sequence where جنگ (battle/war) is the object/topic and جستی is the 2nd‑person singular past tense of جستن used here in the sense ‘go/seek/rush’ (you sought or rushed into battle). به + جنگ + آمدی is a second short clause: به marks motion/direction ‘into/toward’, جنگ is the goal, and آمدی is 2sg past ‘you came’ — together ‘you came into battle’.
segments.s651-g1130: به خشم ... آمدی is an idiomatic sequence meaning ‘you came into (incurred) the anger of…’: به = ‘into/toward’, خشم = ‘anger’, and آمدی again is 2nd‑person past ‘you came’. دلاور نهنگ is a noun phrase in apposition/epithet: دلاور describes the warrior (valiant/hero) and نهنگ is literal ‘whale’ used metaphorically for a gigantic/terrible fighter — together ‘the valiant, whale‑like one’ who is the source of the anger.
segments.s651-g1131: به تاراج دادی is a causative/resultive phrase: به + تاراج (plunder/pillage) + دادی (2sg past of دادن ‘give’) — literally ‘you gave to pillage’, i.e. you let it be plundered or you put it to the sword. همه بوم و رست is a coordinated NP ‘the whole bôm and rast’ (بوم = homeland/soil; رست is a poetic noun here coordinated with بوم to indicate the land/its produce or settlements). The verb دادی governs that NP as the affected thing.
segments.s651-g1132: جهان را is the definite object (‘the world’ + object marker را). به داد و به شمشیر uses instrumental/agentive به ... to mean ‘by/with’ (here: by means of justice and by the sword); داد here is the noun ‘justice’ rather than the verb. جست is the past form of جستن used with the sense ‘leap/attack’ (he sprang upon/assailed). So the clause says he leapt upon/assailed the world by justice and by the sword.
segments.s651-g1133: به کردار is a prepositional idiom meaning ‘in the manner of / like’ (به + noun). خورشید is the noun being compared (the sun). بد رای شاه is a compound noun phrase: بد = ‘bad’, رای = ‘counsel/opinion’, together ‘bad‑counsel’, modifying شاه ‘the king’ — ‘the ill‑advising (ill‑advised) king’. Word order places the simile first (‘in the manner of the sun’) then names the subject (‘the ill‑advised king’).
segments.s651-g1134: که introduces a relative clause describing the sun (’which’/’who’). بر‑تر here is comparative/locative in poetic diction (lit. ‘higher’/‘above’) and pairs with خشکی ‘dryness/dry places’; بتابد is 3sg present/subjunctive from تافتن meaning ‘to shine’ (the sun’s light shines). به راه functions as a locative phrase ‘on/along the road/way’. Overall: ‘which (the sun) shines over the high places and dry land along the road.’
segments.s651-g1135: ندارد is 3sg present of داشتن with negation (‘does not have / does not possess’). The pattern ندارد ... دریغ is idiomatic: ‘does not have (any) reluctance/pity’ i.e. ‘does not begrudge’. ز کس = ‘from anyone’ (ز = poetic ‘from’), روشنایی = ‘light/brightness’; دریغ is the noun ‘reluctance/pailing’ here meaning ‘begrudging’. So: ‘he does not withhold light from anyone’ or ‘he begrudges no one light.’
segments.s651-g1136: چو = ‘when/as’ (temporal), followed by بگذارد (3sg subjunctive/present of گذارد/گذاشتن in this poetic line) used here in the sense of ‘passes/lets pass’; از marks origin ‘from’, چرخ گردنده = ‘the revolving wheel/sky’ (چرخ = sky/heaven; گردنده = turning/rotating), and میغ = ‘blade/edge’ (a poetic image, likely the sun’s ray like a blade). The clause describes the moment when a blade (a ray) passes from the revolving sky.
segments.s651-g1137: A noun phrase made of coordinated items. همش is a pronominal head meaning "all of it"; the list خاک و هم ریگ names the material (خاک = soil, هم before ریگ = "also/sand too"). هم‌رنگ و بوی is an adjectival/nominal description "same-coloured and scent(ed)". There is no finite verb here (verbless nominal clause), a common poetic device — the verb is implied and completed by the next segment.
segments.s651-g1138: A locative clause introduced by در "in". همش (again, "all of it") is the topic; در خوشاب = "in pleasant/sweet water" and هم آب جوی = "and (in) the stream's water" (هم repeats the "also"/"both" sense). The phrase functions as the continuation of the previous nominal description; no new finite verb is expressed.
segments.s651-g1139: فروغ و بلندی is a coordinated noun phrase "radiance and loftiness/exaltation." نبودش is the negated past of بودن with enclitic -ش ("he did not have/was not endowed with them"); ز کس = "from anyone/from any person" marks source. The clause therefore means he did not receive those qualities from anyone. Note: بلندی here is a noun 'height/exaltation' (the provided sense list lacks that sense, see senses).
segments.s651-g1140: Two predicates describing the person: دلفروز (an epithet "heart‑illuminating") و بخشنده ("generous"). او بود = "he was" (بود in the past). و بس literally "and enough/that is all" here functions as an emphatic closing: "and that sufficed" / "and that was all."
segments.s651-g1141: شهنشاه را mَایه این بود is a topicalized construction: شهنشاه را marks the emperor as topic/experiencer, مایه این = "the cause/source of this," and بود is the past copula "was." The clause states that the emperor was the source of (what has been described). The conjunction و connects the following nominal (فر) which continues the thought.
segments.s651-g1142: فر جهان را همی‌داشت is a verbal clause where فر (royal glory) is the subject/topic and جهان را is the definite object (را marks it). همی‌داشت is the older/habitual form (همی + داشت) meaning "kept/held" (imperfective habitual in classical style). در زیر پر is a locative idiom "under the feather/wing" = "under (its) protection."
segments.s651-g1143: ورا is a prepositional/benefactive marker "to/for him." جنگ و بخشش is a coordinated pair "war and largesse/giving." چو بازی = "like a play/game." بدی here is an archaic finite verb form (orthographically written بدی) — most likely an old past form corresponding to بودی/"(he) was" or an emphatic past — serving as the main verb: "for him war and giving were like a game." The printed lexical senses list for بدی only gives the noun sense "evil," which does not match the verbal use here (so see senses mapping).
segments.s651-g1144: ازیران is an adverbial meaning "from beneath/secretly" (here taking the sense "secretly/inwardly"); چنان = "so/such"; بی‌نیازی = "indifference/self-sufficiency" (here "indifference"). The final بدی is the same archaic verb form as above (verbal past), so the literal structure is "secretly he showed such indifference." Because the provided senses for بدی do not include this verbal form, its mapping is marked accordingly.
segments.s651-g929: که is a relative 'that/who' introducing the clause. بیرون شد is a two-word verbal phrase: بیرون (out/away) + شد (past of شدن, 'became'/'came'), so together 'came out' or 'came forth'. از راه گردان سپهر is a prepositional phrase 'from the road/path (راه) of the گردان سپهر', where گردان is an adjective 'revolving' modifying سپهر 'heavens/sky'. The word order (verb after adverbial) is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s651-g930: اگر introduces a conditional 'if' (here used more like 'whether' in a correlative sense). جنگ جوید uses جوید (present/subjunctive 3sg form of جستن/جوییدن) meaning 'seeks/engages in' and جنگ is 'war/battle' — 'if it seeks war'. وگر is an alternative conditional 'or if / but if', and داد و مهر are nouns 'justice and kindness' (i.e. dispensing justice and favour) forming the second alternative; the verb is understood from the first clause.
segments.s651-g931: تو‌گر is a contracted poetic if 'تو اگر' = 'if you'. قیصری روم را is a noun phrase 'the Caesar (of) Rome' with را marking the definite object/topic (classical word order allows the object here to be felt as the thing about which the command applies). مهتری is a noun 'preeminence/superiority' that will be negated by the following verb; the clause is conditional and links to the imperative that follows.
segments.s651-g932: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. بیش here means 'more / to a greater degree' and modifies the action; با تازیان is 'with / toward the Arabs', and داوری is a verbal noun 'judgment' (i.e. judging, adjudication). Together the clause issues the command: 'Do not (show) greater favour in judgement toward the Arabs.'
segments.s651-g933: وگر 'and if / but if' introduces another conditional. میش جویی uses میش 'ewe' as the object and جویی (form of جویدن/جوییدن) as 'seeking/trying to get' — in classical usage this verbal form carries the idea 'you/one seeks'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and چنگال گرگ is the noun phrase 'the wolf's claw/grip'.
segments.s651-g934: گمانی بود is a copular construction: گمانی 'a supposition/guess' + بود (past copula 'was' / 'would be'), then the predicates follow: کژ 'wrong/perverse' and رنجی بزرگ 'a great suffering' (رنجی is a noun 'suffering' modified by بزرگ 'great'). The whole clause evaluates the antecedent as 'a mistaken idea and a great hardship.'
segments.s651-g935: وگر 'and if' introduces the condition. سوی منذر 'toward Munḏir' (سوی = toward; منذر is a proper name here). فرستی is the verb 'you send' (second person form of فرستادن) and سپاه is the object 'an army/host' — 'if you send an army toward Munḏir.'
segments.s651-g936: نمانم is first-person negative of ماندن used here impersonally or as a threat 'there will not remain / I will leave none'. به تو 'for you' marks the beneficiary/possessor, and the following nouns لشکر و تاج و گاه ('army and crown and place/time') are the things that will not remain for you. The verb precedes the indirect object and the list of nouns, a classical Persian order.
segments.s651-g841: بپرسید is a past narrative form 'asked' (3rd person) used at the start; زُ (ز + ضميرِ او contracted) is the ablative 'from/of him'. شاه follows the verb as the subject ('the king asked him'). The second و links to شادی نمود: شادی is the object/phenomenon 'joy' and نمود is past 'showed/appeared' — together 'he showed joy' (i.e. joy manifested).
segments.s651-g842: ز دیدار او is an ablative phrase 'at the sight of him / from seeing him' (ز = 'from/of', دیدار = 'sight/meeting', او = 'him'). روشنایی is the noun 'brightness/light' and فزود is the past of فزودن 'increased' — literal: 'brightness increased (on account of) his being seen.'
segments.s651-g843: جهاندیده is an epithet 'world‑wise' preceding the name منذر (here a proper name). زبان برگشاد is an idiom 'opened (his) tongue' = 'began to speak'; برگشاد is the past 'opened' applied to 'زبان' (speech).
segments.s651-g844: ز روم و ز قیصر (printed here as ز روم وز قیصر) means 'of Rome and of the Caesar'; ز is the ablative 'of/from' and وز should be read as و + ز 'and of'. همی‌کرد is the habitual/continuous past 'was doing / used to do', and یاد here means 'remembrance/mention' — together 'he made mention of Rome and of the Caesar.'
segments.s651-g845: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' with بدو as the indirect-object phrase 'to him.' اگر ... تویی introduces a conditional 'if you are ...' where تویی is the literary 2nd‑person copula 'thou art'. شاه ایران is the predicate nominal 'king of Iran.'
segments.s651-g846: نگهدار 'guardian/preserver' is a predicate noun; پشت دلیران is a genitive phrase 'the support/back of the brave' (پشت used figuratively as 'support/protection'). تویی repeats the copula 'you are', giving an emphatic predicate: 'you are the guardian, the support of the brave.'
segments.s651-g847: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. رومیان is the subject 'the Romans', and شهریاری کنند uses the verb کنن(د) in a subjunctive/optative sense 'should do / would do' with شهریاری 'sovereignty' as the action — rhetorical: 'why should the Romans be sovereign?'
segments.s651-g848: به دشت gives direction 'to/into the plain'. سواران سواری کنند literally 'the horsemen do riding' — here 'سواران' = 'horsemen' and سواری = 'riding/mounted action', with کنند again marking the subjunctive/hypothetical 'would ride / should ride (forth)'. The whole is a parallel rhetorical question about sending cavalry into the plain.
segments.s651-g81: 'مکافات' (retribution) is the theme; 'باید' is a modal verb meaning 'must/ought to'. The sequence 'ب' + 'دان' here functions as an enclitic/prepositional link (= 'to/for that' in classical usage), followed by 'بد' ('evil') and the relative clause 'که کرد' ('that he/it did') where 'کرد' is simple past 'did'. The whole fragment thus says that punishment/recompense must come for the evil that was done.
segments.s651-g82: 'نباید' is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'one should not' or 'must not'. The verb phrase 'غم ... خورد' uses the idiom 'غم خوردن' (literally 'to eat sorrow') meaning 'to grieve' or 'to be afflicted by sorrow'; here 'خورد' is the past form of 'خوردن' used idiomatically. The clause therefore means 'one should not grieve because of a dishonourable man.'
segments.s651-g83: Word order: 'شما' (you) + 'دل' (heart) then a prepositional phrase 'به فرمان یزدان پاک' ('to/under the command of the pure God'). 'به' links 'دل' to 'فرمان' and 'یزدان پاک' is the divine authority described by the adjective 'پاک' (pure). The phrase instructs the addressee to set/keep their heart under God's command.
segments.s651-g84: 'بدارید' is the 2nd-person-plural imperative/subjunctive of داشتن 'to hold/keep' (here: 'keep/hold [your heart]'). 'وز' is an archaic form (functioning like 'از' = 'from/of') linking to 'ما' ('us'); 'مدارید' is a 2nd-person-plural present-subjunctive/imperative-form from a verb meaning 'to be anxious/heed' (here idiomatically 'be afraid/concerned'), and 'باک' means 'fear'. The clause as a whole: 'and (do) not fear us' (i.e., do not be afraid of us).
segments.s651-g85: 'که' introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/for he is'; 'اویست' is an archaic fused form equivalent to 'او است' ('he is'). 'بر' means 'over' or 'above', and the doubled 'پادشا پادشا' is an emphatic formula 'king of kings' (literally 'king, king'). The clause identifies the person just spoken of as supreme over other kings.
segments.s651-g86: A coordinated list of titles/appellations in apposition: 'جهاندار' (sovereign), 'پیروز' (victorious), and 'فرمانروا' (ruling lord). The conjunctions 'و' link the nouns; they function as predicates/appositives to the preceding 'او است'.
segments.s651-g87: فروزنده is an attributive/participial adjective 'radiant/illuminating' that modifies the coordinated nouns تاج و خورشید و ماه ('crown, sun, and moon'). In classical/poetic Persian such an adjective can head the phrase to emphasize the quality ('the radiant crown, sun and moon').
segments.s651-g88: نماینده here functions as an agentive/indicating word (a present-participial/agent sense) meaning 'that which shows/points (out)'. The preceding noun phrase is the logical subject that 'points'; ما را is the patient/object 'us' (را is the definite/object marker). سوی داد راه is a directional phrase 'toward the path of justice' with داد used as the noun 'justice' and راه 'path/way'. The construction thus means '...point/show us toward the way of justice.'
segments.s651-g1385: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' that introduces the clause. بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard' (verb-first order here, a common poetic inversion), with قیصر as the subject that follows it. دلش خیره گشت is a predicative phrase: دلش 'his heart' (possessive suffix ش = 'his') + خیره گشت 'became astonished' (خیره as adjective/participle, گشت = 'became'), giving 'his heart was stunned.'
segments.s651-g1386: ز here functions as the causal preposition 'because of/from' (poetic variant of از, here 'because of'). نوشین‌روان is an epithet 'sweet‑souled' modifying the following رای ('counsel/plan'); او after رای is the possessor 'his.' تیره گشت is a two‑word predicate 'became dark/bleak' (تیره 'dark/bleak', گشت 'became'), so the whole says 'because of the sweet‑souled one's counsel his counsel/purpose turned dark.'
segments.s651-g1387: گزین کرد is a compound verb: گزین (choose/select) + کرد (past auxiliary 'did'), yielding 'he chose.' زان is the prepositional/partitive 'from those/from that,' and فیلسوفان روم is the noun phrase 'philosophers of Rome' (روم postmodifies فیلسوفان).
segments.s651-g1388: سخن‌گوی is the noun 'spokesman/speaker.' با دانش is an adjectival phrase literally 'with knowledge' (= 'learned' or 'learned in scholarship'); و links the following epithet; پاک بوم is a two‑word epithet meaning 'pure‑born' or 'of a pure/native homeland' (پاک 'pure' + بوم 'homeland'). Together they modify سخن‌گوی: 'a learned and pure‑born spokesman.'
segments.s651-g1389: به جای آمد literally 'came into the place' and here means 'came forward' or 'appeared/took his place.' از موبدان is 'from among the mobeds' and شست is the past participle of شستن 'washed/cleansed,' used adjectivally before مرد 'man' to yield 'a man cleansed by the mobeds came forward.'
segments.s651-g1390: This clause expresses appointment: به کسری شدن is 'to the becoming Khosrow' (به + N + شدن = 'to become N'), نامزدشان is 'their candidate/nominee' (نامزد + possessive suffix شان = 'their'), and بکرد is the past verb 'he made/appointed.' Word order places the state ('to become Khosrow') before the object, a poetic/elliptical phrasing meaning 'he made their nominee to become Kisra.'
segments.s651-g1391: پیامی is an indefinite noun 'a message' (ـی suffix marks indefiniteness). فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent.' نزدیک شاه is a goal/locative phrase 'to the presence/near the king' (نزدیک here 'near/into the presence of'), indicating the recipient/place of the sending.
segments.s651-g1392: گرانمایگان (plural) is 'the illustrious/important ones' (nobles, dignitaries) and is the subject. برگرفتند is past plural 'they took up' or 'they lifted/took on,' and paired with راه ('road/way') the idiom برگرفتن راه means 'to set out on the road' — so 'the dignitaries set out.'
segments.s651-g393: نگه کرد is a compound past tense where نگه (lit. ‘gaze’) is the object/noun and کرد (past of کردن) functions as the light verb — together meaning ‘looked’. بابک is the subject placed after the verb phrase (V S order is common in older Persian narrative). پسند آمدش is an idiomatic predication: پسند آمد = ‘it was pleasing’, with the clitic -ش ‘to him’ (آمدش) referring back to Babak, so the whole segment reads ‘Babak looked and it pleased him / he liked it.’
segments.s651-g394: This clause is impersonal/agentless in surface order: شهنشاه را marks the source/agent/topic (‘from the shah’ or ‘the shah’s …’ with را adding emphasis), فرمند (a past-form verb meaning ‘commanded/said’) and آمدش ‘came to him’ (آمد + enclitic -ش = ‘reached him’). The natural reading is ‘the shah’s command (or word) came to him.’
segments.s651-g395: بدو گفت = بدو (‘to him’) + گفت (‘said’) — ‘he said to him’. شاها is a vocative ‘O king’. انوشه is a proper name. بدی here appears in the poem as an archaic 2nd‑person verb form (بدهی / ‘you give’) or imperative; the lexical senses list only the noun sense ‘evil’, so that verb sense is not present in the provided sense list.
segments.s651-g396: روان را is the direct object with را (‘the soul’ or ‘the traveller/soul’). به + فرهنگ توشه = ‘as a provision in the form of learning/culture’ (به here = ‘with/as’ ; فرهنگ = ‘learning/culture’; توشه = ‘provisions’). بدی again is the verb-form meaning ‘(you) give’ (imperative/subjunctive), so the literal order is ‘give the soul, as a provision, learning.’ The provided sense list does not include this verbal sense of بدی, so that sense is marked -1.
segments.s651-g397: بیاراستی is an adverbial/formal intensifier meaning ‘truly / properly / fittingly’. روی کشور is a possessive-like image ‘the face (appearance) of the country/realm’. بداد here is an older verbal or affective form ('he gave/put into effect') rather than the noun sense ‘oppression’ listed in the senses, so its verbal meaning in context is not covered by the provided sense list.
segments.s651-g398: ازین گونه = از + این (here written ین) + گونه = ‘from this kind / of this sort’. داد here functions as ‘deed/justice’ (داد can mean ‘justice’ or the act of giving) and is taken as the noun ‘justice/deed’ in this line. از تو داریم یاد = ‘from you we have (hold) memory’ — داریم یاد = ‘we remember’. Word order is normal: topic/phrase then comment.
segments.s651-g399: دلیری بد = noun + adjective: ‘bravery’ qualified by بد (‘bad/ill’) → ‘ill-advised bravery’ or ‘rash courage’. از بنده = ‘from/by a servant’ (benda = servant). این گفت و گوی = ‘this saying/speech’ (گفت و گوی here functions as ‘this utterance’). The clause expresses disapproval: ‘this rash bravery coming from a servant (in this speech)...’
segments.s651-g400: سزد‌گر is an evaluative particle/word meaning roughly ‘if it be fitting / as is proper’ (the agentive sense in the list is extended here to the modal sense ‘fitting’). نپیچی is the negative 2nd‑person form of پیچیدن ‘to turn’: ن‑ + پیچی = ‘do not turn/you should not turn’ (jussive/imperative flavor). تو is the address ‘you’. از داد روی = ‘from the way/face of justice’ — از = ‘from’, داد = ‘justice’, روی = ‘face/path’. Together: ‘If it is fitting, do not turn you away from the path of justice.’
segments.s651-g961: نگارنده is an agentive noun 'the writer/creator' functioning as the subject. برکشیده is a past participle / perfect of برکشیدن ('to lift, raise') used attributively (no auxiliary present because of poetic compression), and سپهر is its object 'the sky/heavens'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect (subject + participle + object) instead of a prose construction like «نگارنده سپهر را برکشیده است».
segments.s651-g962: کزویست is a contracted, archaic relative phrase (= «که از وی است») meaning 'that/which is from him' (the copula est is fused in). The following nouns are coordinated objects: پرخاش (hostility/assault), آرام (calm, tranquility), and مهر (love/affection); they are linked by و. The phrase says these things 'are from him'.
segments.s651-g963: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in/within the world' (به + گیتی). یکی را is the indefinite/particular object 'one person' marked by را. کند is the present/habitual third-person singular of کردن 'makes/does' (classical narrative present), and تاجور is the predicate noun 'merchant'; together: 'he makes one a merchant' (verb + predicative noun construction).
segments.s651-g964: وزو is a contracted colloquial/poetic form of «و ز او» ('and from him' / 'and by him'); here it links to the previous verb so the verb is understood rather than repeated. به یکی پیش او با کمر is a sequence of prepositional/nominal phrases: به یکی ('to/for another one'), پیش او ('before him/in his presence' — i.e. in rank before others), با کمر ('with a girdle' — the belt as badge of office). The clause therefore continues the idea: 'and from him to another [he makes/sets] before him with a girdle.'
segments.s651-g965: اگر is the conditional 'if'. خود is emphatic 'the very / itself'. سپهر روان is a noun + adjective/participle 'the moving/flowing heavens' (روان here = 'moving/flowing'); word order is compressed for poetry. زان تست is an archaic compound meaning roughly 'that is yours' (زان = 'from that/therefore'; تست here is the old fused copula 'تو + است' indicating 'it is yours' — note this is an archaic contraction rather than the modern word 'test').
segments.s651-g966: سر مشتری is a genitival/compound phrase 'the head (or chief) of Jupiter' (مشتری = the planet/star Jupiter). زیر فرمان is a fixed prepositional-predicate 'under the command/control of'. تست again is the archaic copula 'is yours' (second-person possession/predication). The whole clause states: 'the head of Jupiter is under your command.'
segments.s651-g967: به دیوان is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the diwan' (here 'the annals/records'). نگه کن (imperative of نگاه کردن) is 'look!' or 'consult!' and که introduces the following content clause 'that/for'. رومی‌نژاد is an adjectival/nominal phrase 'of Roman descent' serving as the subject of the clause begun by که.
segments.s651-g968: به تخم کیان is a beneficiary/goal phrase 'to the seed/offspring of the Kians' (تخم = 'seed/offspring', کیان = 'the Kayanian kings'). باژ is an archaic noun (here 'cry/noise' in the lexicon provided) functioning as the object, followed by the adverb هرگز 'never' and the past negative verb نداد 'did not give'. Grammatically this is a simple past negation: 'they never gave (it) to the seed of the Kians.'
segments.s651-g1441: The segment begins with the prepositional phrase 'ازان مرز' ('from that frontier'). 'چون' here functions conjunctionally ('when/once'); it introduces the subordinate clause 'رفتن آمدش رای' in which the infinitive/masdar notion 'رفتن' ('leaving/going') is the content of the counsel. 'آمدش' is past tense 'came' with the enclitic -ش 'to him', so literally 'the counsel to leave came to him.' 'رای' is used as 'counsel/plan.' Word order is literary: prepositional phrase + temporal conjunction + predicate clause with verb carrying the dative enclitic.
segments.s651-g1442: This is the action that follows the coming of the counsel: 'بسپرد' (past tense 'entrusted/handed over') takes 'جای' ('place/post/position') as its object. 'به شیروی بهرام' is the indirect object introduced by 'به' ('to/unto'): 'to the lion-like Bahram.' Note the adjective-like epithet 'شیروی' precedes the name and 'بسپرد' is the finite past verb agreeing with the implied subject from the previous clause.
segments.s651-g1443: 'بدو گفت' is a compact phrase meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = to him). What follows is an indirect/quoted command: 'کاین باژ قیصر بخواه' — 'that this cry (باژ) "Caesar" be demanded/raised.' 'کاین' ('that/this') introduces the content, 'باژ' is 'cry/shout', 'قیصر' is the word to be cried, and 'بخواه' here is an imperative/command form meaning 'ask for' or 'cause to be sounded.'
segments.s651-g1444: 'مکن' is the negative imperative 'do not.' It governs the noun phrase 'هیچ سستی' ('any slackness/weakness'). The temporal phrase 'به‌روز و به ماه' is idiomatic/poetic for 'by day and by night' (literally 'by day and by moon'); it modifies the prohibition, so the full sense is 'do not show any slackness either by day or by night.' Note the coordinate 'و' linking the two temporal terms.
segments.s651-g1445: Here the verb 'ببوسید' ('he kissed') appears before its subject — a literary inversion; 'شیروی' ('the lion-like one') is the subject and 'روی زمین' ('on the ground') is the object/place. 'روی' functions as a locative preposition/adverb here ('upon/on'), so the phrase means 'he kissed the ground.'
segments.s651-g1446: 'همی‌خواند' is an imperfective/continuous past form meaning 'kept singing/kept crying out.' It takes the following prepositional phrase 'بر شهریار' ('for/to the king') and the interjection/substantive 'آفرین' ('bravo/acclamation') as its object/content: he repeatedly cried out praise for the king. The adverbial 'همی-' marks the ongoing/repeated action in older Persian.
segments.s651-g1447: 'که' introduces a wish or exhortation here. 'بیدار دل باش' is an optative/imprecatory formula where 'باش' is an imperative/optative 'be' ('may your heart be awake'). 'بیدار' modifies 'دل' ('heart/mind'); 'و پیروزبخت' adds 'and fortunate/prosperous.' The whole is a concise blessing/wish addressed to the addressee.
segments.s651-g1448: 'مگر' here functions as an exception/conditional 'unless' or 'except.' The noun phrase that follows, 'داد زرد این کیانی درخت', is a compact poetic group: 'داد' used as the noun 'justice/fate', modified by the adjective 'زرد' ('pale/yellow'), and further specified by the genitive 'این کیانی درخت' ('this Kayanian tree' — i.e., the dynastic 'tree' or lineage). The word order places the noun+adjective before the identifying genitive phrase in a literary style.
